definition: An action is virtuous if and only if (i) it proceeds from a stable disposition of character, (ii) it is done knowingly, and (iii) it is done for its own sake.
conditions: proceeds from stable disposition
done knowingly
done for its own sake
counterexample: A man from a stable disposition of generosity knowingly gives a large sum to a fraudulent organisation for its own sake. All three conditions are met, yet the action is not virtuous.
missing_condition: The object of the virtuous action must be genuine, not illusory.
passage: The proposed analysis holds that an action is virtuous if and only if it proceeds from a stable disposition of character, is done knowingly, and is done for its own sake. Upon examination, however, one may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a man who, from a stable disposition of generosity, knowingly gives a large sum to what he takes to be a charitable organisation, and does so purely for its own sake. Unknown to him, the organisation is fraudulent, and his money serves only to enrich its directors. The three conditions are satisfied — the action proceeds from a stable disposition, is done knowingly, and is done for its own sake — yet we should hesitate to call the action virtuous, for it achieves nothing of value and was directed at a phantom object. The definition, it appears, fails to account for the requirement that the object of the virtuous action be genuine.

definition: A distribution of goods is just if and only if (i) each person receives a share proportional to his contribution, (ii) no person is arbitrarily excluded, and (iii) the distribution is made by a competent authority.
conditions: share proportional to contribution
no arbitrary exclusion
made by competent authority
counterexample: A competent authority distributes wages proportionally and excludes nobody, yet the base rate of compensation is so low that even the most productive worker cannot subsist. The three conditions are met, but the distribution seems unjust.
missing_condition: The distribution must meet a minimum threshold of adequacy for each participant.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a distribution of goods is just if and only if each person receives a share proportional to his contribution, no person is arbitrarily excluded, and the distribution is made by a competent authority. Yet one may imagine a case in which these three conditions are satisfied and the resulting distribution is nonetheless unjust. Suppose a factory owner, recognised as a competent authority, distributes wages in strict proportion to each worker's output, and excludes no one from the arrangement. The proportions are scrupulously observed. But the base rate of compensation is set so low that even the most industrious worker cannot earn enough to feed his family. The three conditions are met — proportionality is maintained, nobody is excluded, and the authority is legitimate — yet one feels strongly that the distribution is unjust. It appears that the definition fails to capture a requirement of minimal adequacy: that the shares, however proportional, must not fall below a threshold beneath which human dignity is affronted.

definition: A person is morally obligated to perform an action if and only if (i) he is aware that another person is in need, (ii) he has the capacity to help, and (iii) the cost to himself is not excessive.
conditions: awareness of need
capacity to help
cost not excessive
counterexample: A physician is aware that a distant stranger needs a kidney, has the surgical capacity to help, and the cost to himself is not excessive. Yet we do not ordinarily say he is morally obligated to donate his kidney to every stranger who needs one.
missing_condition: There must be some special relationship or proximity between the agent and the person in need.
passage: One might propose that a person is morally obligated to perform an action if and only if he is aware that another person is in need, he has the capacity to help, and the cost to himself is not excessive. This analysis, though initially plausible, admits of cases that strain our moral intuitions. Consider a physician who reads in the morning paper that a man in a distant country requires a kidney transplant. The physician is aware of the need, possesses the surgical skill to help, and the cost to himself — the inconvenience of travel, say — is not, by most reckonings, excessive. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that the physician is morally obligated to travel across the world to perform the operation. The definition has failed to capture the role of proximity and special relationship in generating obligations; without such a condition, the analysis would impose upon every capable person an obligation to assist every needy stranger, which seems an unacceptable conclusion.

definition: Consent is valid if and only if (i) it is given voluntarily, (ii) the consenting party is competent, and (iii) it is expressed in a recognised form.
conditions: given voluntarily
party is competent
expressed in recognised form
counterexample: A competent adult voluntarily signs a contract in proper form, but has been given materially false information about what he is consenting to. The three conditions are met, yet his consent is arguably invalid.
missing_condition: The consenting party must be adequately informed about the nature of what he consents to.
passage: It is natural to suppose that consent is valid if and only if it is given voluntarily, the consenting party is competent, and it is expressed in a recognised form. Yet this analysis may be shown to be insufficient. Suppose a competent adult is asked to participate in a medical trial. He is told, falsely, that the trial involves a harmless dietary supplement, when in fact it involves an experimental drug with serious risks. He voluntarily signs the consent form — a recognised instrument — and does so with full mental competence. The three conditions are satisfied: the consent is voluntary, the party is competent, and the form is proper. Yet we should surely deny that his consent is valid, for he has consented to something quite different from what he was led to believe. The definition, it appears, omits a crucial requirement: that the consenting party be adequately informed about the nature of the matter to which he gives his consent.

definition: A person deserves a reward if and only if (i) he has performed a meritorious action, (ii) the action was within his control, and (iii) the reward is proportionate to the merit of the action.
conditions: performed meritorious action
action within his control
reward proportionate to merit
counterexample: A man performs a meritorious action entirely within his control and receives a proportionate reward, but the action was performed solely to obtain the reward and not from any genuine concern for its object. We may doubt whether he truly deserves the reward.
missing_condition: The meritorious action must proceed from an appropriate motive, not merely from desire for the reward itself.
passage: One is tempted to say that a person deserves a reward if and only if he has performed a meritorious action, the action was within his control, and the reward is proportionate to the merit of the action. But consider the following case. A wealthy merchant, hearing that the town council offers a prize for the most generous benefactor, donates a large sum to the local hospital — an indisputably meritorious action, entirely within his control. The prize he receives is proportionate to the merit of his donation. The three conditions are thus satisfied. Yet when it is discovered that the merchant's sole motive was to win the prize, and that he cares nothing for the hospital or its patients, one feels that his desert is diminished. He did the right thing, but for the wrong reason. The analysis, it seems, fails to capture the requirement that desert attaches not merely to the outward action but to the motive from which it springs. A man who does good only for the sake of reward does not deserve the reward in the fullest sense.

definition: An agent is morally blameworthy for an outcome if and only if (i) the agent performed an action that caused the outcome, (ii) the agent could have acted otherwise, and (iii) the agent foresaw, or ought to have foreseen, the outcome.
conditions: agent performed an action causing the outcome
agent could have acted otherwise
agent foresaw or ought to have foreseen the outcome
counterexample: Two motorists each drive home after drinking precisely the same quantity of wine. One arrives without incident; the other strikes a child who darts into the road. Both performed the same action, both could have acted otherwise, and both ought to have foreseen the risk. Yet we judge the second far more blameworthy than the first, though the difference is one of luck alone.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of contingent circumstances beyond the agent's control in determining the degree of blame we attach to identical conduct.
passage: A tempting analysis of moral blame holds that an agent is blameworthy for an outcome if and only if the agent performed an action that caused the outcome, the agent could have acted otherwise, and the agent foresaw, or ought to have foreseen, the outcome in question. Each condition captures something important: we do not blame a man for what he did not cause, nor for what he could not have avoided, nor for what no reasonable person could have anticipated. Yet the analysis proves insufficient when we attend to the phenomenon of moral luck. Consider two motorists, each of whom drives home after drinking precisely the same quantity of wine. Their conduct is identical in every respect within their control. The first arrives home without incident. The second strikes a child who darts unexpectedly into the road. Both performed an action that risked harm; both could have refrained from driving; both ought to have foreseen the danger. The three conditions are satisfied equally in each case. Nevertheless, we should be hard pressed to deny that the second motorist attracts a degree of blame quite out of proportion to that which attaches to the first. The difference lies not in anything the agents did or foresaw, but in the brute contingency of a child's movements — a matter wholly outside the agent's control. The definition, it appears, omits the role that fortune plays in shaping our moral assessments, treating outcomes as though they were entirely within the purview of agency.

definition: An action is morally praiseworthy if and only if (i) it fulfils a moral duty, (ii) it is performed from a motive of duty, and (iii) the agent is aware that the action is required of him.
conditions: action fulfils a moral duty
performed from a motive of duty
agent is aware the action is required
counterexample: A soldier throws himself upon a grenade to save his comrades, knowing full well that no regulation or moral code requires such sacrifice. The action fulfils no duty, yet it is universally regarded as morally praiseworthy in the highest degree.
missing_condition: The definition omits the category of supererogatory acts — those which exceed what duty demands and are praiseworthy precisely because they are not required.
passage: One widely held account of moral praiseworthiness maintains that an action is praiseworthy if and only if it fulfils a moral duty, is performed from a motive of duty, and the agent is aware that the action is required of him. Such an analysis captures the intuition that we praise the man who pays his debts because he recognises his obligation and acts upon it. Yet the analysis is embarrassed by a class of actions that resist subsumption under its conditions. Consider a soldier who, in the heat of battle, throws himself upon a live grenade to shield his comrades. He knows perfectly well that no regulation or code of military conduct requires this of him; no reasonable person would hold that he was under an obligation to sacrifice his life. He acts not from a motive of duty but from something that one might call spontaneous heroism. The first condition — that the action fulfil a moral duty — is plainly unsatisfied, for no duty demands self-immolation. Yet we do not hesitate to call the action praiseworthy; indeed, it is praiseworthy to a degree that surpasses ordinary dutiful conduct. The definition has confined praiseworthiness to the sphere of obligation and thereby excluded precisely those actions which exceed obligation — the supererogatory acts that elicit our deepest admiration.

definition: An action is cowardly if and only if (i) the agent faces a perceived danger, (ii) the agent retreats from or avoids the danger, and (iii) the retreat is motivated by fear.
conditions: agent faces a perceived danger
agent retreats from or avoids the danger
retreat is motivated by fear
counterexample: A man perceives a grave danger to his child, feels intense fear, and retreats — pulling the child away from a collapsing structure. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the action is not cowardly but prudent and even courageous.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the retreat be disproportionate to the danger or that it sacrifice some value that the situation demands one uphold.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an action is cowardly if and only if the agent faces a perceived danger, retreats from or avoids that danger, and does so from a motive of fear. This analysis seems to capture the familiar cases: the soldier who flees the battlefield out of terror, the witness who declines to testify because he fears reprisal. In each instance the three conditions are plainly met. One may construct, however, a case that satisfies the conditions yet fails to constitute cowardice. Imagine a father who perceives that a structure is about to collapse upon his child. He is seized by a powerful fear and retreats from the danger — specifically, he seizes the child and pulls her back from the falling debris. The conditions are met: there is a perceived danger, a retreat from it, and the retreat is motivated by fear. Yet one would hardly describe the father's action as cowardly; on the contrary, it is prudent and protective, perhaps even courageous in its swiftness. What the definition omits is the evaluative dimension: cowardice requires not merely that one retreat from danger out of fear, but that the retreat be disproportionate to the actual threat, or that it involve the sacrifice of some duty or value which the circumstances require one to uphold.

definition: A person is grateful if and only if (i) the person has received a benefit, (ii) the person recognises that another agent is the source of the benefit, and (iii) the person feels positively disposed towards the benefactor.
conditions: person has received a benefit
person recognises another agent as the source
person feels positively disposed towards the benefactor
counterexample: A wealthy man gives alms to a beggar solely to humiliate him in front of onlookers. The beggar receives a benefit, recognises its source, and feels positively disposed towards the giver before learning of the motive. Yet we should not say that gratitude is properly owed, for the benefit was conferred as an instrument of degradation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the benefit be conferred with benevolent intent; gratitude misfires when the apparent benefit is actually a means of harm.
passage: A straightforward analysis of gratitude proposes that a person is grateful if and only if the person has received a benefit, recognises that another agent is its source, and feels positively disposed towards the benefactor. This captures a wide range of ordinary cases: the traveller who thanks the stranger for directions, the patient who is grateful to the physician. Yet the analysis falters when the benefit in question is conferred with malicious intent. Consider a wealthy man who, encountering a beggar in a public square, gives him a conspicuous sum of money — not from generosity, but solely to humiliate him before onlookers, to exhibit the gulf between their stations. The beggar receives a genuine material benefit, recognises its source, and is initially well disposed towards the giver. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet once the motive is understood, one should hesitate to say that gratitude is properly owed, for the act was not a benefit in the morally relevant sense but an instrument of degradation. The definition has omitted the requirement that the benefit be conferred with benevolent intent, or at least without the purpose of causing harm through the very act of giving.

definition: A person has forgiven a wrongdoer if and only if (i) the person was wronged by the other, (ii) the person has ceased to feel resentment towards the wrongdoer, and (iii) the cessation of resentment is a deliberate choice.
conditions: person was wronged by the other
person has ceased to feel resentment
cessation of resentment is a deliberate choice
counterexample: A man is gravely wronged by a colleague. After some years, he deliberately resolves to abandon his resentment — not because he regards the wrong as excused or the wrongdoer as reformed, but purely because the resentment interferes with his sleep. All three conditions are met, yet this seems closer to prudent self-management than to genuine forgiveness.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the cessation of resentment be connected to a moral regard for the wrongdoer rather than to mere self-interest.
passage: One might propose that a person has forgiven a wrongdoer if and only if the person was wronged, has ceased to feel resentment towards the wrongdoer, and the cessation of resentment is a deliberate choice rather than mere forgetfulness. This analysis captures the thought that forgiveness is not something that merely happens to one but something one does, and that it involves the relinquishing of a negative attitude. Yet it may be challenged by a case that satisfies its conditions without amounting to genuine forgiveness. Consider a man who has been gravely wronged by a colleague — defrauded, let us say, of a considerable sum. After several years the man finds that his persistent resentment is ruining his sleep and his digestion. He therefore makes a deliberate decision to abandon the resentment, employing various techniques of self-discipline to extinguish it. He succeeds. He was wronged, he has ceased to resent, and the cessation was deliberate. Yet we should hesitate to call this forgiveness, for the man has not altered his moral attitude towards the wrongdoer in any way; he has merely rid himself of a disagreeable emotion for reasons of personal comfort. Forgiveness, it appears, requires something the definition omits — that the relinquishing of resentment proceed from some form of moral regard for the offender, such as recognition of a shared humanity, rather than from mere self-interest.

definition: A person is honest if and only if (i) the person's assertions correspond to what the person believes, (ii) the person does not deliberately omit material information, and (iii) the person intends that the hearer form an accurate picture of the relevant facts.
conditions: assertions correspond to beliefs
no deliberate omission of material information
intention that the hearer form an accurate picture
counterexample: A physician believes her patient is terminally ill and communicates this fully, omitting nothing material, intending that the patient's family grasp the truth. Yet she delivers the news with callous bluntness in a public corridor, showing no regard for the manner of disclosure. All three conditions are met, yet one might question whether such conduct deserves to be called honest in the virtue-laden sense we ordinarily intend.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the honest person display appropriate regard for the context and manner of truth-telling, not merely its content.
passage: One natural account of honesty holds that a person is honest if and only if the person's assertions correspond to what the person believes, the person does not deliberately omit material information, and the person intends that the hearer form an accurate picture of the relevant facts. This analysis works well for familiar cases: the merchant who quotes his true price, the witness who recounts what she saw. Yet one may construct a case in which the three conditions are satisfied and yet the label of honesty fits awkwardly. Consider a physician who believes that her patient is terminally ill. She states this belief plainly, omits nothing of material relevance, and intends that the patient's family should form an accurate picture of the prognosis. All three conditions are met. But she delivers the news with callous bluntness in a crowded hospital corridor, within earshot of strangers, displaying no regard whatsoever for the manner of her disclosure. One might observe that whilst every propositional condition of the definition is satisfied, the person's conduct lacks the quality of integrity and care that we associate with honesty as a character trait. Honesty, it seems, involves not merely the accurate transmission of information but an appropriate sensitivity to the context and manner in which truth is communicated.

definition: A person is loyal to another if and only if (i) the person has a standing relationship with the other, (ii) the person acts in the other's interest when doing so is costly to himself, and (iii) the person does so because of the relationship.
conditions: standing relationship with the other
acts in the other's interest at personal cost
does so because of the relationship
counterexample: A member of a criminal gang has a standing relationship with the gang's leader, acts against his own interest by refusing to testify even when offered immunity, and does so precisely because of their relationship. All three conditions are met, yet we should be reluctant to call this loyalty in the morally commendable sense.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the object of loyalty and the actions it demands be morally permissible.
passage: An appealing analysis holds that a person is loyal to another if and only if the person has a standing relationship with the other, acts in the other's interest when doing so is costly to himself, and does so because of the relationship. This captures much of what we mean by loyalty: the friend who defends one's reputation at risk to his own, the servant who remains with his master through misfortune. Each displays costly action sustained by an enduring bond. Yet consider a member of a criminal organisation who has a close and long-standing relationship with the organisation's leader. When apprehended, he refuses to give evidence against his associates, even though immunity is offered and his silence will result in a severe sentence. He acts against his own interest, and he does so because of the relationship. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be strange to commend this as loyalty in the morally praiseworthy sense; we might call it loyalty of a sort, but of a debased kind that is better described as complicity. The definition, it appears, has failed to incorporate any constraint upon the moral character of the relationship or the actions it demands. Loyalty, if it is to be a virtue, must be loyalty to something that is not itself vicious.

definition: An action is courageous if and only if (i) the agent faces a significant risk of harm, (ii) the agent acts despite awareness of the risk, and (iii) the action is voluntary.
conditions: agent faces significant risk of harm
agent acts despite awareness of the risk
action is voluntary
counterexample: A reckless youth, fully aware of the danger, voluntarily leaps from a great height into shallow water on a wager. He faces significant risk, acts despite his awareness of it, and does so freely. Yet this is not courage but foolhardiness.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the risk be undertaken for a worthy end or in service of some good.
passage: A natural definition proposes that an action is courageous if and only if the agent faces a significant risk of harm, acts despite awareness of the risk, and does so voluntarily. These conditions seem adequate to distinguish the courageous from the timid: the fireman who enters a burning building, the reformer who speaks against a tyrant. In each case, the agent faces danger, knows it, and acts freely. Yet the analysis is too generous in what it admits. Consider a reckless youth who, on a wager, voluntarily leaps from a bridge into shallow water. He faces a significant risk — he may well break his neck. He is fully aware of this and acts of his own free will. The three conditions are met. Yet we should not call this courage; it is foolhardiness or mere bravado, the reckless courting of danger without purpose. What the definition has failed to capture is the teleological dimension of courage: that the risk be undertaken in service of some end that warrants it, be that the safety of others, the defence of principle, or the pursuit of a genuine good. Without this constraint, every act of voluntary risk-taking qualifies as courageous, however pointless or foolish.

definition: An action is benevolent if and only if (i) it is directed at promoting the well-being of another, (ii) the agent intends to benefit the other, and (iii) the action in fact produces a benefit.
conditions: directed at promoting another's well-being
agent intends to benefit the other
action produces a benefit
counterexample: A domineering parent, intending to benefit his adult child, arranges the child's marriage against her wishes. The marriage happens to prove fortunate, and the child's material condition improves. All three conditions are met, yet the paternalistic interference is hardly benevolence in any proper sense.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the requirement that genuine benevolence respect the autonomy and wishes of the beneficiary.
passage: It might be proposed that an action is benevolent if and only if it is directed at promoting the well-being of another, the agent intends to benefit the other, and the action in fact produces a benefit. This analysis captures the standard cases: the philanthropist who builds a hospital, the neighbour who brings food to the infirm. In each, there is an other-directed intention and a resulting good. Yet the analysis admits cases that one would be reluctant to call benevolent. Suppose a domineering parent, genuinely intending to benefit his adult child, arranges her marriage without her consent, selecting a partner of good fortune and character. By coincidence, the marriage proves happy, and the child's material circumstances improve markedly. The action was directed at the child's well-being, was intended as a benefit, and did in fact produce one. All three conditions are satisfied. But one should hesitate to call paternalistic interference of this sort benevolent. It treats the beneficiary as an object to be managed rather than an agent whose own wishes and judgement deserve consideration. The definition, it appears, has overlooked a condition essential to benevolence properly understood — namely, that the action respect, or at least not flagrantly disregard, the autonomy of the person one purports to benefit.

definition: An action is cruel if and only if (i) it causes suffering to another being, (ii) the agent is aware that it causes suffering, and (iii) the agent could have avoided the action.
conditions: action causes suffering to another being
agent is aware it causes suffering
agent could have avoided the action
counterexample: A surgeon amputates a patient's gangrenous limb, knowing that the procedure will cause great suffering and that he could have declined to operate. All three conditions are met, yet the action is not cruel but merciful.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the suffering be inflicted without adequate justification or with indifference to the victim's welfare.
passage: One might define cruelty as follows: an action is cruel if and only if it causes suffering to another being, the agent is aware that it causes suffering, and the agent could have avoided the action. This analysis has an initial plausibility, capturing cases such as the man who torments an animal for amusement or the tyrant who orders a punishment he knows to be excessive. Each involves avoidable, consciously inflicted suffering. Yet the definition is too broad. Consider a surgeon who must amputate a patient's gangrenous limb. The operation causes acute suffering, and the surgeon is well aware of this. He could, moreover, have declined to operate, leaving the patient to die of sepsis. The three conditions are fully satisfied: suffering is caused, its occurrence is foreseen, and the action might have been avoided. Yet it would be absurd to call the operation cruel; it is, on the contrary, an act of mercy. What the definition has failed to incorporate is the motive or justification of the agent. Cruelty requires not merely the knowing infliction of avoidable suffering, but the infliction of suffering without adequate justification — or, more precisely, with indifference to, or even delight in, the suffering of the other.

definition: A person possesses a right to X if and only if (i) the person has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) others have a correlative duty not to interfere with the person's pursuit of X, and (iii) the duty is recognised by the prevailing social or legal order.
conditions: person has a legitimate interest in X
others have a correlative duty of non-interference
duty is recognised by the prevailing social or legal order
counterexample: A slave in a society where slavery is lawful has a legitimate interest in his own liberty, and one might argue that others have a correlative duty not to enslave him. Yet the prevailing legal order does not recognise this duty. On this definition, the slave has no right to liberty — a conclusion most moral philosophers would reject.
missing_condition: The definition makes rights entirely dependent upon social recognition, leaving no room for natural or moral rights that obtain independently of whether they are acknowledged.
passage: A familiar analysis holds that a person possesses a right to X if and only if the person has a legitimate interest in X, others have a correlative duty not to interfere with the person's pursuit of X, and that duty is recognised by the prevailing social or legal order. This analysis accommodates the standard examples: the citizen's right to own property, which is backed by interest, correlative duty, and legal recognition; the right of free worship in a tolerant state. Yet it proves inadequate when pressed against harder cases. Consider a man held in bondage in a society where slavery is lawful and fully accepted. He has, we should suppose, a legitimate interest in his own liberty — as legitimate as any interest a person can have. It is likewise plausible that others have a duty not to enslave him; the wrongness of slavery, one might urge, does not depend upon its legal status. But the third condition fails: the prevailing order does not recognise any such duty. On the proposed analysis, therefore, the slave possesses no right to liberty. This conclusion is one that few moral philosophers would accept, and it reveals the deficiency of making rights contingent upon social recognition. The analysis leaves no space for natural or moral rights — rights that a person possesses simply in virtue of his humanity, whether or not the surrounding order sees fit to acknowledge them.

definition: A person has a duty to perform an action if and only if (i) the action is prescribed by a moral rule applicable to the person's situation, (ii) the person is capable of performing it, and (iii) failure to perform it would constitute a moral fault.
conditions: action is prescribed by an applicable moral rule
person is capable of performing it
failure would constitute a moral fault
counterexample: A man has promised to meet a friend for luncheon, and a moral rule prescribes keeping one's promises. He is capable of keeping the appointment, and failure would normally be a fault. But on his way he encounters a stranger suffering a heart attack; stopping to help means breaking the promise. Two duties conflict, and the definition offers no guidance for resolution.
missing_condition: The definition treats each duty in isolation and fails to provide a principle of priority when two genuine duties conflict.
passage: A familiar account proposes that a person has a duty to perform an action if and only if the action is prescribed by a moral rule applicable to the person's situation, the person is capable of performing it, and failure to perform it would constitute a moral fault. This analysis yields clear verdicts in simple cases: a man who has borrowed money has a duty to repay it, for the rule against default applies to his situation, he is able to pay, and failure to do so would be a fault. Yet the analysis proves inadequate in cases of moral conflict. Suppose a man has promised to meet a friend for luncheon at a specified time. A moral rule prescribes the keeping of promises; he is capable of keeping the appointment; and failure to do so would ordinarily be a fault. On his way, however, he encounters a stranger who has collapsed in the street and appears to be suffering a seizure. A moral rule also prescribes rendering assistance to those in peril; he is capable of helping; and failure to do so would also be a fault. He cannot do both. The definition assigns him two duties simultaneously but provides no principle for adjudicating between them. It treats each duty as though it existed in isolation, failing to account for the structure of moral priority that must govern cases where obligations collide.

definition: A person is temperate if and only if (i) he moderates his appetites, (ii) he does so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) he experiences no conflict in doing so.
conditions: moderates his appetites
does so from settled disposition
experiences no conflict in doing so
counterexample: A recovering drunkard who has, through years of discipline, achieved a settled disposition of sobriety moderates his appetite for drink from a stable character trait. Yet he continues to experience temptation — a quiet but persistent conflict between desire and resolution. The third condition fails. But we should not hesitate to call such a man temperate; indeed, his temperance is the more admirable for being maintained in the face of ongoing temptation.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the temperate man experience no conflict, but temperance may coexist with residual desire; what is essential is that the agent reliably governs his appetites, not that he has altogether extinguished them.
passage: One might define temperance as follows: a person is temperate if and only if he moderates his appetites, he does so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and he experiences no conflict in doing so. The third condition is intended to distinguish the truly temperate man from the merely continent one — the man who controls his desires only by an act of will. The truly temperate man, on this account, feels no pull towards excess. This definition captures the Aristotelian ideal admirably. Yet it excludes a figure whom we should be reluctant to deny the title of temperate. Consider a man who was, for many years, given to excessive drink, and who has, through sustained effort and discipline, achieved a settled disposition of sobriety. He moderates his appetite reliably and from a stable character trait; the first two conditions are satisfied. But he continues to experience temptation. The desire for drink has not been extinguished; it persists as a quiet but real conflict within him. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that his temperance is the more admirable for being maintained against this residual desire. To deny him the name of temperate because he still feels the pull of appetite is to restrict the virtue to those of unusually fortunate constitution and to exclude those who have achieved it through moral labour.

definition: A person acts prudently if and only if (i) he acts to promote his own long-term welfare, (ii) he acts on the basis of careful deliberation, and (iii) his action is successful in achieving its aim.
conditions: acts to promote own long-term welfare
acts on basis of careful deliberation
action successful in achieving its aim
counterexample: A man carefully deliberates and, after weighing all available evidence, invests his savings in a venture that is by every reasonable measure sound. He acts to promote his long-term welfare and does so on the basis of thorough deliberation. Yet the venture fails owing to an unforeseeable catastrophe — an earthquake destroys the enterprise. The third condition fails. But the man acted prudently; the failure was not due to any defect in his reasoning or foresight.
missing_condition: The definition makes success a condition of prudence, but prudence is a quality of deliberation, not a guarantee of outcome. A prudent man may fail through sheer misfortune; what matters is the quality of his practical reasoning, not the contingent result.
passage: One might propose that a person acts prudently if and only if he acts to promote his own long-term welfare, he acts on the basis of careful deliberation, and his action is successful in achieving its aim. The third condition is meant to exclude the man who deliberates wisely but through laziness or cowardice fails to carry out his plans effectively. The definition captures the prudent investor who studies the market and profits, the prudent farmer who reads the weather and brings in his harvest, the prudent traveller who plans his route and arrives safely. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with the role of fortune. Consider a man who carefully deliberates and, after weighing all available evidence, invests his savings in a venture that is by every reasonable standard sound — the enterprise is well managed, the market is favourable, the risks are modest. He acts to promote his long-term welfare and does so on the basis of thorough deliberation. But the venture is destroyed by an unforeseeable earthquake, and his savings are lost. The third condition fails: his action was not successful. Yet the man acted prudently by any reasonable assessment. The failure was due to an event that no amount of deliberation could have anticipated. The definition has confused prudence — which is a quality of practical reasoning — with success — which is partly a matter of luck.

definition: A person has integrity if and only if (i) he acts in accordance with his stated principles, (ii) he does not alter his principles under pressure, and (iii) his principles are morally sound.
conditions: acts in accordance with stated principles
does not alter principles under pressure
principles are morally sound
counterexample: A committed and unwavering racist who acts consistently upon his racist principles and refuses to alter them under any social or legal pressure satisfies the first two conditions. His principles are not morally sound, and so the third condition fails. Yet if we remove the third condition, we are left with a definition that would ascribe integrity to a man whose consistency is devoted to a vicious end. The definition wavers between integrity as formal consistency and integrity as moral excellence.
missing_condition: The definition attempts to build moral soundness into integrity, but this conflates two distinct assessments: the formal coherence of a person's character and the moral quality of his commitments. A person may have integrity in the sense of wholeness and consistency without having morally sound principles, and the definition cannot serve both purposes at once.
passage: One might define integrity as follows: a person has integrity if and only if he acts in accordance with his stated principles, he does not alter his principles under pressure, and his principles are morally sound. The first two conditions capture the notion of consistency and firmness — the person of integrity is not a hypocrite, and he does not bend to threats or inducements. The third condition is meant to exclude the consistent villain — the man who is steadfastly devoted to wicked ends. This definition captures the man we ordinarily admire as having integrity: the judge who rules impartially despite political pressure, the whistle-blower who speaks the truth despite personal cost. Yet the third condition creates a difficulty. Consider a committed and unwavering racist who acts consistently upon his racist principles and refuses to yield to social or legal pressure. He satisfies the first two conditions fully: he says what he believes and he does what he says, without wavering. The third condition fails, for his principles are not morally sound. The definition correctly excludes him. But now one might observe that there is a sense — a regrettable but intelligible sense — in which even such a man has integrity. He is, at least, not a hypocrite; he is consistent and firm. The definition has attempted to pack two distinct evaluations into one concept: the formal evaluation of consistency and the substantive evaluation of moral content. Integrity, it appears, may be a structural feature of character that is separable from the moral quality of the principles around which that character is organised.

definition: A person feels shame if and only if (i) he has done something he regards as wrong, (ii) he believes that others have witnessed or know of his action, and (iii) he experiences a painful emotion of self-reproach.
conditions: has done something he regards as wrong
believes others have witnessed or know of action
experiences painful emotion of self-reproach
counterexample: A man feels profound shame at his poverty — at his threadbare coat, his inability to provide for his children, his dependence upon the charity of others. He has done nothing wrong; his poverty is the result of illness and misfortune. The first condition fails. Yet his shame is real and acute; it is not guilt at a transgression but shame at a condition he regards as degrading.
missing_condition: The definition ties shame to wrongdoing, but shame may be felt at conditions, attributes, or circumstances that involve no moral fault — poverty, physical deformity, social origin. Shame is a response to perceived degradation or inadequacy, not necessarily to moral transgression.
passage: One might define shame as follows: a person feels shame if and only if he has done something he regards as wrong, he believes that others have witnessed or know of his action, and he experiences a painful emotion of self-reproach. This definition assimilates shame to a species of moral emotion attendant upon wrongdoing, and it captures many familiar cases: the man who cheats and is discovered, the child who lies and is found out, the official who takes a bribe and is exposed. In each case the shame arises from a wrong action witnessed by others. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider a man who feels profound shame at his poverty. His coat is threadbare; he cannot provide for his children; he depends upon the charity of neighbours. He has done nothing wrong — his poverty is the result of a prolonged illness that destroyed his livelihood. The first condition fails entirely. Yet his shame is real and intense. He avoids the gaze of his neighbours; he dreads the pity of his friends; he feels himself diminished and degraded. This is not guilt at a transgression but shame at a condition — a state of affairs that he regards as beneath the dignity he wishes to maintain. The definition has modelled shame upon moral failure, but shame extends to conditions and circumstances that involve no fault whatever: poverty, physical deformity, low social origin, the failures of one's relatives. What shame requires is a sense of falling short of a standard one holds for oneself, whether or not that falling short involves any wrong action.

definition: A person is envious if and only if (i) he desires a good that another possesses, (ii) he believes that the other does not deserve the good, and (iii) he wishes that the other would lose the good.
conditions: desires a good another possesses
believes other does not deserve the good
wishes other would lose the good
counterexample: A young scholar admires a senior colleague's command of ancient languages. He desires this mastery intensely and is pained by the comparison between the colleague's fluency and his own halting efforts. He does not believe the colleague undeserving — on the contrary, he acknowledges that the mastery was earned through decades of study. Nor does he wish the colleague to lose his skill. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the young scholar's emotion is recognisably envy: the painful awareness of another's superior good that one desires for oneself.
missing_condition: The definition confines envy to its malicious form — the wish that the other lose what he has — but there exists a non-malicious envy, sometimes called emulative envy, in which one is pained by another's superiority without wishing him ill. The definition excludes this common and important variety.
passage: One might propose that a person is envious if and only if he desires a good that another possesses, he believes that the other does not deserve the good, and he wishes that the other would lose the good. This definition captures the paradigm of malicious envy: the man who covets his neighbour's fortune and hopes to see it destroyed. Yet it excludes a variety of envy that is both common and morally significant. Consider a young scholar who admires a senior colleague's command of ancient languages — his fluent reading of Greek and Latin, his easy familiarity with the texts. The young scholar desires this mastery with painful intensity; the comparison between the colleague's fluency and his own halting efforts causes him genuine distress. But he does not believe the colleague undeserving. On the contrary, he acknowledges that the colleague's skill was earned through thirty years of devoted study. Nor does he wish the colleague to lose his command of languages; he bears the man no ill will whatever. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the young scholar's emotion is recognisably envy. It is the painful awareness of a good in another that one desires for oneself — what the older writers sometimes called emulative envy. The definition has restricted envy to its malicious variety and has overlooked the benign but still painful form that drives men to emulation rather than to spite.

definition: An act is charitable if and only if (i) it transfers resources from the giver to a person in need, (ii) the transfer is voluntary, and (iii) the giver expects no return.
conditions: transfers resources from giver to person in need
transfer is voluntary
giver expects no return
counterexample: A man visits a sick neighbour daily, sitting with him through long afternoons, reading to him, and offering companionship. The man transfers no resources — no money, no food, no material goods — but only his time and his presence. The first condition fails if 'resources' is understood in its ordinary material sense. Yet the act is plainly charitable; the giving of time and attention to the suffering is as genuine an expression of charity as the giving of money.
missing_condition: The definition restricts charity to the transfer of material resources, but charity may consist in the giving of time, attention, comfort, or labour — immaterial goods that are no less valuable to the recipient and no less costly to the giver.
passage: A natural definition holds that an act is charitable if and only if it transfers resources from the giver to a person in need, the transfer is voluntary, and the giver expects no return. This captures the familiar cases of charity: the alms given to the beggar, the donation to the hospital, the provision of food to the destitute. In each case material resources are transferred voluntarily and without expectation of return. Yet the definition proves too narrow when one considers the manifold forms that charity may take. A man visits a sick neighbour every afternoon for a period of months. He sits by the bedside, reads aloud, converses on subjects of interest to the patient, and offers the simple comfort of human presence. He transfers no material resources — no money, no food, no clothing. What he gives is his time, his attention, and his companionship. The first condition fails if 'resources' is understood in its ordinary material sense. Yet the act is plainly charitable, and the sick man values this companionship more highly than any material gift. The definition has modelled charity upon almsgiving — the transfer of tangible goods — and has thereby excluded the charity of presence, attention, and personal devotion, which may be the more demanding and the more valuable form.

definition: An act is merciful if and only if (i) the agent has the power to inflict a deserved punishment, (ii) the agent refrains from inflicting the full punishment, and (iii) the agent is moved by compassion for the offender.
conditions: agent has power to inflict deserved punishment
agent refrains from inflicting full punishment
agent moved by compassion for offender
counterexample: A king pardons a condemned traitor, not from compassion — for he despises the man — but from a calculated judgement that clemency will secure the loyalty of the traitor's followers. The king has the power to punish, and he refrains from inflicting the punishment. But he is not moved by compassion; his motive is purely political. The third condition fails, yet the act is widely described as merciful.
missing_condition: The definition requires compassion as the motive of mercy, but mercy may be extended for reasons of policy, magnanimity, or principled restraint that have nothing to do with fellow-feeling. What is essential is the voluntary withholding of deserved punishment, not the particular emotion that prompts it.
passage: One might define mercy as follows: an act is merciful if and only if the agent has the power to inflict a deserved punishment, the agent refrains from inflicting the full punishment, and the agent is moved by compassion for the offender. The definition captures the sentimental picture of mercy: the judge who weeps as he pardons, the father who cannot bring himself to chastise a beloved child. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider a king who pardons a condemned traitor. The king has full power to execute the man; the punishment is deserved; the evidence is clear. But the king is not moved by compassion. He despises the traitor and feels no pity for him whatever. His motive is purely political: the traitor's family commands a powerful faction, and clemency will secure their loyalty at a critical moment. The king refrains from inflicting the punishment, but he does so from calculation, not from fellow-feeling. The third condition fails. Yet the act is described as merciful by the court, by the chroniclers, and by the traitor's grateful family. The definition has tied mercy to a particular motive — compassion — when in practice mercy may be exercised from magnanimity, from policy, or from a principled belief in restraint. What is essential to mercy is the voluntary withholding of deserved punishment, not the particular emotion that attends the withholding.

definition: A person acts autonomously if and only if (i) he acts from his own will, (ii) he is free from external coercion, and (iii) he has considered and could have chosen alternative courses of action.
conditions: acts from own will
free from external coercion
has considered and could have chosen alternatives
counterexample: A man with a severe phobia of heights refuses to cross a bridge. He acts from his own will — no one compels him — and he is free from external coercion. But he has not genuinely considered the alternative of crossing; his phobia renders the alternative psychologically unavailable to him. He could not, in any realistic sense, have chosen to cross. The third condition fails. Yet one might argue that his refusal is autonomous — it is, after all, his own settled response, expressive of who he is.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agent could have chosen otherwise, but psychological constraints — phobias, compulsions, deeply ingrained habits — may restrict the alternatives genuinely available to an agent without thereby rendering his actions non-autonomous. The boundary between a constraint that undermines autonomy and one that merely shapes it is not captured by the definition.
passage: One might propose that a person acts autonomously if and only if he acts from his own will, he is free from external coercion, and he has considered and could have chosen alternative courses of action. The definition captures the paradigm of autonomous action: the voter who weighs the candidates and casts his ballot freely, the consumer who compares goods and makes his purchase without compulsion. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with psychological constraints that are neither external nor coercive in the ordinary sense. Consider a man with a severe phobia of heights. He refuses to cross a bridge that is the only route to a certain destination. He acts from his own will — no other person compels him. He is free from external coercion — no threat, no force, no inducement is brought to bear upon him. But he has not genuinely considered the alternative of crossing the bridge; his phobia renders that alternative psychologically unavailable. The very thought of crossing produces such intense anxiety that deliberation is foreclosed. The third condition fails: he could not, in any realistic sense, have chosen otherwise. Yet there is a sense in which his refusal is autonomous — it is his own settled response, expressive of his psychology and his values. The definition has drawn the line between autonomy and its absence at the availability of alternatives, but it has not reckoned with the many forms of internal constraint that restrict alternatives without constituting external coercion.

definition: An action is paternalistic if and only if (i) it restricts the liberty of another person, (ii) the restriction is imposed for that person's own good, and (iii) the restriction is imposed without that person's consent.
conditions: restricts liberty of another person
restriction imposed for person's own good
restriction imposed without consent
counterexample: A wife, knowing that her husband is about to make a disastrous business decision, withholds from him a letter containing the offer he intends to accept, and replaces it with a forged letter declining the offer. She does not restrict his liberty — he is free to go where he pleases, to make telephone calls, to visit the other party. What she restricts is his access to information. The first condition fails in its literal sense, for no liberty of movement or action is curtailed. Yet her action is paradigmatically paternalistic: she manipulates his circumstances for what she takes to be his own good, without his consent.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paternalism to interferences with liberty, but paternalism may operate through manipulation of information, alteration of circumstances, or deceptive influence that leaves the agent's formal liberty intact while undermining his capacity for informed choice.
passage: A natural definition of paternalism holds that an action is paternalistic if and only if it restricts the liberty of another person, the restriction is imposed for that person's own good, and the restriction is imposed without that person's consent. This captures the familiar cases: the state that forbids the use of certain drugs for the citizen's own health, the parent who locks the door to prevent a child from running into the street. In each case liberty is curtailed, the motive is benevolent, and consent is absent. Yet the definition proves too narrow. Consider a wife who knows that her husband is about to accept a disastrous business offer. Rather than forbidding him or physically restraining him, she intercepts the letter containing the offer and replaces it with a forged letter declining on his behalf. The husband's liberty is not restricted in any ordinary sense: he is free to go where he pleases, to make his own decisions, to act as he sees fit. What has been restricted is his access to accurate information. He acts freely but upon a false picture of the world that his wife has deliberately constructed. The first condition fails if 'liberty' is understood as freedom of action. Yet the wife's behaviour is paradigmatically paternalistic. She has manipulated his circumstances for what she takes to be his own good, without his knowledge or consent. The definition has modelled paternalism upon coercive interference and has thereby overlooked the manipulative and deceptive varieties, which may be equally objectionable.

definition: A transaction is exploitative if and only if (i) one party benefits disproportionately, (ii) the disadvantaged party had no reasonable alternative, and (iii) the advantaged party was aware of the disparity.
conditions: one party benefits disproportionately
disadvantaged party had no reasonable alternative
advantaged party aware of disparity
counterexample: A skilled negotiator purchases a rare painting from a wealthy collector for well below its market value. The collector had many alternatives — other buyers, auction houses, dealers — and chose freely to accept the low offer because he wished to dispose of the painting quickly. The second condition fails. Yet one might still hold that the transaction is exploitative, for the negotiator took advantage of the collector's impatience to extract a price that did not reflect the painting's worth.
missing_condition: The definition requires the absence of alternatives, but exploitation may occur even when the disadvantaged party has alternatives, provided the advantaged party takes unfair advantage of some vulnerability — impatience, ignorance, emotional distress — that leads the disadvantaged party to accept terms he would otherwise reject.
passage: One might define exploitation as follows: a transaction is exploitative if and only if one party benefits disproportionately, the disadvantaged party had no reasonable alternative, and the advantaged party was aware of the disparity. The definition captures the paradigmatic cases: the employer who pays starvation wages because the workers have no other source of employment, the landlord who charges extortionate rent because the tenants have nowhere else to live. In each case the disadvantaged party is trapped, and the advantaged party knowingly profits from the entrapment. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty when one considers cases in which the disadvantaged party has alternatives but is nonetheless taken advantage of. A skilled negotiator purchases a rare painting from a wealthy collector for well below its market value. The collector is wealthy; he has many alternatives — he could sell at auction, consult a dealer, or simply retain the painting. He chose freely to accept the low price because he wished to be rid of the painting quickly, perhaps owing to a divorce or a change of taste. The second condition fails. Yet one might plausibly hold that the negotiator exploited the collector's impatience. He recognised a vulnerability — not economic desperation but emotional urgency — and extracted terms that did not reflect the painting's worth. The definition has tied exploitation to the absence of alternatives, when exploitation may also consist in the unfair leveraging of any vulnerability, even in circumstances where alternatives exist.

definition: A person is complicit in a wrongful act if and only if (i) he contributes causally to the act, (ii) he intends to assist the wrongdoer, and (iii) he knows that the act is wrongful.
conditions: contributes causally to the act
intends to assist the wrongdoer
knows the act is wrongful
counterexample: A locksmith sells a set of lock-picks to a man who, unbeknownst to the locksmith, uses them to commit a burglary. The locksmith contributes causally to the act — without the picks, the burglary might not have occurred. But the locksmith neither intended to assist in any wrongful act nor knew that the purchaser would use the picks for burglary. The second and third conditions fail. Yet some would argue that a person may be complicit through reckless indifference — selling burglary tools to a stranger without inquiry — even if he lacks specific intent and knowledge.
missing_condition: The definition requires both intention and knowledge, but complicity may arise from reckless indifference or wilful blindness — the deliberate failure to inquire into uses that a reasonable person would foresee. The definition is too narrow to capture these morally significant forms of participation.
passage: One might define complicity as follows: a person is complicit in a wrongful act if and only if he contributes causally to the act, he intends to assist the wrongdoer, and he knows that the act is wrongful. This captures the standard case of the accomplice: the lookout who watches for the police, the driver who waits with the engine running, the clerk who falsifies the records. Each contributes causally, each intends to help, and each knows that the act is wrong. Yet the definition is too restrictive. Consider a locksmith who sells a set of specialised lock-picks to a stranger. The stranger uses the picks to commit a burglary. The locksmith contributed causally — without the picks, the burglary might not have occurred. But the locksmith did not intend to assist in any wrongful act; he merely wished to make a sale. And he did not know that the purchaser would use the picks for burglary; the thought may not have crossed his mind. The second and third conditions fail. Yet there is a sense in which the locksmith's conduct is morally troubling. He sold instruments of burglary to a stranger without making any inquiry into the stranger's purposes. A reasonable man might have asked questions, might have exercised some caution. The locksmith's failure to inquire — his reckless indifference to the foreseeable use of his wares — seems a form of complicity that the definition, with its requirements of intention and knowledge, cannot capture.

definition: An act is a reparation if and only if (i) it compensates a victim for a wrong suffered, (ii) the compensation is proportional to the harm, and (iii) it is performed by the person who committed the wrong.
conditions: compensates victim for wrong suffered
compensation proportional to harm
performed by person who committed wrong
counterexample: A government provides financial compensation to the descendants of persons enslaved two centuries ago. The original wrongdoers are long dead; the compensation is not performed by those who committed the wrong. The third condition fails. Yet many hold that such compensation is a genuine reparation — that the obligation to make amends may pass from the wrongdoer to his successors or to the institutions that perpetuated the wrong.
missing_condition: The definition restricts reparation to acts performed by the original wrongdoer, but reparative obligations may devolve upon successors, inheritors, or institutions, particularly when the wrong was systemic and its benefits have been transmitted across generations.
passage: One might propose that an act is a reparation if and only if it compensates a victim for a wrong suffered, the compensation is proportional to the harm, and it is performed by the person who committed the wrong. This captures the straightforward cases: the man who breaks a window pays for a new one, the debtor who defaults makes good the loss, the thief who steals returns the stolen property. In each case the wrongdoer himself makes the injured party whole. Yet the definition proves too restrictive when one considers historic injustices. A government provides financial compensation to the descendants of persons who were enslaved two centuries ago. The original enslavers are dead; no living person committed the original wrong. The third condition fails. Yet many thoughtful people hold that such compensation is a genuine reparation — that the obligation to make amends does not expire with the death of the wrongdoer but may pass to his successors, to his heirs, or to the institutions and polities that perpetuated and profited from the wrong. The definition has restricted reparation to a transaction between the original wrongdoer and the original victim, but in the case of systemic and enduring injustices, both parties may have been replaced by successors who inherit the relevant obligations and claims.

definition: A person is autonomous if and only if (i) his actions are governed by reasons he endorses upon reflection, (ii) he is not subject to manipulation by others, and (iii) he possesses accurate information about his circumstances.
conditions: actions governed by reasons he endorses on reflection
not subject to manipulation by others
possesses accurate information about circumstances
counterexample: A devout monk voluntarily enters a monastic order and submits to the authority of a superior who directs every aspect of his daily life. The monk acts on reasons he endorses upon reflection — he believes the monastic life is his vocation — and he possesses accurate information. But he is subject to the direction of another person in a manner that closely resembles manipulation, for the superior controls his schedule, his diet, his reading, and his associations. The second condition fails or wavers. Yet the monk's submission is itself a supreme exercise of autonomy — he has freely chosen to subordinate his will.
missing_condition: The definition treats all forms of direction by others as threats to autonomy, but a person may autonomously choose to submit to the authority of another. The definition cannot distinguish between manipulation that undermines autonomy and voluntary subordination that expresses it.
passage: One might hold that a person is autonomous if and only if his actions are governed by reasons he endorses upon reflection, he is not subject to manipulation by others, and he possesses accurate information about his circumstances. The definition captures the liberal ideal of the self-governing individual: the man who thinks for himself, forms his own judgements, and acts upon them without interference. Yet it encounters a difficulty with cases of voluntary submission. Consider a devout monk who, after years of reflection and spiritual inquiry, enters a monastic order and submits to the authority of a superior. The superior directs every aspect of his daily life: when he rises, what he eats, what he reads, how he spends his hours. The monk acts on reasons he endorses upon reflection — he believes that obedience is the path to spiritual perfection — and he possesses accurate information about the nature of monastic life. But he is subject to the pervasive direction of another person, and this direction might well be described as a form of control that borders on manipulation. The second condition fails, or at best wavers. Yet the monk's submission is itself a supreme act of autonomy. He has freely chosen, after mature deliberation, to subordinate his will to that of his superior. The definition cannot distinguish this voluntary subordination — which is an expression of autonomy — from the coercive manipulation that genuinely undermines it.

definition: An intervention is paternalistic if and only if (i) it overrides the expressed preferences of the person affected, (ii) it is motivated by concern for that person's welfare, and (iii) the person affected is a competent adult.
conditions: overrides expressed preferences of person affected
motivated by concern for person's welfare
person affected is competent adult
counterexample: A government imposes a mandatory seatbelt law. Many citizens have no expressed preference either way — they have never considered the matter and have no opinion to override. The first condition fails for such citizens, since no expressed preference is overridden. Yet the law is paradigmatically paternalistic; it compels a course of action for the citizen's own safety regardless of whether the citizen has expressed any contrary preference.
missing_condition: The definition requires that an expressed preference be overridden, but paternalistic intervention may pre-empt preference formation rather than override existing preferences. A law that mandates behaviour before citizens have formed opinions on the matter is no less paternalistic for the absence of articulated dissent.
passage: One might define a paternalistic intervention as one that overrides the expressed preferences of the person affected, is motivated by concern for that person's welfare, and is directed at a competent adult. The third condition excludes the guidance of children, which is not paternalism in the objectionable sense. The definition captures the cases most discussed in the literature: the prohibition of recreational drugs overrides the user's preference to consume them; the requirement of a waiting period before divorce overrides the couple's preference for immediate separation. Yet the definition proves too narrow. Consider a government that imposes a mandatory seatbelt law. A great many citizens have no expressed preference on the matter. They have never thought about whether they wish to wear seatbelts; they have formed no opinion and uttered no dissent. The first condition fails for such citizens, since no expressed preference is overridden. Yet the seatbelt law is paradigmatically paternalistic — it compels a course of action for the citizen's own safety, regardless of the citizen's wishes. The definition has modelled paternalism upon the overriding of articulated dissent, but much paternalistic legislation operates pre-emptively, imposing requirements before citizens have had occasion to form or express contrary preferences. The absence of expressed dissent does not make the intervention any less paternalistic.

definition: A relationship is exploitative if and only if (i) one party systematically benefits from the other's labour, (ii) the benefiting party has superior bargaining power, and (iii) the exchange is governed by a contract or agreement.
conditions: one party systematically benefits from other's labour
benefiting party has superior bargaining power
exchange governed by contract or agreement
counterexample: A family keeps an elderly relative as an unpaid domestic servant. The relative cooks, cleans, and tends the children, while the family provides nothing beyond bare subsistence. There is no contract or agreement — the arrangement is tacit, enforced by the relative's dependence and the family's indifference. The third condition fails. Yet the relationship is manifestly exploitative; the absence of a formal agreement makes the exploitation worse, not better.
missing_condition: The definition requires a governing contract, but some of the most egregious forms of exploitation — domestic servitude, familial coercion, informal labour arrangements — operate entirely outside the framework of contract. The definition has modelled exploitation on the labour market and has thereby excluded exploitation in the domestic and informal spheres.
passage: One might propose that a relationship is exploitative if and only if one party systematically benefits from the other's labour, the benefiting party has superior bargaining power, and the exchange is governed by a contract or agreement. The definition captures the standard cases of workplace exploitation: the factory owner who pays subsistence wages to labourers who have no alternative employment, the landlord who extracts excessive rents from tenants who have nowhere else to live. In each case a contract exists, one party benefits disproportionately, and the other is disadvantaged by an imbalance of bargaining power. Yet the definition proves too narrow. Consider a family that keeps an elderly relative as an unpaid domestic servant. The relative cooks, cleans, tends the children, and performs all the household tasks, while the family provides nothing beyond bare room and board. There is no contract or agreement — no terms have been set, no wages discussed, no duties formally assigned. The arrangement is tacit, sustained by the relative's economic dependence and social isolation. The third condition fails entirely. Yet the relationship is manifestly exploitative, and the absence of a formal agreement makes the exploitation worse, not better, for the relative has no terms to invoke, no obligations she can demand be honoured. The definition has modelled exploitation upon the contractual labour relationship and has thereby excluded the vast domain of informal and domestic exploitation.

definition: A character trait is a virtue if and only if (i) it is a stable disposition to act in a certain way, (ii) its exercise is beneficial to others, and (iii) the agent who possesses it acts from that disposition voluntarily.
conditions: stable disposition to act in a certain way
exercise is beneficial to others
agent acts from the disposition voluntarily
counterexample: Consider a case in which a soldier has, through years of training and habituation, developed the disposition of courage to such a degree that he rushes towards danger without deliberation or conscious choice. His courage is stable, and its exercise on the battlefield benefits his comrades and his country. Yet one might hesitate to say that he acts voluntarily in the relevant sense, for the disposition has become so deeply ingrained that it operates automatically, prior to any exercise of will. The third condition fails, yet courage of this kind has been regarded as the highest form of the virtue since antiquity.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntary exercise, but a virtue may be most fully realised precisely when the disposition has become so deeply habituated that it operates without deliberate choice, as Aristotle himself suggested in characterising the temperate man as one who no longer struggles against temptation.
passage: One might propose that a character trait is a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition to act in a certain way, its exercise is beneficial to others, and the agent who possesses it acts from that disposition voluntarily. This definition captures a good many cases. The honest man is stably disposed to tell the truth, his truthfulness benefits those around him, and he speaks honestly by choice rather than by compulsion. The generous woman gives freely of her resources, her giving benefits the recipients, and she does so of her own accord. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with deeply habituated virtue. Suppose that a soldier has, through years of discipline and the experience of many campaigns, developed the disposition of courage to such a degree that he no longer deliberates before rushing to the aid of a fallen comrade. His body moves before his mind has formed any conscious resolution. His courage is undeniably stable, and its exercise benefits those who depend upon him. But it appears doubtful whether he acts voluntarily in the full sense, for the disposition has become so thoroughly ingrained that it operates as a kind of second nature, prior to any exercise of will. The third condition fails. Yet courage of this unreflective, habituated kind has been celebrated as the highest form of the virtue since the earliest moral philosophy. The definition has demanded voluntary action where the perfection of virtue may consist precisely in the transcendence of deliberate choice.

definition: A distribution of goods is just if and only if (i) it is made according to a rule known to all parties in advance, (ii) the rule applies equally to every person, and (iii) no person receives less than he would under any alternative arrangement.
conditions: made according to a rule known to all parties in advance
rule applies equally to every person
no person receives less than under any alternative arrangement
counterexample: Suppose that a community distributes its harvest according to a rule, long established and known to all, that each family shall receive an equal share regardless of the size of the family or the labour it has contributed. The rule applies equally: no family is exempted or favoured. Yet a large family of eight receives the same portion as a single individual, and the large family would manifestly receive more under an arrangement proportioned to need. The third condition fails, yet few would deny that a distribution made by an equal and publicly known rule possesses a genuine claim to justice.
missing_condition: The definition requires that no person be worse off than under any alternative, but a just distribution may satisfy equality and publicity of rule even when particular individuals would fare better under some other arrangement; justice does not demand optimality for every party.
passage: It is sometimes held that a distribution of goods is just if and only if it is made according to a rule known to all parties in advance, the rule applies equally to every person, and no person receives less than he would under any alternative arrangement. This definition has considerable appeal. A publicly known and impartially applied rule prevents arbitrary favouritism, and the stipulation that no person fares worse than under alternatives appears to safeguard each individual's interest. Yet consider a farming community that has long distributed its harvest in equal shares. The rule is known to all and has been observed for generations. It applies without exception: every family, whether large or small, industrious or idle, receives the same portion. But a family of eight persons receives precisely what a solitary widower receives, and the family of eight would plainly receive more under a distribution proportioned to need or to the number of mouths to be fed. The third condition fails for the large family. Yet few would say that the equal distribution is therefore unjust. It was made openly, by a rule that treats all families alike, and it embodies a principle — equality of share — that has a deep and honourable claim to be called just. The definition has confused justice with the impossible demand that every individual be optimally served, when justice may consist in fidelity to a fair and impartial rule even where that rule does not maximise every person's share.

definition: A person is under a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if (i) failure to perform it would cause harm to another, (ii) the person is able to perform the action, and (iii) no other person is better placed to perform it.
conditions: failure would cause harm to another
person is able to perform the action
no other person is better placed to perform it
counterexample: Suppose that a man has made a solemn promise to visit his elderly mother each Sunday.
