On a particular Sunday, his mother is in good health and good spirits, and his failure to visit would cause her no appreciable harm. Moreover, his brother lives next door and is better placed to provide company. Yet we should hesitate to say that the man is thereby released from his obligation. He promised, and the obligation arises from the promise, not from the prospect of harm or the absence of alternative agents. The first and third conditions fail, yet the obligation persists.
missing_condition: The definition grounds obligation solely in the prevention of harm and the absence of better-placed agents, but obligations may arise from voluntary undertakings such as promises, irrespective of whether harm would follow from non-performance and irrespective of whether others could act in one's stead.
passage: One might propose that a person is under a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if failure to perform it would cause harm to another, the person is able to perform it, and no other person is better placed to do so. This account captures certain obligations well. A physician who encounters an injured stranger is obligated to render aid: failure to act would cause harm, the physician is competent to act, and no one else at the scene possesses the requisite skill. But consider a man who has made a solemn promise to visit his elderly mother each Sunday. On a given Sunday his mother is well, cheerful, and surrounded by attentive neighbours. His failure to appear would cause her no appreciable harm — she would scarcely notice his absence amid other company. His brother, moreover, lives in the adjoining house and is manifestly better placed to provide companionship. The first condition fails, for no harm would result; the third fails, for another is better situated. Yet it appears that the man remains under an obligation to visit. He gave his word, and the obligation flows from the promise itself, not from the expected consequences of its breach. The definition has attempted to reduce all moral obligation to the prevention of harm and the comparative advantage of agents, overlooking those obligations that arise from voluntary commitments. A promise binds the promisor even when its fulfilment prevents no harm and even when another could more conveniently discharge the duty.

definition: A person possesses a right to X if and only if (i) the person has an interest in X, (ii) that interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others, and (iii) the duty is enforceable by some recognised authority.
conditions: person has an interest in X
interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others
duty is enforceable by some recognised authority
counterexample: Suppose that in a remote territory beyond the reach of any government, a settler clears and cultivates a plot of land. He has a manifest interest in the security of his holding, and that interest is sufficiently weighty to impose upon others a duty not to seize his land. Yet there is no recognised authority capable of enforcing the duty: no magistrate, no constable, no court. The third condition fails, yet it appears that the settler possesses a right to his land nonetheless — a right that exists even though no authority stands ready to vindicate it.
missing_condition: The definition makes enforceability a necessary condition of rights, but moral rights may exist in circumstances where no institutional mechanism of enforcement is available; the right is prior to and independent of the machinery that protects it.
passage: It is sometimes maintained that a person possesses a right to X if and only if the person has an interest in X, that interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others, and the duty is enforceable by some recognised authority. This definition captures many familiar cases. A citizen's right to property is grounded in his interest in the security of his holdings, that interest imposes upon others a duty of forbearance, and the courts stand ready to enforce that duty. But consider a settler who has established himself in a remote territory beyond the jurisdiction of any government. He has cleared the land, built a dwelling, and cultivated the soil. His interest in the security of his holding is manifest, and it appears sufficiently weighty to impose upon passing strangers a duty not to dispossess him. Yet there is no recognised authority in that territory — no magistrate, no constable, no court of law. No institution exists that could enforce the duty. The third condition fails. But it seems implausible to conclude that the settler therefore has no right to his land. His right appears to exist independently of the machinery of enforcement, as a moral claim that would be recognised by any reasonable person who considered his situation. The definition has confused the conditions under which a right is effectively protected with the conditions under which a right exists. Rights may obtain even where no authority is prepared to uphold them.

definition: A person deserves a reward if and only if (i) the person has performed an action that exceeds what is ordinarily expected, (ii) the action was performed through the person's own effort, and (iii) the person intended to bring about the beneficial result.
conditions: performed an action exceeding ordinary expectations
action performed through own effort
intended to bring about the beneficial result
counterexample: Suppose that a researcher, working diligently over many years, discovers by accident a compound that cures a devastating disease. She was investigating an entirely different question and did not intend to produce any medical benefit; the curative property was an unforeseen consequence of her experiments. Her work exceeded ordinary expectations, and it was accomplished through her own sustained effort, yet she did not intend to bring about the beneficial result. The third condition fails, yet one might observe that she deserves recognition and reward for her discovery, for it was her skill and perseverance that made it possible.
missing_condition: The definition demands that the beneficial result be intended, but desert may attach to the effort and skill that produced the result, even when the particular benefit was unintended; a person may deserve reward for the excellence of her labour irrespective of whether she foresaw its consequences.
passage: One might hold that a person deserves a reward if and only if the person has performed an action exceeding what is ordinarily expected, the action was performed through the person's own effort, and the person intended to bring about the beneficial result. This definition captures familiar cases of desert. The soldier who deliberately risks his life to save a comrade has exceeded what is expected, has acted through his own courage and effort, and intended to produce the benefit. He deserves commendation. Yet consider a research chemist who has laboured for years upon a problem in organic synthesis. In the course of her work she inadvertently produces a compound that proves to be a remarkably effective medicine for a hitherto incurable disease. Her discovery exceeds anything that was ordinarily expected of her research. The work was manifestly her own, the product of skill, training, and sustained effort. But she did not intend to produce a medical benefit; she was pursuing an entirely different line of inquiry, and the curative property of the compound was an unforeseen accident. The third condition fails. Yet it appears that she deserves recognition and reward for the discovery. Her years of painstaking labour, her technical mastery, and her devotion to her work are the conditions without which the discovery would not have occurred. The definition has insisted upon intention where desert may rest upon the quality of effort and the exercise of skill, even when the particular good that results was never part of the agent's plan.

definition: An agreement is consensual if and only if (i) all parties enter it voluntarily, without coercion or duress, (ii) all parties understand the terms and consequences of the agreement, and (iii) all parties are of sound mind at the time of agreement.
conditions: all parties enter voluntarily without coercion
all parties understand terms and consequences
all parties are of sound mind
counterexample: Consider a case in which a tenant signs a lease containing a clause, buried in dense legal language, that permits the landlord to enter the premises at any time without notice. The tenant signs voluntarily, is of sound mind, and has been given the opportunity to read the document. Yet the clause is so obscurely worded that no person without legal training could reasonably be expected to understand it. In the ordinary sense, the tenant does not understand this term of the agreement. The second condition fails, yet the law and common practice treat the lease as a binding, consensual arrangement.
missing_condition: The definition demands full understanding of all terms, but in practice many agreements are treated as consensual even where certain terms are too complex for the parties to comprehend, provided that the opportunity to seek clarification was available; the line between genuine understanding and mere opportunity to understand is left undrawn.
passage: A natural account holds that an agreement is consensual if and only if all parties enter it voluntarily, without coercion or duress, all parties understand the terms and consequences of the agreement, and all parties are of sound mind at the time of entering into it. This captures the paradigm case: two neighbours agree to share the cost of a fence, each understanding what is expected and each freely assenting. But consider a quite ordinary situation. A tenant signs a lease for a modest dwelling. He enters the agreement freely; no one compels him. He is of sound mind. He has been handed the document and given time to read it. Yet the lease is twenty pages of dense legal prose, and buried within it is a clause granting the landlord the right to enter the premises at any hour without notice. The clause is so obscurely drafted that no person lacking legal training could reasonably be expected to grasp its import. The tenant signs without understanding this term. The second condition fails. Yet the lease is treated, by common practice and by the law, as a binding consensual arrangement. We do not say that the agreement is void for want of consent merely because the tenant failed to understand a particular clause. The definition has demanded a degree of comprehension that is rarely achieved in the agreements of everyday life, where documents are long, language is technical, and parties routinely consent to terms they do not fully understand.

definition: An infliction of suffering is a just punishment if and only if (i) the person punished has committed a wrongful act, (ii) the punishment is proportionate to the wrong, and (iii) the punishment is administered by a legitimate authority.
conditions: person has committed a wrongful act
punishment is proportionate to the wrong
punishment is administered by legitimate authority
counterexample: Suppose that a father, discovering that his young son has stolen sweets from a neighbour's garden, sends the boy to his room without supper. The boy has committed a wrong, and the punishment is proportionate. But the father is not, in any formal sense, a legitimate authority — he holds no office, has received no commission, and derives no power from the state. The third condition fails if we construe legitimacy formally, yet parental punishment of this sort is among the most universally accepted forms of just correction.
missing_condition: The definition requires administration by a legitimate authority in a sense that may exclude the natural authority of parents, yet parental correction has been regarded as a paradigm of just punishment long before any theory of political legitimacy was formulated.
passage: It is sometimes argued that an infliction of suffering is a just punishment if and only if the person punished has committed a wrongful act, the punishment is proportionate to the wrong, and the punishment is administered by a legitimate authority. The definition is plausible in the case of legal punishment: a court, deriving its authority from the state, sentences a convicted thief to a fine proportionate to his offence. But consider a father who discovers that his young son has stolen sweets from a neighbour's garden. The father sends the boy to his room without supper. The child has committed a wrong — he has taken what did not belong to him. The punishment appears proportionate — a minor deprivation for a minor offence. But the father is not a legitimate authority in the sense intended by the definition. He holds no office, has received no commission from the state, and his power over the child derives not from any formal institution but from the natural relation of parent and child. If we construe the third condition as requiring the sort of legitimacy that attaches to magistrates and courts, it fails. Yet parental punishment of this kind is among the most universally accepted forms of just correction. It has been recognised and approved in every society known to history. The definition has drawn its notion of legitimate authority from the sphere of law and government and imposed it upon the sphere of the family, where a different and perhaps more fundamental form of authority obtains.

definition: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that he would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
conditions: uttered words promising to perform a specified action
promisee relied upon the assurance
person failed to perform the action
counterexample: Suppose that a man promises his friend that he will attend the friend's wedding. The friend, knowing the man to be unreliable, places no reliance whatever upon the promise and makes no arrangements that depend upon his attendance. The man fails to attend. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second fails: the promisee did not rely upon the assurance. Yet it appears that the man has broken his promise. A promise is broken by non-performance, not by the disappointment of reliance; the friend's scepticism does not dissolve the obligation.
missing_condition: The definition makes the promisee's reliance a necessary element of promise-breaking, but a promise may be broken even where the promisee placed no reliance upon it; the obligation inheres in the act of promising itself, not in the expectations it happens to generate.
passage: One might define promise-breaking as follows: a person has broken a promise if and only if the person uttered words to the effect that he would perform a specified action, the promisee relied upon the assurance, and the person failed to perform the action. This definition captures many cases naturally. A tradesman promises to deliver goods by Tuesday; the buyer relies upon the promise and plans accordingly; the goods do not arrive. The promise is broken. But suppose that a man promises his friend that he will attend the friend's wedding. The friend, who has had long experience of the man's character, places no reliance whatever upon the promise. He does not reserve a seat for the man, does not count him among the expected guests, and makes no arrangement that depends upon his attendance. The friend expects, with quiet certainty, that the man will not come. And indeed the man does not come. The first condition is satisfied — the words were spoken. The third condition is satisfied — the action was not performed. But the second condition fails, for the promisee did not rely upon the assurance. Yet it seems clear that the man has broken his promise. His failure to attend is a breach of his word regardless of whether the friend expected him to honour it. The definition has imported a condition — reliance — that belongs to the law of contract rather than to the morality of promising, where the obligation attaches to the act of giving one's word and is violated by non-performance alone.

definition: A person is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if (i) the person performed the action that caused the outcome, (ii) the person could have acted otherwise, and (iii) the person foresaw, or ought to have foreseen, the outcome.
conditions: performed the action that caused the outcome
could have acted otherwise
foresaw or ought to have foreseen the outcome
counterexample: Suppose that a factory owner neglects to repair a faulty boiler, and the boiler explodes, injuring several workers. The owner did not perform any action that caused the explosion — he failed to act. He omitted to carry out a repair. Yet we should not hesitate to say that he is morally responsible for the injuries. The first condition, which requires the performance of an action, fails in this case, for the harm was caused by an omission rather than by a positive act.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the person have performed an action, but moral responsibility may arise from culpable omissions — failures to act when action was required — no less than from positive deeds.
passage: A familiar account holds that a person is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if the person performed the action that caused it, the person could have acted otherwise, and the person foresaw, or ought to have foreseen, the result. This captures many standard cases. A careless driver runs a red light and strikes a pedestrian: he performed the action, he could have stopped, and he ought to have foreseen the danger. He is responsible. But consider a factory owner who has been warned repeatedly that a boiler in his works is dangerously corroded and in need of immediate repair. The owner, wishing to avoid the expense, does nothing. The boiler explodes, and several workers are severely injured. The owner did not perform any action that caused the explosion. He did not strike the boiler, tamper with it, or set it to an excessive pressure. His fault lay in omission — in failing to carry out a repair that was plainly necessary. He could have acted otherwise, and he certainly ought to have foreseen the danger, for he had been warned. But the first condition of the definition requires that he have performed an action, and he performed none. The definition cannot accommodate the case. Yet it seems beyond dispute that the factory owner is morally responsible for the injuries his workers suffered. His neglect was culpable, and the harm flowed directly from it. The definition has confined responsibility to positive acts and thereby excluded the large and important class of cases in which persons are responsible for what they failed to do.

definition: Two agents who perform the same action with the same intentions deserve the same moral assessment, if and only if (i) their actions are identical in all relevant respects, (ii) their intentions are the same, and (iii) the consequences of their actions are the same.
conditions: actions identical in all relevant respects
intentions are the same
consequences are the same
counterexample: Suppose that two drivers, equally reckless, run red lights at the same speed with the same indifference to the safety of others. By sheer chance, one strikes and kills a pedestrian, while the other passes through the junction without incident. Their actions are identical in all relevant respects, and their intentions are the same. But the consequences differ: one has caused a death, the other has not. The third condition fails. Yet ordinary moral sentiment assigns far greater blame to the driver who killed, and the law punishes him far more severely. The definition implies they deserve the same assessment, but practice and intuition rebel against this conclusion.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that moral assessment depends only on action and intention, treating divergent consequences as extraneous; but moral luck — the role of factors beyond the agent's control in determining outcomes — infects our actual moral assessments in ways the definition cannot accommodate.
passage: One might maintain that two agents who perform the same action with the same intentions deserve the same moral assessment, provided that their actions are identical in all relevant respects, their intentions are the same, and the consequences of their actions are the same. This principle expresses a deep intuition about fairness in moral judgement: character and intention, not fortune, should determine our assessment of a person. Yet consider two motorists who, on a busy Saturday afternoon, drive through red lights at identical speeds and with identical disregard for the safety of others. The first strikes a child who has stepped into the road and kills her. The second passes through the junction and, by the merest chance, encounters no one. Their actions are the same in every relevant respect. Their intentions — or rather their reckless indifference — are the same. But the consequences are catastrophically different: one has taken a life, the other has not. The third condition of the definition fails, and therefore the definition implies that they do not necessarily deserve the same moral assessment. Yet the divergence in consequences was entirely a matter of chance. Neither driver chose whether a child would be in the road. The difference in outcome was beyond either driver's control. If we accept the definition's implication that differing consequences warrant differing assessments, we admit that luck — mere fortune — plays a role in moral judgement. The definition has yoked moral assessment to consequences in a way that sits uneasily with the very intuition of fairness it was designed to express.

definition: A person possesses the virtue of generosity if and only if (i) the person gives freely of his resources to others, (ii) the giving causes the person some sacrifice, and (iii) the person gives without expectation of return.
conditions: gives freely of resources to others
giving causes the person sacrifice
gives without expectation of return
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man of vast wealth donates a large sum to a hospital. The sum, though large in absolute terms, is negligible relative to his fortune; its loss causes him no sacrifice whatever. He gives freely and without expectation of return. The second condition fails, yet the gift is generous by any ordinary reckoning, and the man displays the virtue of generosity in making it.
missing_condition: The definition requires that generosity involve sacrifice, but a wealthy person may be genuinely generous without suffering any privation; generosity may consist in the disposition to give readily and liberally rather than in the endurance of loss.
passage: A plausible account holds that a person possesses the virtue of generosity if and only if the person gives freely of his resources to others, the giving causes the person some sacrifice, and the person gives without expectation of return. This definition captures certain cases well. A woman of modest means who gives a substantial portion of her income to those in need, at real cost to her own comfort, exemplifies generosity. But consider a man of vast wealth — a fortune so large that he could not spend it in several lifetimes. He donates a very considerable sum to a hospital for the treatment of children. The sum is large enough to build and equip an entire ward. He gives freely and without any expectation of return or recognition. Yet the donation, though large in absolute terms, is negligible in proportion to his wealth. Its loss causes him no sacrifice, no privation, no perceptible diminution of his comfort or security. He is precisely as rich after the gift as before, for all practical purposes. The second condition fails. Yet one might observe that the gift is generous by any ordinary standard, and that the man displays the virtue of generosity in making it. The definition has demanded sacrifice where generosity may consist in the readiness to give liberally, in the openness of hand and heart, rather than in the endurance of personal cost. A man may be generous without being made poorer by his generosity.

definition: A procedure is just if and only if (i) all affected parties have an equal opportunity to present their case, (ii) the decision-maker is impartial, and (iii) the decision is reached by applying a general rule rather than by ad hoc judgement.
conditions: all affected parties have equal opportunity to present their case
decision-maker is impartial
decision reached by applying a general rule
counterexample: Suppose that a wise elder in a small community is called upon to settle a dispute between two families over the use of a well. He hears both sides and is scrupulously impartial. But rather than applying a general rule, he fashions a particular solution suited to the circumstances — granting each family access at specified hours, taking account of the size of each family and the distance each must travel. His judgement is ad hoc in the fullest sense: it is crafted for this case alone. The third condition fails, yet the resolution is widely regarded as just.
missing_condition: The definition requires the application of a general rule, but justice may sometimes be best served by a judgement tailored to the particular circumstances of the case, as in equity or in the exercise of practical wisdom.
passage: One might propose that a procedure is just if and only if all affected parties have an equal opportunity to present their case, the decision-maker is impartial, and the decision is reached by applying a general rule rather than by ad hoc judgement. This definition captures the justice of formal legal proceedings, where rules are applied uniformly and parties are heard equally. Yet consider a dispute in a small community over the use of a shared well. Two families, the Martins and the Greens, each claim priority of access. A respected elder is asked to resolve the matter. He hears both families at length and with evident impartiality. But rather than applying any general rule — first in time, first in right; equal shares to all; priority to the larger household — he devises a particular arrangement suited to the facts. The Martins, who live farther from the well and have more children, are granted access during the morning hours; the Greens, a smaller family living nearby, are granted the afternoon. The arrangement is ad hoc, fashioned for this case alone, with no pretence of deriving from a general principle. The third condition fails. Yet the settlement is accepted by both families and praised by the community as eminently fair. The definition has insisted upon the application of general rules where justice may sometimes require precisely the opposite: a particular judgement, sensitive to the circumstances, that no general rule could have produced.

definition: A person has a moral obligation to assist another if and only if (i) the other person is in a state of need, (ii) the cost to the agent of providing assistance is small relative to the benefit conferred, and (iii) the agent and the person in need stand in some prior relationship.
conditions: other person is in a state of need
cost to agent is small relative to benefit
agent and person stand in some prior relationship
counterexample: Suppose that a man walking along a canal sees a stranger's child drowning in shallow water. The child is in desperate need, and the cost of wading in to rescue her is trivial — wet trousers and a few minutes' delay. But the man has no prior relationship with the child or her family; they are perfect strangers. The third condition fails, yet it appears beyond question that the man is under a moral obligation to save the child.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the obligation of assistance to those with whom one has a prior relationship, but the duty to rescue a person in mortal peril exists regardless of any prior connection between rescuer and victim.
passage: It is sometimes maintained that a person has a moral obligation to assist another if and only if the other person is in a state of need, the cost to the agent of providing assistance is small relative to the benefit conferred, and the agent and the person in need stand in some prior relationship — of kinship, friendship, contract, or community. This definition captures many ordinary obligations of assistance. A parent is obliged to feed his child; a physician to treat her patient; a neighbour to lend a hand when his neighbour's roof is damaged. In each case need, low cost, and a prior relationship converge. But suppose that a man walking along a canal in a town he is merely passing through sees a small child floundering in the shallow water, evidently drowning. The child is in desperate need. The cost of rescue is trivial — the man need only wade into water that is scarcely waist-deep. But the man has no prior relationship with the child. He does not know her, has never met her parents, and has no connection whatever with the town or its inhabitants. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that the man's obligation to rescue the child is among the most urgent and unambiguous obligations that can arise. To walk past would be monstrous. The definition has demanded a prior relationship where the sheer extremity of the need and the negligible cost of action suffice to generate an obligation. Some duties of assistance are owed not to those with whom one stands in some special relation, but to any human being in mortal peril.

definition: A person deserves praise for an action if and only if (i) the action produced a good result, (ii) the action was difficult to perform, and (iii) the person chose to perform it rather than an easier alternative.
conditions: action produced a good result
action was difficult to perform
person chose it over an easier alternative
counterexample: Suppose that a musician of extraordinary natural talent performs a concerto with flawless beauty. She has been gifted with perfect pitch, an unerring sense of rhythm, and an effortless command of her instrument. The performance produces a result of the highest quality. But for her it was not difficult; it came as naturally as breathing. She did not choose a difficult path over an easy one, for there was no struggle involved. The second and third conditions fail, yet she deserves praise for her performance — praise not merely for a pleasant spectacle, but for the exercise of a genuine excellence.
missing_condition: The definition restricts desert of praise to difficult and deliberately chosen actions, but praise may be deserved for the exercise of a natural excellence, even where the action required no effort and no choice between alternatives.
passage: One might hold that a person deserves praise for an action if and only if the action produced a good result, the action was difficult to perform, and the person chose to perform it rather than an easier alternative. This definition captures many familiar cases. A mountaineer who reaches the summit by a perilous route, when an easier path was available, deserves praise for his courage and determination. But consider a musician of extraordinary natural talent who performs a concerto before a large audience. Her playing is of breathtaking beauty — the phrasing exquisite, the intonation faultless, the emotional depth remarkable. The result is undeniably good. Yet for this particular musician the performance was not difficult. She possesses a gift so remarkable that the music flows from her as naturally as speech flows from an articulate man. She did not choose difficulty over ease, for there was no alternative that would have been easier; playing superbly is simply what she does. The second condition fails, for the action was not difficult, and the third fails, for she did not select a hard path over a soft one. Yet it seems plain that she deserves praise. Her performance is an exercise of genuine excellence, and excellence merits commendation even when it comes without struggle. The definition has assumed that desert of praise attaches only to effortful choice, neglecting those cases in which a person deserves admiration for the exercise of a natural gift at its highest pitch.

definition: A person's consent to a practice is valid if and only if (i) the person was informed of all material facts about the practice, (ii) the person was free to refuse without penalty, and (iii) the consent was given explicitly, in words or in writing.
conditions: informed of all material facts
free to refuse without penalty
consent given explicitly in words or writing
counterexample: Consider a case in which a group of friends play a weekly game of football. No one has ever explicitly consented to the rules of the game or to the risk of minor injury. Yet each player, by turning up week after week and joining in the play, implicitly consents to the rules and to the ordinary risks of the sport. The third condition fails, for consent was never given in words or in writing, yet the consent is genuine and is universally so recognised.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit verbal or written consent, but consent may be validly given by conduct — by participation, by continued engagement, by behaviour that would be unintelligible except on the assumption of consent.
passage: A definition of valid consent might hold that a person's consent to a practice is valid if and only if the person was informed of all material facts, the person was free to refuse without penalty, and the consent was given explicitly, in words or in writing. This captures many important cases. A patient who signs a consent form before an operation, having been informed of the risks, gives valid consent. But consider a group of friends who gather each Saturday to play football in the park. No one has ever explicitly consented to the rules of the game, to the division into teams, or to the risk of a twisted ankle or a bruised shin. There is no document, no verbal declaration, no moment at which anyone says the words, I consent to these conditions. Yet each player, by arriving at the appointed hour, putting on his boots, and joining in the play, communicates his consent as plainly as if he had signed a form. If a player is injured in a fair tackle and complains that he never consented to the risk, his companions would rightly reply that his participation was itself a form of consent. The third condition fails, for no explicit consent was given. Yet the consent is real and is recognised as such by everyone involved. The definition has demanded an explicit act of assent where consent may be communicated perfectly well by conduct — by showing up, by joining in, by behaviour that would be inexplicable except upon the assumption of willingness.

definition: Punishment is warranted if and only if (i) it deters the offender from repeating the offence, (ii) it deters others from committing similar offences, and (iii) the suffering inflicted is no greater than necessary for deterrence.
conditions: deters offender from repeating offence
deters others from similar offences
suffering no greater than necessary for deterrence
counterexample: Suppose that an elderly man, now infirm and bedridden, is discovered to have committed a grave fraud many years ago. He is manifestly incapable of repeating the offence, and his punishment would deter no one, since the case is obscure and unlikely to attract public notice. The first and second conditions fail. Yet one might observe that he deserves punishment for the wrong he committed, regardless of any deterrent effect — that justice requires his wrongdoing to be answered, even though no future offence would be prevented.
missing_condition: The definition grounds the rationale for punishment entirely in deterrence, but punishment may be warranted on grounds of desert or retribution — because the wrongdoer deserves to suffer for what he has done — independently of any deterrent consequence.
passage: It is sometimes argued that punishment is warranted if and only if it deters the offender from repeating the offence, it deters others from committing similar offences, and the suffering inflicted is no greater than necessary for deterrence. This purely forward-looking account has a certain appeal, for it ties the infliction of suffering to a practical benefit. But consider an elderly man, now confined to his bed by illness, who is discovered to have committed a grave fraud decades ago. He embezzled a substantial sum from a charitable trust, causing real hardship to its beneficiaries. He is now physically incapable of repeating the offence or indeed of engaging in any activity beyond the confines of his room. And his case is so obscure — an old man, an old crime — that his punishment would attract no public attention and deter no one. The first condition fails, for he cannot reoffend. The second fails, for no deterrent effect would be produced. Yet there is a persistent moral intuition that he ought to be punished — that the wrong he committed calls for an answer, that justice demands some reckoning with what he has done, irrespective of any future benefit. The definition cannot accommodate this intuition. It has reduced the defence of punishment to deterrence and thereby excluded the retributive conviction that punishment is sometimes deserved as an appropriate response to wrongdoing, whatever its consequences for future behaviour.

definition: A person has a right to free expression if and only if (i) the person wishes to communicate an opinion, (ii) the opinion does not cause direct physical harm to another, and (iii) the communication takes place in a public forum.
conditions: person wishes to communicate an opinion
opinion does not cause direct physical harm
communication in a public forum
counterexample: Suppose that a writer composes a novel in the privacy of his study and circulates it only among a few trusted friends. The novel expresses controversial opinions on matters of public concern. The communication does not take place in a public forum; it is confined to private correspondence. The third condition fails, yet it appears that the writer's right to free expression extends to private writing and private circulation no less than to public address.
missing_condition: The definition limits the right of free expression to public forums, but the right to express oneself freely encompasses private writing, private conversation, and private correspondence; expression need not be public to fall within the scope of the right.
passage: One might define the right to free expression as follows: a person has such a right if and only if the person wishes to communicate an opinion, the opinion does not cause direct physical harm to another, and the communication takes place in a public forum. This definition captures the case of the citizen who addresses a meeting or publishes a pamphlet. But consider a writer who composes a novel in the privacy of his own study. The novel treats delicate subjects with candour and expresses opinions that many would find objectionable. The writer does not publish it in the ordinary way; he copies it by hand and sends it privately to a small circle of friends whose judgement he values. The communication is entirely private. No public forum is involved. The third condition therefore fails. Yet it seems clear that the writer possesses a right to express himself in this way. The right of free expression does not evaporate when the study door is closed and the audience is small. A person's right to set down his thoughts, to share them with chosen companions, to correspond freely upon any subject — these are aspects of free expression that are no less important for being private. The definition has taken the public exercise of expression, which is perhaps its most visible form, and treated it as its only form, thereby excluding the private dimensions of the right.

definition: A person is morally responsible for the actions of another if and only if (i) the person commanded or directed the other to act, (ii) the other acted in compliance with that command, and (iii) the person had authority over the other.
conditions: commanded or directed the other to act
other acted in compliance with that command
person had authority over the other
counterexample: Suppose that a father, without issuing any command, sets a consistent example of dishonesty before his children — cheating in small transactions, speaking falsely to neighbours, evading his obligations whenever it suits him. His son, having observed this behaviour throughout his childhood, grows up to be dishonest in the same way. The father never directed the son to behave dishonestly; indeed, he may have told the son to be honest. The son did not act in compliance with any command. Yet one might observe that the father bears some measure of moral responsibility for his son's character, for he shaped it by his example.
missing_condition: The definition restricts vicarious responsibility to cases of explicit command and obedient compliance, but a person may be morally responsible for another's conduct through the formative influence of example, neglect, or the creation of conditions that make wrongdoing likely.
passage: A common account holds that a person is morally responsible for the actions of another if and only if the person commanded or directed the other to act, the other acted in compliance with that command, and the person had authority over the other. This captures clear cases of responsibility through direction — the officer who orders a subordinate to commit an unlawful act is responsible for the result. But consider a father who, throughout his son's childhood, sets a persistent example of dishonesty. He cheats in his dealings with tradesmen, speaks falsely when it suits his convenience, and treats his obligations as matters of discretion rather than duty. He never tells his son to behave in this way; indeed, on the few occasions when the subject arises, he instructs the boy to be honest. But the boy watches, and what he watches shapes him more profoundly than what he is told. He grows up to be dishonest in much the same way as his father. The father issued no command; the son did not act in compliance with any direction. The first and second conditions fail. Yet there is a strong moral intuition that the father bears responsibility for the character he has formed, not by instruction but by the silent and pervasive influence of his example. The definition has confined responsibility for others to the relation of command and obedience, and has thereby overlooked the formative power of example, which may be a deeper and more consequential source of influence than any explicit directive.

definition: A promise is binding if and only if (i) the promisor and promisee are both competent adults, (ii) the promise was made freely, without coercion, and (iii) the promised action is possible at the time of promising.
conditions: promisor and promisee are both competent adults
promise made freely without coercion
promised action is possible at time of promising
counterexample: Suppose that a man promises his dying wife that he will never remarry. At the time of promising, the action — refraining from remarriage — is certainly possible. The promise is made freely, by a competent adult, to a competent adult. All three conditions are satisfied. But twenty years later, circumstances have changed profoundly: the man is lonely, his children have grown and departed, and he has met a woman who would make him a good companion. One might ask whether the promise remains binding in these altered circumstances, and many persons of good moral sense would say that it does not — that a promise made to a dying spouse, decades ago, under the pressure of extreme emotion, ought not to govern the whole of the rest of one's life. The definition provides no resources for releasing the promisor, for all three conditions were satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition specifies conditions for a promise to be binding at the time of its making but provides no account of the conditions under which a binding promise may cease to bind — through radical change of circumstances, the passage of great stretches of time, or the recognition that continued adherence would be unreasonable.
passage: One might hold that a promise is binding if and only if the promisor and promisee are both competent adults, the promise was made freely and without coercion, and the promised action is possible at the time of promising. This definition appears to state necessary and sufficient conditions for the bindingness of a promise. And in many cases it serves well enough. But consider a man who promises his dying wife that he will never remarry. At the time of the promise, all three conditions are satisfied. Both are competent adults. The promise is made freely — the wife asks, and the husband, moved by love and grief, gives his word. The action is possible: he can refrain from remarriage. The promise is binding. But twenty years pass. The wife has long been dead. The man's children have grown and departed. He lives alone, in circumstances his wife could not have foreseen. He meets a woman of excellent character who would be a true companion to him in his remaining years. Must he refuse her, and live in solitude, because of words spoken two decades ago at a deathbed? Many persons of mature moral judgement would say that the promise, though genuinely binding at its making, has been overtaken by changes so profound that it no longer commands observance. Yet the definition provides no mechanism for release. It states the conditions under which a promise is binding but says nothing about the conditions under which it may cease to be so. It captures the genesis of obligation but not its dissolution.

definition: A person's moral character is determined solely by (i) the choices the person has made, (ii) the intentions with which those choices were made, and (iii) the effort the person has exerted in cultivating good habits.
conditions: determined by choices made
determined by intentions behind choices
determined by effort in cultivating good habits
counterexample: Suppose that two persons of identical natural temperament are born into very different circumstances. One is raised in a stable and loving household, surrounded by examples of honesty and kindness; the other is raised in conditions of neglect and brutality, surrounded by examples of cruelty and deceit. The first grows up to be a person of excellent character; the second becomes hardened and callous. If we consider only their choices, intentions, and efforts, we may find that each has responded to his circumstances with roughly the same degree of moral exertion. Yet their characters are profoundly different, shaped by forces — the accident of birth, the quality of upbringing — over which neither had any control.
missing_condition: The definition locates moral character entirely in the agent's voluntary activity, but character is profoundly shaped by circumstances beyond the agent's control — by upbringing, social environment, natural temperament, and the opportunities for virtue that fortune provides or withholds.
passage: It is sometimes held that a person's moral character is determined solely by the choices the person has made, the intentions with which those choices were made, and the effort the person has exerted in cultivating good habits. This view has an appealing austerity: it places character entirely within the domain of the will and makes each person the author of his own moral standing. Yet consider two persons of identical natural temperament who are born into vastly different circumstances. The first is raised in a stable household by parents of good character. He is taught honesty, shown kindness, and given every opportunity to develop the virtues. The second is raised in conditions of neglect and cruelty. He is surrounded from infancy by dishonesty and violence and has never witnessed a sustained example of moral excellence. Each grows to adulthood. The first is a man of generous and upright character; the second is suspicious, guarded, and prone to unkindness. One might examine their respective choices and find that each has responded to his circumstances with comparable moral effort — the second man has perhaps struggled harder against his formation than the first. Their intentions in making choices may be equally sincere. Yet their characters are profoundly different, and the difference is attributable not to any disparity of choice or effort but to the accident of birth and upbringing. The definition insists that character is the product of will alone, but these cases suggest that circumstances beyond the agent's control — the family into which one is born, the society in which one is raised, the examples one is given — shape character in ways that no amount of voluntary effort can fully overcome.

definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) the possessor of T reliably acts well in circumstances that call upon T, (ii) the possessor of T takes pleasure in so acting, and (iii) the possessor of T would not act well in the relevant circumstances were T removed from his character.
conditions: the possessor of T reliably acts well in circumstances that call upon T
the possessor of T takes pleasure in so acting
the possessor of T would not act well in the relevant circumstances were T removed from his character
counterexample: Consider a man who possesses the virtue of temperance and reliably abstains from excess in food, drink, and bodily pleasure. He would not abstain were the disposition removed from his character. Yet he takes no pleasure in his abstinence; he finds it a perpetual struggle, a daily mortification. He is temperate, but he is not glad of it. The second condition fails, yet temperance exercised with difficulty has traditionally been regarded as genuine virtue, and some moralists have held that virtue displayed under conditions of inner resistance is more praiseworthy than virtue that costs the agent nothing.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the virtuous agent take pleasure in virtuous action, but a trait may be a genuine virtue even when its exercise is attended by discomfort, struggle, or inner resistance. What is wanted is not that the agent enjoy the exercise of the trait but that he be committed to its exercise as part of a coherent moral character.
passage: One might propose that a character trait is a virtue if and only if its possessor reliably acts well in the circumstances that call upon the trait, takes pleasure in so acting, and would not act well in those circumstances were the trait removed from his character. The definition has considerable initial appeal. The courageous man reliably faces danger, takes a certain satisfaction in standing firm, and would falter were his courage removed. The generous woman reliably gives to those in need, delights in giving, and would withhold her aid were generosity absent from her character. Yet one must consider the case of the temperate man who struggles. Suppose a man possesses the virtue of temperance and reliably abstains from excess in food, drink, and bodily pleasure. His temperance is not in question; he has maintained it for decades, and those who know him regard him as a model of self-discipline. Were the disposition removed from his character, he would certainly lapse into indulgence. But he takes no pleasure in his abstinence. He finds it a perpetual struggle, a daily exercise in self-denial. The sight of the feast-table fills him with longing, and he resists not with equanimity but with effort. The second condition of the definition fails: he does not take pleasure in his temperate conduct. Yet temperance exercised with difficulty has traditionally been accounted genuine virtue, and some moralists have maintained that the man who is temperate despite powerful contrary inclinations displays a more admirable quality than the man for whom abstinence costs nothing. The definition has mistaken a feature of the most comfortable exercise of virtue — the pleasure of the fully habituated agent — for a necessary condition of virtue itself.

definition: An arrangement A is just if and only if (i) A distributes benefits and burdens equally among all persons affected, (ii) the distribution under A was agreed to by all persons affected, and (iii) no person under A receives less than he received under the arrangement that A replaced.
conditions: A distributes benefits and burdens equally among all persons affected
the distribution under A was agreed to by all persons affected
no person under A receives less than he received under the arrangement that A replaced
counterexample: Consider a scheme of progressive taxation under which the wealthy contribute a larger proportion of their income than the poor. Benefits and burdens are not distributed equally; the wealthy bear a heavier burden. Not every person affected agreed to the scheme; some opposed it vigorously. And some persons receive less under the new arrangement than under the old, for their tax liability has increased. Yet progressive taxation is widely regarded as a paradigm of distributive justice, precisely because it adjusts contributions to capacity and ensures that the costs of public goods fall more heavily upon those best able to bear them.
missing_condition: The definition demands equality of distribution, universal agreement, and Pareto improvement, but justice may require unequal distributions that impose greater burdens upon those with greater means. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to proportionality or fairness of distribution relative to capacity, rather than a requirement of strict equality.
passage: One might propose that an arrangement is just if and only if it distributes benefits and burdens equally among all persons affected, the distribution was agreed to by all persons affected, and no person receives less than he received under the preceding arrangement. The definition appears sound when applied to simple cases. If two labourers perform the same work, an equal division of the wages seems just; both agreed to the terms of employment, and neither is made worse off. But one must consider the case of progressive taxation. Under a scheme of progressive taxation, the wealthy contribute a larger proportion of their income to the public treasury than the poor. The distribution of burdens is emphatically not equal; a man earning ten thousand pounds per annum may pay thirty per cent in tax, whilst a man earning five hundred pounds pays nothing at all. The first condition fails. Not every person affected agreed to the scheme; wealthy taxpayers may have opposed the measure in Parliament and continue to regard it as confiscatory. The second condition fails. And some persons receive less under the new arrangement than under the old, for their disposable income has been reduced by the higher rate of taxation. The third condition fails. Yet progressive taxation is widely regarded as a paradigm of distributive justice, and the arguments in its favour — that contributions to the common good ought to be proportioned to the capacity to contribute, that a pound taken from a rich man diminishes his welfare less than a pound taken from a poor one — are among the most powerful in the literature of political economy. The definition, by demanding strict equality, universal consent, and the absence of any individual loss, has excluded precisely the kind of arrangement that justice most frequently requires.

definition: A person S has a moral obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) S has explicitly promised to perform A, (ii) the person to whom S made the promise is alive and capable of receiving the benefit of A, and (iii) performing A does not require S to sacrifice any significant personal interest.
conditions: S has explicitly promised to perform A
the person to whom S made the promise is alive and capable of receiving the benefit
performing A does not require S to sacrifice any significant personal interest
counterexample: Consider a physician who encounters a gravely injured stranger by the roadside. The physician has made no promise to this stranger, the stranger may be unconscious and incapable of requesting aid, and rendering assistance may require the physician to sacrifice significant personal interests — arriving late to his own engagements, soiling his clothing, or exposing himself to danger. Yet the physician has a moral obligation to render aid. The obligation arises not from any promise but from his capacity to help and from the stranger's desperate need.
missing_condition: The definition grounds moral obligation exclusively in explicit promising, but obligations may arise from the mere capacity to prevent serious harm, from professional roles, or from the bare fact of another's urgent need. What is wanted is a broader ground of obligation that encompasses duties not rooted in voluntary undertakings.
passage: One might propose that a person has a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if he has explicitly promised to perform it, the person to whom the promise was made is alive and capable of receiving its benefit, and performing the action does not require the sacrifice of any significant personal interest. This definition captures a familiar class of obligations tolerably well. If a man has promised his neighbour to mend a fence, the neighbour is alive and waiting, and the mending costs the man nothing more than an afternoon's labour, we should say that the man is obligated to mend the fence. But one must consider the physician by the roadside. Suppose a physician, travelling along a country road, comes upon a stranger who has been thrown from his horse and lies bleeding in the ditch. The physician has made no promise to this stranger; the two have never met. The stranger may be unconscious and wholly incapable of requesting aid. And rendering assistance will require the physician to sacrifice interests that are not negligible — he must dismount, soil his clothing, delay his journey by some hours, and perhaps expose himself to the risk of infection from the stranger's wounds. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet we should say, with considerable confidence, that the physician has a moral obligation to render aid. The obligation arises not from any promise, explicit or implied, but from the physician's capacity to prevent serious harm and from the stranger's desperate need. The definition has confined moral obligation to the domain of voluntary undertaking, when in truth some of the most pressing obligations arise unbidden, from circumstances that no one has chosen and no one has promised to address.

definition: A person S possesses a right to X if and only if (i) there exists a legal statute or constitutional provision conferring X upon S, (ii) S is aware of the existence of this statute or provision, and (iii) S has the practical capacity to exercise X without external assistance.
conditions: there exists a legal statute or constitutional provision conferring X upon S
S is aware of the existence of this statute or provision
S has the practical capacity to exercise X without external assistance
counterexample: Consider the right of a newborn infant not to be killed. No statute need be cited for us to affirm that the infant possesses this right; indeed, the right is commonly held to exist prior to and independently of any legal enactment. The infant is not aware of any statute, for the infant is not aware of anything in the relevant sense. And the infant has no practical capacity to exercise the right without assistance, for the infant can do nothing without assistance. Yet the infant's right not to be killed is among the most firmly held moral convictions of civilised society.
missing_condition: The definition reduces rights to positive legal enactments and conditions them upon the rightholder's awareness and capacity, but moral rights may exist independently of legislation, may be held by persons incapable of awareness, and may require the active protection of others for their realisation.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses a right to something if and only if there exists a legal statute or constitutional provision conferring that entitlement, the person is aware of the statute, and the person has the practical capacity to exercise the entitlement without external assistance. This definition accommodates certain straightforward cases. A property owner possesses the right to exclude trespassers because the law of real property confers this right, the owner is aware of the law, and the owner can lock his gate or erect a fence without anyone's help. But one must consider the right of a newborn infant not to be killed. This right is affirmed by virtually every moral tradition and is regarded as among the most fundamental of all moral claims. Yet there need be no specific statute conferring it; the right is held to exist prior to and independently of any legislative enactment. Many would insist that the infant possessed this right even in a state of nature, before any government had been constituted. The infant is assuredly not aware of any statute or provision, for the infant is incapable of awareness in the relevant sense. And the infant has no practical capacity to exercise the right without assistance; the infant is entirely dependent upon others for its survival and can do nothing whatsoever to protect itself. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet the infant's right not to be killed is among the most deeply held moral convictions of civilised society, and any account of rights that cannot accommodate it is thereby shown to be gravely deficient. The definition has confused the legal recognition and individual enforcement of rights with the existence of rights themselves.

definition: A person S deserves a reward R if and only if (i) S has performed an action A that was effortful and personally costly, (ii) S performed A with the conscious intention of earning R, and (iii) R is proportionate to the effort S expended in performing A.
conditions: S has performed an action that was effortful and personally costly
S performed the action with the conscious intention of earning the reward
the reward is proportionate to the effort expended
counterexample: Consider a gifted mathematician who solves a long-standing problem with apparent ease, arriving at the proof during an afternoon's walk. The solution was not effortful in any ordinary sense; it came to him with the fluency that marks exceptional talent. He did not solve the problem with the intention of earning a reward; he solved it because the problem fascinated him. Yet he deserves the recognition and honours that follow, for desert in intellectual achievement attaches to the quality of the accomplishment, not to the effort expended or the intention with which it was pursued.
missing_condition: The definition ties desert to effort and intention, but desert may attach to the quality or significance of an achievement irrespective of the effort involved or the motives of the agent. What is wanted is a condition concerning the value of what was accomplished, not merely the cost at which it was accomplished.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves a reward if and only if he has performed an action that was effortful and personally costly, he performed the action with the conscious intention of earning the reward, and the reward is proportionate to the effort expended. This definition captures a familiar intuition: the labourer who toils through a long day in the sun deserves his wages, for his labour was effortful, he worked in order to be paid, and his wages are proportionate to his effort. But one must consider the gifted mathematician. Suppose that a mathematician of exceptional ability solves a problem that has resisted the efforts of his colleagues for decades. He arrives at the proof not through years of grinding labour but during a quiet afternoon's walk, when the key insight presents itself to him with sudden clarity. The solution was not effortful in any ordinary sense; the mathematician himself may describe it as having come easily, almost unbidden. He did not pursue the problem with the conscious intention of earning a reward; he pursued it because the problem captivated his imagination and he could not leave it alone. And the recognition that follows — prizes, honours, the esteem of the mathematical community — is not proportionate to the effort expended, for the effort was slight compared to the magnitude of the achievement. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet the mathematician deserves the recognition he receives. His desert rests not upon the effort he expended but upon the significance and quality of what he accomplished. The definition has mistaken the grounds of desert in manual labour for the grounds of desert in general, overlooking the vast domain of intellectual and artistic achievement in which desert attaches to the value of the result rather than to the cost of its production.

definition: A person S has given valid consent to an action A if and only if (i) S has uttered the words 'I consent' or an equivalent verbal formula, (ii) S uttered these words in the presence of witnesses, and (iii) S was not subjected to physical force at the moment of utterance.
conditions: S has uttered the words 'I consent' or an equivalent verbal formula
S uttered these words in the presence of witnesses
S was not subjected to physical force at the moment of utterance
counterexample: Consider a man who, under threat of the ruin of his family's reputation, is compelled to agree to a disadvantageous contract. He utters the words 'I consent' before witnesses, and no physical force is applied to his person. Yet his consent is not valid, for it was extracted by coercion of a kind that, though it involved no bodily compulsion, left him with no genuine alternative. The conditions of the definition are satisfied, but the consent is vitiated by duress.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to verbal utterance, the presence of witnesses, and the absence of physical force, but valid consent requires freedom from all forms of coercion, including threats, manipulation, and the exploitation of desperate circumstances. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to the genuine voluntariness of the agent's will, not merely to the outward form of the consenting act.
passage: One might propose that a person has given valid consent to an action if and only if he has uttered the words 'I consent' or some equivalent verbal formula, he uttered these words in the presence of witnesses, and he was not subjected to physical force at the moment of utterance. This definition captures a range of ordinary cases. When a man agrees to the terms of a contract before a solicitor and the solicitor's clerk, speaking freely and without bodily compulsion, we are inclined to regard his consent as valid. But one must consider the case of consent extracted under duress that falls short of physical force. Suppose a man of modest station is informed by a powerful creditor that, unless he signs over a portion of his property at a grossly unfavourable price, the creditor will publish certain private matters that would bring lasting disgrace upon the man's family. The man, seeing no alternative that would preserve his family's standing, utters the words 'I consent' before the creditor's solicitor and a clerk who serves as witness. No physical force is applied to his person; no hand is laid upon him; no weapon is brandished. Every condition of the proposed definition is satisfied. Yet his consent is not valid. It was extracted by a form of coercion that, though it involved no bodily compulsion, was no less effective in overbearing his will. He consented not because he wished to but because the consequences of refusal were intolerable. The definition, by attending only to the verbal form of consent, the presence of witnesses, and the absence of physical force, has overlooked the deeper requirement that consent must proceed from a will that is genuinely free — free not merely from bodily compulsion but from all forms of pressure that render the agent's agreement something other than a voluntary act.

definition: An imposition of suffering P upon a person S constitutes just punishment if and only if (i) S has been found guilty of a specific offence by a duly constituted tribunal, (ii) the severity of P is proportionate to the gravity of S's offence, and (iii) P serves the purpose of deterring others from committing similar offences.
conditions: S has been found guilty of a specific offence by a duly constituted tribunal
the severity of P is proportionate to the gravity of S's offence
P serves the purpose of deterring others from committing similar offences
counterexample: Consider the punishment of a man who committed a grave offence many years ago and has since reformed his character entirely, living an exemplary life. He is found guilty, and the severity of the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of his offence. Yet the punishment serves no deterrent purpose, for the case is so old and the circumstances so unusual that no potential offender could draw a practical lesson from it. The third condition fails. Yet many would hold that the punishment is just, on the ground that justice requires that wrongdoing be answered, irrespective of any deterrent effect.
missing_condition: The definition requires that punishment serve a deterrent purpose, but just punishment may be grounded in retributive considerations — in the idea that wrongdoing deserves to be answered — independently of any forward-looking effect upon the conduct of others.
passage: One might propose that an imposition of suffering upon a person constitutes just punishment if and only if the person has been found guilty of a specific offence by a duly constituted tribunal, the severity of the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence, and the punishment serves the purpose of deterring others from committing similar offences. The definition accommodates many familiar cases. When a thief is convicted and sentenced to a term of imprisonment proportionate to his crime, and the sentence serves as a warning to would-be thieves, we are inclined to regard the punishment as just. But one must consider the case of the reformed offender whose crime is old. Suppose that a man committed a grave offence — let us say, a serious fraud — some thirty years ago. In the intervening decades he has reformed his character entirely. He has lived an exemplary life, made restitution where he could, and become a man of conspicuous integrity. Owing to a chance discovery of new evidence, he is at last brought before a tribunal, found guilty, and sentenced. The severity of the sentence is proportionate to the gravity of the offence. Yet the punishment serves no deterrent purpose whatsoever. The case is so old, the circumstances so peculiar, and the connection between the offence and the punishment so attenuated by the passage of time that no potential offender could plausibly be deterred by the example. The third condition fails. Yet a great many thoughtful persons would hold that the punishment is just, on the ground that justice requires wrongdoing to be answered, that a grave offence does not cease to call for punishment merely because many years have elapsed since its commission. The definition has made deterrence a necessary condition of just punishment, when in truth the justness of punishment may rest upon retributive grounds that have nothing to do with the effect of the punishment upon the future conduct of others.

definition: A person S is bound by a promise to perform an action A if and only if (i) S made the promise voluntarily and without coercion, (ii) the performance of A is within S's power at the time performance is due, and (iii) the promisee P continues to desire the performance of A.
conditions: S made the promise voluntarily and without coercion
the performance of A is within S's power at the time performance is due
the promisee continues to desire the performance of A
counterexample: Consider a man who promises his dying father to maintain the family estate and never sell it. The father dies, and the promisee no longer desires anything, being dead. The third condition fails, for the promisee has no continuing desires. Yet the son regards himself as bound by the promise, and most persons of reflective moral sensibility would agree that a promise made to the dying retains its binding force after the promisee's death.
missing_condition: The definition conditions the binding force of a promise upon the promisee's continuing desire, but a promise may remain binding even when the promisee can no longer desire anything. What is wanted is a recognition that the binding force of a promise may derive from the solemnity of the undertaking and the relationship in which it was made, not merely from the promisee's present wishes.
passage: One might propose that a person is bound by a promise to perform an action if and only if the promise was made voluntarily and without coercion, the performance of the action is within the promisor's power when performance is due, and the promisee continues to desire the performance. This definition captures a good many cases. If a man promises his friend to return a borrowed book, and the friend still wishes to have the book returned, and the man is able to return it, we should say he is bound. But one must consider the promise made to the dying. Suppose that a man, sitting at his father's bedside in the father's final hours, promises that he will maintain the family estate and never sell the land that has been in the family for generations. The father, comforted by this assurance, dies peacefully. The son made the promise voluntarily; no coercion was involved. The performance of the promise — the maintenance of the estate — is within his power. But the promisee no longer desires the performance of the action, for the promisee is dead and has no desires at all. The third condition fails. Yet the son considers himself bound by the promise, and most persons of reflective moral sensibility would concur. A promise made to the dying is widely regarded as among the most solemn of all undertakings, and its binding force does not evaporate with the promisee's last breath. The definition, by requiring the promisee's continuing desire as a condition of the promise's binding force, has overlooked the class of promises whose moral weight derives from the gravity of the circumstances in which they were made and from the relationship between the parties, rather than from any present wish of the promisee.

definition: A person S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S performed a voluntary action that caused O, (ii) S foresaw that his action would cause O, and (iii) S desired that O should come about.
conditions: S performed a voluntary action that caused O
S foresaw that his action would cause O
S desired that O should come about
counterexample: Consider a factory owner who, in order to reduce costs, discharges toxic waste into a river. He foresees that this may cause illness among the villagers downstream, but he does not desire their illness; he is indifferent to it. His action is voluntary, and it causes the harm. But he did not desire the outcome. The third condition fails. Yet the factory owner is morally responsible for the illness of the villagers, for he knowingly imposed a serious risk upon them for the sake of his own profit, and his indifference to their welfare does not diminish his responsibility.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agent desired the harmful outcome, but moral responsibility may attach to actions whose harmful consequences were foreseen but not desired. What is wanted is a condition concerning the agent's awareness of the risk and his willingness to impose it upon others, not a requirement that he positively wished for the harm.
passage: One might propose that a person is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if he performed a voluntary action that caused it, he foresaw that his action would cause it, and he desired that the outcome should come about. This definition captures the clearest cases of moral responsibility. The man who deliberately poisons another, foreseeing and desiring the victim's death, is responsible in the fullest sense. But one must consider the case of the negligent factory owner. Suppose that the owner of a manufactory, in order to reduce the costs of production, orders the discharge of toxic waste into a river that flows through several villages downstream. He is aware that the waste may cause illness among the villagers; his foreman has warned him of this possibility, and he has read reports of similar cases in other districts. He does not desire the villagers' illness; he gives it no thought beyond a passing acknowledgement of the risk. His concern is entirely with the reduction of costs and the increase of profit. The villagers fall ill. The owner performed a voluntary action — he ordered the discharge — and the action caused the illness. He foresaw that the action might cause the illness. But he did not desire it; he was merely indifferent to it. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the factory owner is morally responsible for the suffering of the villagers. His responsibility arises from the fact that he knowingly imposed a grave risk upon innocent persons for the sake of his own pecuniary advantage, and his indifference to their welfare, far from diminishing his responsibility, is itself a moral failing. The definition has required desire where foresight and indifference to the welfare of others are sufficient grounds for the ascription of responsibility.

definition: The moral worth of a person's action is determined solely by factors within the agent's control, such that (i) two agents who perform the same action with the same intentions in the same circumstances are equally praiseworthy or blameworthy, (ii) outcomes that depend upon chance do not affect the moral assessment of the action, and (iii) the agent's moral character is judged only by what he chose to do, not by what happened as a result.
conditions: two agents performing the same action with the same intentions are equally praiseworthy or blameworthy
outcomes depending upon chance do not affect moral assessment
the agent's moral character is judged only by what he chose to do
counterexample: Consider two drivers, each of whom drives recklessly through a village at the same speed, with the same degree of negligence and the same disregard for the safety of others. One driver arrives at the other side of the village without incident. The other strikes and kills a child who stepped into the road at the wrong moment. The two drivers performed the same action with the same intentions in the same circumstances, and the difference in outcome was entirely a matter of chance. Yet we judge the second driver far more harshly than the first. He is charged with a graver offence, sentenced more severely, and regarded with greater moral opprobrium.
