The first and second conditions of the definition are violated by our actual moral practice.
passage: One might propose that the moral worth of a person's action is determined solely by factors within the agent's control. On this view, two agents who perform the same action with the same intentions in identical circumstances are equally praiseworthy or blameworthy, outcomes that depend upon chance do not affect the moral assessment, and the agent's character is judged only by what he chose to do rather than by what happened as a result. The proposal has a certain austere plausibility; it seems unjust that a man's moral standing should depend upon factors beyond his control. Yet one must consider the case of the two reckless drivers. Suppose two men drive their motor-cars through a village at an excessive speed, with the same degree of negligence and the same disregard for the safety of pedestrians. The circumstances are as nearly identical as one could wish. One driver passes through without incident; no one happens to be crossing the road. The other strikes and kills a child who stepped from behind a parked conveyance at precisely the wrong moment. The difference in outcome is entirely a matter of chance — of whether a child happened to be in the road at that instant. Yet our moral responses to the two drivers differ profoundly. The driver who killed the child is charged with a graver offence, suffers a heavier sentence, and is regarded by his neighbours with a degree of moral opprobrium that the other driver escapes entirely. Both performed the same action with the same intentions, yet one bears a moral burden that the other does not. The proposed definition, which insists that moral worth depends only upon what is within the agent's control, cannot accommodate this feature of our moral practice, and any attempt to dismiss the asymmetry as mere irrationality must contend with its deep and persistent hold upon moral thought.

definition: A person S possesses the virtue of generosity if and only if (i) S gives freely of his material resources to those in need, (ii) S gives without expectation of reciprocation, and (iii) the giving diminishes S's own material holdings.
conditions: S gives freely of his material resources to those in need
S gives without expectation of reciprocation
the giving diminishes S's own material holdings
counterexample: Consider a schoolmaster who, possessing no wealth of his own, gives freely of his time, attention, and encouragement to his pupils. He stays after the appointed hours to help those who struggle, writes letters of recommendation, and offers counsel in moments of difficulty. He gives without expectation of reciprocation, and his giving does diminish something — his leisure, his energy, his time — but it does not diminish his material holdings, for he has none to diminish. Yet the schoolmaster is generous, and his generosity may be more admirable than that of the wealthy man who gives from abundance.
missing_condition: The definition confines generosity to the giving of material resources, but generosity may consist in the giving of time, attention, labour, or care. What is wanted is a broader conception of what may be given generously, encompassing non-material goods.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses the virtue of generosity if and only if he gives freely of his material resources to those in need, gives without expectation of reciprocation, and thereby diminishes his own material holdings. This definition captures the most conspicuous instances of generosity tolerably well. The wealthy philanthropist who endows a hospital, expecting nothing in return and reducing his fortune thereby, is generous by any reckoning. But one must consider the case of the schoolmaster. Suppose a schoolmaster of modest circumstances, possessing no wealth beyond his salary, gives freely of his time, his attention, and his encouragement to his pupils. He remains at the school long after the appointed hours to assist those who find their lessons difficult. He writes careful letters of recommendation for pupils who seek positions. He offers counsel and support in moments of personal difficulty, and does so without any thought of return. His giving diminishes something — his leisure, his energy, the hours that might be spent upon his own pursuits — but it does not diminish his material holdings, for he has no material holdings to speak of. Every condition of the definition fails save the second: he gives without expectation of reciprocation. Yet the schoolmaster is generous. His generosity is real, consequential, and widely recognised by those who benefit from it. Indeed, it may be argued that his generosity is more admirable than that of the rich man who gives from abundance, for the schoolmaster gives the only things he has to give. The definition, by confining generosity to the giving of material resources, has overlooked the entire domain of generous action in which what is given is time, care, labour, or attention rather than money or goods.

definition: A judicial proceeding P is just if and only if (i) the accused is permitted to speak in his own defence, (ii) the tribunal is composed of persons who have no prior acquaintance with the accused, and (iii) the verdict is determined by the unanimous agreement of the members of the tribunal.
conditions: the accused is permitted to speak in his own defence
the tribunal is composed of persons who have no prior acquaintance with the accused
the verdict is determined by the unanimous agreement of the members of the tribunal
counterexample: Consider a village tribunal in a small community where every person is acquainted with every other. A dispute arises, and the village elders sit in judgement. They all know the accused; indeed, they have known him since his childhood. The verdict is reached not by unanimous agreement but by the decision of the senior elder after consultation with the others. Yet the proceeding may be just if the elders are impartial in their deliberation, if they weigh the evidence fairly, and if the accused is treated with the respect due to any person whose conduct is under scrutiny.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the tribunal members have no prior acquaintance with the accused and that the verdict be unanimous, but justice in adjudication may be secured by impartiality and fair deliberation rather than by the absence of acquaintance or the mechanism of unanimity.
passage: One might propose that a judicial proceeding is just if and only if the accused is permitted to speak in his own defence, the tribunal is composed of persons who have no prior acquaintance with the accused, and the verdict is determined by the unanimous agreement of the tribunal's members. This definition reflects the ideals of certain modern legal systems in which juries are drawn from a wide population and are expected to reach unanimous verdicts. But one must consider the village tribunal. In a small community — a rural parish, let us say, numbering a few hundred souls — every person is acquainted with every other. When a dispute arises and the village elders are called upon to adjudicate, they cannot be strangers to the accused, for there are no strangers in the village. They have known the accused since his birth; they know his family, his character, his history. The second condition of the definition fails entirely. The verdict, moreover, is reached not by unanimous agreement but by the decision of the senior elder, who consults the others and weighs their views but ultimately pronounces the judgement himself. The third condition fails as well. Yet such proceedings may be just. If the elders are impartial in their deliberations — if they set aside personal sympathies and enmities and attend to the evidence before them — and if the accused is treated with the respect due to any person whose conduct is called into question, the proceeding satisfies the demands of justice as fully as any jury trial in a great assize court. The definition has mistaken the procedural features of one particular form of adjudication for the essential requirements of justice in judicial proceedings.

definition: A person S deserves praise for an action A if and only if (i) A was performed after a period of conscious deliberation by S, (ii) S considered and rejected at least one alternative course of action before performing A, and (iii) S can articulate the reasons for which he performed A.
conditions: A was performed after a period of conscious deliberation
S considered and rejected at least one alternative course of action
S can articulate the reasons for which he performed A
counterexample: Consider a woman who, upon seeing a child fall into a canal, leaps into the water without a moment's hesitation and pulls the child to safety. She did not deliberate; the action was instantaneous. She did not consider and reject alternatives; there was no time for reflection. And she may be unable to articulate her reasons; when asked why she did it, she says only that she could not have done otherwise. Yet she deserves praise, and the spontaneity of her action is precisely what makes it praiseworthy — she acted from a character so thoroughly good that the right response was immediate.
missing_condition: The definition requires deliberation, the rejection of alternatives, and the articulation of reasons, but praiseworthy action may be spontaneous, unreflective, and inarticulate. What is wanted is a recognition that desert of praise may attach to the quality of the agent's character as revealed in action, not merely to the process of deliberation that preceded it.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves praise for an action if and only if the action was performed after a period of conscious deliberation, the agent considered and rejected at least one alternative, and the agent can articulate the reasons for his choice. This definition reflects a rationalist picture of praiseworthy action: the agent surveys his options, selects the best, and can explain his selection. But one must consider the rescuer who acts without thought. Suppose that a woman, walking along a canal, sees a young child topple from the bank into the water. Without a moment's hesitation — without, as she will later report, any conscious thought at all — she leaps into the canal and pulls the child to safety. She did not deliberate; the action was instantaneous, preceding any process of reflection. She did not consider and reject alternatives; there was no interval in which alternatives could have been weighed. And when asked, afterwards, to articulate her reasons, she finds herself unable to do so in any satisfying way; she says only that she saw the child fall and acted, that she could not have done otherwise, that the thought of standing by was simply not available to her. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet she deserves praise. Indeed, the spontaneity of her action is precisely what elevates it: she acted from a character so thoroughly good that the right response was immediate, unmediated by calculation. The definition has made deliberation, the weighing of alternatives, and the capacity for rational articulation into prerequisites for deserving praise, when in truth the most praiseworthy actions may be those that spring from a character in which goodness has become second nature.

definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition of the agent — that is, T is not a passing inclination but a settled feature of the agent's character, (ii) T is exercised voluntarily — the agent acts from T by choice rather than by compulsion, and (iii) T produces beneficial consequences for those affected by the agent's actions.
conditions: T is a stable disposition of the agent, not a passing inclination
T is exercised voluntarily — the agent acts from T by choice
T produces beneficial consequences for those affected by the agent's actions
counterexample: Consider the virtue of courage. A soldier who throws himself upon a grenade to save his comrades acts from a stable disposition and does so voluntarily. Yet the consequence for the soldier himself is death. The action produces beneficial consequences for those around him, but one might equally note that the soldier's children are left fatherless and his wife a widow; the consequences, taken in their totality, are a mixture of benefit and harm. More troubling still is the case of a man who courageously tells an unpopular truth that brings ruin upon himself and distress to his audience. The third condition fails, yet courage remains a virtue.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a virtue produce beneficial consequences, but some virtues may demand actions whose consequences are harmful to the agent or to others. What is wanted is a condition regarding the intrinsic nobility or rightness of the disposition, not the contingent consequences of its exercise.
passage: One might propose that a character trait is a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition, exercised voluntarily, that produces beneficial consequences for those affected by the agent's actions. The definition captures a good deal of what is ordinarily meant by virtue. Generosity is a stable disposition; the generous man gives freely and by choice; and the recipients of his generosity are benefited. Honesty, similarly, is a settled feature of character; the honest man speaks the truth voluntarily; and those who rely upon his word are well served by his truthfulness. Yet one must consider courage. A soldier who throws himself upon a live grenade to save his comrades acts from a stable disposition of character — he is not merely startled into action — and he does so voluntarily, by a deliberate act of will. But the consequences of his action are not uniformly beneficial. His comrades are saved, to be sure, but the soldier himself is killed. His children are left without a father, his wife without a husband, and his parents without a son. The consequences, taken in their totality, are a mixture of benefit and harm, and it is by no means obvious that the beneficial consequences outweigh the harmful ones. One might consider, further, the case of a man who tells an unpopular truth — who speaks out against a powerful injustice at great personal cost. His honesty brings no benefit to his audience, who are distressed and angered by what he says, and it brings ruin upon himself. The third condition of the definition fails. The analysis has mistaken a frequent accompaniment of virtue for its essence. What makes courage a virtue is not that it tends to produce good consequences but that it is intrinsically noble — that it involves the willing acceptance of danger in the service of something worthy.

definition: A distribution of goods D is just if and only if (i) D is the product of voluntary exchange — each party to the distribution has freely consented to the arrangement, (ii) no party to D has been deceived regarding the nature or value of the goods exchanged, and (iii) each party to D receives goods in proportion to what he has given.
conditions: D is the product of voluntary exchange — each party has freely consented
no party has been deceived regarding the nature or value of the goods
each party receives goods in proportion to what he has given
counterexample: Consider the distribution of goods that results when a wealthy man gives alms to a beggar. The beggar gives nothing in return; the distribution is entirely one-sided. The third condition fails, for the beggar receives goods wholly out of proportion to what he has given, which is nothing. Yet it would be strange to call this distribution unjust. The wealthy man gives freely and without deception, and the beggar's need is genuine. If anything, the distribution seems to be an instance of justice rather than a violation of it, for it corrects an imbalance that a merely proportional scheme would leave untouched.
missing_condition: The definition requires proportionality of exchange, but just distributions may include gifts, charitable transfers, and provisions for need that are not proportional to any contribution. What is wanted is a condition that accommodates the claims of need alongside the claims of contribution.
passage: One might hold that a distribution of goods is just if and only if it is the product of voluntary exchange, no party has been deceived regarding the nature or value of the goods exchanged, and each party receives goods in proportion to what he has given. The definition has an attractive simplicity. When two merchants exchange cloth for grain, each freely consenting and each fully informed of what he receives, and each receiving a quantity proportional to what he offers, the resulting distribution seems paradigmatically just. Yet one must consider the case of a wealthy man who gives alms to a beggar. The beggar gives nothing in return. He has no goods to offer, no labour to exchange, no service to render. The distribution is entirely one-sided: the wealthy man parts with a portion of his substance and receives nothing. By the lights of the definition, this distribution is unjust, for the beggar receives goods wholly out of proportion to what he has contributed, which is nothing at all. But this conclusion is difficult to accept. The wealthy man gives freely and without deception, moved by compassion for the beggar's evident need. The beggar is hungry and cold, and the alms relieve his suffering. Far from being unjust, the distribution seems to be required by justice, or at the very least permitted by it, for it addresses a genuine need that the beggar cannot satisfy by his own efforts. A scheme of distribution that attended only to proportionality of exchange would leave the destitute to perish, and it is difficult to see how such a scheme could claim the title of justice. The definition has taken a feature of one important class of just distributions — the proportionality of commercial exchange — and elevated it to a necessary condition of all just distributions, thereby excluding the charitable and the needs-based transfers that are among the most widely recognised demands of justice.

definition: An agent A has a moral obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has explicitly undertaken to perform X — that is, A has made a promise, entered into a contract, or otherwise given his word, (ii) A is able to perform X — the action is within A's power, and (iii) performing X does not require A to violate any other obligation.
conditions: A has explicitly undertaken to perform X — by promise, contract, or giving his word
A is able to perform X
performing X does not require A to violate any other obligation
counterexample: Consider a man walking along a riverbank who sees a child drowning. He has made no promise to rescue the child, entered into no contract, and given no one his word. He has never met the child before and owes him nothing by way of any prior undertaking. The first condition fails. Yet it would be monstrous to suggest that the man has no moral obligation to rescue the child. He is able to swim, the water is not dangerously deep, and no competing obligation prevents him from acting. The moral obligation arises not from any explicit undertaking but from the mere fact that another human being is in peril and he is in a position to help.
missing_condition: The definition restricts moral obligation to cases of explicit undertaking, but obligations may arise from the sheer fact of another's need or peril, independently of any promise or contract. What is wanted is a condition that encompasses the obligations imposed by the circumstances of human vulnerability.
passage: One might propose that an agent has a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if he has explicitly undertaken to perform it, he is able to perform it, and performing it does not require him to violate any other obligation. The definition has considerable plausibility in certain domains. A man who has promised to repay a debt has undertaken to do so, is presumably able to repay, and is obligated to do so unless repayment would require him to break some other, more pressing obligation. A physician who has entered into a contract with a patient has undertaken to provide care, and her obligation derives from that undertaking. Yet one must consider the case of a man walking along a riverbank who sees a child drowning in the shallows. The man has made no promise to rescue the child. He has entered into no contract, given no one his word, and assumed no responsibility for the child's welfare. He has never met the child before and has no connection to him whatever. The first condition of the definition fails entirely. Yet it would be monstrous to suggest that the man has no moral obligation to rescue the child. He can swim; the water is not dangerously deep; no competing obligation prevents him from acting. The moral demand upon him is immediate and overwhelming, and it arises not from any explicit undertaking but from the bare fact that a human being is in mortal peril and he is in a position to render assistance. The definition has taken the source of one important class of obligations — the voluntary assumption of a duty through promise or contract — and treated it as the only source of moral obligation, thereby excluding the urgent obligations that arise from the mere circumstance of another's need.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) X is recognised by the positive law of P's jurisdiction — that is, X is enshrined in statute or constitutional provision, (ii) P is able to enforce X through the legal institutions of his jurisdiction, and (iii) X has been formally claimed by P or by someone acting on P's behalf.
conditions: X is recognised by the positive law of P's jurisdiction
P is able to enforce X through legal institutions
X has been formally claimed by P or by someone on P's behalf
counterexample: Consider a slave in a jurisdiction where slavery is lawful. The positive law of his jurisdiction does not recognise a right to personal liberty on his part; indeed, the law explicitly denies it. He has no ability to enforce such a right through the legal institutions of his jurisdiction, for those institutions are the very instruments of his subjection. And he has not formally claimed the right, for any such claim would be futile and perhaps dangerous. All three conditions fail. Yet one is strongly inclined to say that the slave has a right to liberty — a right that the positive law of his jurisdiction violates rather than confers.
missing_condition: The definition restricts rights to those recognised by positive law, but there may be natural or moral rights that exist independently of legal recognition. What is wanted is a notion of rights that can serve as a standard by which positive law is itself to be judged.
passage: One might hold that a person has a right to something if and only if that thing is recognised by the positive law of his jurisdiction, he is able to enforce it through the legal institutions of his jurisdiction, and it has been formally claimed by him or by someone acting on his behalf. The definition has a certain clarity. A citizen who holds a deed to a parcel of land has a right to that land; the right is recognised by statute, enforceable through the courts, and has been claimed by the citizen in the act of purchasing the property. A defendant in a criminal trial has a right to legal counsel; the right is enshrined in constitutional provision, enforceable through the judiciary, and claimed by the defendant or his advocate. Yet one must consider the case of a slave in a jurisdiction where slavery is lawful. The positive law of his jurisdiction does not recognise a right to personal liberty on his part. On the contrary, the law treats him as property — as a thing to be bought, sold, and disposed of at the pleasure of his owner. He has no ability to enforce a right to liberty through the legal institutions of his jurisdiction, for those institutions are the very instruments by which his bondage is maintained. He has not formally claimed such a right, for any such claim would be met with punishment rather than adjudication. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet one is powerfully inclined to say that the slave has a right to liberty — a right that the laws of his jurisdiction violate rather than create. The abolitionist who denounces slavery as a violation of the slave's rights is not speaking nonsense; he is appealing to a standard that transcends the positive law. The definition has identified rights with their legal recognition, overlooking the possibility that there are rights — natural rights, moral rights — that exist independently of any legal system and that serve as the very standard by which legal systems are to be judged.

definition: A person P deserves outcome O if and only if (i) P has performed some action A that is causally responsible for bringing about O, (ii) A was performed voluntarily — P was not coerced or compelled, and (iii) A required effort or sacrifice on P's part.
conditions: P has performed some action causally responsible for bringing about O
the action was performed voluntarily
the action required effort or sacrifice
counterexample: Consider a child born with an extraordinary natural talent for music. By the age of five, and without any particular effort or training, she plays the piano with a facility that surpasses most adult performers. She has not performed any action that is causally responsible for her talent; it is a gift of nature. She has not exerted effort or made sacrifice in any significant sense; the music comes to her as easily as breathing. The first and third conditions fail. Yet one is inclined to say that she deserves recognition for her talent, or at the very least that she deserves the opportunity to develop it. The definition has confused desert with merit earned through labour.
missing_condition: The definition restricts desert to outcomes earned through voluntary effortful action, but persons may deserve things on the basis of their natural endowments, their needs, or their inherent dignity, independently of any action or effort. What is wanted is a broader notion of desert that is not exhausted by the earning of rewards through labour.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves an outcome if and only if he has performed some action causally responsible for bringing it about, the action was performed voluntarily, and the action required effort or sacrifice. The definition captures a familiar moral intuition. A farmer who has laboured through the seasons — ploughing, sowing, weeding, and harvesting — deserves the fruits of his harvest. His actions are causally responsible for the crop; he was not coerced into farming; and the work required sustained effort and sacrifice of leisure. A student who studies diligently for an examination deserves a good mark; her voluntary efforts are causally connected to her success, and the study required genuine exertion. Yet one must consider the case of a child born with an extraordinary natural talent for music. By the age of five, and without any particular instruction or training, she plays the piano with a facility and expressiveness that surpass most adult performers. She has not performed any action that is causally responsible for her musical gift; the talent is innate, a product of the natural lottery of birth. She has made no effort to acquire it; music comes to her as effortlessly as breathing comes to the rest of us. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet one is strongly inclined to say that she deserves something on account of her talent — recognition, at the very least, and the opportunity to develop her gift. It would seem unjust to deny her these things on the ground that she did not earn her talent through voluntary effort. The definition has taken one important basis for desert — the earning of rewards through effortful action — and treated it as the only basis, thereby excluding the claims that arise from natural endowment, from need, or from the inherent dignity of the person.

definition: A person P has given valid consent to X if and only if (i) P has expressed agreement to X by means of a verbal or written declaration, (ii) P was aware, at the time of the declaration, of the nature and consequences of X, and (iii) P was free from external coercion at the time of the declaration.
conditions: P has expressed agreement by verbal or written declaration
P was aware of the nature and consequences of X
P was free from external coercion
counterexample: Consider a man who is drowning and calls out to a passing boatman for rescue. The boatman agrees to rescue him on condition that he signs over the deed to his house. The drowning man, gasping for air, verbally agrees. He is aware of the nature and consequences of the bargain — he understands that he is surrendering his house in exchange for his life. He is free from coercion in the narrow sense that no human agent is threatening him; the peril arises from natural circumstances rather than from any person's coercive will. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one is reluctant to say that valid consent has been given, for the man's agreement was extracted under conditions of desperate necessity that left him no genuine alternative.
missing_condition: The definition requires only freedom from external coercion, but valid consent may also require that the consenting party is not under conditions of desperate necessity that eliminate any genuine alternative. What is wanted is a condition regarding the availability of reasonable alternatives, not merely the absence of a coercive agent.
passage: One might hold that a person has given valid consent to something if and only if he has expressed his agreement by means of a verbal or written declaration, he was aware of the nature and consequences of the matter at the time of the declaration, and he was free from external coercion. The definition captures many ordinary instances of consent. A man who signs a contract for the sale of his horse, knowing the terms and free from any threat, has given valid consent. A patient who, fully informed of the risks, agrees verbally to a surgical procedure has given valid consent. Yet one must consider a rather different case. A man is drowning in the open sea. A boatman passes by and offers to rescue him, but only on condition that the drowning man agrees to sign over the deed to his house. The drowning man, clinging to a piece of wreckage and gasping for air, verbally agrees. He is aware of the nature and consequences of the bargain; he understands perfectly well that he is surrendering his house in exchange for his life. And he is free from coercion in the narrow sense intended by the definition, for no human agent is threatening him with violence or harm. His peril arises from natural circumstances — the storm, the waves, the cold — rather than from any person's coercive will. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one is strongly reluctant to say that valid consent has been given. The man's agreement was extracted under conditions of desperate necessity. He had no genuine alternative; the only option other than agreement was death. An agreement made under such circumstances, though formally voluntary and fully informed, lacks the quality of genuine freedom that valid consent requires. The definition has attended to the form of consent — the declaration, the awareness, the absence of a coercive agent — whilst neglecting the substantive conditions under which alone consent can be meaningfully given.

definition: An imposition of suffering S upon a person P constitutes just punishment if and only if (i) P has been found guilty of a specific offence by a duly constituted tribunal, (ii) S is proportional to the gravity of the offence, and (iii) S serves to deter P and others from committing similar offences in the future.
conditions: P has been found guilty by a duly constituted tribunal
S is proportional to the gravity of the offence
S serves to deter P and others from future offences
counterexample: Consider the punishment of a man who has committed a very minor theft — he has stolen a loaf of bread to feed his starving family. He is found guilty by a duly constituted tribunal. The punishment imposed is proportional to the gravity of the offence — a small fine, let us say. But suppose that this particular offence, given the circumstances, cannot possibly serve a deterrent function: the man stole from desperation, and others who steal from desperation will not be deterred by the example of his fine, for desperation overrides the calculations upon which deterrence depends. The third condition fails. Yet the punishment may nevertheless be just, for it vindicates the rule of law and holds the man accountable for his action, independently of any deterrent effect.
missing_condition: The definition requires that punishment serve a deterrent function, but just punishment may be justified on grounds of retribution, accountability, or the vindication of the law, independently of whether it deters. What is wanted is a condition that allows for non-consequentialist justifications of punishment.
passage: One might propose that an imposition of suffering upon a person constitutes just punishment if and only if the person has been found guilty of a specific offence by a duly constituted tribunal, the suffering is proportional to the gravity of the offence, and the suffering serves to deter the person and others from committing similar offences in the future. The definition unites procedural, proportional, and consequentialist elements in a manner that has considerable appeal. When a thief is convicted by a court, sentenced to a term of imprisonment proportional to the severity of his crime, and the sentence serves as a warning to other would-be thieves, the punishment seems paradigmatically just. Yet one must consider the case of a man who has committed a very minor theft — he has stolen a loaf of bread to feed his starving children. He is found guilty by a duly constituted tribunal; the proceedings are fair and the verdict is sound. The punishment imposed — a small fine — is proportional to the gravity of the offence. But the third condition presents a difficulty. The man stole not from greed or malice but from desperate necessity. Others who find themselves in similar circumstances — starving, with no lawful means of obtaining food — will not be deterred by the example of his punishment, for desperation overrides the prudential calculations upon which deterrence depends. The fine imposed upon this man will not prevent the next starving father from stealing bread. The deterrent condition fails. Yet the punishment may nevertheless be just. It holds the man accountable for his action; it vindicates the principle that the law is to be respected even when one's circumstances are dire; and it affirms the rights of the person from whom the bread was taken. These are grounds for just punishment that have nothing to do with deterrence. The definition has required a consequentialist justification for all just punishment, overlooking the possibility that punishment may be justified on retributive or expressive grounds that are independent of its effects.

definition: A promise P made by agent A to agent B is binding if and only if (i) A has communicated P to B in explicit terms — that is, A has used words clearly indicating an undertaking, (ii) B has accepted P — that is, B has signalled his willingness to rely upon A's undertaking, and (iii) the fulfilment of P is within A's control at the time the promise is made.
conditions: A has communicated P to B in explicit terms
B has accepted P and signalled willingness to rely on A's undertaking
fulfilment of P is within A's control at the time of the promise
counterexample: Consider the implicit promise that arises between a physician and a patient. When a patient enters a physician's consulting room and the physician begins to examine him, no explicit words of undertaking are spoken. The physician does not say, 'I promise to act in your best interest and to maintain the confidentiality of what you tell me.' The patient does not formally accept any such promise. Yet there is a binding obligation upon the physician — an obligation that arises from the nature of the relationship and the reasonable expectations it generates. The first and second conditions fail, yet the promise is binding.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit communication and formal acceptance, but binding promises may arise implicitly from the nature of a relationship and the reasonable expectations it generates. What is wanted is a condition that encompasses implicit as well as explicit undertakings.
passage: One might hold that a promise is binding if and only if the promisor has communicated it to the promisee in explicit terms, the promisee has accepted it, and its fulfilment is within the promisor's control at the time the promise is made. The definition captures the standard case of a bilateral promise. When a man says to his neighbour, 'I promise to return your ladder by Friday,' and the neighbour nods his acceptance, and the man is able to return the ladder, the promise is binding by all three conditions. The explicit communication fixes the content of the undertaking; the acceptance creates the reliance; and the capacity to perform ensures that the obligation is not a mere empty gesture. Yet one must consider the case of a physician and his patient. When a patient enters the physician's consulting room and the physician begins to examine him, no words of explicit undertaking are spoken. The physician does not say, 'I promise to act in your best interest and to keep your confidences.' The patient does not formally accept any such promise; he does not say, 'I accept your undertaking.' Yet there is a binding obligation upon the physician — an obligation to exercise due care, to act in the patient's interest, and to maintain the confidentiality of what the patient reveals. This obligation is widely recognised, and its violation is regarded as a serious moral failure. The first and second conditions of the definition fail. No explicit communication has occurred, and no formal acceptance has been given. The obligation arises not from any exchange of words but from the nature of the relationship itself — from the fact that the patient has placed himself in a position of vulnerability and the physician has assumed a role that carries with it certain expectations. The definition has restricted binding promises to the explicit and the formally accepted, overlooking the vast domain of implicit obligations that arise from the roles we occupy and the relationships we enter.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) A intended to bring about O — that is, A acted with the purpose of producing O, (ii) A's action was the proximate cause of O, and (iii) A was aware, at the time of acting, that his action would bring about O.
conditions: A intended to bring about O
A's action was the proximate cause of O
A was aware that his action would bring about O
counterexample: Consider a factory owner who, in order to reduce costs, neglects the maintenance of his machinery. He does not intend for any worker to be injured; his purpose is solely to save money. He is not aware that a specific accident will occur; he merely knows, in a vague and general way, that poorly maintained machinery is more dangerous. One day a machine fails and a worker is killed. The factory owner did not intend the death, and he was not aware that his specific act of neglect would produce this specific outcome. The first and third conditions fail. Yet one is strongly inclined to hold him morally responsible, for the death resulted from his culpable negligence — his wilful failure to take precautions that he knew, in general terms, to be necessary.
missing_condition: The definition requires intention and specific awareness, but moral responsibility may arise from negligence — from the culpable failure to take precautions that a reasonable person would take. What is wanted is a condition that encompasses responsibility for negligent as well as intentional harm.
passage: One might propose that an agent is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if he intended to bring it about, his action was the proximate cause of it, and he was aware, at the time of acting, that his action would bring it about. The definition captures the clearest cases of moral responsibility. A man who deliberately strikes another, intending to injure him, knowing that his blow will cause harm, and whose blow is the proximate cause of the injury, is morally responsible in the fullest sense. The three conditions are manifestly satisfied, and no one would dispute the attribution of responsibility. Yet one must consider the case of a factory owner who, in order to reduce his costs, neglects the maintenance of the machinery in his factory. He does not intend for any worker to be injured; his sole purpose is to increase his profits by reducing expenditure. He is not specifically aware that an accident will occur; he knows, in a vague and general manner, that poorly maintained machinery poses a greater risk, but he does not foresee any particular incident. One day, a machine whose gears have not been inspected for months fails catastrophically, and a worker standing nearby is killed. The factory owner did not intend the worker's death. He was not aware, at the time of his neglect, that this specific outcome would follow from his specific omission. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet one is powerfully inclined to hold him morally responsible for the worker's death. His neglect was culpable; he knew that maintenance was necessary for safety and chose to forgo it for the sake of profit. A reasonable man in his position would have foreseen the risk and taken precautions. The definition has confined moral responsibility to cases of deliberate, knowing action, thereby excluding the important category of negligence — the culpable failure to exercise due care — which is among the most significant grounds of moral responsibility in both law and morals.

definition: The moral worth of an agent's action is determined if and only if (i) the action is assessed solely on the basis of the agent's intentions at the time of acting, (ii) factors beyond the agent's control are excluded from the moral assessment, and (iii) two agents who act with identical intentions in identical circumstances are assigned identical moral worth.
conditions: the action is assessed solely on the basis of the agent's intentions
factors beyond the agent's control are excluded from moral assessment
agents with identical intentions in identical circumstances receive identical moral worth
counterexample: Consider two drivers, each of whom drives recklessly through a residential neighbourhood at the same speed, with the same degree of negligence, and with precisely the same intentions — namely, the desire to arrive home quickly without any thought for the safety of pedestrians. By sheer chance, one driver arrives home without incident, whilst the other strikes and kills a child who steps into the road. The two drivers acted with identical intentions in circumstances that were, so far as their own agency is concerned, identical. By the lights of the definition, they should receive identical moral assessments. Yet in practice, and upon reflection, most persons judge the driver who killed the child far more harshly than the one who did not. The second condition — the exclusion of factors beyond the agent's control — fails to capture the moral reality of the situation.
missing_condition: The definition excludes factors beyond the agent's control from moral assessment, but the actual outcomes of our actions, even when those outcomes depend upon chance, may properly affect the moral assessment of what we have done. What is wanted is an account that acknowledges the role of fortune in moral life without reducing morality entirely to intention.
passage: One might hold that the moral worth of an agent's action is determined solely by his intentions at the time of acting, that factors beyond his control are to be excluded from the assessment, and that two agents who act with identical intentions in identical circumstances are to be assigned identical moral worth. The definition reflects a powerful and venerable moral intuition — the intuition that a man is to be judged by what he wills, not by what fortune brings about through his action. A man who intends to give a charitable donation and is prevented from doing so by a banking error is no less virtuous than one who succeeds in giving; the virtue lies in the will, not in the outcome. Yet one must consider the case of two motorists, each of whom drives recklessly through a residential neighbourhood at the same speed, with the same degree of carelessness, and with precisely the same intentions — namely, the wish to arrive home quickly without any thought for the safety of those around him. The two cases are, so far as the agents' intentions and circumstances are concerned, identical in every respect. But by sheer chance, one driver arrives home without incident, whilst the other strikes and kills a child who steps unexpectedly into the road. The definition requires that the two drivers receive identical moral assessments, since their intentions were identical and the difference in outcome was due entirely to factors beyond their control. Yet most persons, upon reflection, judge the driver who killed the child far more harshly than the one who did not. The driver who killed bears a moral burden — guilt, responsibility, a stain upon his character — that the other does not. The actual outcome of the action, though dependent upon chance, enters into the moral assessment and alters it. The definition has insisted upon the irrelevance of fortune to moral worth, but the moral life as it is actually lived and judged is pervaded by fortune at every turn.

definition: An action A is virtuous if and only if (i) A conforms to a moral rule — that is, A is of a type that is prescribed by a sound moral principle, (ii) A is performed consistently — the agent performs actions of type A as a matter of habit, and (iii) A is performed without inner conflict — the agent does not experience reluctance or struggle in performing A.
conditions: A conforms to a moral rule
A is performed consistently as a matter of habit
A is performed without inner conflict
counterexample: Consider a man who is by nature irascible and quick to anger. Through sustained effort of will, he restrains himself from retaliating when he is insulted. He experiences considerable inner conflict — his natural inclination is to strike back, and the restraint costs him great effort. The third condition fails; the action is performed with reluctance and struggle. Yet one is inclined to say that his action is not merely virtuous but more virtuous, not less, for having been achieved against the grain of his natural temperament. The ease and absence of conflict that the definition requires may be the mark of a fortunate temperament rather than of virtue.
missing_condition: The definition requires the absence of inner conflict, but virtuous action may be most admirable precisely when it is performed against strong contrary inclination. What is wanted is a condition that allows for the moral significance of struggle and self-mastery.
passage: One might propose that an action is virtuous if and only if it conforms to a moral rule, is performed consistently as a matter of habit, and is performed without inner conflict. The definition captures certain features of the virtuous life as it is ordinarily conceived. The temperate man abstains from excess not by a painful act of self-denial but naturally and easily, as a matter of settled disposition. His temperance is habitual, rule-conforming, and free from inner turmoil. Yet one must consider the case of a man who is by nature irascible and quick to anger — a man whose first impulse, upon being insulted, is to retaliate with violence. Through a sustained and painful effort of will, he restrains himself. He holds his tongue, unclenches his fists, and walks away. The action conforms to the moral rule that forbids violent retaliation, and let us suppose that he performs such acts of restraint consistently, as a matter of settled practice. But the action is not performed without inner conflict. On the contrary, the conflict is intense. His natural inclination pulls him powerfully towards retaliation, and the restraint costs him enormous effort. He feels the anger surging within him and must wrestle it down by an act of deliberate will. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet one is strongly inclined to say that his restraint is not merely virtuous but more virtuous, not less, for having been achieved in the teeth of a powerful contrary inclination. The man who restrains himself easily, because his temperament is naturally mild, may be fortunate rather than virtuous. The man who restrains himself with difficulty, because his temperament is violent and his self-mastery is hard-won, displays a quality of moral strength that the definition cannot accommodate. The definition has confused the fruits of a fortunate temperament with the essence of virtue.

definition: A legal system L is just if and only if (i) the laws of L are applied uniformly to all persons within its jurisdiction, (ii) the laws of L are publicly promulgated — that is, they are made known to those who are subject to them, and (iii) the laws of L are enforced by impartial tribunals.
conditions: the laws are applied uniformly to all persons
the laws are publicly promulgated
the laws are enforced by impartial tribunals
counterexample: Consider a legal system in which the laws are applied uniformly, publicly promulgated, and enforced by scrupulously impartial judges, but the content of the laws is grossly unjust — the laws mandate the persecution of a particular religious minority, the confiscation of their property, and their exclusion from public life. The three conditions are satisfied: the laws are applied uniformly to all members of the minority, they are publicly known, and the tribunals enforce them without bias or favour. Yet the legal system is manifestly unjust. The injustice lies not in the administration of the law but in the law itself.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to the formal or procedural features of a legal system — uniformity, publicity, impartiality — but justice requires that the content of the law be just as well. What is wanted is a substantive condition regarding the justice of the laws themselves, not merely of their administration.
passage: One might hold that a legal system is just if and only if its laws are applied uniformly to all persons within its jurisdiction, publicly promulgated, and enforced by impartial tribunals. The definition captures important procedural virtues. A legal system that applies its laws capriciously — punishing one man for theft whilst permitting another to steal with impunity — is unjust, and the definition rightly condemns it. A legal system whose laws are secret — unknown to those who must obey them — is unjust, and the definition rightly condemns it. A legal system whose tribunals are corrupt or partial — deciding cases on the basis of bribery or personal connexion rather than on the merits — is unjust, and the definition rightly condemns it. Yet one must consider a legal system in which all three conditions are satisfied but the content of the laws is grossly unjust. Suppose that a jurisdiction enacts laws mandating the persecution of a particular religious minority — laws requiring the confiscation of their property, their exclusion from public office, and their confinement to designated areas. These laws are applied uniformly; every member of the minority is subjected to the same treatment. They are publicly promulgated; the laws are printed in the official gazette and posted in every public square. And they are enforced by tribunals that are scrupulously impartial in the sense that they apply the laws consistently and without regard to bribery or personal favour. The three conditions of the definition are fully satisfied. Yet the legal system is manifestly unjust. Its injustice consists not in any defect of procedure or administration but in the substance of the laws themselves. The definition has attended only to the formal features of justice — uniformity, publicity, and impartiality — whilst overlooking the possibility that a legal system may be formally impeccable and substantively monstrous.

definition: An agent A is morally obligated to refrain from action X if and only if (i) X causes direct physical harm to another person, (ii) A is aware that X causes such harm, and (iii) A has the capacity to refrain from X without suffering undue hardship.
conditions: X causes direct physical harm to another person
A is aware that X causes such harm
A has the capacity to refrain without undue hardship
counterexample: Consider a man who systematically deceives his elderly mother regarding the management of her finances, gradually transferring her savings into his own accounts. The deception causes no direct physical harm; the mother suffers no bodily injury and is in good health. Yet she is wronged. Her autonomy is violated, her trust is betrayed, and her material security is undermined. The first condition fails — no direct physical harm is inflicted — yet one is strongly inclined to say that the man is morally obligated to refrain from the deception.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the basis of obligation to direct physical harm, but moral obligations to refrain from action may arise from harms that are not physical — deception, betrayal of trust, violation of autonomy, psychological cruelty. What is wanted is a broader notion of harm that encompasses non-physical injuries.
passage: One might propose that an agent is morally obligated to refrain from an action if and only if the action causes direct physical harm to another person, the agent is aware that it causes such harm, and the agent has the capacity to refrain without suffering undue hardship. The definition captures clear cases of moral prohibition. A man who strikes another without provocation causes direct physical harm; he is aware of what he does; and he could refrain without hardship. He is morally obligated to refrain, and the definition correctly identifies the obligation. Yet one must consider a rather different case. A man systematically deceives his elderly mother regarding the management of her finances. Over a period of years, he gradually transfers her savings into his own accounts, presenting her with falsified statements that suggest her assets are intact. The mother suffers no physical harm whatever. She is in good health, well fed, and comfortable. No bodily injury is inflicted upon her, and she is unaware that anything is amiss. The first condition of the definition fails; the action causes no direct physical harm. Yet the mother is wronged, and wronged grievously. Her autonomy is violated — she is denied the ability to make informed decisions about her own resources. Her trust is betrayed — the person upon whom she relies most completely has exploited that reliance for his own enrichment. Her material security is quietly destroyed. The moral obligation to refrain from this deception is powerful and unmistakable, yet it does not rest upon the infliction of physical harm. The definition has taken one species of morally relevant harm — physical injury — and treated it as the only species, thereby excluding the vast domain of non-physical wrongs: deception, betrayal, exploitation, and the violation of trust, which are among the gravest moral injuries one person can inflict upon another.

definition: A person P possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) P acts in the face of danger or difficulty rather than withdrawing, (ii) P acts from a settled disposition — that is, P's courageous acts are not isolated or aberrant but proceed from a stable character trait, and (iii) P is motivated by a recognition that the end to be achieved is good and worth the risk.
conditions: P acts in the face of danger or difficulty rather than withdrawing
P acts from a settled disposition rather than in isolated or aberrant fashion
P is motivated by a recognition that the end to be achieved is good and worth the risk
counterexample: Consider a soldier who, having been given a drug that suppresses all feeling of fear, advances unflinchingly under heavy fire. He acts in the face of danger, and he does so consistently — the drug ensures that he never withdraws. Moreover, he believes the cause for which he fights to be just and recognises the end as good and worth the risk. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to call this courage. The soldier does not overcome fear; he does not feel it at all. His steadfastness is a pharmaceutical artefact rather than a victory of character over natural inclination. The virtue of courage, it appears, requires not merely the absence of withdrawal but the presence of fear that is mastered — and the definition fails to capture this.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the courageous agent must act despite experiencing fear or apprehension. Courage involves the mastery of fear, not its absence, and a person who faces danger without any felt resistance does not display the virtue in its proper sense.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses the virtue of courage if and only if the person acts in the face of danger or difficulty rather than withdrawing, does so from a settled disposition of character, and is motivated by a recognition that the end to be achieved is good and worth the risk. The definition has much to recommend it. The soldier who charges a fortified position because he recognises that his comrades will be lost unless the position is taken — who does so not on a single occasion only but as a matter of habitual character — seems to exemplify courage in its fullest sense. The firefighter who enters a burning building, the physician who treats patients during a plague, the reformer who speaks against tyranny at personal cost — all of these satisfy the three conditions, and all are rightly called courageous. Yet one must consider a rather different case. Suppose a soldier is administered a pharmacological agent that entirely suppresses the feeling of fear. Under its influence he advances calmly under the most withering fire, exposes himself to dangers that would cause any untreated man to falter, and does so consistently over many engagements. He satisfies the first condition: he acts in the face of danger without withdrawing. He satisfies the second: his behaviour proceeds from what is, during the period of treatment, a stable and reliable disposition. And he satisfies the third: he believes his cause to be just, and he recognises the end as good and worthy of the risk he runs. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this courage. The soldier does not overcome fear, for he does not feel it. His steadfastness is not a triumph of character over the natural inclination to flee, but a chemical suppression of the very inclination that courage is called upon to master. One might observe that what distinguishes the courageous person from the merely fearless is precisely the presence of fear — fear that is acknowledged, confronted, and overcome through strength of will and commitment to the good. The definition has identified courage with its outward manifestation — steady action in the face of danger — whilst omitting the inner struggle that gives the virtue its moral worth.

definition: A distribution D of goods among the members of a society is just if and only if (i) D is the result of voluntary exchange — that is, each transfer of goods was freely agreed to by the parties involved, (ii) no force, fraud, or coercion was employed in any transaction leading to D, and (iii) the initial holdings from which the chain of exchanges began were themselves legitimately acquired.
conditions: D is the result of voluntary exchange
no force, fraud, or coercion was employed in any transaction
the initial holdings were legitimately acquired
counterexample: Consider a society in which the initial holdings were legitimately acquired, all subsequent exchanges were voluntary, and no force or fraud was employed at any stage. Over the course of several generations, however, the cumulative effect of individually fair transactions is that a single family comes to own nearly all the arable land, whilst the remaining families subsist on wages so meagre that they cannot afford adequate food or shelter. The distribution satisfies all three conditions — every exchange was voluntary, every transaction was free from coercion, and the initial holdings were legitimate. Yet it appears that a distribution in which one family controls the necessities of life whilst hundreds of families go hungry is not just, however impeccable the process by which it arose.
missing_condition: The definition attends only to the procedural history of the distribution — how goods were acquired and transferred — and ignores the substantive outcome. A just distribution, it appears, must also satisfy some constraint upon the pattern of holdings themselves, such that extreme concentrations of essential resources leaving others destitute are ruled out regardless of procedural fairness.
passage: One might hold that a distribution of goods among the members of a society is just if and only if the distribution is the result of voluntary exchange, no force, fraud, or coercion was employed in any transaction leading to it, and the initial holdings from which the chain of exchanges began were themselves legitimately acquired. The definition captures a powerful intuition about justice: that what matters is not the pattern of who holds what, but the process by which the holdings came about. If every exchange was freely entered into, if no party was deceived or compelled, and if the original acquisitions were fair, then — on this view — the resulting distribution is just, whatever it may look like. One might observe that this accords well with our ordinary moral thinking about small-scale transactions. If I trade my horse for your plough, and both of us consent freely and without deception, the resulting distribution of goods seems perfectly fair. Yet one must consider the long-term consequences of such a principle applied across generations. Suppose a society begins with holdings that are, by any standard, legitimately acquired and fairly distributed. Over the course of several generations, through a long series of individually voluntary exchanges — each one free from force and fraud — a single family comes gradually to own nearly all the arable land in the region. The remaining families, dispossessed by degrees through transactions that were always technically voluntary but increasingly driven by economic necessity, find themselves without land, without savings, and dependent upon wages set by the landowning family. The wages are barely sufficient for subsistence; children go hungry; the sick cannot be tended. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied throughout: the initial holdings were legitimate, every transaction was voluntary, and no force or fraud was employed at any stage. Yet it appears that the resulting distribution — in which one family controls the means of life whilst hundreds suffer deprivation — is not just. We should hesitate to call just a state of affairs in which the necessities of life are concentrated in a few hands whilst the many lack the means of a decent existence, however clean the procedural history may be. The definition has attended exclusively to how the distribution came about and has ignored altogether what the distribution looks like — it has no resources to condemn even the most grotesque inequality, provided the history is clean.

definition: A person P has a moral obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) P has explicitly promised, pledged, or vowed to perform A, (ii) the person to whom the promise was made is still living and has not released P from the commitment, and (iii) performing A does not require P to violate any other moral obligation.
conditions: P has explicitly promised to perform A
the promisee is still living and has not released P from the commitment
performing A does not require P to violate another moral obligation
counterexample: Consider a physician who, walking along a deserted road, comes upon a stranger lying injured in a ditch. The physician has made no promise to this stranger, has never seen him before, and has entered into no commitment whatever regarding his welfare. Yet it appears that the physician is under a moral obligation to render assistance — an obligation that arises not from any explicit promise but from the stranger's need, the physician's capacity to help, and the general moral requirement to aid those in distress when one can do so without excessive cost. The first condition fails entirely: there is no promise, pledge, or vow. Yet the obligation is real.
missing_condition: The definition grounds moral obligation exclusively in explicit promises, but obligations may arise from other sources — from the needs of others, from one's special capacities, from the requirements of common humanity. What is wanted is an account that recognises obligations generated by circumstances as well as those generated by voluntary commitments.
passage: One might propose that a person has a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if the person has explicitly promised, pledged, or vowed to perform the action, the person to whom the promise was made is still living and has not released the promisor from the commitment, and performing the action does not require the violation of any other moral obligation. The definition has a certain attractiveness. It grounds obligation in the voluntary act of promising, which accords with the liberal intuition that we are bound only by commitments we have freely undertaken. And the further conditions ensure that the obligation is still in force — the promisee has not released us — and that it does not conflict with other duties. Many of our most pressing obligations do indeed arise from promises: the obligation to repay a debt, to keep an appointment, to fulfil the terms of a contract. Yet one must consider a case in which no promise has been made at all. A physician, walking along a deserted country road, comes upon a stranger lying in a ditch, bleeding from a wound. The physician has never met this man before, has made no promise to him, and has entered into no agreement of any kind regarding his welfare. The stranger is a complete unknown. Yet it appears — and it would be difficult to deny — that the physician is under a moral obligation to stop, to render what assistance she can, and to ensure that the injured man receives proper care. This obligation arises not from any promise, pledge, or vow, but from the conjunction of the stranger's urgent need, the physician's capacity to help, and the moral requirement — felt by persons of ordinary conscience — to aid those in distress when one can do so without excessive sacrifice. The first condition of the definition fails entirely: there is no explicit promise. The definition has treated one source of moral obligation — voluntary commitment through promising — as the sole source, overlooking the obligations that arise from need, from capacity, from the sheer fact of another's suffering and one's power to relieve it.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P's claim to X is recognised by the legal system of the society in which P lives, (ii) the legal system provides mechanisms for the enforcement of P's claim, and (iii) violation of P's claim is subject to legal penalty or remedy.
conditions: P's claim to X is recognised by the legal system of P's society
the legal system provides mechanisms for enforcement
violation of P's claim is subject to legal penalty or remedy
counterexample: Consider a slave in a society whose legal system not only fails to recognise any right of the slave to personal liberty but positively sanctions the institution of slavery and provides legal mechanisms for the enforcement of the slaveholder's claims. The slave has no legally recognised claim to freedom, no mechanism of enforcement is available to him, and no legal penalty attaches to the denial of his liberty. All three conditions fail. Yet it appears that the slave has a right to personal liberty — a right that the legal system of his society fails to recognise but does not thereby extinguish. The abolitionist who declares that the slave has a right to freedom is not merely predicting a future change in the law; she is asserting that the right exists now, notwithstanding the law's denial of it.
missing_condition: The definition identifies rights with legal recognition, but there may be moral or natural rights that exist prior to and independently of any legal system. A right that a legal system fails to recognise is not thereby a right that does not exist; it is a right that the legal system has failed to acknowledge.
passage: One might hold that a person has a right to something if and only if the person's claim is recognised by the legal system of the society in which the person lives, the legal system provides mechanisms for enforcement, and violation of the claim is subject to legal penalty or remedy. The definition has the advantage of precision. Rights, on this account, are determinate legal facts: one either has a legally recognised claim or one does not, and the question can be settled by examining the statute books and the decisions of the courts. The property owner has a right to his land because the legal system recognises his title, provides courts in which he may seek redress against trespassers, and imposes penalties upon those who encroach upon his holding. The citizen has a right to vote because the law confers the franchise, the electoral machinery enforces it, and interference with the exercise of the franchise is punishable. Yet one must consider the position of a slave in a society that not only fails to recognise any right of the slave to personal liberty but positively sanctions the institution of slavery. The legal system of such a society treats the slave as property, provides mechanisms for the enforcement of the slaveholder's dominion, and imposes penalties upon those who assist the slave in escaping. The slave has no legally recognised claim to freedom; no mechanism of enforcement is available to him; no legal penalty attaches to the continued denial of his liberty. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet it appears, with a force that is difficult to resist, that the slave has a right to personal liberty — a right that the legal system violates rather than one that it merely fails to confer. The abolitionist who declares that the slave has a right to freedom is not merely advocating a reform of the law; she is asserting that the right already exists, that the legal system has failed to recognise something that is real and binding independently of its recognition. The definition has identified rights with their legal acknowledgement, but there appear to be rights that exist prior to and independently of any legal system — rights that a legal system may recognise or deny but cannot create or destroy.

definition: A person P deserves a reward R if and only if (i) P has performed an action A that brought about a beneficial result, (ii) P performed A voluntarily — that is, without compulsion or coercion, and (iii) P intended to bring about the beneficial result that A in fact produced.
conditions: P has performed an action that brought about a beneficial result
P performed the action voluntarily
P intended to bring about the beneficial result
counterexample: Consider a research chemist who is attempting to synthesise a particular compound for industrial use. In the course of her experiments she makes an error — she mixes the wrong reagents in the wrong proportions — and the result, quite unexpectedly, is a substance that proves to be an extraordinarily effective treatment for a serious disease. The beneficial result is immense: thousands of lives are saved. She performed her action voluntarily, and the action brought about a beneficial result. But she did not intend the beneficial result that the action produced; she intended to synthesise an industrial compound and failed even at that. The third condition is not satisfied. Yet one might observe that she deserves some recognition and reward, for it was her skill, her training, and her dedicated labour in the laboratory that created the conditions under which the discovery could occur.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agent have intended the specific beneficial outcome, but desert may arise from the exercise of skill, effort, and dedication that creates the conditions for a beneficial result, even when the particular result was unintended. What is wanted is a notion of desert that can accommodate the role of effort and competence alongside that of intention.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves a reward if and only if the person has performed an action that brought about a beneficial result, performed it voluntarily, and intended to bring about the beneficial result that the action produced. The definition has an intuitive appeal. The physician who devises a new treatment for a disease and thereby saves many lives deserves recognition: she acted voluntarily, the result was beneficial, and she intended precisely that result. The soldier who undertakes a dangerous mission to rescue captured comrades, and succeeds, deserves commendation for the same reasons. Intention seems important because it connects the agent's will to the outcome: the agent aimed at the good result and achieved it, and this connection seems essential to the claim of desert. Yet one must consider the case of a research chemist who is labouring to synthesise a compound for industrial purposes. In the course of her experiments she commits an error — she mixes the wrong reagents in incorrect proportions — and the result, entirely unexpected, is a substance that proves to be an extraordinarily effective treatment for a grave disease. The beneficial result is immense: thousands of patients are cured, and the chemist is celebrated in the popular press as a benefactor of humanity. She performed her action voluntarily; no one compelled her to work in her laboratory. The action brought about a beneficial result of the first magnitude. But she did not intend to produce a pharmaceutical agent; she intended to synthesise an industrial compound, and she failed even at that modest goal. The specific beneficial result that her action produced was no part of her plan. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet it appears that the chemist deserves some measure of recognition and reward. Her long training, her mastery of chemical technique, her dedication to the painstaking work of the laboratory — these created the conditions under which the accidental discovery could be made and recognised for what it was. Another person, lacking her skill and her training, might have produced the same mixture and discarded it without recognising its potential. The definition has required that desert flow from the intention to produce a particular good result, but it appears that the exercise of skill, effort, and competence that creates the conditions for a beneficial outcome may itself ground a claim of desert, even when the specific outcome was entirely unintended.

definition: A person P has given valid consent to an action A if and only if (i) P has communicated agreement to A by an explicit verbal or written declaration, (ii) P was in possession of all material facts relevant to A at the time of the declaration, and (iii) P was under no duress or undue influence at the time of the declaration.
conditions: P communicated agreement by explicit verbal or written declaration
P was in possession of all material facts relevant to A
P was under no duress or undue influence
counterexample: Consider a patient in a hospital who is informed by her physician that a minor surgical procedure is necessary. The physician explains the procedure, its risks, and its benefits in clear and accurate terms. The patient verbally agrees. She possesses all material facts. She is under no duress. All three conditions are satisfied. But the patient is fourteen years old, and though she understands the words the physician has used, she lacks the maturity of judgement to weigh the long-term consequences of the procedure against its short-term benefits. Her agreement, though explicit, informed, and uncoerced, does not constitute valid consent, for she lacks the capacity for the kind of deliberation that consent presupposes.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement of decisional capacity — the cognitive and emotional maturity to understand the significance of the decision and to deliberate about its consequences. A person who lacks this capacity — whether by reason of youth, mental impairment, or other cause — cannot give valid consent, however explicit, informed, and uncoerced the declaration may be.
passage: One might hold that a person has given valid consent to an action if and only if the person has communicated agreement by an explicit verbal or written declaration, was in possession of all material facts at the time, and was under no duress or undue influence. The definition captures several important features of consent. The requirement of an explicit declaration ensures that consent is outward and verifiable rather than merely inferred. The requirement of full information ensures that the consenting party was not deceived or kept in ignorance of facts that would have affected the decision. And the requirement that the declaration be free from duress ensures that the agreement was genuine rather than coerced. A person who signs a contract after having read its terms, who understands what she is agreeing to, and who signs freely and without pressure has, on this account, given valid consent. Yet one must consider the case of a patient in a hospital. A physician informs her that a minor surgical procedure is necessary and explains, in clear and accurate terms, the nature of the procedure, the risks it carries, and the benefits it is expected to produce. The patient listens attentively, asks several questions, and then declares that she agrees to the procedure. She possesses all material facts, and she is under no duress or undue influence — the physician has not pressured her, and no one else has exerted any coercion. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But the patient is fourteen years old. She has understood the words the physician used, and she has grasped the immediate import of the information conveyed to her.
