Yet she lacks the maturity of judgement — the capacity for deliberation about long-term consequences, the ability to weigh competing considerations with the seriousness they require — that consent to a medical procedure presupposes. Her agreement, though explicit, informed, and uncoerced, falls short of valid consent, for she has not yet developed the cognitive and emotional capacities necessary to make decisions of this gravity on her own behalf. The definition has attended to the external marks of consent — the declaration, the information, the absence of pressure — but has overlooked the internal condition upon which the validity of consent ultimately depends: the capacity for mature deliberation.

definition: An act of punishment is morally justified if and only if (i) the person punished has committed a moral wrong, (ii) the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the wrong, and (iii) the punishment is inflicted by a legitimate authority — that is, by an agent or institution with the standing to impose penalties.
conditions: the person punished has committed a moral wrong
the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the wrong
the punishment is inflicted by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Consider a totalitarian regime that enacts a law forbidding the practice of a particular religion. A citizen who continues to practise that religion in private is arrested and sentenced to a term of imprisonment by a duly constituted court. The regime's courts are legitimate authorities in the formal sense — they are the recognised institutions for the administration of justice within that society. The punishment is proportionate: the sentence is not excessive given the offence as defined by the regime's law. And the citizen has violated the law. Yet the citizen has committed no moral wrong. The practice of religion in private harms no one and violates no moral principle. The law itself is unjust. The first condition fails: there is no moral wrong. Yet all other conditions are satisfied, and the punishment has the full apparatus of legitimacy behind it.
missing_condition: The definition takes the commission of a moral wrong as a condition but does not require that the rule under which the person is punished itself be morally legitimate. A punishment inflicted under an unjust law — for conduct that is not morally wrong — is not morally justified, even if it is formally authorised and proportionate.
passage: One might propose that an act of punishment is morally justified if and only if the person punished has committed a moral wrong, the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the wrong, and the punishment is inflicted by a legitimate authority. The definition accords with much of our ordinary thinking about the justification of punishment. The thief who steals from his neighbour has committed a moral wrong; the sentence imposed by the court is proportionate to the offence; the court is a legitimate authority empowered to impose penalties. The punishment seems clearly justified. Similarly, the person who commits an assault and receives a proportionate penalty from a duly constituted tribunal has been justly punished. The definition appears to capture what is essential. Yet one must consider the following case. A totalitarian regime enacts a law forbidding the practice of a particular religion. A citizen of that regime continues to practise the religion in private — in her own home, with members of her own family. She is discovered, arrested, and brought before the regime's courts, which are the duly constituted institutions for the administration of justice within that society. The court sentences her to a term of imprisonment. The punishment is proportionate in the sense that the sentence is not excessive given the offence as the law defines it; other persons convicted of the same offence have received similar sentences. And the court is a legitimate authority in the formal sense — it is the recognised organ of adjudication within the legal system of the regime. Yet the citizen has committed no moral wrong. The practice of a religion in private, with willing fellow adherents, harms no one and violates no moral principle that a reasonable person would accept. The law under which she is convicted is itself unjust — it prohibits conduct that ought not to be prohibited. The first condition of the definition fails: there is no moral wrong. But the definition, as formulated, cannot explain what has gone wrong, for it does not require that the rule under which the person is punished be itself morally legitimate. The definition has assumed that the identification of a moral wrong is a straightforward matter, when in truth what counts as a wrong may be contested, and a punishment inflicted for conduct that is not genuinely wrong is not justified, however proportionate and however authoritative.

definition: A person P is morally bound to keep a promise if and only if (i) P made the promise freely and without coercion, (ii) P made the promise to a specific person or persons who relied upon it, and (iii) keeping the promise will not cause serious harm to any innocent party.
conditions: P made the promise freely and without coercion
P made the promise to a specific person who relied upon it
keeping the promise will not cause serious harm to any innocent party
counterexample: Consider a man who, twenty years ago, freely promised his childhood friend that he would one day return a book that the friend had lent him. The friend relied upon the promise at the time and was pleased by it. The friend has since emigrated to a distant country, and the two men have had no contact for many years. The book is a cheap paperback of no sentimental or monetary value, and the friend — now prosperous and settled — has in all probability forgotten both the book and the promise. All three conditions are satisfied: the promise was freely made, it was made to a specific person who relied upon it, and keeping it will cause no harm. Yet one might observe that the man is not meaningfully bound to keep this promise. The reliance that generated the obligation has long since lapsed; the promisee has no current expectation and no current interest in the performance; the fulfilment of the promise would serve no purpose that either party cares about. The definition fails to capture the possibility that a promise may lose its binding force over time as the circumstances that gave it meaning cease to obtain.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the promisee still has a current interest in or expectation of performance. A promise may lose its binding force when the reliance that grounded it has lapsed and the promisee no longer has any stake in the performance.
passage: One might hold that a person is morally bound to keep a promise if and only if the promise was made freely and without coercion, was made to a specific person who relied upon it, and keeping the promise will not cause serious harm to any innocent party. The definition appears sound in many ordinary cases. A tradesman who promises to deliver goods by a certain date, and whose customer relies upon the promise in planning his affairs, is bound to keep the promise — provided he made it freely and keeping it will cause no harm. The conditions seem individually necessary and jointly sufficient. Yet one must consider a rather different case. Suppose a man, when he was a young student, freely promised his childhood friend that he would one day return a book — a cheap paperback novel — that the friend had lent him. The friend was pleased by the promise and relied upon it in a mild and undemanding way; he expected eventually to have his book back. Twenty years pass. The two men lose contact entirely. The friend emigrates to a distant country, becomes prosperous, and — in all probability — forgets both the book and the promise. The book itself is a tattered paperback of no sentimental or monetary value. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: the promise was made freely, it was made to a specific person who relied upon it, and returning the book would cause no harm to anyone. Yet it appears that the man is not meaningfully bound to keep this promise. The reliance that gave the promise its moral force has long since evaporated. The promisee has no current expectation of performance, no current interest in the book, and no awareness that the obligation is still being considered by the promisor. The fulfilment of the promise would be a meaningless gesture — a letter to a forgotten address containing a worthless book in response to a forgotten request. The definition has captured the conditions under which a promise is initially binding but has failed to account for the possibility that those conditions may cease to obtain, and that a promise whose ground has fallen away may no longer bind.

definition: A person P is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) P performed an action A that was a cause of O, (ii) P could have acted otherwise — that is, P had the ability and the opportunity to refrain from performing A, and (iii) P foresaw, or could reasonably have been expected to foresee, that A would lead to O.
conditions: P performed an action that was a cause of O
P could have acted otherwise
P foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen that A would lead to O
counterexample: Consider a factory owner who, over the course of many years, discharges waste into a river. At the time of the discharges, the best scientific opinion holds that the waste is harmless. The owner has consulted chemists and has been assured that no danger exists. Twenty years later, it is discovered that the waste contains a compound that, through a process not understood at the time, has contaminated the water supply of a downstream town and caused serious illness among the inhabitants. The owner performed the action that caused the harm, and he could have acted otherwise — he chose to discharge the waste when he might have disposed of it by other means. But he did not foresee, and could not reasonably have been expected to foresee, that his action would lead to this outcome. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that the owner bears some responsibility: it was his waste, his factory, his decision to discharge rather than to dispose by other means, and the harm would not have occurred but for his action.
missing_condition: The definition requires foreseeability, but there may be cases in which an agent bears responsibility for an outcome that was not foreseeable at the time of acting, particularly when the agent voluntarily undertook an activity that carried unknown risks and could have chosen a safer alternative. What is wanted is an account that can accommodate responsibility for unforeseeable consequences of voluntary risk-creating conduct.
passage: One might propose that a person is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if the person performed an action that was a cause of the outcome, could have acted otherwise, and foresaw or could reasonably have been expected to foresee that the action would lead to that outcome. The definition has considerable plausibility. If I throw a stone through a window, I am responsible for the broken glass because I performed the act that caused the damage, I could have refrained from throwing the stone, and I foresaw — or certainly should have foreseen — that throwing a stone at a window would break it. The foreseeability condition seems essential: it would be unjust to hold a person responsible for consequences that no reasonable person could have anticipated. Yet one must consider a rather different case. A factory owner operates a chemical works on the bank of a river. Over the course of many years he discharges the waste products of his manufacturing process into the water. At the time of the discharges, the best available scientific opinion holds that the substances in question are entirely harmless. The owner has taken the precaution of consulting chemists, who have assured him that no danger to public health exists. He has acted in accordance with the prevailing standards of industrial practice. Twenty years later, however, it is discovered that the waste contains a compound which, through a biochemical process not understood at the time, has accumulated in the sediment of the river, has contaminated the water supply of a town downstream, and has caused serious illness among the inhabitants. The owner performed the action — the discharge of waste — that was the cause of the harm. He could have acted otherwise: he might have chosen to dispose of the waste by other means, at greater expense. But he did not foresee, and could not reasonably have been expected to foresee, that his action would lead to this outcome; the causal chain was unknown to science at the time. The third condition fails. Yet it appears that the owner bears some measure of responsibility. It was his factory, his waste, his decision to discharge it into the river, and the harm would not have occurred but for his choice. The definition demands foreseeability, but there are cases in which the agent's voluntary creation of a risk — even a risk not recognised at the time — may ground a claim of responsibility that the foreseeability condition cannot capture.

definition: Two agents A1 and A2 are equally blameworthy for an action-type T if and only if (i) A1 and A2 performed the same type of action with the same degree of recklessness, (ii) A1 and A2 had the same intentions and the same state of mind at the time of acting, and (iii) A1 and A2 were subject to the same moral norms and had the same capacity for moral deliberation.
conditions: A1 and A2 performed the same type of action with the same degree of recklessness
A1 and A2 had the same intentions and state of mind
A1 and A2 were subject to the same moral norms and had the same capacity for moral deliberation
counterexample: Consider two motorists who drive home from a dinner party having drunk the same quantity of wine. Each drives with the same degree of recklessness — slightly too fast, slightly inattentive — and each has the same intention, the same state of mind, and the same capacity for moral deliberation. The only difference between them is a matter of luck: a child runs into the road in the path of the first motorist, who strikes and kills the child, whilst no one appears in the path of the second motorist, who arrives home without incident. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: the motorists performed the same type of action with the same recklessness, had the same intentions, and were subject to the same norms. The definition therefore declares them equally blameworthy. Yet our moral practice — and, one might observe, our moral feeling — treats them very differently. The first motorist bears the weight of a death; the second does not.
missing_condition: The definition ignores the role of actual outcomes in the assignment of blame. Our moral practice appears to make blameworthiness sensitive to what actually happens as a consequence of one's action, not merely to the quality of one's will at the time of acting. Whether this feature of our practice is defensible is a further question, but the definition fails to capture it.
passage: One might hold that two agents are equally blameworthy for an action-type if and only if they performed the same type of action with the same degree of recklessness, had the same intentions and state of mind, and were subject to the same moral norms and possessed the same capacity for moral deliberation. The definition expresses a principle that many philosophers have found attractive: that blameworthiness is a function of the quality of the agent's will, not of the consequences that happen to follow from the agent's action. If two persons act with identical recklessness, identical intentions, and identical capacities for moral reflection, then — on this view — they deserve identical blame, regardless of whether fortune smiles upon one and frowns upon the other. Yet one must consider the following pair of cases. Two motorists leave a dinner party at which each has drunk the same quantity of wine — enough to impair judgement but not enough to incapacitate. Each drives home along the same road, at the same speed, with the same degree of inattention. Their intentions are the same: each wants to get home quickly and has chosen, recklessly, to drive rather than to call a taxi. Their states of mind are identical; their capacities for moral deliberation are the same; they are subject to the same moral norms. The only difference is a matter of chance. A child darts into the road from behind a parked car in the path of the first motorist, who cannot stop in time and strikes the child, who dies. No child appears in the path of the second motorist, who arrives home without incident and goes to bed. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: the two motorists are, by the standard the definition provides, equally blameworthy. Yet our moral practice treats them very differently. The first motorist is condemned — by the law, by public opinion, and by his own conscience — as the agent of a terrible harm. The second is merely a person who drove when he should not have driven. The definition insists that blameworthiness is independent of actual outcomes, but our deepest moral responses appear to resist this conclusion, making the degree of blame sensitive to what in fact results from the agent's conduct.

definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act in certain ways across a range of circumstances, (ii) the actions to which T disposes the agent are generally beneficial to others or to the community, and (iii) the agent who possesses T exercises it voluntarily and with understanding of what he does.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act in certain ways across a range of circumstances
The actions to which T disposes the agent are generally beneficial to others or to the community
The agent possesses and exercises T voluntarily and with understanding
counterexample: Consider a man who has been so thoroughly conditioned by a rigorous upbringing that he invariably tells the truth. His truthfulness is stable across circumstances; his truthful utterances are generally beneficial to those around him; and he acts voluntarily in the sense that no external force compels him on any particular occasion. Yet one might observe that he tells the truth not because he grasps the value of honesty but because deviation from truthfulness produces in him a kind of unreflective anxiety. He has never considered whether truthfulness is good; he simply cannot bring himself to lie. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should hesitate to call his trait a virtue, for it lacks the quality of reflective endorsement that distinguishes genuine virtue from mere habit.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent endorse the disposition upon reflection — that he regard the trait as good and choose to cultivate it for reasons he can articulate, rather than merely possessing it as an unreflective habit or product of conditioning.
passage: One might propose that a character trait qualifies as a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition to act in certain ways, the actions it produces are generally beneficial, and the agent exercises the trait voluntarily and with understanding. This definition has the merit of capturing the Aristotelian intuition that virtue is a matter of settled character rather than of isolated acts, and that virtuous action must be performed knowingly and willingly. The requirement of benefit ensures that mere idiosyncrasy does not count as virtue: a stable disposition to arrange one's books alphabetically, however firmly entrenched, would not qualify unless it conferred some benefit upon others. Yet one might observe that the definition remains, in a certain respect, too permissive. Consider a man reared under conditions of strict discipline who has acquired an unshakeable habit of truthfulness. His disposition is stable; his truthful reports are beneficial to his associates; and he speaks the truth voluntarily, without external coercion. Nevertheless, his truthfulness is not the product of deliberation or reflective choice. He has never asked himself whether honesty is good; he simply finds that he cannot bring himself to dissemble. The anxiety he would feel upon lying is no different in kind from the discomfort a man might feel upon wearing mismatched socks — it is an aversion, not a reasoned commitment. We should, it appears, hesitate to call such a disposition a virtue in the fullest sense, for what is missing is the element of reflective endorsement — the agent's recognition that the trait is good and his deliberate cultivation of it for reasons he understands and affirms.

definition: A distribution of goods D within a society is just if and only if (i) D allocates goods according to a rule that applies equally to all members of the society, (ii) the rule governing D was established through a procedure in which all affected parties had an opportunity to participate, and (iii) no member of the society is left without the minimum goods necessary for subsistence.
conditions: D allocates goods according to a rule applying equally to all members
The rule was established through a procedure open to all affected parties
No member is left without minimum goods necessary for subsistence
counterexample: Consider a society that distributes educational resources according to a rule — competitive examination — that applies equally to every citizen without exception. The rule was adopted through a democratic assembly in which all had a voice. And a welfare provision ensures that no citizen lacks food, shelter, or basic medical care. All three conditions are met. Yet the competitive examination presupposes a level of prior preparation that is available only to children of wealthy families, so that the distribution of educational resources in practice mirrors and reinforces the existing distribution of wealth. The rule applies equally in form but not in substance, and the resulting distribution is one that many would call unjust.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the rule of distribution not merely apply formally to all but operate substantively to afford all members a genuine opportunity to benefit — that is, it omits a condition concerning the background conditions necessary for formal equality to yield substantive fairness.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a distribution of goods is just if and only if it follows a rule applying equally to all, the rule was adopted through a participatory procedure, and no one is left destitute. Such a definition has clear appeal: it combines formal equality with procedural legitimacy and a floor of material provision. The requirement of equal application guards against arbitrary favouritism; the procedural condition ensures that the rule reflects the collective will rather than the dictate of a few; and the subsistence floor prevents the worst forms of deprivation. Yet one might observe that formal equality of application is compatible with deep substantive inequality. Suppose a society distributes places at its universities by competitive examination, and the rule is applied without exception to every citizen. The procedure was adopted democratically. And a welfare system ensures that no one starves. All three conditions are satisfied. But the examinations test skills — fluency in classical languages, say, or facility with mathematical proofs — that are cultivated almost exclusively in expensive private schools. The children of the poor, though formally eligible, have in practice no realistic prospect of success. The distribution that results mirrors the pre-existing distribution of wealth with near-perfect fidelity. It appears, then, that the definition has failed to distinguish between formal and substantive equality. What is wanted is a further condition requiring that the background circumstances be such as to afford all members a genuine, and not merely nominal, opportunity to benefit from the rule.

definition: An agent A is morally obligated to perform an action X if and only if (i) A is capable of performing X, (ii) failure to perform X would foreseeably result in significant harm to another person, and (iii) performing X would not require A to sacrifice anything of comparable moral importance.
conditions: A is capable of performing X
Failure to perform X would foreseeably result in significant harm to another person
Performing X would not require A to sacrifice anything of comparable moral importance
counterexample: Consider a physician who is capable of treating a patient suffering from a rare disease. If she fails to treat him, the patient will foreseeably suffer significant harm. And treating the patient would not require the physician to sacrifice anything of comparable moral importance — it would cost her merely an hour of her time. Yet suppose the patient has explicitly and competently refused treatment, preferring to manage his condition by other means. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should hesitate to say the physician is morally obligated to treat him, for to insist upon treatment in the face of competent refusal would violate the patient's autonomy.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the person to be aided has not competently refused the assistance — it fails to include a condition respecting the autonomy of the potential beneficiary.
passage: One might define moral obligation as follows: an agent is morally obligated to perform an action if and only if she is capable of performing it, failure to perform it would foreseeably result in significant harm to another, and performing it would not require the sacrifice of anything of comparable moral importance. This definition captures a widely shared intuition — that we are obligated to prevent harm when we can do so at modest cost to ourselves. The capability condition embodies the principle that ought implies can; the foreseeability condition limits obligation to cases where the agent can reasonably anticipate the consequences of inaction; and the proportionality condition ensures that obligation does not demand heroic self-sacrifice. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, generate obligations in cases where obligation seems clearly absent. Consider a physician who is capable of administering a treatment that would prevent significant deterioration in a patient's condition. The harm of non-treatment is foreseeable, and the cost to the physician is modest. But the patient, in full possession of his faculties, has refused the treatment. He has weighed the options and chosen a different course. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet it appears wrong to say the physician is obligated to treat him against his will. The difficulty is that the definition takes no account of the wishes of the person who stands to benefit. It treats moral obligation as a function solely of the agent's capacity, the foreseeability of harm, and the cost of action, without recognising that the potential beneficiary may have a right to decline the benefit. What is missing is a condition requiring that the person to be helped has not competently refused the assistance.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legitimate interest in X — that is, X contributes to P's well-being or to the exercise of P's capacities, (ii) the interest is of sufficient importance to impose a duty on others to respect or provide X, and (iii) P's interest in X is recognised by the legal or moral norms of the community.
conditions: P has a legitimate interest in X
The interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others
P's interest is recognised by the legal or moral norms of the community
counterexample: Consider an enslaved person in a society whose legal and moral norms explicitly deny that enslaved persons have any rights whatsoever. The enslaved person has a legitimate interest in freedom — freedom contributes fundamentally to his well-being and to the exercise of his capacities. This interest is of sufficient importance to impose duties on others. Yet the third condition — recognition by the community's norms — is not satisfied. The definition thus yields the conclusion that the enslaved person has no right to freedom, which is precisely the conclusion we should wish to reject. Conversely, the definition entails that rights cannot exist in advance of their recognition, which makes the moral criticism of unjust institutions unintelligible.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly makes the existence of a right dependent upon its recognition by prevailing norms, thereby rendering impossible the claim that a community's norms are unjust because they fail to recognise rights that persons genuinely possess. A condition grounding rights independently of social recognition is needed.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something if and only if he has a legitimate interest in it, the interest is of sufficient importance to impose a duty on others, and the interest is recognised by the norms of his community. This definition has the apparent advantage of combining an interest-based account of rights with a social condition that ties rights to actual normative practice. The interest condition ensures that rights are not arbitrary claims but reflect genuine features of human well-being. The importance condition provides a threshold, so that trivial interests do not generate rights. And the recognition condition connects rights to the institutions that give them practical effect. Yet one might observe that the third condition introduces a difficulty that is, on reflection, fatal. Consider a society in which the institution of slavery is legally sanctioned and morally accepted. The enslaved person has a legitimate interest in his own freedom — an interest that is surely of sufficient importance to impose duties on others. But the prevailing norms of the society deny that he has any such right. According to the definition, he has none. The definition thus commits us to the view that the enslaved person's right to freedom came into existence only when the community's norms changed to recognise it — that before abolition, the right simply did not exist. But this is precisely the conclusion that the abolitionist rejects, and rightly so. The moral force of the abolitionist's argument depends upon the claim that the enslaved person's right existed all along, and that the community's norms were wrong to deny it. The definition, by making rights dependent upon recognition, renders this claim incoherent.

definition: A person P deserves an outcome O if and only if (i) P has performed actions or possesses qualities that are relevant to O, (ii) those actions or qualities were within P's voluntary control, and (iii) the connection between P's actions or qualities and O is recognised by an established social practice or institution.
conditions: P has performed actions or possesses qualities relevant to O
Those actions or qualities were within P's voluntary control
The connection between P's qualities and O is recognised by an established practice or institution
counterexample: Consider a gifted musician who, through years of disciplined practice entirely within her voluntary control, has developed extraordinary skill. She performs brilliantly in a competition, and by every musical standard she is the finest performer. Her actions are relevant to the prize; they were within her voluntary control; yet the competition's judges, corrupted by bribery, award the prize to a less accomplished performer. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied by the corrupt winner — the judges' decision constitutes the relevant institutional recognition — and they are not satisfied by the gifted musician, who lacks the institutional endorsement. Yet one would say that she deserves the prize and the corrupt winner does not.
missing_condition: The definition errs in making desert dependent upon institutional recognition rather than upon the intrinsic relationship between the person's qualities and the outcome. Desert, it appears, is a normative notion that can stand in judgement upon institutions, not one that derives its force from them.
passage: One might suppose that desert is a matter of performing actions or possessing qualities relevant to an outcome, where those actions or qualities are within one's voluntary control, and where the connection is recognised by an established institution. This definition captures the common-sense intuition that desert must be earned rather than merely received, and that the earning must involve effort or choice rather than mere luck. The institutional condition is meant to anchor desert in social practice, so that claims of desert are not free-floating moral assertions but are tied to the norms of a community. Yet one might observe that the institutional condition creates a difficulty. Consider a competition in which the judges have been bribed. The winner, whose performance was mediocre, receives the institutional recognition — the prize, the public endorsement, the official verdict. The conditions of the definition are satisfied in her case. Meanwhile, the finest performer, whose years of disciplined practice produced a performance of evident superiority, lacks the institutional endorsement. The definition yields the result that the corrupt winner deserves the prize and the superior performer does not. But this is precisely the reverse of what we should wish to say. The difficulty is that the definition makes desert parasitic upon institutional recognition, whereas desert, properly understood, is a standard by which institutions may themselves be judged. What is wanted is a condition that grounds desert in the intrinsic connection between merit and outcome, independently of whether that connection is recognised by any institution.

definition: A person P has consented to an arrangement A if and only if (i) P has expressed agreement to A through words or conduct, (ii) P was aware of the essential terms and consequences of A at the time of expression, and (iii) P's expression of agreement was not obtained through physical force or explicit threats of violence.
conditions: P expressed agreement through words or conduct
P was aware of the essential terms and consequences of A
P's expression was not obtained through physical force or explicit threats of violence
counterexample: Consider a tenant who signs a lease containing onerous terms — forfeiture of the entire deposit for any infraction, however minor, and waiver of all rights to contest eviction. The tenant expresses agreement by signing; she is aware of the terms, having read them carefully; and no physical force or explicit threat of violence is employed. Yet the tenant signs because she has been searching for housing for months in a market where affordable dwellings are scarce, and she knows that refusal will leave her and her children without shelter. All three conditions are satisfied, yet one might doubt whether genuine consent has been given, for the tenant's agreement was extracted by the pressure of desperate circumstances rather than by violence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the consenting party possess a reasonable alternative to agreement — that is, it excludes only the grossest forms of coercion (physical force and explicit threats) while permitting subtler forms of pressure, such as economic duress or the exploitation of desperate circumstances, to vitiate consent without being recognised as doing so.
passage: It is tempting to define consent as the expression of agreement, given with awareness of the essential terms, and not obtained through force or threats of violence. Such a definition draws a clear line between voluntary and coerced agreement, and its simplicity is attractive. The first condition captures the public, communicative character of consent — it is something one does, not merely something one feels. The awareness condition ensures that consent is informed rather than blind. And the exclusion of force and threats guards against the most egregious forms of coercion. Yet one might observe that coercion is not exhausted by physical violence and explicit threats. Consider a tenant in a housing market where affordable accommodation is desperately scarce. She is presented with a lease whose terms are, by any reasonable standard, unconscionable — she forfeits her deposit for the slightest infraction and waives her right to contest eviction. She reads the terms, understands them fully, and signs the lease. No one threatens her; no one lays a hand upon her. But she signs because the alternative is homelessness for herself and her children. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet it appears that something essential to genuine consent is missing. The tenant's agreement was extracted not by violence but by the pressure of circumstances that left her, in practical terms, with no real choice. The definition, it seems, has drawn the line between consent and coercion too narrowly, attending only to physical force and explicit threats while ignoring the subtler but no less effective forms of pressure that economic deprivation and the absence of alternatives can exert.

definition: An imposition of suffering S upon a person P constitutes legitimate punishment if and only if (i) P has been found to have violated a rule of the community, (ii) S is imposed by an authority recognised as having the power to impose it, and (iii) S is proportionate to the severity of P's violation.
conditions: P has been found to have violated a community rule
S is imposed by a recognised authority
S is proportionate to the severity of the violation
counterexample: Consider a man found to have violated a rule prohibiting the public expression of political dissent. He is punished by a recognised authority — the state — and the punishment, a modest fine, is proportionate to the infraction as the legal code defines it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the rule he violated is itself unjust, being an infringement upon the freedom of expression. The punishment, though legitimate by the definition's lights, is an instrument of oppression rather than of justice. One would hesitate to call it legitimate in any sense that carries moral weight.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the rule whose violation occasions the punishment must itself be morally defensible. Without such a condition, the definition legitimises punishment for the violation of unjust rules.
passage: One might define legitimate punishment as the imposition of suffering upon a person who has violated a community rule, by a recognised authority, in a manner proportionate to the violation. This definition captures several important features of punishment as ordinarily understood. The requirement that a rule be violated ensures that punishment is responsive to conduct rather than to status or identity. The authority condition distinguishes punishment from private revenge. And the proportionality condition guards against excessive or vindictive responses. Yet one might observe that the definition is silent on a question of considerable importance: whether the rule in question is itself just. Consider a regime that prohibits the public expression of political dissent. A citizen who speaks against the government is found to have violated this rule. The punishment — let us suppose a modest fine — is imposed by the recognised authorities and is proportionate to the infraction as the legal code defines proportionality. All three conditions are satisfied. But the rule that the citizen violated is itself an unjust restriction upon the freedom of expression, and the punishment, far from being a legitimate exercise of communal authority, is an instrument by which an unjust regime suppresses the very speech that might bring about its reform. The definition, it appears, legitimises punishment for the violation of any rule, however oppressive, provided only that the punishment is imposed by the right authority in the right measure. What is missing is a condition requiring that the rule whose violation is punished be itself morally defensible.

definition: A person P is bound by a promise to do X if and only if (i) P has made a voluntary declaration of intent to do X, addressed to a specific person Q, (ii) Q has received and understood the declaration, and (iii) P's declaration was made in circumstances that both P and Q understood to be serious rather than frivolous.
conditions: P made a voluntary declaration of intent to do X addressed to Q
Q received and understood the declaration
The declaration was made in circumstances understood by both to be serious
counterexample: Consider a man who, at the bedside of a dying friend, solemnly promises to care for the friend's cat. The promise is voluntary, addressed to a specific person, received and understood, and made in circumstances of the utmost seriousness. Yet suppose the man discovers, after the friend's death, that the cat has already been taken in by a loving family who are devoted to it and that the cat is thriving in their care. All three conditions remain satisfied — the promise was made and received — yet one might question whether the man remains bound, for the purpose that animated the promise has been fulfilled by other means, and insisting upon its literal performance would disrupt a satisfactory arrangement to no one's benefit.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition concerning the persistence of the circumstances that gave the promise its point. When the purpose of a promise has been fulfilled by other means or when circumstances have changed so radically that performance would serve no one's interest, it is unclear that the obligation persists, yet the definition provides no mechanism for its dissolution.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a promise binds if and only if it was voluntarily made, addressed to and received by a specific person, and made in serious circumstances. This definition captures the essential features of promising as a social institution: the voluntary undertaking, the relational character — a promise is made to someone — and the exclusion of jests and theatrical performances. The first condition ensures that the promisor has chosen to bind himself; the second ensures that the promisee has a legitimate expectation; and the third prevents casual remarks from generating obligations. Yet one might observe that the definition is insensitive to changes in circumstance that may arise between the making and the performance of the promise. Consider a man who promises a dying friend that he will care for the friend's cat. The promise is made voluntarily, received and understood, and made in circumstances of unquestionable seriousness. But after the friend's death, the man discovers that a neighbouring family has already adopted the cat and that the animal is flourishing in their care. The three conditions of the definition remain satisfied — the promise was made, received, and serious — yet insisting upon literal performance would require the man to remove the cat from a happy home and take it into his own, an outcome that serves no one's interest and defeats the very purpose for which the promise was made. It appears, then, that the definition has failed to accommodate the possibility that the circumstances giving a promise its point may cease to obtain. What is wanted is a condition recognising that the obligation of a promise is, in part, dependent upon the persistence of the reasons that made the promise reasonable in the first place.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) A performed an action that causally contributed to O, (ii) A could have acted otherwise — that is, it was within A's power to refrain from the action, and (iii) A foresaw, or could reasonably have been expected to foresee, that the action would contribute to O.
conditions: A performed an action that causally contributed to O
A could have acted otherwise
A foresaw or could reasonably have been expected to foresee the contribution to O
counterexample: Consider a railway signalman who is presented with a situation in which a runaway train is heading towards five workers on the main line. The signalman can divert the train to a siding where one worker is stationed. He diverts the train, and the single worker is killed. The signalman performed an action causally contributing to the worker's death; he could have refrained from pulling the lever; and he foresaw that diverting the train would result in the death of the worker on the siding. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to hold him morally responsible in the full sense, for his action was performed under a kind of duress — the imminent death of five people — that rendered his situation one in which any course of action would have resulted in death, and his choice was the lesser of two evils.
missing_condition: The definition omits a condition concerning the quality of the alternatives available to the agent. When every available course of action leads to serious harm, the fact that the agent could have acted otherwise does not suffice to ground full moral responsibility, for the alternative was itself morally fraught.
passage: One might define moral responsibility as follows: an agent is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if he performed an action causally contributing to it, he could have acted otherwise, and he foresaw or could reasonably have been expected to foresee the outcome. This definition combines a causal condition, a freedom condition, and a foresight condition, and it captures much of what we ordinarily mean when we hold persons responsible. The causal requirement ensures that we do not blame people for outcomes to which they made no contribution. The freedom condition embodies the common-sense principle that a person cannot be blamed for what he could not help doing. And the foresight condition limits responsibility to consequences that the agent anticipated or should have anticipated. Yet one might observe that the definition fails to account for situations in which the agent's alternatives are all morally compromised. Consider a signalman who can divert a runaway train from a track where five workers will be killed to a siding where one worker will be killed. He pulls the lever, and the single worker dies. He performed the action; he could have refrained; he foresaw the death. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition assigns him full moral responsibility for the worker's death. But this seems wrong, or at least incomplete, for the signalman was not choosing between a good option and a bad one but between two bad options, and he chose the less bad. The definition, it appears, treats the mere existence of an alternative as sufficient for responsibility, without attending to the moral quality of that alternative. What is wanted is a condition that takes account of the nature of the choices available to the agent.

definition: Two agents A and B are equally blameworthy for their conduct if and only if (i) A and B performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness or negligence, (ii) A and B acted with the same intentions and for the same reasons, and (iii) A and B were in relevantly similar circumstances at the time of acting.
conditions: A and B performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness
A and B acted with the same intentions and for the same reasons
A and B were in relevantly similar circumstances
counterexample: Consider two motorists who each drive home after drinking the same quantity of wine, with the same degree of recklessness, the same intention to reach home quickly, and in relevantly similar circumstances — the same kind of road, the same time of night, the same weather. One motorist arrives home without incident. The other strikes and kills a pedestrian who stepped into the road at an unlucky moment. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: the two motorists performed the same type of action with the same recklessness, the same intentions, and in similar circumstances. The definition therefore yields the conclusion that they are equally blameworthy. Yet in both ordinary moral judgement and in law, the motorist who killed the pedestrian is judged far more harshly than the one who arrived home safely.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role that actual outcomes play in our attributions of blame. Although the two agents are identical in intention, recklessness, and circumstance, the difference in outcome — one killed a person, the other did not — generates a difference in blame that the definition cannot accommodate.
passage: It seems plausible to say that two agents are equally blameworthy if they performed actions of the same type with the same recklessness, acted with the same intentions, and were in relevantly similar circumstances. This definition embodies the appealing principle that blame should track the features of action that are within the agent's control — the quality of his will, the degree of his care, the nature of his intentions — rather than the vagaries of fortune. If two agents are identical in all the respects that matter morally, then surely they deserve the same degree of censure, regardless of what happens as a result of their actions. Yet one might observe that this principle, however attractive in the abstract, fails to capture the way we actually apportion blame. Consider two motorists, each of whom drives home after an evening of drinking. Each is equally reckless; each acts with the same intention — to arrive home as quickly as possible; each drives on a similar road in similar conditions. One arrives home without incident. The other kills a pedestrian who stepped into the road at an unfortunate moment. The three conditions of the definition are satisfied, and equal blameworthiness follows. But ordinary moral sentiment does not endorse this conclusion. We judge the motorist who killed the pedestrian more severely than the one who did not, and the law does the same, imposing vastly different penalties. The difficulty, it appears, is that the definition has excluded from its account a factor — the actual outcome — that plays a persistent and perhaps ineliminable role in our moral assessments, however much we might wish that blame tracked only what is within the agent's control.

definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act in a certain manner across relevantly similar situations, (ii) the exercise of T tends to promote human flourishing or well-being, and (iii) the possession of T is the product of deliberate habituation rather than mere natural endowment.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act in a certain manner across relevantly similar situations
The exercise of T tends to promote human flourishing or well-being
The possession of T is the product of deliberate habituation rather than mere natural endowment
counterexample: Consider a person who possesses an unusually calm temperament from birth, never having cultivated it through effort or practice. This calmness is a stable disposition exhibited across relevantly similar situations, and it plainly tends to promote human flourishing, both for the possessor and for those around him. Yet one might hesitate to call it a virtue in the full sense, for it was never deliberately acquired.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the requirement that the agent be capable of exercising the trait in response to the right reasons; mere temperamental placidity, however beneficial and stable, lacks the rational responsiveness that distinguishes genuine virtue from fortunate constitution.
passage: One might propose that a character trait qualifies as a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition to act in a certain manner across relevantly similar situations, tends to promote human flourishing, and is the product of deliberate habituation rather than mere natural endowment. This definition captures much of what the philosophical tradition has taken to be essential to virtue: stability ensures that the trait is not a passing fancy or ephemeral mood, the connection to flourishing secures the evaluative dimension, and the requirement of habituation reflects the Aristotelian conviction that virtues are neither purely innate gifts nor the result of theoretical instruction alone. Yet one might observe that a person born with an extraordinarily calm temperament satisfies all three conditions in a somewhat misleading fashion. Suppose this individual, never having exerted any deliberate effort to cultivate serenity, simply finds that his natural placidity persists across all manner of trying circumstances, and that it plainly conduces to his own well-being and that of his associates. He is stably disposed, his disposition promotes flourishing, and — if we stretch the notion of habituation to include the mere reinforcement of natural tendencies through repeated exercise — one could argue that the trait has been habituated. All the same, we should hesitate to regard this as a genuine virtue, for the person has never chosen to act calmly in the face of reasons that might have prompted a different response. What appears to be missing is the element of rational responsiveness: the capacity to recognise the considerations that favour equanimity and to act from those considerations rather than from brute temperamental inclination.

definition: A distribution of goods D is just if and only if (i) D is the result of a procedure that treats all parties as equals, (ii) D leaves no party worse off than that party would have been in the absence of cooperation, and (iii) D is consented to by all parties affected.
conditions: D is the result of a procedure that treats all parties as equals
D leaves no party worse off than that party would have been in the absence of cooperation
D is consented to by all parties affected
counterexample: Consider a community of five persons who agree unanimously to divide the proceeds of a joint venture equally, each receiving one fifth. The procedure treats all as equals, no one is worse off than before cooperation, and all consent. Yet suppose that one member contributed vastly more labour and skill than the others. The resulting distribution, though satisfying the stated conditions, appears to many to be unjust, for it takes no account of differential contribution.
missing_condition: The definition omits any condition requiring that the distribution be proportional to the contributions or deserts of the parties involved; a distribution may satisfy procedural equality, the no-worse-off constraint, and unanimous consent while still being unfair to those who have contributed disproportionately.
passage: It appears natural to define a distribution of goods as just if and only if it results from a procedure treating all parties as equals, leaves no party worse off than that party would have been in the absence of cooperation, and is consented to by all parties affected. Such a definition has the considerable merit of combining procedural fairness with a baseline constraint and a unanimity requirement. The first condition ensures that the process by which goods are allocated does not privilege any participant over any other. The second ensures that cooperation is, at worst, harmless to each individual, thereby providing a rational incentive to participate. The third ensures that the outcome reflects the autonomous choices of all concerned. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of these conditions taken together. Suppose five individuals undertake a joint venture, and one of them — call him A — performs the vast majority of the labour, supplies the capital, and exercises the skill upon which the venture's success depends. The others contribute little beyond their nominal participation. If the proceeds are divided equally and all parties consent, every condition is satisfied. A is no worse off than he would have been alone, since the venture's proceeds exceed what he could have earned in isolation. The procedure treats all as equals, and consent is unanimous. But one might well object that the distribution is unjust, for it ignores the principle that those who contribute more are entitled to a greater share. What is missing, it appears, is any requirement that the distribution bear some relation to the desert or contribution of each party.

definition: An agent A has a moral obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A is capable of performing X, (ii) the failure to perform X would result in avoidable harm to another person, and (iii) the cost to A of performing X is not disproportionate to the harm prevented.
conditions: A is capable of performing X
The failure to perform X would result in avoidable harm to another person
The cost to A of performing X is not disproportionate to the harm prevented
counterexample: Suppose a bystander witnesses a stranger drowning in a shallow pond. The bystander can easily wade in at negligible personal cost, and the stranger will certainly die without assistance. All three conditions are satisfied. Now consider a second case: the same bystander passes the same pond, but this time there are one hundred drowning strangers, each requiring individual rescue. The bystander can save each in succession, each rescue costing little, yet the aggregate cost becomes enormous. The conditions appear to generate an obligation to save all one hundred, which strikes many as excessively demanding.
missing_condition: The definition fails to impose any limit on the cumulative or iterative demands that may be placed upon an agent; a condition restricting the total burden of obligation, rather than merely the cost of each individual act, is required.
passage: One might define moral obligation by stipulating that an agent has a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if the agent is capable of performing it, the failure to perform it would result in avoidable harm to another person, and the cost to the agent of performing it is not disproportionate to the harm prevented. This definition has an appealing simplicity: it connects obligation to capacity, grounds it in the prevention of harm, and tempers its demands by a proportionality constraint. The requirement of capability ensures that we do not impose obligations upon those who cannot fulfil them. The harm condition ensures that obligations are not generated in the absence of genuine need. And the proportionality condition ensures that the demands placed upon the agent remain reasonable in relation to the good achieved. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, generate obligations of extraordinary scope when applied iteratively. Suppose a person passes a pond in which one hundred strangers are drowning, each requiring a separate rescue. Each individual rescue costs the agent little — perhaps a few minutes and the inconvenience of wet clothing. The proportionality condition is satisfied in each case, since the cost of each rescue is trivial relative to the harm of death. The agent is capable of performing each rescue in sequence, and each failure to rescue would result in an avoidable death. Thus the definition appears to require the agent to perform all one hundred rescues, an aggregate demand that most persons would regard as excessively burdensome, despite the fact that each component obligation satisfies the stated conditions individually.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legitimate interest in X that is of sufficient importance, (ii) the interest imposes a correlative duty on others to refrain from interfering with P's enjoyment of X, and (iii) the right is recognised by the prevailing moral or legal framework.
conditions: P has a legitimate interest in X that is of sufficient importance
The interest imposes a correlative duty on others to refrain from interfering with P's enjoyment of X
The right is recognised by the prevailing moral or legal framework
counterexample: Consider a person living under a regime that denies freedom of expression. This person has a legitimate interest in speaking freely, and that interest is of great importance. The interest plausibly imposes a correlative duty on others not to silence him. Yet the prevailing legal and moral framework of his society does not recognise such a right. According to the definition, he therefore lacks the right, which seems to many to be the wrong conclusion.
missing_condition: The definition errs in making the existence of a right contingent upon its recognition by the prevailing framework; a condition distinguishing between the existence of a right and its recognition or enforcement would avoid this difficulty.
passage: It appears initially plausible to say that a person has a right to something if and only if the person has a legitimate interest in it of sufficient importance, the interest imposes a correlative duty upon others not to interfere with the person's enjoyment of it, and the right is recognised by the prevailing moral or legal framework. This definition has the advantage of connecting rights to interests, linking them to correlative duties in the manner suggested by a number of prominent theorists, and grounding them in the institutional structures that give rights their practical force. The interest condition ensures that rights are not mere abstract entitlements disconnected from what matters to persons. The correlative duty condition captures the relational character of rights — the fact that one person's right entails another's obligation. And the recognition condition acknowledges the social dimension of rights. Yet one might construct a case which reveals a fundamental difficulty with this analysis. Consider an individual living under a despotic regime that systematically denies its citizens the freedom to express their views. This person has a legitimate and important interest in free expression, and that interest is of the sort that imposes upon others a duty of non-interference. All the same, the prevailing framework of his society fails to recognise such a right. If we follow the definition strictly, we are compelled to deny that the person possesses the right in question. But this conclusion strikes many as deeply unsatisfactory, for it conflates the question of whether a right exists with the question of whether it has been acknowledged by those in power.

definition: A person P deserves outcome O if and only if (i) P has performed some action A or possesses some quality Q that is relevantly connected to O, (ii) the connection between A or Q and O is such that O is a fitting response to A or Q, and (iii) P is responsible for A or Q in the sense that A or Q is attributable to P's own choices or efforts.
conditions: P has performed some action A or possesses some quality Q that is relevantly connected to O
The connection between A or Q and O is such that O is a fitting response to A or Q
P is responsible for A or Q in the sense that A or Q is attributable to P's own choices or efforts
counterexample: Consider a gifted musician whose extraordinary talent is largely the result of genetic endowment. She has practised diligently, to be sure, but her capacity for practice — her patience, her ear, her dexterity — is itself largely inherited. One might say she deserves recognition for her performances, since her talent is relevantly connected to musical excellence and recognition is a fitting response. Yet if responsibility requires that the quality be attributable to the agent's own choices rather than to natural fortune, it is unclear that she is responsible for the talent in the requisite sense.
missing_condition: The definition does not adequately distinguish between those qualities for which an agent may be said to be genuinely responsible and those which are the product of genetic or circumstantial fortune beyond the agent's control; a more refined account of the basis of responsibility is needed.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves an outcome if and only if the person has performed some action or possesses some quality that is relevantly connected to the outcome, the connection is such that the outcome is a fitting response, and the person is responsible for the action or quality in the sense that it is attributable to the person's own choices or efforts. This analysis captures a great deal of ordinary moral thinking about desert: we believe that those who work hard deserve the fruits of their labour, that those who act courageously deserve commendation, and that those who commit wrongs deserve censure. The connection between the desert basis and the deserved outcome is meant to be more than merely causal; it must be a relation of fittingness, such that the outcome is the appropriate or proportionate response. And the responsibility condition ensures that we do not ascribe desert on the basis of qualities for which the person is not accountable. Yet one might observe that this definition encounters a difficulty when applied to cases involving natural talent. Consider a musician of extraordinary ability, whose gifts are largely the product of genetic endowment. She has practised assiduously, but her very capacity for assiduous practice — her patience, her musical ear, her manual dexterity — is itself substantially inherited. Recognition of her performances appears to be a fitting response to her excellence, and her talent is certainly connected to that excellence. But if we insist that responsibility requires attributability to the agent's own choices, it becomes doubtful whether she is responsible for the quality upon which her desert claim rests. The definition thus threatens either to deny desert to the naturally gifted or to require an implausibly sharp distinction between chosen and unchosen aspects of character.

definition: An agent A has validly consented to arrangement R if and only if (i) A has expressed agreement to R through some recognisable act of communication, (ii) A was not subject to coercion or duress at the time of expressing agreement, and (iii) A possessed adequate information about the nature and consequences of R.
conditions: A has expressed agreement to R through some recognisable act of communication
A was not subject to coercion or duress at the time of expressing agreement
A possessed adequate information about the nature and consequences of R
counterexample: Suppose a worker agrees to accept hazardous employment conditions after being fully informed of the risks and without any direct coercion. However, the worker's only alternative is destitution, since no other employment is available in his region. He has expressed agreement, he is not coerced in the narrow sense of being physically threatened, and he possesses adequate information. Yet many would say his consent is not genuinely voluntary, since his circumstances leave him with no real choice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the consenting agent possess a reasonable range of acceptable alternatives; consent given in the absence of genuine options, though technically uncoerced and informed, may not be truly voluntary.
passage: It appears natural to say that an agent has validly consented to an arrangement if and only if the agent has expressed agreement through some recognisable act of communication, was not subject to coercion or duress, and possessed adequate information about the nature and consequences of the arrangement. This definition captures much of what we ordinarily mean by valid consent: expression ensures that consent is not merely internal or hypothetical, the absence of coercion ensures voluntariness, and informational adequacy ensures that the agent is not consenting in ignorance. Each condition addresses a well-known source of defective consent. Yet one might construct a case which reveals a gap in the analysis. Suppose a labourer in a region with no alternative employment is offered work under hazardous conditions. He is fully informed of the dangers, no one threatens or pressures him, and he signs the contract of his own apparent volition. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might reasonably doubt that his consent is genuinely voluntary. His choice is not between this arrangement and some tolerable alternative, but between this arrangement and starvation. The absence of a reasonable set of alternatives means that his agreement, though formally uncoerced and well-informed, is extracted by circumstance rather than freely given. What the definition appears to lack is a condition requiring that the agent have access to a reasonable range of acceptable options, such that the choice to consent is not merely the lesser of two desperate evils.

definition: An imposition of suffering S upon a person P constitutes legitimate punishment if and only if (i) P has been found to have committed a wrongful act W through a fair procedure, (ii) S is proportionate to the severity of W, and (iii) S is imposed by an authority with the recognised power to punish.
conditions: P has been found to have committed a wrongful act W through a fair procedure
S is proportionate to the severity of W
S is imposed by an authority with the recognised power to punish
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person is found through a scrupulously fair trial to have committed a minor theft, and a recognised authority imposes a penalty that is proportionate to the offence. However, the purpose of the punishment is not to deter, reform, or express moral censure, but merely to satisfy the private vindictiveness of the presiding official. All three conditions are met, yet one might hesitate to call this a legitimate exercise of the punitive power.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the punishment be imposed for a legitimate purpose — such as deterrence, reform, or the expression of communal moral censure — rather than for the private satisfaction of those administering it.
passage: One might define legitimate punishment as the imposition of suffering upon a person who has been found through a fair procedure to have committed a wrongful act, where the suffering is proportionate to the wrong and imposed by an authority with the recognised power to punish. This definition has the merit of incorporating several conditions that are widely regarded as necessary. The requirement of a fair procedure protects against the punishment of the innocent. Proportionality ensures that the punishment is not excessive relative to the offence. And the requirement of legitimate authority distinguishes punishment from vigilante retribution. Yet one might observe that the definition is silent on the question of purpose. Consider a case in which a judge, having conducted a fair trial and determined that the accused has committed a minor theft, imposes a proportionate fine — but does so not out of any concern for justice, deterrence, or reform, but solely to gratify his personal animosity towards the accused. The accused has been fairly tried, the penalty is proportionate, and the judge possesses the recognised authority. All conditions are met. Yet one might reasonably object that this is not a legitimate exercise of the punitive power, for punishment, if it is to be distinguished from mere officially sanctioned cruelty, must be animated by some purpose beyond the private satisfaction of those who administer it. The definition, it appears, requires supplementation by a condition specifying the legitimate aims that punishment may properly serve.

definition: A person P is bound by a promise to do X if and only if (i) P has voluntarily uttered words or performed acts that conventionally count as undertaking to do X, (ii) the promisee Q has accepted or relied upon P's undertaking, and (iii) the content of the promise — namely X — is not itself immoral.
conditions: P has voluntarily uttered words or performed acts that conventionally count as undertaking to do X
The promisee Q has accepted or relied upon P's undertaking
The content of the promise — namely X — is not itself immoral
counterexample: Suppose a person promises a friend to attend his lecture, and the friend relies upon this assurance in planning the event. The promise is voluntarily given, accepted, and its content is perfectly moral. But suppose that, on the day of the lecture, the promiser encounters a stranger in urgent need of medical assistance, and rendering that assistance is incompatible with attending the lecture. All three conditions of the promise remain satisfied, yet it seems clear that the promiser ought to break the promise in favour of the more pressing moral demand.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition acknowledging that a promise may be overridden by competing moral obligations of greater urgency; the binding force of a promise is not absolute but defeasible in the face of more pressing duties.
passage: It appears plausible to analyse the obligation of promise-keeping by saying that a person is bound by a promise to do something if and only if the person has voluntarily performed acts that conventionally count as undertaking to do it, the promisee has accepted or relied upon that undertaking, and the content of the promise is not itself immoral. This definition has considerable intuitive support. The voluntariness condition ensures that the promiser has freely assumed the obligation. The acceptance or reliance condition ensures that the promise has been taken up in a way that gives it social and moral weight. And the morality condition prevents the generation of binding obligations to perform wicked acts. Together, these conditions appear to capture the essential features of promissory obligation. Yet one might observe that the definition treats the obligation as unconditional once the three conditions are met, and this leads to difficulty. Suppose a man promises his colleague to attend a lecture, and the colleague relies upon the assurance in making arrangements. On the morning of the lecture, the promiser comes upon a stranger who has collapsed in the street and requires immediate medical attention. Rendering this assistance is incompatible with keeping the promise. All conditions of the original promise remain satisfied: it was voluntary, relied upon, and its content is perfectly moral. Yet most persons would agree that the promiser ought to break his promise and assist the stranger. What the definition lacks is an acknowledgement that promissory obligations are defeasible — that they may be overridden by moral demands of greater urgency without thereby ceasing to have been genuine obligations in the first instance.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) A performed an action that was a cause of O, (ii) A acted voluntarily, in the sense that no external force compelled A's action, and (iii) A foresaw, or could reasonably have foreseen, that the action would lead to O.
conditions: A performed an action that was a cause of O
A acted voluntarily, in the sense that no external force compelled A's action
A foresaw, or could reasonably have foreseen, that the action would lead to O
counterexample: Suppose a chemist voluntarily prepares a standard reagent, fully foreseeing that it will be used in a routine experiment. Unbeknownst to her, a colleague substitutes a different chemical for one of the ingredients, and the resulting compound causes an explosion. The chemist's action was a cause of the explosion in the sense that, had she not prepared the reagent, the explosion would not have occurred. She acted voluntarily and could foresee that her preparation would lead to a chemical reaction. Yet one would hesitate to hold her responsible, since the intervening act of the colleague broke the chain of responsibility.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that no intervening voluntary act by another agent have redirected the causal chain in a way that the original agent could not have anticipated; responsibility should be sensitive to the independence of intervening causes.
passage: One might propose that an agent is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if the agent performed an action that was a cause of the outcome, acted voluntarily, and foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen that the action would lead to the outcome. This analysis captures a substantial portion of our ordinary judgements about moral responsibility. The causal condition ensures that we do not hold persons responsible for outcomes to which their actions bear no connection. The voluntariness condition ensures that agents who are physically compelled are excused. And the foreseeability condition ensures that agents are not held to account for genuinely unforeseeable consequences of their conduct. Yet one might construct a case which reveals a gap in this account. Consider a chemist who voluntarily prepares a standard laboratory reagent, knowing full well that it will be used in an experiment. Before the experiment is conducted, a colleague surreptitiously substitutes a different substance for one of the ingredients, and the altered compound causes an explosion. The chemist's preparation was a cause of the explosion: had she not prepared the reagent, the explosion would not have occurred. She acted voluntarily, and she could foresee that her preparation would result in a chemical reaction. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it seems plainly wrong to hold the chemist responsible for the explosion. The intervening voluntary act of the colleague — an act entirely beyond the chemist's control or anticipation — redirected the causal chain in a way that severs the connection between the chemist's action and the harmful outcome. The definition, it appears, requires a further condition specifying that the causal chain between the agent's action and the outcome must not have been broken by an independent intervening voluntary act.

definition: Two agents A and B are equally blameworthy for their respective actions if and only if (i) A and B performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness, (ii) A and B acted from the same quality of will or intention, and (iii) A and B had the same degree of control over the relevant circumstances.
conditions: A and B performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness
A and B acted from the same quality of will or intention
A and B had the same degree of control over the relevant circumstances
counterexample: Consider two drivers who both run a red light with equal recklessness and identical intentions. One strikes a pedestrian who happens to be crossing and kills him; the other passes through the intersection without incident because no pedestrian is present. Both performed the same type of action with the same recklessness, the same quality of will, and the same degree of control. Yet common moral sentiment — and the law — treats the first driver as far more culpable than the second.
