missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the influence of actual outcomes upon moral assessment; it assumes that blameworthiness is determined entirely by factors within the agent's control, whereas our moral practices systematically assign greater blame when harmful outcomes actually obtain.
passage: It appears natural to say that two agents are equally blameworthy for their respective actions if and only if they performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness, acted from the same quality of will or intention, and had the same degree of control over the relevant circumstances. This definition embodies a principle that many philosophers have found attractive: that moral assessment ought to track only those factors that are within the agent's control. If two persons act identically in every respect that is up to them, then they ought to receive identical moral appraisal, regardless of how things happen to turn out. The appeal of this principle is evident: it seems unfair to blame one person more than another on account of factors that neither could influence. Yet one might observe that this definition is in tension with deeply entrenched features of our moral practice. Consider two motorists who both run a red light with identical recklessness and identical intentions. One happens to strike and kill a pedestrian; the other passes through the intersection without incident, simply because no pedestrian was present. The two agents satisfy all three conditions: same action, same intention, same control. Yet almost everyone — including, notably, the drivers themselves — regards the first driver as more blameworthy than the second. The law reinforces this judgement by imposing far severer penalties upon the driver who causes death. If the definition is correct, then our ordinary moral practice is systematically mistaken. But one might equally conclude that the definition is incomplete, and that actual outcomes play a legitimate role in the determination of blameworthiness that the stated conditions fail to capture.

definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition of the agent, (ii) T reliably produces actions that conduce to the flourishing of the agent and those affected by the agent's actions, and (iii) the exercise of T is accompanied by the appropriate affective responses.
conditions: T is a stable disposition of the agent
T reliably produces actions conducive to flourishing
The exercise of T is accompanied by appropriate affective responses
counterexample: Consider a soldier who has been so thoroughly drilled in the habits of combat that he charges into danger without hesitation, rescuing his comrades at great personal risk. His courage is a stable disposition, it reliably produces actions that conduce to the flourishing of those he protects, and he feels the appropriate surge of resolve and concern while acting. Yet if we learn that his dispositions were instilled entirely through rigorous conditioning — that he never reflected upon or endorsed the values underlying his bravery — we should hesitate to say the trait is a virtue in the fullest sense, for the agent exercises no rational governance over the disposition.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the agent endorse or rationally govern the disposition, rather than merely possessing it as a product of external conditioning.
passage: One might propose that a character trait qualifies as a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition, reliably produces actions conducive to flourishing, and is accompanied by the appropriate affective responses. This proposal has much to recommend it: it excludes fleeting inclinations by insisting on stability, it ties the virtue to the production of good outcomes, and it captures the intuition that the virtuous agent must feel rightly as well as act rightly. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when we consider agents whose dispositions have been instilled through conditioning rather than through rational reflection. A soldier drilled from youth in the habits of bravery may charge unhesitatingly into danger, rescuing his comrades at great personal risk. His courage is stable, it reliably conduces to the flourishing of those he protects, and he feels the appropriate emotions — resolve, concern, a willingness to endure hardship. All three conditions are met. Yet if we discover that the soldier has never reflected upon the values underlying his bravery, that his disposition was produced entirely by external conditioning and never subjected to rational scrutiny, one might reasonably doubt whether the trait deserves to be called a virtue rather than a well-drilled habit. The virtuous person, it appears, must exercise some degree of rational governance over her dispositions — she must, in some measure, have chosen or endorsed the traits she possesses — and this the definition does not require.

definition: A distribution of goods among members of a society is just if and only if (i) each member receives a share proportional to that member's contribution to the cooperative enterprise, (ii) no member is coerced into accepting a share smaller than proportionality would dictate, and (iii) the rules governing the distribution are publicly known and consistently applied.
conditions: Each member receives a share proportional to contribution
No member is coerced into accepting less than proportionality dictates
The rules are publicly known and consistently applied
counterexample: Consider a society in which the rules of distribution are public and consistently applied, no one is coerced, and each person's share is strictly proportional to contribution. A person born with a severe disability, who through no fault of his own is unable to contribute to the cooperative enterprise, receives nothing. The three conditions are satisfied, yet the distribution seems manifestly unjust, for it penalises a person for circumstances entirely beyond his control.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the distribution make some provision for those who are unable to contribute through no fault of their own, thereby confusing contribution with desert.
passage: One might define a just distribution of goods as one in which each member of a society receives a share proportional to contribution, no member is coerced into accepting less than proportionality would dictate, and the governing rules are publicly known and consistently applied. This definition captures the intuitions underlying meritocratic conceptions of justice: it rewards effort and productivity, it prohibits exploitation, and it demands transparency. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises when we consider persons who are unable to contribute to the cooperative enterprise through no fault of their own. Suppose a child is born with a severe physical disability that prevents any productive labour. In a society governed by the proposed definition, this child would receive nothing, for his contribution is nil. The rules are public, consistently applied, and no coercion is involved — the three conditions are all met. Yet the distribution appears manifestly unjust. The child has not chosen his disability; he is penalised for a circumstance entirely beyond his control. What the definition misses is any provision for those whose inability to contribute is not a reflection of choice or desert but of misfortune. A complete account of justice, it appears, must incorporate some principle of need or compassion alongside the principle of proportional contribution.

definition: An agent S has a moral obligation to perform action A if and only if (i) S has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform A, (ii) the performance of A is within S's power, and (iii) the failure to perform A would cause harm to another person who is relying upon S's performance.
conditions: S has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform A
The performance of A is within S's power
Failure to perform A would cause harm to another who relies on S
counterexample: Consider a bystander who witnesses a child drowning in a shallow pond. The bystander has not voluntarily undertaken any commitment to rescue children, and no one is relying upon him specifically. Yet if he can wade into the pond at trivial cost to himself and save the child's life, it appears that he has a moral obligation to do so. The first condition is not met — no voluntary commitment was made — and the third condition is strained, since the child is not relying upon the bystander in any antecedent sense. Yet the obligation seems undeniable.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to account for obligations that arise from the sheer capacity to prevent serious harm at trivial cost, independent of any prior voluntary commitment.
passage: One might propose that a moral obligation obtains when an agent has voluntarily undertaken a commitment, the action is within the agent's power, and failure to act would cause harm to someone relying upon the agent. This analysis captures a wide range of obligations: the obligation to repay a debt, to fulfil a contract, to keep a promise, to discharge the duties of an office one has freely accepted. In each of these cases, the three conditions are plainly satisfied. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is the case of the easy rescue. Suppose a competent swimmer is walking past a shallow pond in which a small child is drowning. The swimmer has made no commitment to the child, and the child is not relying upon the swimmer in any antecedent sense — the child does not even know the swimmer is there. The first condition is not met, and the third is at best strained. Yet it appears nearly indisputable that the swimmer has a moral obligation to wade in and save the child, particularly when the cost of doing so is trivial — wet clothing, a minor inconvenience. The obligation arises not from any voluntary undertaking but from the sheer capacity to prevent a grave harm at negligible expense. The definition, by tethering all obligation to voluntary commitment, cannot accommodate obligations of this sort, which appear to arise from the bare conjunction of capacity and proximity to serious harm.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) this interest is sufficient to ground a duty in others to refrain from interfering with P's access to X, and (iii) P's interest in X is recognised by the legal or moral framework governing the society in which P lives.
conditions: P has a legitimate interest in X
This interest grounds a duty in others not to interfere
P's interest is recognised by the prevailing legal or moral framework
counterexample: Consider a person living under a regime that does not recognise freedom of expression. The person has a legitimate interest in speaking freely, and this interest is of sufficient weight to ground a duty in others to refrain from silencing her. Yet the prevailing legal and moral framework of her society does not recognise this interest. The third condition is not met. It would be strange, however, to conclude that she has no right to free expression — one might instead say that her right exists but is violated by the regime.
missing_condition: The definition confuses the recognition of a right with the existence of a right, failing to allow for the possibility that a right may obtain even when the prevailing framework does not acknowledge it.
passage: One might define a right as obtaining when a person has a legitimate interest in some good, that interest is sufficient to impose a duty of non-interference upon others, and the interest is recognised by the prevailing legal or moral framework. This definition has the merit of connecting rights to interests, grounding them in duties, and tying them to institutional recognition. It captures the standard cases well: the right to property, to bodily integrity, to contractual performance — all involve interests that are both weighty and recognised. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the rights of persons living under unjust regimes. Suppose a citizen of a totalitarian state has a profound interest in expressing her political views. Her interest is legitimate and of sufficient weight to ground a duty of non-interference. But the legal and moral framework of her society does not recognise this interest — on the contrary, the framework explicitly denies it. The third condition fails. Yet to conclude that the citizen has no right to free expression seems precisely backwards: we are far more inclined to say that she possesses the right and that the regime violates it. The definition makes the existence of a right dependent upon its recognition, but the most urgent assertions of rights in human history have been assertions against frameworks that refused to recognise them. A right, it appears, may exist precisely in defiance of the prevailing order.

definition: A person P deserves outcome O if and only if (i) P has performed an action A that is relevantly connected to O, (ii) A was performed voluntarily and with full awareness of its nature, and (iii) the connection between A and O is recognised by the community's shared norms.
conditions: P has performed an action relevantly connected to O
The action was performed voluntarily and with full awareness
The connection between action and outcome is recognised by shared norms
counterexample: Consider a firefighter who rushes into a burning building and rescues a family, suffering serious injuries in the process. She deserves, one might say, recognition and gratitude. She performed the action voluntarily, with full awareness, and the community's norms recognise the connection between heroic rescue and deserved honour. All three conditions are met. But now consider that the building was set ablaze by an arsonist whose act was equally voluntary and fully aware, and the community's norms connect arson to deserved punishment. The symmetry is troubling: the definition provides no resources to explain why the firefighter deserves reward and the arsonist deserves punishment beyond appeal to whatever the community happens to recognise.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include an independent normative criterion for distinguishing deserved reward from deserved punishment, leaving the content of desert entirely at the mercy of contingent communal norms.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves an outcome when she has performed a voluntary and fully aware action relevantly connected to that outcome, and the connection is recognised by the community's shared norms. This analysis captures many intuitive cases: the diligent student deserves high marks, the criminal deserves punishment, the generous benefactor deserves gratitude. In each case, a voluntary act is connected to an outcome by norms that the community endorses. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when we press upon the role played by community norms. Consider two agents: a firefighter who voluntarily rushes into a burning building to rescue a family, and an arsonist who voluntarily set the building alight. Both performed their actions with full awareness. The community's norms connect heroism to honour and arson to punishment. The three conditions are met in both cases, yet the definition provides no independent ground for the normative asymmetry. If we ask why the firefighter deserves reward and the arsonist deserves punishment, the definition can only reply that the community's norms say so. But desert, one might argue, is the sort of thing that ought to constrain communal norms rather than be constituted by them. A community that honoured arsonists and punished firefighters would not thereby make arsonists deserving of honour — it would simply have defective norms. The definition lacks an independent criterion by which the appropriateness of the desert-base can be judged.

definition: An agent S has given valid consent to an action A if and only if (i) S has expressed agreement to A, (ii) S was informed of the material facts relevant to A, and (iii) S was free from coercion at the time of expressing agreement.
conditions: S expressed agreement to A
S was informed of the material facts
S was free from coercion
counterexample: Consider a patient who is told, truthfully, that a proposed surgery carries a five percent risk of a specific complication. The patient expresses agreement, is informed of the material facts, and is under no coercion. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the patient is a child of eight years, who understands the words spoken to her but lacks the maturity to appreciate the gravity of the decision. One would hesitate to say that valid consent has been given, for the child lacks the capacity to weigh the information in the manner required for genuine consent.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the consenting agent possess the cognitive and emotional maturity necessary to appreciate the significance of the facts disclosed.
passage: One might define valid consent as obtaining when an agent has expressed agreement, been informed of the material facts, and acted free from coercion. This definition captures the principal concerns surrounding consent: that it be explicit rather than merely imputed, that it be informed rather than blind, and that it be free rather than extracted under duress. In many contexts — commercial contracts between competent adults, medical procedures explained to a mature patient, participation in research by a willing volunteer — the three conditions appear both necessary and sufficient. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises when we consider the consent of those who lack the capacity to appreciate what they are consenting to. An eight-year-old child, told in simple terms that a surgery carries a five percent risk of a named complication, may understand the words perfectly well and express agreement cheerfully and without coercion. She has been informed of the material facts, she has expressed agreement, and no one has pressured her. All three conditions are met. Yet one would rightly hesitate to accept this as valid consent, for the child lacks the maturity to appreciate the gravity of the decision — she cannot weigh a five percent risk of surgical complication against the alternative of living with her condition. The definition captures the external marks of consent but overlooks the internal capacity that makes consent meaningful: the ability to appreciate, and not merely to receive, the information upon which the decision rests.

definition: An imposition of hardship H upon a person P constitutes legitimate punishment if and only if (i) P has been found to have committed a wrong, (ii) H is imposed by an authority with jurisdiction over P, and (iii) H is proportional to the severity of the wrong committed.
conditions: P has been found to have committed a wrong
H is imposed by an authority with jurisdiction
H is proportional to the severity of the wrong
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person is tried under a law that was enacted after the act was committed. The person performed the act when it was perfectly lawful, and only subsequently did the legislature criminalise it. The person is found to have committed a wrong under the new law, the punishment is imposed by a court with jurisdiction, and the sentence is proportional. All three conditions are met. Yet the punishment seems deeply illegitimate, for the person could not have known, at the time of acting, that his conduct would later be declared criminal.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the rule under which the person is punished was in force at the time the act was committed, thereby permitting retroactive punishment.
passage: One might propose that legitimate punishment obtains when a person has been found to have committed a wrong, the hardship is imposed by an authority with jurisdiction, and the punishment is proportional to the wrong. This definition captures the central elements of most accounts of punishment: wrongdoing as a precondition, legitimate authority as the source, and proportionality as the measure. It excludes vigilante justice, disproportionate sentences, and the punishment of the innocent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is that of retroactive criminalisation. Suppose a man, living in a society where the cultivation of a certain plant is lawful, tends his garden in full compliance with the law. The legislature subsequently enacts a statute criminalising the cultivation of that plant and makes the statute retroactive. The man is tried, found to have committed the newly created wrong, and sentenced by a court with jurisdiction to a punishment proportional to the offence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the punishment strikes us as manifestly illegitimate. The man could not have known, at the time of acting, that his conduct would later be declared criminal; he had no opportunity to conform his behaviour to the law. What the definition misses is the requirement that the rule under which punishment is imposed must have been in force at the time the conduct occurred — the ancient principle that no person shall be punished under a law enacted after the fact.

definition: An utterance U by agent S constitutes a binding promise if and only if (i) U expresses S's intention to perform some future action A, (ii) U is directed to a specific promisee R who understands U as a commitment, and (iii) S intends, at the time of uttering U, to perform A.
conditions: U expresses S's intention to perform a future action A
U is directed to a promisee R who understands it as a commitment
S intends at the time of uttering to perform A
counterexample: Consider a promise extracted under extreme duress — a hostage who, with a gun to his head, promises his captor that he will deliver a ransom. The utterance expresses an intention to perform a future action, the captor understands it as a commitment, and the hostage may sincerely intend, at that moment, to comply in order to preserve his life. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to regard this as a binding promise, for the circumstances under which the commitment was extracted vitiate its moral force.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the promise be given under conditions of sufficient voluntariness, free from duress or coercion that would undermine its binding character.
passage: One might define a binding promise as an utterance expressing the speaker's intention to perform a future action, directed to a promisee who understands it as a commitment, where the speaker sincerely intends to perform the action at the time of utterance. This definition captures the essential features of promising: the forward-looking character of the commitment, the interpersonal dimension that distinguishes a promise from a mere resolution, and the requirement of sincerity that excludes empty words. It accommodates a wide range of ordinary promises — to repay a loan, to attend a meeting, to care for a friend's property. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider promises made under extreme duress. A hostage, with a weapon pressed to his head, promises his captor that he will deliver a specified sum of money upon release. His utterance expresses an intention to perform a future action; the captor understands it as a commitment; and the hostage may well intend, at that moment, to comply, for compliance is the only route to survival. The three conditions are all satisfied. Yet it seems clear that no binding promise has been made. The promise was extracted by a threat to the hostage's life, and its moral force is vitiated by the circumstances of its extraction. What the definition misses is a condition on the voluntariness of the promissory act — the requirement that a binding promise be given freely, under conditions that allow the promisor a genuine choice.

definition: An agent S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S performed an action A that causally contributed to O, (ii) S foresaw, or could reasonably have foreseen, that A would contribute to O, and (iii) S was not compelled to perform A by an irresistible external force.
conditions: S performed an action causally contributing to O
S foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen the contribution
S was not compelled by irresistible external force
counterexample: Consider a driver who, without any negligence or imprudence, strikes a pedestrian who dashes into the road from behind a parked vehicle. The driver performed an action — driving — that causally contributed to the injury. She could reasonably have foreseen that driving carries a risk of collisions. And she was not compelled by any external force. All three conditions are met. Yet it seems wrong to hold her morally responsible for the outcome, for she did everything a reasonable person could do to avoid it; the pedestrian's sudden appearance rendered avoidance impossible.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent had a reasonable opportunity to avoid the harmful outcome, thereby attributing responsibility for harms that were causally connected to the agent's action but practically unavoidable.
passage: One might propose that an agent is morally responsible for an outcome when the agent's action causally contributed to it, the agent foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen the contribution, and the agent was not compelled by irresistible external force. This definition captures a broad range of intuitive cases: the negligent driver who speeds through a pedestrian crossing, the factory owner who knowingly pollutes a river, the bystander who pushes someone into traffic. In each case, the action contributes causally, the contribution is foreseeable, and no irresistible force compels the act. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises with the careful driver. Suppose a motorist, driving at the legal speed, with full attention to the road, strikes a pedestrian who dashes from behind a parked vehicle at the last possible moment. The motorist's action — driving — causally contributed to the injury. She could reasonably have foreseen that driving carries a risk of collision. She was not compelled by external force. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet to hold her morally responsible seems unjust: she did everything within her power to avoid the harm, and the pedestrian's sudden appearance gave her no opportunity to react. The definition attributes responsibility wherever causal contribution, foreseeability, and freedom from compulsion coincide, but it neglects the crucial question of whether the agent had a reasonable opportunity to prevent the outcome. Moral responsibility, it appears, requires not merely that the agent could have foreseen the risk in the abstract but that she had a concrete opportunity to avert it.

definition: Two agents who perform the same action in the same circumstances with the same intentions deserve the same moral appraisal if and only if (i) their actions are identical in all deliberate respects, (ii) they possess the same relevant mental states at the time of acting, and (iii) the moral appraisal is based solely on factors within the agents' control.
conditions: Actions are identical in all deliberate respects
Agents possess the same relevant mental states
Moral appraisal is based solely on factors within agents' control
counterexample: Consider two motorists who each drive home after drinking the same amount of alcohol, with identical degrees of impairment, on the same road. One arrives home without incident. The other strikes and kills a pedestrian who steps into the road. Their actions are identical in all deliberate respects, their mental states are the same, and the difference in outcome is attributable entirely to the presence or absence of the pedestrian — a factor beyond either driver's control. The definition dictates that they deserve identical moral appraisal. Yet in practice, and upon reflection, we appraise the killer far more harshly than the one who arrived safely, suggesting that our moral judgements are not in fact based solely on factors within the agents' control.
passage: One might define equal moral desert as obtaining when two agents perform the same action in the same circumstances with the same intentions, and moral appraisal is based solely on factors within their control. This principle has deep intuitive appeal: it would be unjust, one might think, to judge two people differently for identical choices merely because the consequences, which neither could control, happened to diverge. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis — or at least its failure to capture our actual moral practice — is the familiar case of the two drunken drivers. Two motorists drive home with the same degree of intoxication, exercising the same degree of negligent care. Their deliberate actions are identical, their mental states are the same, and neither could control whether a pedestrian would step into the road. One arrives home safely; the other strikes and kills someone. The definition insists that they deserve the same appraisal, since the difference lies entirely in factors beyond their control. Yet our moral and legal practices diverge sharply: the driver who kills is charged with a graver offence and receives far harsher censure. This is not, or not obviously, mere confusion on the part of our moral practice. It may reflect a deep feature of moral appraisal — that we are unable, or unwilling, to detach our judgement of agents entirely from the outcomes of their actions, even when those outcomes depend upon circumstances beyond their control. The definition captures a principle that is perhaps too pure for the moral life as we actually live it.

definition: An agent A has a moral duty to perform action X if and only if (i) X is an action within A's power, (ii) the failure to perform X would result in foreseeable harm to another person, and (iii) no competing obligation of equal or greater weight renders X impermissible.
conditions: X is an action within A's power
Failure to perform X would result in foreseeable harm to another person
No competing obligation of equal or greater weight renders X impermissible
counterexample: Consider a physician who is walking past a road accident and notices that one of the injured persons is a patient whom she has been treating in confidence for a serious communicable disease. She can render first aid — an action within her power — and her failure to act would result in foreseeable harm. No competing obligation of equal weight forbids her from helping. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the physician's duty to assist is complicated by the circumstance that rendering aid may require her to reveal her patient's condition to bystanders, thereby breaching a duty of confidentiality that the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the requirement that the performance of the duty must not entail the violation of a distinct moral obligation owed to the very person one is bound to help or to a third party.
passage: One might propose that a moral duty to act arises whenever the action is within the agent's power, its omission would cause foreseeable harm, and no competing obligation of equal or greater weight forbids it. This analysis has the virtue of simplicity: it grounds duty in capacity, consequence, and the absence of countervailing considerations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed as follows. A physician walking past the scene of a road accident recognises among the injured a patient whom she has been treating in strict confidence for a communicable disease. The physician can render first aid — it is within her power — and her failure to do so would foreseeably worsen the patient's injuries. No obligation of equal weight appears to forbid her from helping. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition pronounces that she has a duty to act. But the situation is more complicated than the definition acknowledges. To render effective aid, the physician may need to communicate with other medical personnel at the scene and, in doing so, disclose her patient's condition. This would breach the duty of confidentiality — a duty owed specifically to the patient she is attempting to help. The difficulty is not that a competing obligation forbids her from helping, but that the very performance of the duty entails the violation of another duty owed to the same person. One might observe that a satisfactory account of moral duty must attend not only to whether competing obligations prohibit the action but also to whether the action itself involves the breach of obligations that are implicated in its performance.

definition: A person P has given morally valid consent to an action X if and only if (i) P has expressed agreement to X through words or conduct, (ii) P was not subject to coercion or duress at the time of agreement, and (iii) P possessed the mental capacity to understand the nature of X.
conditions: P expressed agreement to X through words or conduct
P was not subject to coercion or duress
P possessed mental capacity to understand the nature of X
counterexample: Consider a patient who agrees to a surgical procedure after being told by her physician that the operation carries minimal risk. She expresses her agreement clearly and in writing. She is under no coercion, and she possesses full mental capacity. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the physician has omitted to mention that there is an alternative, less invasive treatment with a higher success rate, which he has declined to recommend because the surgical procedure is more remunerative. The patient's consent, though freely and competently given, was based on an incomplete picture of her options, and one might hesitate to call it morally valid.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person giving consent has been provided with all material information relevant to the decision, including the existence of alternatives, without which the expression of agreement cannot constitute genuine informed consent.
passage: It appears natural to define morally valid consent as the expression of agreement, free from coercion, by a person with the mental capacity to understand what is proposed. This definition captures the central intuitions: consent must be voluntary, it must be articulate, and the consenting party must be competent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a patient agrees to undergo a surgical procedure after being informed by her physician that the operation carries minimal risk and offers a good prospect of relief. She signs the consent form without hesitation. She is under no coercion — indeed, she is grateful for the recommendation. She is fully competent and understands the nature of the operation. All three conditions are met. But the physician has failed to disclose that a less invasive treatment exists, one with a higher success rate and fewer complications, which he has not recommended because the surgical procedure is considerably more remunerative for his practice. The patient's agreement was genuine, uncoerced, and competent, but it was given in ignorance of a material fact — the existence of a superior alternative. One might observe that consent given without access to material information is not morally equivalent to consent given with full disclosure, however voluntary and competent it may be. The definition, by attending only to the manner in which consent is given and not to the informational conditions under which it is given, permits cases in which agreement is formally valid but substantively deficient.

definition: The infliction of a penalty upon a person P is morally justified if and only if (i) P has committed a wrongful act, (ii) the penalty is proportionate to the severity of the wrongful act, and (iii) the penalty is imposed by a legitimate authority.
conditions: P has committed a wrongful act
The penalty is proportionate to the severity of the wrong
The penalty is imposed by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Consider a case in which a citizen of an occupied territory has committed an act of sabotage against the occupying power — an act which, under the laws of the occupying authority, is a serious crime. The occupying authority, which governs by force and without the consent of the governed, imposes a penalty proportionate to the offence as defined by its own legal code. All three conditions appear to be met: a wrongful act (by the occupier's lights) has been committed, the penalty is proportionate, and the authority imposing it holds de facto governmental power. Yet one might hesitate to say the punishment is morally justified, for the authority in question lacks moral legitimacy — its power rests on conquest, not consent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the legitimate authority derives its standing from a morally defensible source — such as the consent of the governed or the protection of fundamental rights — rather than from mere de facto power.
passage: One might define morally justified punishment as the infliction of a proportionate penalty upon a wrongdoer by a legitimate authority. This definition is appealing in its economy: it insists that only the guilty be punished, that the severity of the penalty be fitting, and that the power to punish be vested in a recognised authority rather than exercised by private individuals. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the concept of legitimate authority more carefully. Suppose a citizen of an occupied territory has committed an act of sabotage against the occupying power. Under the occupier's legal code, the act constitutes a serious offence. The occupying authority, which exercises de facto governmental control over the territory, imposes a penalty that is, by its own standards, proportionate to the crime. A wrongful act has been committed (at least under the positive law of the occupying power), the penalty is proportionate, and the authority imposing it holds effective governmental power. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it appears doubtful that the punishment is morally justified. The occupying authority governs by force and without the consent of the population it rules. Its legitimacy is merely de facto — it has the power to punish but not the moral standing to do so. One might observe that the definition's requirement of legitimate authority is satisfied too easily, for it does not distinguish between authority that rests on morally defensible grounds — the consent of the governed, the protection of fundamental rights — and authority that rests solely on the exercise of superior force.

definition: A person P has forgiven a person Q for a wrongful act W if and only if (i) P was genuinely wronged by Q's performance of W, (ii) P has ceased to harbour resentment toward Q on account of W, and (iii) P's cessation of resentment was a result of a deliberate decision rather than mere forgetfulness or indifference.
conditions: P was genuinely wronged by Q's performance of W
P has ceased to harbour resentment toward Q on account of W
P's cessation of resentment resulted from deliberate decision rather than forgetfulness or indifference
counterexample: Consider a woman who was defrauded by a business partner some years ago. She was genuinely wronged. She has since ceased to feel resentment — not through forgetfulness, but through a deliberate process of reflection in which she decided that harbouring bitterness was damaging to her own well-being. Her decision was entirely self-regarding: she let go of her resentment for her own sake, without any consideration of the wrongdoer's character, contrition, or circumstances. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to describe this as forgiveness, for the deliberate release of resentment was motivated purely by self-interest and involved no moral engagement with the wrongdoer whatsoever.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the cessation of resentment involve some moral re-engagement with the wrongdoer — such as a recognition of the wrongdoer's humanity, an acknowledgement of contrition, or a willingness to restore the moral relationship — rather than a merely prudential decision to abandon a burdensome emotion.
passage: One might propose that forgiveness consists in the deliberate cessation of resentment by a person who has been genuinely wronged. This proposal captures several features that seem essential to the concept: there must be a genuine wrong (one cannot forgive where one has not been injured), the resentment must actually cease (mere verbal pronouncement is insufficient), and the cessation must be deliberate rather than the product of mere forgetfulness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. A woman was defrauded by a business partner several years ago, and she harboured considerable resentment for some time. Eventually, upon reflection, she decided that the resentment was injurious to her health and peace of mind, and she resolved to let it go. She succeeded: the resentment dissipated, and she no longer feels bitterness when she thinks of her former partner. Her decision was entirely deliberate. But it was also entirely self-regarding. She did not consider whether her partner had shown contrition. She did not reflect upon his character or circumstances. She did not seek to understand or to restore the moral relationship between them. She simply decided that resentment was bad for her and discarded it as one might discard an uncomfortable garment. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet one might observe that what has occurred is more accurately described as emotional self-management than as forgiveness. Forgiveness, it appears, requires some form of moral re-engagement with the wrongdoer — a recognition of shared humanity, or a response to contrition, or a willingness to restore the relationship — that the definition, in attending only to the deliberate abandonment of resentment, does not capture.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) A performed an action that was a proximate cause of O, (ii) A acted voluntarily and was not compelled by external force, and (iii) A could foresee that the action might lead to O.
conditions: A performed an action that was a proximate cause of O
A acted voluntarily without external compulsion
A could foresee that the action might lead to O
counterexample: Consider a factory owner who, over many years, discharges a chemical effluent into a river in full compliance with the regulations of his time. He acts voluntarily and he can foresee — indeed, he does foresee — that the effluent enters the river. Decades later, scientific research reveals that the chemical accumulates in the food chain and has caused serious illness in the downstream population. His action was a proximate cause of the harm, he acted voluntarily, and he foresaw that the effluent would enter the river. Yet he could not foresee that the chemical would cause illness, for this consequence was unknown to anyone at the time of his action.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between foreseeability of the immediate consequences of an action and foreseeability of its harmful effects, thereby attributing responsibility in cases where the agent could foresee the action's proximate results but could not, given the state of available understanding, foresee the harm those results would produce.
passage: One might define moral responsibility as follows: an agent is responsible for an outcome when the agent's voluntary action was a proximate cause of the outcome and the agent could foresee that the action might lead to it. This definition has an attractive simplicity. It grounds responsibility in agency, voluntariness, and foresight — the three features most commonly invoked in ordinary moral judgement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of industrial practice. A factory owner, operating in full compliance with the regulations of his day, discharges a chemical effluent into a river for many years. He acts entirely voluntarily. He foresees — indeed, he intends — that the effluent should enter the river, for this is the established method of waste disposal. Decades later, advances in toxicology reveal that the chemical accumulates in the tissues of fish and, through the food chain, in the human population downstream, causing serious illness. The factory owner's action was undeniably a proximate cause of the harm. He acted voluntarily. And he could foresee that the effluent would enter the river. But he could not foresee that this would cause illness, for the toxicological properties of the chemical were unknown to anyone at the time of his action. The definition's foreseeability condition is ambiguous: it is satisfied with respect to the immediate consequences of the action (the effluent entering the river) but not with respect to the harmful effects (the illness). One might observe that moral responsibility requires not merely foreseeability of an action's proximate consequences but foreseeability of the harm those consequences produce, and that the definition, by failing to draw this distinction, extends responsibility to cases where no reasonable person could have anticipated the injurious outcome.

definition: A transaction between parties A and B is exploitative if and only if (i) A derives a significant benefit from the transaction, (ii) B is in a position of vulnerability or disadvantage relative to A, and (iii) B receives less than a fair share of the gains produced by the transaction.
conditions: A derives significant benefit from the transaction
B is in a position of vulnerability or disadvantage relative to A
B receives less than a fair share of the gains
counterexample: Consider a pharmaceutical company that offers a new and expensive medicine to patients in a desperately poor region at a substantially reduced price — far below what it charges in wealthy nations, though still at a profit. The company derives significant benefit. The patients are in a position of extreme vulnerability. Yet the patients also benefit greatly: without the company's offer, they would have no access to the medicine at all, and the price, though profitable for the company, is lower than anything the patients could obtain elsewhere. The patients arguably receive less than a fair share of the total gains, yet the transaction leaves them materially better off than any available alternative.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to consider whether the transaction, despite being unequal, leaves the disadvantaged party better off than any available alternative, which would suggest that the unfairness of the distribution of gains is not sufficient for exploitation when the transaction is genuinely beneficial to the weaker party.
passage: It appears plausible to define an exploitative transaction as one in which a party in a position of advantage derives significant benefit while the disadvantaged party receives less than a fair share of the gains. This analysis captures the central cases of exploitation — the sweatshop, the usurious loan, the unconscionable bargain — in which one party's vulnerability is leveraged to produce an unjust distribution of benefits. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider transactions that are unequal but nonetheless genuinely beneficial to the weaker party. A pharmaceutical company develops a medicine that is expensive to produce and sells it in wealthy nations at a high price. In a desperately poor region where the disease is prevalent and no alternative treatment exists, the company offers the medicine at a substantially reduced price — far below the price in wealthy nations, though still at a profit. The company derives a significant benefit. The patients are in a position of extreme vulnerability. And the patients receive, by any plausible measure, less than a fair share of the total gains generated by the transaction. All three conditions are met, and the definition pronounces the transaction exploitative. Yet one should hesitate before accepting this verdict. The patients, without the company's offer, would have no access to the medicine at all. The transaction, unequal as it is, leaves them materially better off than any available alternative. One might observe that a definition of exploitation which cannot distinguish between transactions that worsen the position of the vulnerable and transactions that improve it — however unequally — fails to capture what is genuinely objectionable about exploitation.

definition: A person P acts with integrity if and only if (i) P's actions are consistent with P's professed moral principles, (ii) P adheres to these principles even when doing so is personally costly, and (iii) P does not make exceptions for herself that she would not allow for others.
conditions: P's actions are consistent with P's professed moral principles
P adheres to these principles even when personally costly
P does not make self-serving exceptions
counterexample: Consider a colonial administrator who professes a sincere commitment to the principle that every people has the right to govern itself, and who adheres to this principle at great personal cost — declining promotions that would require him to suppress local governance, and enduring the censure of his superiors. He makes no self-serving exceptions. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the principles he serves were adopted unreflectively from his liberal education, and he has never seriously examined whether they are sound or how they ought to be applied in the complex circumstances of colonial governance. His consistency is admirable, but it is the consistency of unreflective habit rather than of considered moral commitment.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the moral principles to which the agent adheres have been subjected to critical reflection, so that the consistency in question is the product of considered judgement rather than of unreflective habit or inherited conviction.
passage: One might propose that integrity consists in the consistent adherence to one's professed moral principles, even at personal cost, without self-serving exceptions. This is an appealing analysis: it captures the steadfastness, the personal sacrifice, and the impartiality that we associate with persons of integrity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be described as follows. Consider a colonial administrator who professes a sincere commitment to the principle that every people has the right to govern itself. He adheres to this principle with remarkable consistency. He declines appointments that would require him to override local governance. He suffers the displeasure of his superiors and the contempt of his colleagues. He makes no exceptions for himself. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition pronounces him a person of integrity. But upon closer examination, it emerges that his principles were adopted in his youth from the liberal tradition of his education and have never been subjected to critical scrutiny. He has not considered how the principle of self-governance applies in circumstances of ethnic conflict, or whether it extends to governance structures that themselves suppress the rights of minorities within the territory. His consistency is genuine, but it is the consistency of unreflective adherence to an unexamined conviction. One might observe that integrity, properly understood, requires not merely that one's actions match one's principles but that one's principles have been formed and maintained through genuine moral reflection, and that a definition which requires only consistency permits a form of integrity that is indistinguishable from principled stubbornness.

definition: An action X performed by an agent A is supererogatory if and only if (i) X is morally praiseworthy, (ii) X goes beyond what A is morally required to do, and (iii) the omission of X would not be morally blameworthy.
conditions: X is morally praiseworthy
X goes beyond what A is morally required to do
Omission of X would not be morally blameworthy
counterexample: Consider a wealthy philanthropist who donates a large sum to establish a hospital in a region lacking medical facilities. The action is morally praiseworthy, it goes beyond what she is required to do, and no one would blame her for not making the donation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the philanthropist's wealth was accumulated through business practices that, while legal, involved the closure of the very factories that had provided employment to the community now lacking medical care. Her donation alleviates a harm to which her own prior conduct substantially contributed. One might hesitate to call the donation supererogatory, for it appears less like a generous act that exceeds the demands of duty and more like a partial reparation for prior injury.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the praiseworthy action be independent of any prior conduct by the agent that contributed to the very need the action addresses, for where the agent's own behaviour created the conditions calling for the beneficent act, the act may be closer to reparation than to supererogation.
passage: One might define a supererogatory action as one that is morally praiseworthy, exceeds the demands of duty, and whose omission would not be blameworthy. This definition captures the familiar examples — the soldier who throws himself on a grenade, the stranger who donates a kidney — in which an agent performs an act of extraordinary moral value that no one could reasonably demand. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. A wealthy philanthropist donates a considerable sum to establish a hospital in a region that lacks adequate medical facilities. The donation is morally praiseworthy by any reckoning. It exceeds what she is required to do; no moral principle demands that she build hospitals. And no one would blame her had she declined to make the gift. All three conditions are met. But the philanthropist's wealth was accumulated through business practices that, though legal, involved the closure of the factories that had been the principal source of employment in the community now suffering from inadequate medical care. The economic decline that produced the community's inability to maintain its own medical infrastructure was, in significant part, a consequence of her commercial decisions. In this light, her donation appears less as a generous act that goes beyond duty and more as a partial remedy for a condition she helped to create. One might observe that genuine supererogation requires that the praiseworthy action not be connected to the agent's own prior contribution to the need it addresses, and that the definition, by omitting this requirement, permits cases in which what appears to be generosity is more accurately described as belated and incomplete reparation.

definition: An agent A displays courage in performing action X if and only if (i) X involves a genuine risk of serious harm to A, (ii) A is aware of the risk and performs X despite it, and (iii) X is undertaken in pursuit of a morally worthy end.
conditions: X involves genuine risk of serious harm to A
A is aware of the risk and acts despite it
X is undertaken in pursuit of a morally worthy end
counterexample: Consider a firefighter who enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child. The risk is genuine and serious. She is fully aware of it. The end — saving a child's life — is morally worthy. All three conditions are satisfied, and we should rightly call her courageous. But now consider a case in which the same firefighter, by long training and habituation, has come to feel no fear whatsoever in such situations. She enters the building with the same calm composure with which she might enter a shop. The objective risk is the same, her awareness of it is the same, and the end is equally worthy. Yet one might hesitate to ascribe courage in the fullest sense, for the concept seems to require not merely awareness of risk but some affective response to it — fear, or at least apprehension — that is overcome in the performance of the act.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the agent experience some affective response to the perceived risk — such as fear or apprehension — that must be overcome or managed in the performance of the action, for courage seems to consist not merely in acting despite known danger but in acting despite felt danger.
passage: It appears natural to define courage as the performance of a risky action in aware pursuit of a morally worthy end. This definition has considerable plausibility: it excludes the foolhardy (who act without awareness of risk), the self-destructive (who risk harm for no worthy purpose), and the merely reckless (who disregard danger from indifference rather than moral conviction). Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described as follows. A firefighter of long experience enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child. The risk of serious harm is genuine — the building is structurally compromised and may collapse at any moment. She is fully aware of the danger. Her purpose is entirely worthy. All three conditions are met. But this particular firefighter, through years of training and repeated exposure to dangerous situations, has become so habituated to risk that she experiences no fear whatever. She enters the burning building with the same equanimity with which she might enter her own kitchen. The objective danger has not diminished, her awareness of it has not diminished, and the worthiness of her end has not diminished. Yet one might observe that something is missing from the account. Courage, as ordinarily understood, appears to require not only that one act in the face of known danger but that one act in the face of felt danger — that there be some apprehension or fear, however well controlled, that is mastered in the performance of the act. The definition, by requiring only awareness of risk and not any affective response to it, includes cases of cool, fearless competence that, however admirable, seem to belong to a different category than courage properly so called.

definition: A person P deserves a reward R if and only if (i) P has performed an action or series of actions that produced a valuable outcome, (ii) the valuable outcome is attributable to P's own effort rather than to luck or circumstance, and (iii) P performed the action voluntarily and with the intention of producing the valuable outcome.
conditions: P performed actions that produced a valuable outcome
The outcome is attributable to P's own effort rather than luck
P acted voluntarily with the intention of producing the outcome
counterexample: Consider a medical researcher who devotes twenty years of painstaking effort to developing a vaccine for a tropical disease. She works voluntarily, with the explicit intention of producing the vaccine, and her effort is the primary cause of the breakthrough — it cannot be attributed to luck. All three conditions are met, and one would readily say she deserves recognition and reward. But now consider that the same researcher, had she been born in a country without a functioning university system, would never have had access to the laboratory, the training, or the scientific literature that made her work possible. Her effort was genuine, but it was exercised within a framework of institutional support that she did not create and to which others contributed.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the requirement that the agent's effort be assessed in light of the enabling conditions — institutional, social, and material — that made the effort possible, for desert based solely on individual effort ignores the contributions of the broader framework without which the effort could not have been exercised.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves a reward when the person has voluntarily and intentionally produced a valuable outcome through effort that is attributable to the person rather than to luck. This definition captures the intuition that desert is earned rather than bestowed: it requires genuine effort, genuine voluntariness, and a genuine causal connection between the agent's labour and the valuable result. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. A medical researcher devotes twenty years of diligent effort to the development of a vaccine for a tropical disease that afflicts millions. She works voluntarily and with the explicit intention of producing the vaccine. Her breakthrough is the product of her own sustained labour and ingenuity — it cannot be attributed to fortunate accident. All three conditions are met, and no one would deny that she deserves recognition. But one might observe that her effort, however genuine, was exercised within a framework of institutional support that she did not create: the university that trained her, the laboratory that housed her work, the scientific literature that informed her methods, the funding bodies that supported her research. Had she been born in a country without these institutions, her intelligence and determination would have availed her nothing. The definition, by attributing desert solely to individual effort and excluding luck, draws the boundary between effort and circumstance too narrowly. The enabling conditions of effort — the institutions, the training, the accumulated work of predecessors — are themselves matters of circumstance, and a definition that ignores them ascribes to individual merit what is in part the product of social provision.

definition: A person P owes a debt of gratitude to a benefactor B if and only if (i) B performed an action that conferred a benefit upon P, (ii) B performed the action voluntarily and not under compulsion, and (iii) P is aware that the benefit was conferred by B.
conditions: B performed an action that conferred a benefit upon P
B acted voluntarily and not under compulsion
P is aware that B conferred the benefit
counterexample: Consider a wealthy industrialist who donates a large sum to a hospital, thereby enabling the treatment of a gravely ill child. The donation confers an undeniable benefit upon the child. The industrialist acts voluntarily — no one compels his generosity. The child's family is informed of the donation and knows precisely who made it possible. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the industrialist made the donation solely to obtain a tax advantage, and he has publicly stated that he has no interest in the welfare of the patients his money assists. One might hesitate to say the child's family owes a debt of gratitude, for the benefit was a mere by-product of the industrialist's self-interested calculation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the benefactor acted with at least some degree of benevolent intention toward the recipient, and thereby admits cases in which the benefit is a purely incidental consequence of a self-serving act.
passage: One might propose that a debt of gratitude arises whenever a benefit has been voluntarily conferred and the recipient is aware of its source. This analysis captures the typical case: a neighbour who helps repair one's roof acts voluntarily, the help is beneficial, and the recipient knows who provided it. Gratitude seems plainly owed. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed as follows. A wealthy industrialist donates a substantial sum to a hospital, and his donation enables the successful treatment of a gravely ill child whose family could not otherwise have afforded the procedure. The donation is voluntary — the industrialist is under no legal or social compulsion. The family is informed and fully aware that the industrialist's money saved their child. All three conditions are satisfied. But the industrialist has donated exclusively to obtain a tax advantage; he has said publicly that he regards the hospital and its patients as incidental to his financial planning. The benefit to the child, though real, was a mere by-product of a transaction whose purpose was entirely self-interested. One might observe that gratitude, in its fullest moral sense, seems to presuppose that the benefactor acted at least partly out of concern for the recipient's welfare. Where the benefit is an unintended side-effect of a self-serving calculation, the conditions of the definition are met, but the moral sentiment of gratitude appears to lack its proper object. The definition, by requiring only voluntariness and awareness, does not distinguish between genuine benevolence and incidental benefit.

definition: An action X is supererogatory if and only if (i) X is morally praiseworthy, (ii) X goes beyond what is morally required of the agent, and (iii) the omission of X is not morally blameworthy.
conditions: X is morally praiseworthy
X exceeds what is morally required
Omission of X is not blameworthy
counterexample: Consider a soldier who throws himself upon a grenade to save his comrades. The action is morally praiseworthy in the highest degree; it exceeds what duty requires, for no military code demands that a soldier sacrifice his life in this manner; and his failure to do so would not be blameworthy — no one would condemn a soldier for failing to make such a sacrifice. All three conditions are met, and the definition classifies the act as supererogatory. Yet one might hesitate, for the action is performed in a context of extreme urgency in which there is no time for deliberation, and the soldier may have acted from instinct rather than from a considered moral choice. The definition does not distinguish between a deliberately chosen act of extraordinary generosity and an impulsive response to crisis.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent perform the action with some degree of deliberation or moral awareness, thereby admitting as supererogatory acts that, however admirable in their consequences, may have been performed without the reflective moral engagement that supererogation seems to presuppose.
passage: One might define a supererogatory act as one that is morally praiseworthy, exceeds what is morally required, and whose omission would not be blameworthy. This definition captures the essential contour of saintly and heroic actions: the charitable person who gives far more than duty demands, the rescuer who risks life and limb for a stranger. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers actions performed under conditions of extreme urgency. A soldier, seeing a grenade land among his comrades, throws himself upon it and is killed. The action is morally praiseworthy — no one would deny this. It exceeds what duty requires, for no military regulation demands self-sacrifice of this kind. And the soldier's failure to act would occasion no blame; his comrades would not reproach a man for declining to die. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the soldier had no time to deliberate: he acted in a fraction of a second, driven perhaps by instinct, training, or reflex rather than by a considered moral judgement. Supererogation, as ordinarily understood, seems to involve a kind of moral generosity — a deliberate choice to do more than one must. The soldier's action, however admirable, may not have involved the reflective engagement that distinguishes supererogatory conduct from merely fortunate impulse. The definition, by attending only to praiseworthiness, excessiveness, and the blamelessness of omission, does not require that the agent have acted with the deliberation that appears to be a condition of genuine moral supererogation.

definition: Two agents A and B are equally morally culpable for performing the same type of reckless action if and only if (i) both A and B performed the action with the same degree of recklessness, (ii) both were aware of the same risks, and (iii) neither was subject to any mitigating circumstance not shared by the other.
conditions: Both performed the action with the same degree of recklessness
Both were aware of the same risks
Neither had any unshared mitigating circumstance
counterexample: Consider two motorists who each drive home after drinking the same quantity of wine, along the same road, at the same speed, in the same weather conditions. Each is equally reckless, equally aware of the risks, and subject to no unshared mitigating circumstances. All three conditions are met, and the definition pronounces them equally culpable. Yet one motorist arrives home without incident, while the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who stepped into the road. The moral judgement of ordinary persons — and the law — treats the second motorist as far more culpable than the first, despite the identity of their reckless conduct.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of outcome in moral assessment, neglecting the widely held intuition that the actual consequences of an action — even when they depend upon factors beyond the agent's control — bear upon the degree of moral culpability ascribed to the agent.
passage: One might propose that two agents who perform the same reckless action with the same awareness of risk and under the same circumstances are equally culpable. This principle has considerable appeal: it grounds moral assessment in what the agent does and what the agent knows, which are the factors within the agent's control. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the familiar scenario of the two drunken motorists. Suppose two persons, each having consumed the same quantity of wine, drive home along the same road at the same hour. Each is reckless to the same degree; each is aware of the same risks — the possibility of impaired reflexes, the danger to pedestrians, the chance of losing control. Neither has any mitigating circumstance not shared by the other. The conditions are identical in every morally relevant respect that the definition recognises. Yet one motorist arrives home safely, while the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who happened to step off the kerb at that moment. The moral verdict of the community is decisive: the second motorist is treated as far more culpable than the first. The law punishes the second more severely; friends and neighbours regard the second with a horror they do not direct at the first. One might observe that this asymmetry of judgement cannot be explained by the definition, which insists that culpability depends solely on the agent's conduct, awareness, and circumstances. The difference lies in the outcome — a factor entirely beyond the agents' control — and the definition, by ignoring outcomes, fails to capture the moral significance that ordinary moral practice ascribes to the consequences of reckless action.

definition: A person P is morally complicit in a wrongful act W performed by another agent Q if and only if (i) P provided assistance or encouragement that facilitated Q's performance of W, (ii) P was aware that Q intended to perform W, and (iii) P acted freely and not under duress.
conditions: P provided assistance or encouragement facilitating W
P was aware of Q's intention to perform W
P acted freely
counterexample: Consider a locksmith who sells a set of standard lock-picks to a customer in the ordinary course of business. The locksmith is aware that lock-picks can be used for burglary, but the customer gives no indication of criminal intent and presents himself as a fellow tradesman. The locksmith sells the tools freely, and the customer subsequently uses them to commit a burglary. The locksmith provided the instruments that facilitated the crime, and one might argue that he was aware, in a general sense, that lock-picks could be so used. He acted freely. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet it seems extravagant to call the locksmith complicit in the burglary.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's awareness be specific rather than merely general — that P be aware of Q's particular intention to commit the specific wrongful act, rather than merely aware that the means provided could in principle be misused.
passage: One might define moral complicity as the free provision of assistance or encouragement to a wrongdoer, performed with awareness of the wrongdoer's intentions. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases: the lookout who watches for the police while his associate robs a house, the adviser who counsels a tyrant on how to suppress dissent. In each case, assistance is provided, the assistant is aware of the principal's wrongful purpose, and the assistant acts freely. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a locksmith who sells a set of standard lock-picks to a customer in the ordinary course of trade. The customer presents himself as a fellow locksmith, makes a routine purchase, and departs. He later uses the picks to commit a burglary. The locksmith provided the tools that facilitated the crime. He was aware, in a general way, that lock-picks can be employed for criminal purposes — any locksmith is aware of this. And he acted freely: no one compelled him to make the sale. The three conditions are satisfied, at least on a generous reading of what constitutes awareness of intention. Yet one should hesitate to call the locksmith complicit. His awareness was entirely general: he was aware that lock-picks could be misused, not that this particular customer intended to misuse them. The definition, by requiring only that P be aware that Q intended to perform W, does not specify the degree of particularity that such awareness must possess, and thereby extends complicity to cases in which the provider of assistance had no more than a generic understanding that the means supplied could, in principle, be put to wrongful use.

definition: A person P has genuinely forgiven a wrongdoer Q for a wrong W if and only if (i) P has ceased to harbour resentment toward Q on account of W, (ii) P has relinquished any claim to exact retribution for W, and (iii) P has communicated to Q, by word or conduct, that the wrong is no longer held against Q.
conditions: P has ceased to harbour resentment toward Q
P has relinquished any claim to retribution
P has communicated that the wrong is no longer held against Q
counterexample: Consider a wife whose husband has been unfaithful. She tells him she forgives him, and she sincerely means it. She relinquishes any wish for retribution and does not hold the wrong against him. Years pass, and though she never again raises the matter and bears no conscious resentment, she finds that she cannot trust him as she once did. Her involuntary wariness — a flinch when he is late, an anxious glance at his correspondence — persists despite her genuine desire to forgive completely. All three conditions are met: resentment has ceased, retribution has been relinquished, and forgiveness has been communicated. Yet one might hesitate to say that forgiveness is complete, for the residual distrust bespeaks a wound that the definition cannot register.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the restoration of trust or the overcoming of the involuntary emotional residue of betrayal, and thereby counts as complete forgiveness what may be only its outward and deliberate component.
passage: One might define genuine forgiveness as the cessation of resentment, the relinquishment of claims to retribution, and the communication of pardon. This definition has the merit of identifying the principal elements that distinguish forgiveness from mere forbearance or strategic overlooking. The person who forgives does not merely refrain from punishment; she surrenders her resentment and lets the wrongdoer know it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of intimate betrayal. Suppose a wife whose husband has been unfaithful tells him, with complete sincerity, that she forgives him. She relinquishes any desire for retribution. She does not raise the matter again, and she bears no conscious resentment. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet years later, she discovers that she cannot trust her husband as she once did. When he is late returning home, she feels an involuntary anxiety. When she notices an unfamiliar name on his correspondence, her heart quickens. These responses persist despite her genuine desire to forgive fully, and despite the absence of any conscious resentment. One might observe that what remains is not resentment but its residue — an involuntary wariness that is not within the control of the will. The definition, by requiring only the cessation of resentment, the relinquishment of retribution, and the communication of pardon, captures the deliberate and volitional aspects of forgiveness but does not address the involuntary emotional aftermath that may persist long after the will has done its work. It appears that forgiveness, in its fullest sense, involves something more than the definition provides — the restoration of a trust that cannot simply be willed into existence.

definition: A person P possesses moral integrity if and only if (i) P consistently acts in accordance with P's avowed moral principles, (ii) P does not abandon these principles under social pressure or personal inconvenience, and (iii) P's outward conduct corresponds to P's inward convictions.
conditions: P consistently acts in accordance with avowed principles
P does not abandon principles under pressure
P's outward conduct matches inward convictions
counterexample: Consider a magistrate in a colonial territory who consistently applies the laws of the colonial power with scrupulous impartiality. He never bends the rules under social pressure; he never deviates from his stated principles of strict legal application; and his conduct in court perfectly reflects his inward conviction that the law must be applied as written. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the laws he applies are those of an unjust regime — laws that dispossess the indigenous population and enforce a system of racial subordination. The magistrate possesses consistency, steadfastness, and sincerity, but one should hesitate to attribute moral integrity to a man whose principles are themselves unjust.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the moral principles with which the agent's conduct is consistent are themselves defensible or sound, and thereby attributes integrity to agents who steadfastly adhere to morally objectionable commitments.
