passage: One might define moral integrity as the consistent alignment of conduct with avowed principles, maintained under pressure and reflecting genuine inward conviction. This definition captures an important moral ideal: the person of integrity is not a hypocrite, does not bend with the prevailing wind, and means what she says. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the annals of colonial administration. Consider a magistrate serving in a colonial territory who applies the laws of the occupying power with unfailing consistency. He never succumbs to social pressure to show favouritism; he never abandons his principles for personal convenience; and his public conduct faithfully mirrors his private convictions. He believes the law should be applied as written, and he applies it so. All three conditions are met. Yet the laws he administers are the instruments of an unjust regime: they dispossess the indigenous population of their lands, they restrict the movements and occupations of those whom the colonial power regards as inferior, and they enforce a system of racial subordination. The magistrate is consistent, steadfast, and sincere, but the principles to which he adheres are themselves morally defective. One might observe that integrity, in the morally admirable sense, seems to require not merely consistency with one's principles but the possession of principles that are themselves worthy of consistent adherence. The definition, by attending only to the relation between conduct and conviction, takes no account of the moral quality of the convictions themselves.

definition: A person P deserves a reward R for an achievement A if and only if (i) P brought about A through P's own deliberate efforts, (ii) A is of a kind that the relevant community recognises as meritorious, and (iii) R is proportionate to the merit of A.
conditions: P brought about A through deliberate effort
A is recognised as meritorious by the community
R is proportionate to the merit of A
counterexample: Consider two students who sit the same examination and achieve the same mark. Each has worked with equal diligence over the term. The first student, however, was educated at an excellent school with extensive resources, while the second attended a school with overcrowded classrooms and few textbooks. Both brought about the achievement through deliberate effort; the achievement is recognised as meritorious; and any reward would be proportionate to the identical mark. All three conditions are satisfied equally for both students. Yet one might observe that the second student's achievement, accomplished against far greater obstacles, represents a more impressive exercise of effort and ability, and that the two students do not deserve the same reward despite satisfying the same conditions.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to account for the circumstances and obstacles against which the achievement was accomplished, so that it treats achievements produced by equal effort under unequal conditions as equally deserving of reward.
passage: One might propose that desert is a matter of deliberate effort directed toward an achievement that the community recognises as meritorious, with the reward proportionate to the merit. This analysis has the virtue of grounding desert in the agent's own exertions rather than in fortune or circumstance. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the practice of academic examination. Suppose two students sit the same examination and achieve identical marks. Each has prepared with equal diligence; each has devoted the same number of hours to study over the same period. The first student was educated at a school renowned for the quality of its teaching, its well-stocked library, and its small class sizes. The second attended a school in a deprived district, with overcrowded classrooms, a meagre library, and teachers overburdened by administrative duties. Both students brought about their achievement through deliberate effort; the achievement is recognised as meritorious; and any prize would be proportionate to the identical mark. The three conditions are satisfied equally in both cases. Yet it appears that the second student's achievement is, in a morally significant sense, more impressive than the first's. The same effort, expended against greater obstacles, has produced the same result, and one might think that the second student deserves more rather than the same. The definition, by attending only to effort, community recognition, and proportionality, takes no account of the differential obstacles that the agents faced. It treats two achievements alike when the moral situation of the achievers is importantly different.

definition: A transaction between parties A and B is exploitative if and only if (i) A derives a benefit from the transaction that is disproportionate to what B receives, (ii) B enters the transaction because B has no reasonable alternative, and (iii) A is aware of B's lack of alternatives.
conditions: A derives a disproportionate benefit
B has no reasonable alternative
A is aware of B's lack of alternatives
counterexample: Consider a pharmaceutical company that sells a life-saving medicine at a price far above its production cost to patients in a developing country where no alternative treatment exists. The company derives a vastly disproportionate benefit; the patients have no reasonable alternative; and the company is well aware of the patients' situation. All three conditions are met. But now suppose a charitable organisation steps in and subsidises the purchase, so that the patients pay nothing and the company receives the full price from the charity. The transaction between the company and the charity satisfies none of the three conditions — the charity acts freely, has alternatives, and the distribution of benefit is agreed upon. Yet the underlying structure of exploitation remains: the company continues to extract a disproportionate price for a life-saving product, and the exploitation has merely been displaced rather than eliminated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that exploitation can be a structural feature of the terms on which a product is offered, independent of whether any particular transaction instantiates the three conditions, and thereby misses cases in which the exploitative character inheres in the pricing structure rather than in the bilateral relationship.
passage: One might define exploitation as a transaction in which one party derives a disproportionate benefit from another who lacks reasonable alternatives, with the advantaged party aware of this asymmetry. This definition captures the intuitive features of paradigmatic exploitation: the employer who pays starvation wages to workers with no other employment, the moneylender who charges ruinous interest to borrowers with no access to credit. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when the exploitative transaction is mediated by a third party. Suppose a pharmaceutical company sells a life-saving medicine to patients in a developing country at a price vastly exceeding its production cost. The patients have no alternative treatment; the company is aware of this; and the benefit to the company is disproportionate. All three conditions are satisfied, and the transaction is exploitative by the definition's lights. Now suppose a charitable organisation intervenes and purchases the medicine on the patients' behalf at the company's full asking price, distributing it to the patients without charge. The transaction between the company and the charity does not satisfy the three conditions: the charity acts freely, has alternatives (it could fund other programmes), and the allocation of benefit is voluntary. The definition pronounces the transaction non-exploitative. Yet one might observe that nothing has changed in the company's conduct: it continues to charge a disproportionate price for a product whose recipients, absent the charity, would have no recourse. The exploitation has been masked but not remedied. The definition, by focusing on the bilateral relationship between the immediate parties, cannot detect exploitation that inheres in the structural terms of the transaction rather than in the particular circumstances of a given exchange.

definition: An action X performed by an agent A is courageous if and only if (i) X involves a significant risk of harm to A, (ii) A is aware of the risk at the time of acting, and (iii) A performs X in pursuit of a morally worthy end.
conditions: X involves significant risk of harm to A
A is aware of the risk
A acts in pursuit of a morally worthy end
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who attempts a dangerous ascent of an unclimbed peak during the monsoon season. The risk of harm is extreme and the mountaineer is fully aware of it. He undertakes the climb in order to raise funds for famine relief — a morally worthy end. All three conditions are met. Yet the risk is so disproportionate to any likely benefit — the chance of success is negligible, and death is near-certain — that one might hesitate to call the act courageous rather than foolhardy. The conditions are satisfied, but the action appears to cross the line between admirable courage and reckless self-destruction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the risk undertaken bear a reasonable proportion to the end pursued, and thereby conflates courage with foolhardiness by admitting as courageous actions whose risks are so extravagant as to render the pursuit of the worthy end irrational.
passage: One might define a courageous action as one that involves significant risk, is performed with awareness of that risk, and is directed at a morally worthy end. This definition captures the essential features of the actions we most admire: the firefighter who enters a burning building to rescue a child, the whistleblower who risks her career to expose wrongdoing. In each case, risk is present, the agent is aware of it, and the end is morally worthy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the risk is so disproportionate to the end that the action ceases to be admirable and becomes reckless. Suppose a mountaineer undertakes a dangerous ascent during the monsoon season, when the chance of reaching the summit is negligible and the probability of death is very high. His purpose is to raise funds for famine relief — a morally worthy end beyond question. He is fully aware of the risk. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the action, considered in the round, appears less courageous than foolhardy. The risk is so excessive in relation to the likely benefit that it seems irrational to proceed. Aristotle, one might recall, placed courage as a mean between cowardice and rashness, and the mountaineer's action falls on the side of rashness. The definition, by requiring only that the risk be significant and the end worthy, imposes no constraint on the proportion between the two, and thereby admits as courageous actions that a more discriminating moral vocabulary would classify as recklessly self-destructive.

definition: An action X performed by an agent A is benevolent if and only if (i) X is intended to promote the well-being of another person B, (ii) X does in fact promote B's well-being, and (iii) A performs X without expectation of personal gain.
conditions: X is intended to promote the well-being of another
X does in fact promote their well-being
A acts without expectation of personal gain
counterexample: Consider a wealthy patron who, motivated entirely by concern for a young artist's welfare and expecting nothing in return, finances the artist's training at a prestigious academy. The patron intends to promote the artist's well-being; the training does in fact advance the artist's career and happiness; and the patron expects no personal gain. All three conditions are met. Yet the patron, in his eagerness to help, has chosen the academy without consulting the artist, who would have preferred a different path altogether — perhaps a quieter apprenticeship in a provincial workshop. The patron's action has promoted the artist's well-being as measured by external criteria, but it has overridden the artist's own wishes and judgement about what constitutes his well-being.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the benefactor's action be guided by the recipient's own understanding of his well-being, and thereby counts as benevolent actions that, however well-intended and externally beneficial, impose the benefactor's conception of the good upon the person they purport to help.
passage: One might define benevolence as action intended to promote another's well-being, which does in fact promote it, and which is performed without expectation of personal gain. This definition identifies the core moral features of the benevolent act: altruistic motive, beneficial result, and the absence of self-interest. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of patronage. Suppose a wealthy patron, motivated entirely by concern for a young artist's welfare and expecting nothing whatever in return, finances the artist's training at a prestigious academy. The training proves beneficial by all external measures: the artist acquires technical mastery, gains recognition, and achieves a level of material comfort he would not otherwise have attained. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the patron chose the academy without consulting the artist, who had expressed a preference for an entirely different mode of training — a quiet apprenticeship in a provincial workshop, closer to the traditions that inspired him. The artist's external well-being has been promoted, but his own understanding of what would make his life go well has been overridden. One might observe that benevolence, properly understood, involves attending to the recipient's own conception of his good, and that an action which imposes the benefactor's judgement about what is best for another person, however sincerely motivated and however beneficial in its outward effects, partakes more of paternalism than of genuine benevolence. The definition, by requiring only that well-being be promoted, takes no account of whose conception of well-being is being served.

definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act in a manner that accords with practical reason, (ii) the exercise of T characteristically promotes the flourishing of the agent who possesses it, and (iii) T is acquired through habitual practice rather than present from birth.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act in a manner that accords with practical reason
The exercise of T characteristically promotes the flourishing of the agent who possesses it
T is acquired through habitual practice rather than present from birth
counterexample: Consider the case of a soldier whose courage in battle is the product not of habitual practice and rational cultivation but of a congenital insensitivity to fear, owing to a rare neurological condition. The soldier acts with perfect steadiness under fire, and this steadiness is a stable disposition that accords with practical reason and promotes his flourishing. All three conditions appear to be met, since we may imagine that the disposition is indistinguishable from habitual courage in its outward manifestation. Yet one might observe that the soldier has never chosen to be courageous; his composure is a biological given rather than an achievement of character.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent has cultivated T through voluntary effort and rational self-governance, so that dispositions arising from mere biological accident may count as virtues.
passage: One might attempt to characterise virtue as a stable disposition to act in accordance with practical reason, one that promotes the flourishing of its possessor and is acquired through habitual practice. This proposal captures much of what the Aristotelian tradition has regarded as essential to virtue: the role of reason, the connection to human flourishing, and the importance of habituation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose a soldier displays unfailing courage in battle, and suppose that this courage is a stable disposition to act as practical reason dictates in the face of danger. Suppose further that his composure promotes his flourishing, earning him the esteem of his comrades and enabling him to discharge his duties. Yet suppose that this courage arises not from any process of habituation but from a congenital insensitivity to fear, attributable to a rare neurological condition. He has never faced the inner struggle of mastering fear through repeated practice. One might observe that the third condition — acquisition through habitual practice — is technically violated, yet the case presses harder than this: it forces us to ask whether a disposition that results from biological fortune, however beneficial, possesses the moral significance we attribute to virtue. It appears that the definition, by attending to stability, rationality, and flourishing, does not adequately distinguish between dispositions that are morally achieved and those that are merely naturally endowed.

definition: A distribution of goods D is just if and only if (i) D allocates goods in proportion to each person's contribution to the common enterprise, (ii) D is arrived at through a procedure to which all affected parties have consented, and (iii) no person receives less than is necessary for subsistence.
conditions: D allocates goods in proportion to each person's contribution to the common enterprise
D is arrived at through a procedure to which all affected parties have consented
No person receives less than is necessary for subsistence
counterexample: Consider a society in which the severely disabled, who are unable to contribute to the common enterprise through no fault of their own, receive goods in proportion to their contribution — which is nil. The distribution is arrived at through a consented procedure, and every person receives enough for subsistence. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this distribution just, for it assigns no share of the surplus to those whose incapacity is involuntary, and it treats the disabled as having no claim upon the community beyond bare survival.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that D make provision for persons whose inability to contribute is involuntary, so that the blameless incapacitated may be denied any share of goods beyond subsistence.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a distribution of goods is just when it rewards contribution, rests upon consent, and ensures that no one falls below subsistence. This tripartite criterion captures several intuitions that have a long pedigree in political philosophy: the connection between labour and reward, the importance of procedural legitimacy, and the floor of basic need. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers persons who are severely disabled from birth. Such persons may be unable to contribute to the common enterprise through no fault of their own. Under the proposed definition, they would receive goods in proportion to their contribution, which amounts to nothing beyond the subsistence minimum. The procedure was consented to, and subsistence is guaranteed. Nevertheless, one might observe that justice seems to demand more than bare survival for those who bear no responsibility for their incapacity. A distribution that confines the involuntarily disabled to the lowest rung, while the able-bodied enjoy abundant surplus, offends the sense of fairness that the definition was designed to capture. It appears that proportionality to contribution, while sound in cases where all parties possess roughly equal capacities, cannot serve as the sole distributive principle when capacities differ through no voluntary action.

definition: An agent A has a moral obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) X is within A's power to perform, (ii) failure to perform X would result in serious harm to another person, and (iii) performing X would not impose an unreasonable burden upon A.
conditions: X is within A's power to perform
Failure to perform X would result in serious harm to another person
Performing X would not impose an unreasonable burden upon A
counterexample: Consider a wealthy passer-by who sees a stranger's hat blow into a river. Retrieving the hat is within his power, failing to retrieve it would result in serious financial harm to the stranger (for whom the hat represents a month's wages), and the retrieval would impose no unreasonable burden. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to say the passer-by has a moral obligation to retrieve the hat, for the harm in question, though serious to the stranger, is of a kind — loss of property — that does not ordinarily generate obligations upon strangers.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the kind of harm that generates obligation, so that trivial or merely financial harms may trigger the same duties as threats to life or bodily integrity.
passage: One might propose that a moral obligation arises whenever an agent can prevent serious harm to another without unreasonable cost to himself. This proposal captures a widely shared moral intuition: we feel that we ought to rescue a drowning child if we can do so at little risk. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be constructed as follows. A wealthy gentleman is walking beside a river when a gust of wind carries a stranger's hat into the current. The hat is of considerable value to the stranger — it represents, let us suppose, a full month's wages. Retrieving it is well within the gentleman's power and would impose no unreasonable burden; he need only extend a walking stick. The failure to act would result in what is, for the stranger, a serious financial loss. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we do not ordinarily regard the loss of a hat, however costly to its owner, as the sort of harm that places a moral obligation upon a passing stranger. The definition, by speaking of serious harm without qualification, conflates harms of very different kinds. It appears that the account, by attending only to the magnitude of harm and the ease of prevention, cannot distinguish between the kinds of harm that generate genuine obligations and those that do not.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) the interest is of sufficient importance to impose a duty upon others to respect or provide X, and (iii) the duty is recognised by the moral community to which P belongs.
conditions: P has a legitimate interest in X
The interest is of sufficient importance to impose a duty upon others to respect or provide X
The duty is recognised by the moral community to which P belongs
counterexample: Consider a society in the antebellum American South where enslaved persons have a legitimate interest in their own liberty, and this interest is of the highest importance. Yet the moral community of that society does not recognise any duty to respect the liberty of the enslaved. All three conditions are not met only because the third fails, and the definition therefore implies that the enslaved have no right to freedom. Yet one might observe that this is precisely the sort of case in which we should wish to say that a right exists despite the community's refusal to recognise it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between rights that depend upon social recognition and rights that hold independently of whether any particular community recognises them, so that the most urgent moral claims may be denied simply because prevailing opinion refuses to honour them.
passage: It has sometimes been supposed that a person has a right to something when he has a legitimate interest of sufficient importance to impose a duty upon others, and that duty is recognised by his moral community. This account has the virtue of connecting rights to interests and duties, and of grounding both in the social practices that give them practical force. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is readily constructed. Consider an enslaved person in a society where the moral community refuses to recognise any duty to respect his liberty. He has a legitimate interest in freedom, and that interest is of the greatest importance. Yet the third condition — community recognition — fails. The definition therefore implies that he has no right to liberty. One might observe that this conclusion is deeply unsatisfactory. If rights exist only when recognised, then the most egregious injustices — those in which an entire community conspires to deny what is owed — become invisible to the moral vocabulary. It appears that the definition, by requiring community recognition as a necessary condition, cannot accommodate the category of rights that exist precisely in defiance of prevailing social norms.

definition: A person P deserves outcome O if and only if (i) P has performed an action A that is relevantly connected to O, (ii) A was performed voluntarily and with full awareness of its nature, and (iii) O is proportionate to the merit or demerit of A.
conditions: P has performed an action A that is relevantly connected to O
A was performed voluntarily and with full awareness of its nature
O is proportionate to the merit or demerit of A
counterexample: Consider a musician who practises diligently for years, performs voluntarily and with full awareness, and whose resultant skill merits a prize at a competition. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet on the day of the competition she is surpassed by a prodigy who has practised far less but possesses an extraordinary natural gift. One might observe that the prodigy's victory feels undeserved — not because the prodigy did anything wrong, but because the outcome is determined largely by unchosen talent rather than by voluntary effort.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the connection between the action and the outcome be free from the distorting influence of unearned natural advantages, so that outcomes heavily determined by innate talent may count as deserved even when effort plays a minor role.
passage: One might characterise desert as follows: a person deserves an outcome when she has performed a voluntary and informed action relevantly connected to it, and the outcome is proportionate to the action's merit. This proposal captures the common intuition that reward ought to follow effort and that punishment ought to follow wrongdoing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises in the domain of competitive achievement. Suppose a musician practises her instrument with great diligence over many years, performing always voluntarily and with full awareness of her efforts. Her resultant skill is considerable, and the prize at a competition would be proportionate to the merit of her dedication. She satisfies all three conditions. Nevertheless, she is surpassed on the day by a prodigy whose natural gifts far exceed her own and who has practised comparatively little. The prodigy's victory satisfies the conditions as well — the prodigy has performed voluntary actions relevantly connected to the prize. Yet one might observe that the outcome is determined overwhelmingly by unchosen natural talent rather than by voluntary effort. It appears that the definition, by requiring only voluntariness and proportionality, cannot distinguish outcomes that track genuine effort from those that reward the sheer accident of natural endowment.

definition: A person P has validly consented to an arrangement R if and only if (i) P has expressed agreement to R through words or conduct, (ii) P was not subject to coercion or fraud at the time of expression, and (iii) P possessed the mental capacity to understand the terms of R.
conditions: P has expressed agreement to R through words or conduct
P was not subject to coercion or fraud at the time of expression
P possessed the mental capacity to understand the terms of R
counterexample: Consider a traveller stranded in a remote area who agrees to pay an exorbitant price for water from the only vendor available. The traveller expresses agreement, is not subject to coercion in any narrow sense — no one threatens him with violence — and possesses full mental capacity. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the traveller's consent is given under conditions of extreme necessity that leave him with no genuine alternative, and it appears doubtful that we should regard such consent as truly voluntary.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P possess a reasonable range of alternatives at the time of consenting, so that agreements extracted under conditions of desperation or monopoly may count as valid consent.
passage: One might propose that valid consent consists in the uncoerced, informed expression of agreement by a person of sound mind. This proposal is attractive in its simplicity and captures the conditions most frequently cited in discussions of contractual and moral consent. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Suppose a traveller is stranded in a desert and encounters the only vendor of water within a day's march. The vendor demands a price equal to the traveller's entire savings. The traveller, understanding the terms perfectly and subject to no threat of violence, agrees to pay. He satisfies all three conditions: he expresses agreement, is not coerced in the standard sense, and possesses full mental capacity. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a case of valid consent. The traveller's situation is one of such extreme necessity that his so-called agreement is extracted under conditions leaving him no genuine choice. It appears that the definition, by defining coercion narrowly as the use or threat of force, cannot capture the more subtle unfreedom that arises from situational desperation and the absence of alternatives.

definition: The infliction of a penalty upon a person P is morally justified if and only if (i) P has committed an act that violates a moral norm, (ii) the penalty is proportionate to the severity of the violation, and (iii) the penalty is imposed by a legitimate authority.
conditions: P has committed an act that violates a moral norm
The penalty is proportionate to the severity of the violation
The penalty is imposed by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Consider a political regime that enacts a law forbidding public criticism of the sovereign. A citizen criticises the sovereign and is punished by a fine proportionate to the gravity assigned to the offence by the regime. The citizen has violated a legal norm that the regime regards as a moral norm, the penalty is proportionate by the regime's own reckoning, and it is imposed by the recognised authority. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this punishment morally justified, for the underlying norm itself — that citizens may not speak freely — is morally objectionable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the norm whose violation occasions the penalty be itself morally legitimate, so that punishment for violating an unjust law may satisfy the conditions while remaining morally unjustified.
passage: A natural account of justified punishment holds that the infliction of a penalty is morally warranted when the offender has violated a moral norm, the penalty is proportionate, and it is imposed by a legitimate authority. This account captures the retributive intuition that punishment must be deserved and the institutional intuition that it must proceed from proper authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the context of political repression. Suppose a regime enacts a law prohibiting public criticism of the sovereign and regards this prohibition as a moral norm essential to social order. A citizen speaks critically of the sovereign and is fined an amount that, by the regime's own standards, is proportionate to the offence. The penalty is imposed by the duly constituted authority. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the punishment is morally unjustified, for the underlying norm is itself morally illegitimate. The right to express one's views on political matters is, one might argue, among the most fundamental of moral entitlements. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that a moral norm has been violated without examining whether the norm is itself defensible, permits the justification of punishments that serve to enforce tyranny.

definition: A person P is morally bound to keep a promise to Q if and only if (i) P has freely made an explicit undertaking to Q to perform some action, (ii) Q has relied upon the undertaking in forming his plans, and (iii) the performance of the promised action remains within P's power.
conditions: P has freely made an explicit undertaking to Q to perform some action
Q has relied upon the undertaking in forming his plans
The performance of the promised action remains within P's power
counterexample: Consider a person who promises a friend to attend a social gathering. The friend relies upon this promise and makes arrangements accordingly. Attending remains within the promisor's power. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose that, between the making of the promise and the date of the gathering, the promisor discovers that a stranger is in urgent need of assistance — say, a neighbour has fallen gravely ill and requires someone to drive him to hospital. One might observe that the moral force of the promise to attend the gathering is overridden by the urgent need, yet none of the three conditions has ceased to obtain.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition permitting the moral force of the promise to be overridden when a competing moral obligation of substantially greater urgency arises, so that every promise must be kept regardless of the moral cost of performance.
passage: One might propose that a person is morally bound to keep a promise when the undertaking was freely made, the promisee has relied upon it, and performance remains possible. This account reflects the widely held conviction that promises generate obligations and that these obligations are strengthened when others act in reliance upon them. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man promises a friend that he will attend a dinner party on Saturday evening. The friend, relying upon the promise, purchases provisions and arranges the seating. Saturday arrives, and attendance remains well within the promisor's power. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that, on Saturday afternoon, the promisor discovers that his neighbour has suffered a serious fall and requires immediate assistance to reach hospital. No other person is available to help. One might observe that in these circumstances the promisor's obligation to assist his neighbour overrides his obligation to attend the dinner — and yet none of the three conditions of the definition has ceased to hold. The definition, by specifying only the conditions under which a promise binds, makes no provision for the possibility that the obligation may be overridden by a more pressing moral claim.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) A's action was a cause of O, (ii) A acted voluntarily and was not compelled by external force, and (iii) A was aware, or should have been aware, that his action could lead to O.
conditions: A's action was a cause of O
A acted voluntarily and was not compelled by external force
A was aware, or should have been aware, that his action could lead to O
counterexample: Consider a chemist who voluntarily mixes two common reagents, fully aware that the combination produces a mild effervescence. Unknown to the chemist, and unknown to the entire scientific community at the time, the particular batch of one reagent contains an impurity that, when combined with the other, releases a toxic gas. A laboratory assistant is harmed. The chemist's action was a cause of the harm, the chemist acted voluntarily, and one might argue that the chemist should have been aware of the risk. Yet the impurity was undetectable by any method available at the time, and no reasonable person could have foreseen the result.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the foreseeability of O be assessed relative to what could reasonably have been discovered given the state of available methods, so that agents may be held responsible for consequences that no one could have anticipated.
passage: It is tempting to hold that an agent is morally responsible for an outcome whenever his voluntary action caused it and he was or should have been aware that such an outcome could follow. This account captures the central elements of responsibility — causation, voluntariness, and foreseeability — and suffices for many ordinary cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the experimental sciences. Suppose a chemist voluntarily combines two common reagents, fully aware that the mixture produces a mild and harmless effervescence. The reaction has been performed thousands of times without incident. But suppose that one batch of a reagent contains an impurity — undetectable by any available analytical method — that causes the release of a toxic gas. A laboratory assistant is harmed. The chemist's action was a cause of the harm. The action was entirely voluntary. And one might argue, invoking the 'should have been aware' clause, that the chemist bore a duty to consider all possible outcomes. Yet it appears unreasonable to hold the chemist responsible for a consequence that no one in the profession could have foreseen, given the state of analytical chemistry at the time. The definition, by requiring only that the agent should have been aware, without specifying the standard of foreseeability, permits the attribution of responsibility in cases where no reasonable diligence could have revealed the danger.

definition: Two agents A and B are equally morally culpable if and only if (i) A and B performed the same type of action with the same intention, (ii) A and B exercised the same degree of care, and (iii) A and B acted under relevantly similar circumstances.
conditions: A and B performed the same type of action with the same intention
A and B exercised the same degree of care
A and B acted under relevantly similar circumstances
counterexample: Consider two motorists who both drive through a residential area at the same excessive speed, with the same intention to arrive promptly, exercising the same (insufficient) degree of care, under relevantly similar circumstances. One motorist arrives safely because no pedestrian happens to cross the road; the other strikes and injures a child who steps out from behind a parked vehicle. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition declares them equally culpable. Yet one might observe that the law and ordinary moral sentiment hold the second motorist far more culpable than the first, precisely because of the harm that resulted.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role that the actual consequences of an action play in our assessment of culpability, so that it cannot accommodate the widely felt moral difference between an agent whose recklessness causes harm and one whose identical recklessness, by good fortune, does not.
passage: One might propose that two agents are equally culpable when they perform the same action with the same intention, exercise the same care, and act under similar circumstances. This proposal embodies the plausible principle that moral assessment should track what is within the agent's control — his choices, intentions, and precautions — rather than what fortune determines. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this principle is easily constructed. Suppose two motorists drive through a residential quarter at identical excessive speeds, with identical intentions and identical degrees of carelessness. The circumstances are relevantly similar in every respect. Yet one motorist passes through safely because no pedestrian enters the road, while the other strikes and gravely injures a child. All three conditions of the definition are met, and the definition accordingly pronounces the two motorists equally culpable. Nevertheless, one might observe that we do not in practice regard them as morally identical. The motorist who injures the child bears a weight of culpability — and of remorse — that the fortunate motorist escapes. It appears that the definition, by excluding the actual consequences of the act from the assessment of culpability, cannot capture the moral significance that we in fact attach to outcomes, even when those outcomes lie beyond the agent's control.

definition: An action is benevolent if and only if (i) the agent intends to benefit another person, (ii) the action in fact produces a benefit for that person, and (iii) the agent derives no personal advantage from the action.
conditions: The agent intends to benefit another person
The action in fact produces a benefit for that person
The agent derives no personal advantage from the action
counterexample: A philanthropist donates a large sum to a hospital, intending genuinely to benefit the sick. The donation does benefit patients. Yet the philanthropist also derives considerable personal advantage: a tax reduction and enhanced public reputation. All three conditions except the third are met, and the third fails only because the agent happens to benefit as well. Yet we should not deny that the action is benevolent merely because it incidentally serves the agent's interests.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between actions whose primary motive is the benefit of another and actions undertaken for self-interest, requiring instead the impossibly strict condition that no personal advantage whatever attach to the act.
passage: One might propose that an action is benevolent if and only if the agent intends to benefit another, the action produces a benefit, and the agent derives no personal advantage. This characterisation captures the altruistic core of benevolence and excludes the merely self-interested. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises with conspicuous philanthropy. Consider a wealthy man who donates a substantial sum to a public hospital, moved genuinely by the suffering of the sick. The donation funds a new ward, and patients benefit materially. Yet the donor receives, as a consequence of his generosity, a significant reduction in his tax liability and a marked improvement in his public standing. The first two conditions are satisfied: the intention was beneficent, and the benefit was real. But the third condition fails, for the agent has derived personal advantage. The definition would therefore classify the action as not benevolent. One might observe, however, that we should hesitate to deny the benevolence of an action whose primary motive was the good of others merely because it also, as an incidental consequence, served the agent's interests. It appears that the definition, by requiring the complete absence of personal advantage, sets a standard that virtually no human action can satisfy, since even the most selfless benefactor may derive some satisfaction from his own generosity.

definition: An action is cruel if and only if (i) the action causes suffering to another sentient being, (ii) the agent is aware that the action will cause suffering, and (iii) the agent takes pleasure in the suffering caused.
conditions: The action causes suffering to another sentient being
The agent is aware that the action will cause suffering
The agent takes pleasure in the suffering caused
counterexample: A factory owner, aware that reducing wages will cause hardship to his workers, proceeds with the reduction not from any pleasure in their suffering but from a cold indifference to it, motivated solely by the desire to increase profits. The action causes suffering, the agent is aware of this, yet the agent takes no pleasure in it whatever. The definition would classify the action as not cruel, yet one might observe that the owner's callous disregard for the suffering he knowingly inflicts is precisely what we ordinarily mean by cruelty.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that cruelty may consist not only in the enjoyment of suffering but also in a wilful indifference to the suffering one knowingly causes, so that the coldly calculating agent escapes the charge of cruelty.
passage: One might attempt to define cruelty as the knowing infliction of suffering accompanied by pleasure in the suffering caused. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases of the torturer and the bully, who delight in the pain they produce. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from commercial life. Consider a factory owner who, in pursuit of greater profit, reduces the wages of his workers to a level that he is fully aware will cause them and their families real hardship. He takes no pleasure in their suffering; indeed, he gives it no thought at all. His motive is purely pecuniary. The first two conditions are satisfied: suffering is caused, and the agent is aware of it. But the third fails, for the owner derives no enjoyment from the workers' distress. The definition would therefore deny that the action is cruel. Yet one might observe that what makes the factory owner's conduct cruel is not the presence of sadistic enjoyment but the callous disregard for suffering that he knows his action will produce. It appears that the definition, by making pleasure in suffering a necessary condition, confines cruelty to the sadistic and thereby excludes the coldly indifferent infliction of pain, which is perhaps the more common and the more insidious form.

definition: A person is honest if and only if (i) the person does not assert what he believes to be false, (ii) the person does not conceal information that the hearer has a right to receive, and (iii) the person's assertions are made with the intention of conveying the truth.
conditions: The person does not assert what he believes to be false
The person does not conceal information that the hearer has a right to receive
The person's assertions are made with the intention of conveying the truth
counterexample: A witness in a dispute states only truths and conceals nothing the tribunal has a right to know, yet he arranges his true statements in a sequence calculated to create a misleading impression. Each sentence is true; no relevant fact is withheld; the intention behind each individual assertion is to convey something true. Yet the cumulative effect is that the hearer forms a false picture of what occurred. All three conditions are satisfied, yet one might observe that the witness is not honest.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the overall impression created by the person's assertions be veridical, so that a person may satisfy every condition while systematically misleading through the artful arrangement of truths.
passage: One might define honesty as the absence of false assertion and concealment, combined with the intention to convey the truth. This definition captures the central prohibitions — against lying and against withholding what is owed — and adds an intentional element. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the phenomenon of misleading truth-telling. Suppose a witness before a tribunal recounts only truths and conceals nothing that the tribunal has a right to know. Each sentence he utters is accurate, and each is offered with the intention of stating what is true. Yet the witness has arranged his statements in a sequence calculated to suggest a false chronology, creating the impression that event A preceded event B when in fact B preceded A. The tribunal forms a mistaken picture of the matter. Every individual condition of the definition is satisfied: no falsehood is asserted, no relevant information is concealed, and each assertion is intended to convey something true. Nevertheless, one should hesitate to call the witness honest. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the truth of individual assertions and the absence of concealment, cannot capture the requirement that the totality of one's communication not be designed to mislead. Honesty, it seems, demands not merely that each part be true but that the whole convey the truth.

definition: A person P is loyal to a person or institution Q if and only if (i) P maintains a steady allegiance to Q over time, (ii) P does not act to undermine Q's interests, and (iii) P continues to support Q even when doing so is inconvenient or costly.
conditions: P maintains a steady allegiance to Q over time
P does not act to undermine Q's interests
P continues to support Q even when doing so is inconvenient or costly
counterexample: Consider an officer in a corrupt regiment who maintains steady allegiance to the regiment, never acts against its interests, and continues to support it at personal cost, even as the regiment engages in systematic brutality against civilians. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that this is not loyalty in any morally creditable sense but complicity, for the officer's unwavering support enables wrongdoing.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object of loyalty be worthy of allegiance, or that loyalty be constrained by independent moral limits, so that steadfast devotion to an evil cause may count as loyalty.
passage: One might attempt to characterise loyalty as the maintenance of steady allegiance over time, the refusal to undermine the interests of the object of allegiance, and the willingness to bear cost in its support. This proposal captures the temporal stability, the protective orientation, and the sacrificial dimension of loyalty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from military life. Consider an officer serving in a regiment that has become systematically corrupt, engaging in the mistreatment of civilians. The officer maintains unwavering allegiance to the regiment, never acts against its interests, and continues to support it at considerable personal cost — declining transfer, defending its reputation, and covering for its misdeeds. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to commend this as genuine loyalty. It appears rather to be a form of complicity, in which steadfastness of allegiance has become an instrument of wrongdoing. One might observe that the definition, by omitting any moral constraint upon the object of allegiance, cannot distinguish the loyalty that is a virtue from the loyalty that is a vice. Genuine loyalty, it seems, must be qualified by some requirement that the object of allegiance be not wholly unworthy of it.

definition: An action is courageous if and only if (i) the agent faces a significant risk of harm, (ii) the agent acts despite awareness of that risk, and (iii) the action is undertaken voluntarily rather than under compulsion.
conditions: The agent faces a significant risk of harm
The agent acts despite awareness of that risk
The action is undertaken voluntarily rather than under compulsion
counterexample: A reckless youth, seeking a thrill, voluntarily climbs a crumbling bridge in full awareness of the danger. He faces a significant risk, acts despite that awareness, and does so voluntarily. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the action is not courageous but foolhardy, for the risk is undertaken not for any worthy end but merely for the sake of excitement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the risk be undertaken for a worthy purpose or in the service of some good, so that reckless thrill-seeking may be classified as courageous.
passage: One might define courage as the voluntary acceptance of significant risk in full awareness of the danger. This analysis captures three features commonly associated with courage: the presence of genuine peril, the agent's conscious confrontation of it, and the absence of coercion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be easily constructed. Consider a youth who, in search of excitement, voluntarily scales the rusting girders of a derelict railway bridge. He is fully aware that the structure may collapse beneath him. He acts without compulsion. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we do not commend this as courage but condemn it as recklessness. The difference between the courageous soldier who advances under fire and the reckless youth who climbs a collapsing bridge lies not in the presence of risk or the voluntariness of action but in the purpose for which the risk is borne. The soldier risks his life for the protection of others or the discharge of duty; the youth risks his for nothing more than a passing thrill. It appears that the definition, by attending only to risk and voluntariness, cannot distinguish courage from recklessness, and that a further condition — that the risk be undertaken for a worthy end — is required.

definition: A person P has a duty to perform action X if and only if (i) a moral rule requires P to perform X, (ii) P is capable of performing X, and (iii) failure to perform X would constitute a violation of the moral rule.
conditions: A moral rule requires P to perform X
P is capable of performing X
Failure to perform X would constitute a violation of the moral rule
counterexample: Consider a moral rule requiring that one repay one's debts. A man owes money to a friend, and repaying the debt is within his means. However, the friend has recently been kidnapped and is being held for ransom; repaying the debt would provide the kidnappers with funds to continue their criminal enterprise. All three conditions are met: the moral rule requires repayment, the man is capable, and failure to repay would violate the rule. Yet one might observe that the man should not repay under these circumstances, for doing so would facilitate serious harm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the performance of duty not conflict with a weightier moral consideration, so that duties may be discharged in circumstances where their fulfilment would produce grave harm.
passage: One might characterise duty as the requirement, imposed by a moral rule, that a capable agent perform a specified action, such that failure would constitute a violation. This proposal captures the obligatoriness and action-guiding force of duty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when duties come into conflict with other moral considerations. Suppose a man owes a sum of money to a friend and is fully capable of repaying it. A moral rule — that one should repay one's debts — requires repayment, and failure to pay would constitute a violation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the friend has been kidnapped and is being held for ransom, and that the money, if repaid, would be seized by the kidnappers to finance further criminal activity. One might observe that the man should not repay the debt under these circumstances, for doing so would facilitate serious wrongdoing. The definition, by attending solely to the requirements of a single moral rule, cannot accommodate the possibility that a genuine duty may be overridden by a weightier moral consideration. It appears that the definition treats duties as indefeasible when they are in fact defeasible — subject to being outweighed by competing moral demands.

definition: A person A has genuinely forgiven wrongdoer B if and only if (i) B has wronged A, (ii) A has relinquished all desire for retribution against B, and (iii) B has expressed remorse for the wrong.
conditions: B has wronged A
A has relinquished all desire for retribution against B
B has expressed remorse for the wrong
counterexample: Consider a victim of a serious wrong whose offender has died without ever expressing remorse. The victim, after years of reflection, relinquishes all desire for retribution and arrives at a state of genuine peace regarding the offence. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but (iii) is not, since the offender expressed no remorse. The definition would deny that forgiveness has occurred. Yet one might observe that the victim's moral achievement is precisely what we mean by forgiveness, regardless of the offender's attitude.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that forgiveness is fundamentally a change in the victim's moral stance rather than a transaction requiring the wrongdoer's participation, so that forgiveness of the unremorseful or the dead is excluded.
passage: One might define forgiveness as requiring that the victim relinquish retributive desire in response to the wrongdoer's remorse. This characterisation captures what might be called the transactional model of forgiveness: the offender apologises, and the victim lets go of anger. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this model arises when the wrongdoer is unavailable. Consider a man who was gravely wronged by a colleague many years ago. The colleague has since died without ever acknowledging the wrong. The man, through a sustained effort of moral reflection, arrives at a state in which he no longer harbours any desire for retribution. He regards the wrong with equanimity and bears no malice towards the memory of the deceased. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met: he was wronged, and he has relinquished all retributive desire. Yet condition (iii) fails, for the wrongdoer never expressed remorse. The definition would hold that forgiveness has not occurred. One might observe, however, that the man's achievement is a paradigmatic instance of what we call forgiveness — indeed, forgiving the unremorseful dead may represent a deeper moral accomplishment than forgiving the penitent living. It appears that the definition, by requiring the wrongdoer's remorse as a necessary condition, misconceives forgiveness as a bilateral transaction rather than a unilateral transformation of the victim's moral stance.

definition: A person is morally responsible for the consequences of an action if and only if (i) the person performed the action voluntarily, (ii) the person could foresee the consequences, and (iii) the consequences were the intended result of the action.
conditions: The person performed the action voluntarily
The person could foresee the consequences
The consequences were the intended result of the action
counterexample: A physician administers a standard dose of a well-tested medication to a patient. The action is voluntary and performed with the intention of healing. The physician foresees the standard effects. Yet the patient suffers a rare and unforeseeable allergic reaction and dies. Conditions (i) and (ii) are partially met — the action was voluntary and the standard consequences foreseeable — but condition (iii) fails entirely, for the death was not the intended result. Nevertheless, one might observe that we do, in certain contexts, hold the physician to some degree of moral responsibility for the outcome, especially if the possibility of rare reactions ought to have prompted further enquiry.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for responsibility attaching to unintended but reasonably foreseeable consequences, so that agents who produce harmful side-effects through voluntary action may escape moral scrutiny entirely.
passage: One might propose that a person is morally responsible for the consequences of an action when those consequences are the intended result of a voluntary action whose effects the agent could foresee. This definition makes intention central and thereby restricts responsibility to outcomes that the agent actively sought to produce. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from medical practice. A physician voluntarily administers a well-tested medication to a patient, foreseeing the therapeutic effects and intending to heal. The patient suffers a rare allergic reaction and dies. The death was not the intended result; the physician sought to cure, not to harm. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. The definition would therefore absolve the physician entirely. Yet one might observe that if the possibility of allergic reactions, however rare, was a recognised risk that the physician could have investigated further or warned about, we should not be wholly content to say that no moral responsibility attaches. It appears that the definition, by making intention a necessary condition, confines responsibility to the sphere of the deliberately sought and thereby permits agents to evade scrutiny for consequences that, though unintended, were within the penumbra of what a careful person might have anticipated.

definition: An action is supererogatory if and only if (i) it goes beyond what any moral rule requires, (ii) it is morally praiseworthy, and (iii) omitting it would incur no moral blame.
conditions: It goes beyond what any moral rule requires
It is morally praiseworthy
Omitting it would incur no moral blame
counterexample: A man voluntarily donates a kidney to a stranger, going beyond what any moral rule requires. The action is morally praiseworthy. Omitting it would incur no blame. All three conditions are met. Yet after the donation the man publicly demands gratitude, parades his sacrifice, and uses it to extract favours from others for years afterward. One might observe that the self-aggrandising aftermath taints our assessment of the action's moral quality, even though the act itself satisfies every condition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's motive be at least partially altruistic, so that actions performed solely for the purpose of self-glorification may qualify as supererogatory provided they meet the structural conditions.
passage: One might characterise supererogatory action as that which exceeds duty, is morally praiseworthy, and would incur no blame if omitted. This proposal captures the three features most commonly cited in discussions of supererogation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one attends to the agent's motive. Suppose a man donates a kidney to a complete stranger. No moral rule demands such a sacrifice; its omission would incur no blame; and the act is, on its face, morally praiseworthy. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose further that the man undertakes the donation entirely for the purpose of self-glorification — he arranges extensive publicity, demands public gratitude, and uses the sacrifice as a means of extracting social advantages for years to come. One might observe that while the act itself remains beneficial to the recipient, the agent's exclusive motive of self-aggrandisement disturbs our willingness to call the action supererogatory in the full moral sense. It appears that the definition, by requiring only structural features — exceeding duty, meriting praise, incurring no blame if omitted — cannot distinguish the genuinely supererogatory from the ostentatiously self-serving, and that some reference to the quality of the agent's motive is needed.

definition: A punishment is just if and only if (i) the offender committed the offence for which the punishment is imposed, (ii) the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence, and (iii) the punishment is imposed by a duly constituted authority.
conditions: The offender committed the offence for which the punishment is imposed
The punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence
The punishment is imposed by a duly constituted authority
counterexample: Consider a man convicted of theft under a legal system that, though duly constituted, systematically denies persons of his social class the right to present a defence. The man did commit the theft, and the punishment is proportionate. The authority is duly constituted by the laws of the land. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the punishment is not just, for the process by which it was arrived at was fundamentally unfair.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the punishment be arrived at through a procedure that affords the offender a fair opportunity to contest the charge, so that punishments imposed through unjust processes may qualify as just.
passage: One might propose that a punishment is just when it is imposed by a duly constituted authority upon an offender who committed the offence, and the punishment is proportionate to the offence. This account captures the retributive core of just punishment: that the guilty be punished, that the punishment fit the crime, and that it proceed from legitimate authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises when the procedure leading to punishment is itself defective. Consider a society in which the legal system is duly constituted — courts exist, judges are appointed, statutes are enacted — but in which persons of a particular social class are systematically denied the opportunity to speak in their own defence. A man of that class commits a theft and is convicted without being permitted to offer any explanation or present witnesses. The punishment is proportionate. The authority is legitimate. He did commit the offence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the punishment is not just, for it was reached through a procedure that denied the accused a fair hearing. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the act, the proportionality, and the authority, overlooks the requirement that the procedure itself must be fair if the punishment is to be just.

definition: An action A performed by an agent S constitutes the fulfilment of a duty if and only if (i) S is under an obligation to perform A, (ii) S performs A voluntarily, and (iii) S performs A because S recognises the obligation to do so.
conditions: S is under an obligation to perform A
S performs A voluntarily
S performs A because S recognises the obligation to do so
counterexample: Consider a soldier who is under orders to stand watch through the night. He fully recognises his obligation and intends to fulfil it. However, a sleeping draught is secretly placed in his water, and he falls unconscious at his post. His commanding officer, unaware of the drugging, orders a subordinate to prop the soldier upright at the watchtower. The soldier remains technically present at his post through the night, but entirely without volition or awareness. He has not fulfilled his duty, despite appearances.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that S's performance of A be causally sustained by S's own agency throughout the relevant period — that the action not merely begin voluntarily but continue under S's control.
passage: One might propose that duty is fulfilled whenever an agent, recognising an obligation, voluntarily performs the required action. This characterisation has a certain intuitive appeal, for it captures the elements of obligation, volition, and moral motivation that we ordinarily associate with dutiful conduct. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed as follows. Suppose a soldier is ordered to stand watch through the night. He recognises the obligation and intends to comply. Unknown to him, a sleeping draught is placed in his water, rendering him unconscious shortly after taking his position. His commanding officer, unaware of the drugging, instructs a subordinate to prop the soldier upright at the watchtower, so that it appears the watch is being kept. The soldier is present at his post throughout the night; his initial positioning was voluntary; and he undertook the task because he recognised his obligation. All three conditions of our definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the soldier has fulfilled his duty. He has not maintained vigilance; he has not been in a position to raise the alarm; his presence at the post is sustained not by his own agency but by the actions of another. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the initiation of the action, fails to require that the agent's own volition sustain the performance throughout the period in which the duty demands it.

definition: An agent S displays courage in performing an action A if and only if (i) A involves a significant risk of harm to S, (ii) S is aware of the risk, and (iii) S performs A despite the risk in pursuit of some worthy end.
conditions: A involves a significant risk of harm to S
S is aware of the risk
S performs A despite the risk in pursuit of some worthy end
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who attempts a perilous ascent in winter conditions, fully aware that the route poses severe danger of frostbite and avalanche. She undertakes the climb to raise funds for a children's hospital — a worthy end by any reckoning. Yet she suffers from a neurological condition that renders her constitutionally incapable of experiencing fear. She proceeds with the same equanimity with which one might stroll through a garden. All three conditions are met, but one might question whether the term 'courage' properly applies to conduct that involves no inner resistance to be overcome.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S experience some degree of fear or apprehension in the face of the risk and act in spite of that affective response — that courage involve the mastery of a natural aversion rather than its mere absence.
passage: A natural proposal for the analysis of courage would hold that an agent displays courage when she knowingly undertakes a risky action in pursuit of a worthy end. This captures the features most commonly associated with courageous conduct: danger, awareness, and noble purpose. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be imagined without great difficulty. Suppose a mountaineer of considerable skill undertakes a winter ascent of a notoriously dangerous peak. She is fully informed of the risks — avalanche, exposure, and the failure of equipment are all realistic possibilities. Her purpose is admirable: she climbs to raise funds for a hospital serving impoverished children. However, owing to a rare neurological condition, she is constitutionally incapable of experiencing fear. The prospect of death or injury produces in her no more apprehension than the prospect of an afternoon's walk. She does not struggle against an impulse to retreat; she feels no tremor of anxiety to be mastered. All three conditions of our definition are fulfilled. Yet one might observe that something essential to courage appears to be absent. The ancient moralists were inclined to regard courage not as the mere absence of timidity but as the governance of fear by reason. If this is correct, then a definition that omits the requirement of affective resistance cannot distinguish between the truly courageous agent and one who is merely fearless by constitution.

definition: A statement S made by an agent A is honest if and only if (i) S accurately represents A's beliefs at the time of utterance, (ii) A intends that the hearer accept S as a sincere communication, and (iii) A does not deliberately withhold information that would materially alter the hearer's understanding of the matter.
conditions: S accurately represents A's beliefs at the time of utterance
A intends that the hearer accept S as a sincere communication
A does not deliberately withhold information that would materially alter the hearer's understanding of the matter
counterexample: Consider a physician who tells a patient that the results of a blood test are normal. The physician sincerely believes this, intends the patient to accept it as a truthful report, and withholds no information she considers material. Yet the physician has negligently failed to read the laboratory addendum, which flags an anomalous marker. The statement meets all three conditions but is based upon a culpably uninformed belief. The patient is misled, not by dishonesty of intention, but by a deficiency of care that the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's belief be formed with reasonable diligence — that the accuracy of the representation reflect not merely what the agent happens to believe but what the agent ought to have ascertained given the circumstances.
passage: One might characterise honesty in communication as the accurate representation of one's beliefs, coupled with sincere intent and the absence of material omission. This proposal seems to capture the core of what we mean when we call a statement honest: the speaker says what she believes, means it to be taken at face value, and does not suppress relevant truths. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a physician informs a patient that the results of a blood test are entirely normal. She sincerely believes this. She intends the patient to accept the statement as a faithful report. She withholds nothing that she regards as material. However, attached to the laboratory report is an addendum — clearly marked — that flags an anomalous marker requiring further investigation. The physician has failed to read this addendum, not from any intent to deceive, but from negligence. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the statement represents her beliefs, she is sincere in her communication, and she does not deliberately suppress information. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call this communication honest in the fullest sense. The patient has been misled — not by duplicity, but by a failure of diligence that is arguably incompatible with the moral demands of honest dealing. It appears that honesty requires not merely that one say what one believes, but that one's beliefs be responsibly formed.

definition: A distribution D of goods among a group of persons is just if and only if (i) D accords to each person what is proportionate to that person's contribution, (ii) the procedure by which D is determined is impartial, and (iii) no person is left without the means to satisfy basic needs.
conditions: D accords to each person what is proportionate to that person's contribution
The procedure by which D is determined is impartial
No person is left without the means to satisfy basic needs
counterexample: Consider a farming commune in which the harvest is distributed strictly in proportion to each member's labour, by an impartial committee, and with a guaranteed minimum that ensures no one goes hungry. A member who has been secretly coerced into signing away half her share under threat of violence receives only half the proportion her labour warrants, yet the coerced agreement is processed through the same impartial mechanism. The committee, unaware of the coercion, records the distribution as proportionate to her stated claim. All three conditions are met, yet the distribution is manifestly unjust.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that each person's participation in the distributive arrangement be free from coercion — that the proportionality condition reflect genuine voluntary agreement rather than a share determined under duress.
passage: A plausible account of distributive justice might hold that a distribution is just when it is proportionate to contribution, determined by impartial procedure, and ensures that basic needs are met. This formulation has the merit of combining desert, procedural fairness, and a humanitarian minimum. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be readily constructed. Suppose a farming commune distributes its harvest according to each member's labour, with an impartial committee overseeing the allocation and a guaranteed minimum for every member. One member, however, has been threatened with violence by another and coerced into signing a document relinquishing half her rightful share. The committee processes this document in good faith, recording the reduced share as consistent with her stated claim. The distribution is proportionate to stated contributions, the procedure is impartial as between persons, and the guaranteed minimum ensures she is not left destitute. All three conditions of the definition are fulfilled. Nevertheless, the distribution is unjust: it reflects not the free agreement of the parties but the exercise of illegitimate power. One might observe that the definition, by attending to proportionality and procedure, fails to require that the terms upon which each person participates be free from coercion. Without such a requirement, the appearance of justice may coexist with what is, in substance, exploitation.

definition: An agent S forgives an agent T for a wrong W if and only if (i) T has committed a wrong W against S, (ii) S relinquishes resentment toward T on account of W, and (iii) S does so in response to some feature of T or of the situation that S regards as a reason for overcoming resentment.
conditions: T has committed a wrong W against S
S relinquishes resentment toward T on account of W
S does so in response to some feature of T or of the situation that S regards as a reason for overcoming resentment
counterexample: Consider a woman who has been wronged by a former friend. She harbours deep resentment for several years. One morning she suffers a minor stroke that damages the region of the brain associated with the sustained experience of anger and resentment. She finds that she no longer feels resentment toward the friend and, upon reflection, regards the neurological change as a kind of liberation — a reason to move forward. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might doubt that the mere neurological extinction of an emotion, even when retrospectively endorsed, constitutes genuine forgiveness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the relinquishing of resentment be brought about through a deliberative process in which S actively chooses to overcome the emotion — that forgiveness be an exercise of moral agency rather than a passively undergone alteration of affect.
passage: One might analyse forgiveness as the relinquishing of resentment toward a wrongdoer in response to some reason the aggrieved party recognises as adequate. This proposal captures several features central to forgiveness: a prior wrong, the overcoming of a reactive attitude, and a rational basis for doing so. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be described as follows. Suppose a woman has been deeply wronged by a former friend and harbours intense resentment for several years.
