One morning she suffers a minor cerebrovascular event that damages the region of her brain responsible for sustaining feelings of anger and resentment. She discovers, upon recovering, that she simply no longer feels resentment toward the friend. Reflecting upon this change, she regards it as a welcome deliverance and considers the neurological alteration itself a reason to leave the past behind. All three conditions of the definition are fulfilled: a wrong was committed, resentment has been relinquished, and the agent identifies a feature of the situation that she takes as a reason. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to describe this as forgiveness in the morally significant sense. The resentment was not overcome by an act of will or a deliberative engagement with the wrongdoer's character; it was simply extinguished by a neurological accident. It appears that forgiveness, properly so called, requires that the agent actively choose to relinquish resentment — that the overcoming be an exercise of moral agency, not merely a change that happens to the agent.

definition: An action A performed by an agent S is benevolent if and only if (i) A is intended to promote the well-being of another person T, (ii) S acts without expectation of reciprocal benefit, and (iii) A does in fact tend to promote the well-being of T.
conditions: A is intended to promote the well-being of another person T
S acts without expectation of reciprocal benefit
A does in fact tend to promote the well-being of T
counterexample: Consider a wealthy patron who anonymously donates a large sum to fund a young artist's education abroad. She expects nothing in return and sincerely wishes to promote the artist's flourishing. The funds do indeed enable the artist to study in Paris. Yet the patron's motive, though she is not fully conscious of it, is rooted in a desire to re-enact and vicariously redeem her own thwarted artistic ambitions. The artist is, in effect, an instrument of the patron's unresolved psychological need. All three conditions are met, but the action has a manipulative quality that sits uneasily with the concept of benevolence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's intention to promote T's well-being be directed toward T's good as an end in itself — that T not be treated merely as a vehicle for the satisfaction of S's own unrecognised needs.
passage: A natural analysis of benevolence would hold that an action is benevolent when it is intended to promote another's well-being, performed without expectation of return, and genuinely conducive to that end. This seems to capture what we ordinarily mean by the term: selfless concern for another's good, issuing in effective aid. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be imagined without difficulty. Suppose a wealthy patron anonymously funds a young artist's studies abroad. She expects no reciprocation and sincerely believes herself to be acting for the artist's benefit. The funds do, in fact, enable the artist to receive an excellent education. However, the patron's motivation — of which she is only dimly aware — is rooted in a desire to relive and redeem her own thwarted artistic career. She has identified the young artist as a surrogate through whom her own unfulfilled ambitions might be vicariously realised. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that there is something troubling about this arrangement. The artist is being treated not as an end in herself but as an instrument of the patron's psychological needs. The apparent benevolence conceals a form of self-serving projection. It appears that the definition, by attending only to conscious intention, disinterestedness, and efficacy, fails to require that the beneficiary be regarded as an end in her own right rather than as a means to the agent's unrecognised purposes.

definition: An agent S is loyal to an entity E if and only if (i) S bears a special relationship to E that generates particular obligations, (ii) S consistently acts in accordance with those obligations even when doing so is costly, and (iii) S's actions on behalf of E are motivated by attachment to E rather than by self-interest.
conditions: S bears a special relationship to E that generates particular obligations
S consistently acts in accordance with those obligations even when doing so is costly
S's actions on behalf of E are motivated by attachment to E rather than by self-interest
counterexample: Consider a civil servant who bears a special relationship to the state, consistently fulfils his obligations at considerable personal cost, and is genuinely motivated by attachment to the nation rather than by career advancement. Yet the government he serves is prosecuting an unjust war, and his loyal service directly facilitates the perpetration of grave wrongs. All three conditions are satisfied, but one might question whether conduct that sustains manifest injustice deserves the honourable designation of loyalty.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the entity to which loyalty is directed, or the actions loyalty demands, not be fundamentally at odds with basic moral requirements — that loyalty be constrained by a moral minimum below which faithful service becomes complicity.
passage: One might characterise loyalty as the consistent, costly, and affection-driven fulfilment of obligations arising from a special relationship. This account captures much of what we admire in loyal conduct: steadfastness, sacrifice, and genuine attachment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed without extravagance. Suppose a civil servant bears a deep and special relationship to the state he serves. He consistently meets his obligations, even at great personal cost — enduring long separations from his family, accepting meagre compensation, and declining more lucrative positions in the private sector. His motives are irreproachable: he acts from genuine patriotic attachment, not from self-interest. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. However, the government he serves is conducting an unjust campaign of territorial aggression, and his faithful administrative work directly enables the prosecution of that campaign. We should hesitate to say that his conduct, however steadfast and self-sacrificing, constitutes loyalty in the morally commendable sense. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the agent's relationship, consistency, and motivation, fails to impose any constraint upon the moral character of the ends that loyalty serves. Without such a constraint, loyalty becomes indistinguishable from complicity.

definition: An action A performed by an agent S is supererogatory if and only if (i) A goes beyond what is morally required of S, (ii) A is morally praiseworthy, and (iii) the omission of A is not morally blameworthy.
conditions: A goes beyond what is morally required of S
A is morally praiseworthy
The omission of A is not morally blameworthy
counterexample: Consider a surgeon who volunteers to perform a dangerous operation on a stranger at considerable risk to her own health, and who succeeds in saving the stranger's life. The action goes beyond any obligation; it is clearly praiseworthy; and no one would blame her for declining. Yet suppose the surgeon is motivated entirely by a desire to humiliate a rival colleague who had declared the operation impossible. Her purpose is vindictive self-aggrandisement, not beneficence. All three conditions are satisfied, but there is something unsatisfying about calling an action supererogatory when its animating motive is spite.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's motive for performing the action be at least partly moral in character — that supererogation involves not merely exceeding the demands of duty in outward performance but doing so from a morally creditable motive.
passage: A standard account of supererogation identifies it with action that goes beyond duty, merits praise, and whose omission attracts no blame. This analysis has the virtue of distinguishing supererogatory acts from both obligatory ones and from acts that are merely permissible without being praiseworthy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be readily constructed. Suppose a surgeon of distinguished skill volunteers to perform a dangerous operation upon a stranger, at considerable risk to her own health, and succeeds in saving the patient's life. The action exceeds any moral requirement: no one would hold her obligated to risk her well-being for a stranger. It is praiseworthy: the saving of a life through personal sacrifice is widely admired. The omission would attract no blame: she was under no duty to volunteer. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet suppose further that the surgeon's sole motive is to humiliate a rival colleague who had publicly declared the operation impossible. She acts not from compassion or moral seriousness but from a desire for vindictive triumph. One might observe that there is something incongruous in classifying as supererogatory an action whose animating purpose is spite. It appears that the definition, by focusing exclusively upon the outward character of the action and its relation to duty, neglects the role of motive. If supererogation is to mark a distinctive moral achievement, it seems that the agent must act, at least in part, from a morally creditable impulse.

definition: An action A performed by an agent S is cruel if and only if (i) A causes suffering to a sentient being T, (ii) S is aware that A causes or is likely to cause suffering, and (iii) S performs A without adequate moral justification.
conditions: A causes suffering to a sentient being T
S is aware that A causes or is likely to cause suffering
S performs A without adequate moral justification
counterexample: Consider a schoolmaster who imposes a harsh regimen of physical discipline upon his pupils, fully aware of the pain it causes, and without any justification that would satisfy modern moral sensibilities. He does so not from malice but from a sincere, if misguided, conviction that suffering builds character. He takes no pleasure in the pupils' distress and would cease if persuaded the method were ineffective. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet there is reason to distinguish this conduct from cruelty in the paradigmatic sense — the conduct of one who takes satisfaction in the suffering of another.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that S derive satisfaction, indifference, or callous disregard from the suffering inflicted — that cruelty involve a characteristic defect of sympathy rather than merely the unjustified infliction of pain.
passage: One might analyse cruelty as the knowing infliction of suffering without adequate moral justification. This captures the thought that cruelty is distinguished from unfortunate necessity by the absence of exculpating reasons and from accidental harm by the presence of awareness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from educational practice. Suppose a schoolmaster in the early part of the last century imposes a severe regimen of corporal discipline upon his pupils. He is fully aware of the pain he inflicts. His justification — that suffering strengthens moral fibre — is one that, upon examination, we should reject as inadequate. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: suffering is caused, the agent is aware of it, and the justification is wanting. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call this schoolmaster cruel in the paradigmatic sense. He takes no pleasure in the children's pain; he does not seek out occasions for its infliction; he would willingly abandon the practice if shown a more effective method of moral training. His fault is one of misguided belief rather than of defective sympathy. It appears that the definition, by omitting any reference to the agent's attitude toward the suffering, fails to distinguish between the genuinely cruel person — who is characterised by indifference to or satisfaction in another's pain — and the merely misguided one who causes suffering from honest error about what morality requires.

definition: An agent S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S performed an action A that contributed causally to O, (ii) S performed A voluntarily and with awareness of what A involved, and (iii) O was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of A.
conditions: S performed an action A that contributed causally to O
S performed A voluntarily and with awareness of what A involved
O was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of A
counterexample: Consider two motorists who each drive home after drinking to excess. Both drive with equal recklessness along identical roads. One arrives safely; the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who steps unexpectedly into the road. The action, the voluntariness, and the foreseeability of a harmful outcome are the same in both cases. Yet our moral judgements of the two drivers diverge sharply: we hold the second far more responsible than the first. The definition, which attends only to the agent's action and its foreseeability, cannot account for this divergence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address the role of factors beyond the agent's control — specifically, the actual occurrence and severity of the outcome — in determining the degree of moral responsibility, thereby neglecting the phenomenon whereby luck partly constitutes the moral standing of an agent's conduct.
passage: One might propose that moral responsibility for an outcome requires a voluntary action, awareness of what the action involves, and the reasonable foreseeability of the consequence. This account has the appeal of grounding responsibility squarely in what lies within the agent's control: her choice, her awareness, and the predictability of the result. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is not difficult to describe. Suppose two motorists each drive home after consuming an excessive quantity of alcohol. Both drive with identical recklessness along roads of the same character. The first arrives home without incident; the second strikes and kills a pedestrian who steps into the road from behind a parked vehicle. The action in each case is the same: reckless driving whilst intoxicated. The voluntariness is the same. The foreseeability of a harmful outcome is the same. Yet our moral assessment of the two drivers is markedly different. We judge the second driver far more harshly — not merely legally but morally. The definition, which identifies responsibility solely with action, awareness, and foreseeability, cannot account for this divergence. It appears that the actual outcome — a matter largely beyond the agent's control — plays a role in our attribution of moral responsibility that the definition cannot accommodate. Whether this feature of our moral thinking is defensible is a further question, but any adequate analysis must at least recognise its presence.

definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts in the face of danger, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary.
conditions: S regularly acts in the face of danger
S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse
S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary
counterexample: Consider a professional stunt performer who regularly faces physical danger, does so from a settled occupational disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and acts entirely voluntarily. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the stunt performer may be wholly indifferent to the moral significance of the dangers she faces; she courts risk for pay and spectacle, not because she recognises that something of genuine value is at stake. We should hesitate to call her courageous in the full moral sense, for her disposition is mercenary rather than responsive to considerations of worth.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's disposition to face danger be governed by a correct apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking harm for — that the agent face danger because and only because something of sufficient moral importance demands it.
passage: One might propose that courage consists in a settled, voluntary disposition to act in the face of danger. This formulation captures the regularity, stability, and freedom from compulsion that are commonly associated with the courageous character. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the world of professional risk-taking. Suppose a stunt performer spends her career leaping from burning buildings, driving motorcars at extraordinary speeds, and falling from great heights. She does so regularly, from a disposition cultivated over many years of training, and she acts with full voluntariness — no one compels her to perform. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to attribute to her the virtue of courage in its proper moral sense. Her willingness to face danger is not governed by any apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking harm for; she courts peril for financial remuneration and the applause of spectators, remaining entirely indifferent to whether the danger she faces serves any morally significant end. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the regularity, settledness, and voluntariness of the agent's behaviour in the face of danger, cannot distinguish the genuinely courageous person — who faces danger because something of real moral importance is at stake — from the professional daredevil whose disposition, however settled, is governed by considerations that have nothing to do with moral worth.

definition: A distribution of goods among members of a society is just if and only if (i) the distribution was produced by a procedure to which all members consented, (ii) the procedure was applied impartially, without favouring any individual or group, and (iii) every member received some positive share of the goods distributed.
conditions: The distribution was produced by a procedure to which all members consented
The procedure was applied impartially, without favouring any individual or group
Every member received some positive share of the goods distributed
counterexample: Suppose the inhabitants of a small community unanimously consent to a lottery for the distribution of farmland. The lottery is conducted with scrupulous impartiality, and every member receives at least a small parcel. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet by sheer chance the lottery assigns the most fertile acreage to the wealthiest families, who already possess abundant resources, while the poorest families receive barren tracts unsuitable for cultivation. The resulting distribution, though procedurally impeccable, leaves the most vulnerable members materially worse off than before and concentrates productive resources among those who least need them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the resulting distribution bear some substantive relation to the needs, merits, or prior disadvantages of the recipients — that procedural fairness issue in outcomes that are not grossly disproportionate to what considerations of desert or need would demand.
passage: One might analyse a just distribution as one produced by a procedure to which all members consent, applied impartially, and yielding some positive share for every participant. This characterisation captures the procedural features — consent, impartiality, universality — that many theorists have taken to be central to justice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose the inhabitants of a rural community unanimously agree to distribute their collective farmland by means of a fair lottery. The lots are drawn with punctilious impartiality; no one's name is favoured, no weight is given to prior holdings or social standing. Every member receives at least a small parcel. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet by sheer chance the lottery assigns the richest and most fertile acreage to the families who are already the wealthiest in the community, whilst the poorest families — those with the greatest need for productive land — receive barren tracts scarcely capable of yielding a crop. The resulting distribution, though procedurally beyond reproach, leaves the most vulnerable members materially worse off and concentrates the community's productive resources among those who had least need of additional wealth. One might observe that justice in distribution appears to demand something beyond procedural regularity: it requires that the outcomes bear some substantive relation to the needs, merits, or prior disadvantages of the persons among whom goods are distributed.

definition: A person S has a moral obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) performing A would prevent harm to another person, (ii) S is aware that performing A would prevent such harm, and (iii) performing A is within S's physical power.
conditions: Performing A would prevent harm to another person
S is aware that performing A would prevent such harm
Performing A is within S's physical power
counterexample: Suppose a surgeon is aware that she could prevent harm to a patient by performing an emergency operation, and the operation is well within her physical capacities. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the patient has explicitly and competently refused the operation on grounds of religious conviction. In such a case, we should not say that the surgeon has a moral obligation to operate; to do so would be to override the patient's autonomous refusal, which itself generates a powerful moral constraint against intervention.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person to be aided has not competently and explicitly refused the assistance — that the obligation to prevent harm is not overridden by the autonomous wishes of the person who would be harmed.
passage: One might propose that a moral obligation to act arises whenever one is aware that one could prevent harm to another and possesses the physical capacity to do so. This formulation has considerable intuitive appeal, for it captures the thought that the ability to prevent suffering, conjoined with awareness of that ability, generates a duty to act. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a surgeon is presented with a patient who requires an emergency operation to survive. The surgeon is fully aware that performing the procedure would prevent grave harm, and the operation lies well within her physical and professional capacities. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the patient, in full possession of his faculties and with a clear understanding of the consequences, has explicitly refused the operation on grounds of deeply held religious conviction. In such circumstances, we should hesitate to say that the surgeon is morally obligated to operate. To perform the surgery would be to override the patient's autonomous decision — a decision that itself generates a powerful moral constraint. It appears that the awareness of one's capacity to prevent harm does not, by itself, suffice to create an obligation; the definition fails to attend to the possibility that the person to be aided may have competently and deliberately declined the assistance. One might observe that moral obligation cannot be analysed solely in terms of the agent's awareness and capacity without regard to the rights and autonomous choices of those who would be affected.

definition: A person S deserves a reward R if and only if (i) S has performed an action A that is praiseworthy, (ii) R is proportionate to the praiseworthiness of A, and (iii) S performed A intentionally.
conditions: S has performed an action A that is praiseworthy
R is proportionate to the praiseworthiness of A
S performed A intentionally
counterexample: Suppose a man intentionally dives into a swollen river to rescue a drowning child, performing an act that is praiseworthy in the highest degree, and he is subsequently awarded a medal of valour proportionate to his deed. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose it later emerges that the man dived into the river solely because he mistook the child for his own son and would not have lifted a finger had the child belonged to a stranger. He performed the act intentionally and the act itself is praiseworthy, yet his motive was narrowly self-interested rather than morally admirable. One might question whether he truly deserves the honour.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's motives for performing the praiseworthy action be themselves morally creditable — that the agent acted from a commendable motive rather than from one that is morally indifferent or self-serving.
passage: One might analyse desert as the relation between a praiseworthy intentional act and a proportionate reward. This formulation attends to the quality of the action, the proportionality of the recognition, and the voluntariness of the agent's conduct — features that are commonly taken to ground desert. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man, walking along a riverbank, sees a child being swept away by floodwaters. He leaps into the torrent and, at great personal risk, brings the child safely to shore. The act is praiseworthy by any reckoning. He is subsequently awarded a medal of valour, a reward that appears proportionate to the gravity of his deed. And he performed the rescue intentionally — he chose to jump, knowing the danger. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose it later comes to light that the man dived into the river solely because he mistook the child for his own son; had he realised the child was a stranger's, he would not have stirred. His motive, far from being morally admirable, was narrowly parental and, indeed, would have left the child to drown had the facts been otherwise. We should hesitate to say that he deserves the medal in the same way that a person moved by genuine concern for a stranger's life would deserve it. It appears that the definition, by attending to the external features of the act and its intentional performance, cannot distinguish desert grounded in morally creditable motives from desert claimed on the basis of an act whose underlying motive is morally arbitrary.

definition: A person S has validly consented to an arrangement X if and only if (i) S has expressed agreement to X, (ii) S was not subjected to physical coercion at the time of agreeing, and (iii) S had the mental capacity to understand the terms of X.
conditions: S has expressed agreement to X
S was not subjected to physical coercion at the time of agreeing
S had the mental capacity to understand the terms of X
counterexample: Suppose a tenant, facing no physical coercion and in full possession of her mental faculties, signs a lease agreement whose terms she understands perfectly well. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the landlord is the only provider of housing in a remote region, and the tenant will become homeless if she does not sign. The landlord, aware of this, has set the rent at three times the fair market value. The tenant agrees because she has no viable alternative, not because she regards the terms as acceptable. We should hesitate to call her consent valid in the morally relevant sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the consenting party have reasonable alternatives available — that her agreement not be extracted by exploiting a situation in which refusal would leave her with no tolerable option.
passage: One might define valid consent as the expression of agreement by a mentally competent person who is free from physical coercion. This formulation captures the elements of voluntariness, capacity, and manifest assent that are standardly associated with consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from situations of economic duress. Suppose a tenant in a remote rural district is presented with a lease agreement by the only landlord in the region. She is subjected to no physical coercion whatsoever; no one threatens her person or her property. She possesses full mental capacity and understands the terms of the lease perfectly well. She signs. All three conditions are met. Yet the landlord, knowing himself to be the sole provider of housing for miles around, has set the rent at three times the fair market value. The tenant signs not because she regards the terms as fair or acceptable but because the only alternative is homelessness in a region where no other accommodation exists. Her agreement is extracted not by force but by the exploitation of her absence of alternatives. One might observe that consent secured under such circumstances is not morally valid, even though no physical coercion was applied and the tenant possessed full mental capacity. It appears that the definition, by attending only to physical coercion, mental capacity, and expressed agreement, cannot distinguish genuine consent from agreement wrung from a person who has no tolerable option but to comply.

definition: The infliction of a penalty P upon a person S is morally justified if and only if (i) S has been found guilty of a wrongful act by a competent authority, (ii) P is proportionate to the gravity of the wrongful act, and (iii) the infliction of P serves a deterrent purpose.
conditions: S has been found guilty of a wrongful act by a competent authority
P is proportionate to the gravity of the wrongful act
The infliction of P serves a deterrent purpose
counterexample: Suppose a court of competent jurisdiction finds a man guilty of theft and sentences him to a fine proportionate to the offence. The sentence also serves a clear deterrent purpose, discouraging others from similar conduct. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the man committed the theft under extreme duress — a criminal gang credibly threatened to kill his family unless he stole the goods in question. In such circumstances, we should hesitate to say that punishment is morally justified, for the man's wrongful act was compelled by circumstances that substantially diminished his moral culpability.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the guilty party's moral culpability not be substantially diminished by extenuating circumstances such as duress, coercion, or necessity — that the conditions under which the wrongful act was committed be such as to sustain full moral responsibility.
passage: One might propose that punishment is morally justified whenever a competent authority has found the offender guilty, the penalty is proportionate, and the punishment serves a deterrent purpose. This formulation captures the institutional, proportional, and consequentialist elements that are commonly invoked in discussions of punitive justice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed by attending to the circumstances under which offences are committed. Suppose a man is brought before a court of competent jurisdiction and found guilty of theft. The court imposes a fine proportionate to the gravity of the offence, and the fine plainly serves a deterrent function: it discourages others from committing similar acts. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose it subsequently emerges that the man committed the theft under extreme duress. A criminal gang had credibly threatened to kill his wife and children unless he stole the goods in question. He acted not from greed or malice but from a desperate concern for the lives of his family, in circumstances where no lawful means of protection was available to him. In such a case, we should hesitate to say that the punishment is morally justified, notwithstanding the finding of guilt, the proportionality of the sentence, and its deterrent efficacy. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the institutional verdict, the proportionality of the penalty, and its deterrent function, cannot accommodate the moral relevance of the conditions under which the wrongful act was performed. One might observe that justification for punishment requires not merely that the offender be found guilty but that his moral culpability not be substantially diminished by extenuating circumstances.

definition: A person S is morally bound by a promise to perform an action A if and only if (i) S has freely uttered the words constituting the promise, (ii) the person to whom the promise was made understood S's utterance as a promise, and (iii) performing A is not impossible for S.
conditions: S has freely uttered the words constituting the promise
The person to whom the promise was made understood S's utterance as a promise
Performing A is not impossible for S
counterexample: Suppose a man freely promises his neighbour that he will water the neighbour's garden while the neighbour is away on holiday. The neighbour understands this as a promise. Watering the garden is well within the man's power. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that, while the neighbour is away, a severe drought leads the local authority to impose an emergency ban on all non-essential water use, with criminal penalties for violations. In such a case, performing the promised act would require the man to break the law and deprive others of scarce water. We should not say he remains morally bound by the promise.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the circumstances prevailing at the time of performance have not changed so as to make fulfilment of the promise morally impermissible or seriously harmful — that no supervening moral obligation overrides the commitment originally undertaken.
passage: One might analyse the moral bindingness of a promise in terms of the promisor's free utterance, the promisee's understanding, and the possibility of performance. This formulation captures the communicative and volitional elements that are commonly associated with promissory obligation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from situations in which circumstances change after the promise is made. Suppose a man freely promises his neighbour that he will water the neighbour's garden during the neighbour's absence on holiday. The neighbour understands the utterance as a promise. The task of watering the garden is entirely within the man's physical capacity. All three conditions are met. However, while the neighbour is abroad, a severe drought strikes the region, and the local authority imposes an emergency prohibition on all non-essential water use, attaching criminal penalties to violations. The water supply is barely sufficient for drinking and sanitation. In such circumstances, performing the promised action would require the man to break the law, risk criminal prosecution, and contribute to the depletion of a resource upon which the health of the community depends. We should hesitate to say that the man remains morally bound by his promise. It appears that the definition, by requiring only free utterance, mutual understanding, and physical possibility, cannot accommodate the way in which supervening circumstances may render the fulfilment of a promise morally impermissible. One might observe that promissory obligation is not absolute but is subject to defeasance when changed circumstances make performance seriously harmful or morally prohibited.

definition: A person S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S's action was a cause of O, (ii) S foresaw that O would result from the action, and (iii) S was not acting under external compulsion.
conditions: S's action was a cause of O
S foresaw that O would result from the action
S was not acting under external compulsion
counterexample: Suppose a factory owner foresees that the fumes from his plant will cause respiratory illness in a neighbouring village, his operation of the factory is a clear cause of those fumes, and no one compels him to run the factory. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the factory produces the sole supply of a medication without which hundreds of thousands of people in the wider region would die. The owner runs the factory not out of indifference to the villagers but because halting production would cause a catastrophe of far greater magnitude. We should hesitate to say he bears full moral responsibility for the villagers' illness in the same way as someone who pollutes for mere profit.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent was not confronted with a genuine moral dilemma in which every available course of action would produce serious harm — that the agent's causal contribution and foresight be assessed against the alternatives actually available to him.
passage: One might analyse moral responsibility as the conjunction of causation, foresight, and freedom from compulsion. This formulation attends to the causal, cognitive, and volitional dimensions that are standardly associated with responsible agency. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to situations of tragic choice. Suppose the owner of a chemical plant is fully aware that the fumes generated by his facility cause respiratory illness among the inhabitants of a neighbouring village. His operation of the plant is plainly a cause of those fumes. No one compels him to run the factory; he acts of his own volition. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the plant produces the only available supply of a life-saving medication upon which hundreds of thousands of people in the surrounding region depend. If the owner shuts the plant, the respiratory illness in the village will cease, but the wider population will be deprived of a drug without which they will die. The owner, confronted with this dilemma, continues production — not from indifference to the suffering of the villagers but because closing the factory would cause harm of incomparably greater magnitude. One might observe that we should hesitate to attribute to this man the same degree of moral responsibility that we would assign to an industrialist who pollutes purely for profit. It appears that the definition, by requiring only causation, foresight, and absence of compulsion, cannot distinguish responsibility in ordinary cases from responsibility in cases where every available course of action produces serious harm and the agent has chosen the lesser evil.

definition: A person S has a right to X if and only if (i) S has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) others have a duty not to interfere with S's access to X, and (iii) S's interest in X is recognised by the normative framework of S's community.
conditions: S has a legitimate interest in X
Others have a duty not to interfere with S's access to X
S's interest in X is recognised by the normative framework of S's community
counterexample: Consider a society in which the normative framework does not recognise the interest of women in owning property. A woman in this society has a legitimate interest in owning the house she has built with her own labour. We might well say that others have a duty not to dispossess her, even if that duty is widely violated. Yet the normative framework of her community does not recognise her interest. Condition (iii) fails, and the definition entails that she has no right to the property. But it seems plain that she does have such a right, regardless of what her community's norms happen to say.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a person may possess a right even when the normative framework of her community does not recognise the relevant interest — that rights may exist independently of their social or institutional recognition.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something whenever she has a legitimate interest in it, others are under a duty of non-interference, and the interest is recognised by her community's normative framework. This formulation captures the elements of interest, correlative duty, and social recognition that feature prominently in discussions of rights. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from societies whose norms are unjust. Consider a society in which the prevailing normative framework does not recognise the interest of women in owning property. Suppose a woman in this society has built a house with her own hands, on land she has cultivated for decades. She has a legitimate interest in that property. We might well hold that others have a duty not to dispossess her of it — a duty grounded in the labour she has invested and the life she has built around it. Yet the normative framework of her community does not recognise her interest. The third condition is not met, and the proposed definition entails that she has no right to her property. But it appears that she does have such a right — a right that obtains regardless of, and indeed in defiance of, the norms prevailing in her society. One might observe that the definition, by requiring recognition within the community's normative framework, cannot accommodate the possibility of rights that exist prior to or independently of their social recognition. It conflates the question of whether a right exists with the question of whether it is recognised, and thereby renders the concept of an unrecognised right unintelligible.

definition: Two agents who perform the same type of action with the same intentions under the same circumstances are equally morally assessable if and only if (i) their actions are of the same kind, (ii) their intentions are identical, and (iii) the circumstances in which they act are relevantly similar.
conditions: Their actions are of the same kind
Their intentions are identical
The circumstances in which they act are relevantly similar
counterexample: Suppose two motorists each drive home after drinking the same quantity of alcohol, with the same reckless intention and under relevantly similar conditions of road, weather, and traffic. One arrives home without incident; the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who steps unexpectedly into the road. All three conditions are met — the actions are of the same kind, the intentions are identical, and the circumstances are relevantly similar. Yet the second motorist is universally regarded as more culpable and faces far graver moral censure than the first, solely because of an outcome that lay beyond his control.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of outcomes in moral assessment — it assumes that moral assessability is fully determined by intention, action-type, and circumstance, without acknowledging that the actual results of an action, even when they depend on factors beyond the agent's control, may properly affect the degree of moral censure or praise the agent receives.
passage: One might propose that agents who perform the same type of action, with the same intentions, under relevantly similar circumstances, are equally morally assessable. This principle has considerable intuitive force, for it seems to ground moral assessment in those features of conduct that lie within the agent's control. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this principle may be drawn from common experience. Suppose two motorists each drive home after an evening of drinking. Each has consumed the same quantity of alcohol. Each drives with the same reckless indifference to the safety of others. The roads they travel are similar; the weather conditions are the same; the traffic is comparable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one motorist arrives home without incident, whilst the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who steps unexpectedly from behind a parked vehicle. The presence of the pedestrian at that precise moment was a matter of pure chance — a contingency that lay wholly beyond either motorist's control. Nevertheless, the second motorist is universally regarded as more blameworthy. He faces graver legal penalties, more severe social censure, and — one might observe — a heavier burden of moral guilt than the first, who was equally reckless but merely fortunate. It appears that the proposed principle, by confining moral assessment to the agent's intentions, the type of action performed, and the circumstances of acting, cannot explain the widespread and seemingly legitimate practice of assessing agents differently on the basis of outcomes that depend upon factors wholly external to their volition.

definition: A character trait T constitutes a genuine virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition of the agent, (ii) T reliably produces actions that are beneficial to others, and (iii) the agent possesses T as a result of deliberate cultivation.
conditions: T is a stable disposition of the agent
T reliably produces actions that are beneficial to others
The agent possesses T as a result of deliberate cultivation
counterexample: Consider a person who has, through years of disciplined practice, cultivated a stable disposition to give generously to charitable causes. This trait reliably produces actions beneficial to others, and it was acquired through deliberate effort. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the agent cultivated this disposition solely to secure social admiration and tax advantages, and that the giving is accompanied by a secret contempt for the recipients. We should hesitate to call this a genuine virtue, for the inner orientation of the agent is entirely self-serving despite the beneficial outward effects.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's disposition be accompanied by the appropriate internal orientation — that the agent act from a concern for the good of others rather than from purely self-interested motives that happen to produce beneficial consequences.
passage: One might propose that a genuine virtue is a stable character trait, deliberately cultivated, which reliably produces actions beneficial to others. This characterisation has considerable appeal, for it captures the notion that virtues are not mere accidents of temperament but achievements of sustained moral effort, and it ties virtue to the production of genuine good. Yet one may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation. Suppose that a wealthy merchant, acutely aware that philanthropy enhances social standing and provides handsome tax relief, undertakes a rigorous programme of self-discipline to ensure that generous giving becomes second nature. Over the course of many years, the merchant succeeds: the disposition to give is now thoroughly stable, the gifts reliably benefit their recipients, and the trait was cultivated with full deliberateness. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that the merchant harbours a private contempt for those who receive the gifts, regarding them as instruments of personal advancement. The generosity is, in its inner character, entirely self-serving. It appears that we should hesitate to ascribe genuine virtue to such a person. What the definition fails to require is that the disposition be animated by the right sort of internal orientation — a concern for the good of others that is not merely incidental to the pursuit of private advantage.

definition: A distribution of goods D is just if and only if (i) D was produced by a procedure that treats all parties equally, (ii) every party consented to the procedure prior to its application, and (iii) no party is left worse off than they were before the procedure began.
conditions: D was produced by a procedure that treats all parties equally
Every party consented to the procedure prior to its application
No party is left worse off than they were before the procedure began
counterexample: Suppose that three neighbours agree to divide a communal garden plot by drawing lots — a procedure that treats all equally, to which each has consented. The lots are drawn, and by chance one neighbour receives a strip of fertile soil while the other two receive strips of barren rock. No one is worse off than before, since none previously had any individual claim to the garden. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the resulting distribution, in which one party receives nearly all the benefit while the others receive almost none, appears manifestly unjust.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the outcome of the procedure bear a reasonable relation to the needs, contributions, or deserts of the parties — that procedural equality alone, without substantive fairness in the result, is insufficient to guarantee justice.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a distribution of goods is just when it issues from a procedure that treats all parties equally, to which all have consented, and which leaves no one worse off than before. Such a characterisation captures much of what we ordinarily mean by fairness: it excludes coercion, respects autonomy, and incorporates a minimal standard of non-harm. Yet one may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account. Let us imagine three neighbours who share a communal garden plot but wish to divide it into individual allotments. They agree upon a lottery — a procedure that manifestly treats each party alike. Each neighbour consents freely. The lots are drawn, and by pure chance one neighbour receives the sole fertile strip of earth, whilst the other two are left with portions of barren rock, unsuitable for cultivation. Since none of the three had any prior individual claim to any particular section, no one is strictly worse off than before. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Yet one might observe that the resulting arrangement is difficult to regard as just. One party receives nearly the whole of the benefit, whilst the others receive next to nothing, and this disparity bears no relation to need, effort, or desert. It appears that a merely procedural account of justice, however scrupulously equal in form, cannot ensure substantive fairness in the outcome, and that our concept of a just distribution demands something more than the satisfaction of procedural constraints.

definition: An agent A has a moral obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A is capable of performing X, (ii) performing X would prevent significant harm to another person, and (iii) no other agent is better positioned to perform X.
conditions: A is capable of performing X
Performing X would prevent significant harm to another person
No other agent is better positioned to perform X
counterexample: Suppose a skilled surgeon walks past an accident scene where a gravely injured stranger lies bleeding. The surgeon is capable of providing life-saving first aid. Doing so would prevent the stranger's death. No other medically trained person is present. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that performing the first aid would require the surgeon to miss an appointment at the hospital where she is the only person capable of performing an emergency operation on a child whose life also hangs in the balance. We should hesitate to say that the surgeon has an obligation to aid the stranger at the roadside, for doing so would result in the death of the child at the hospital.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that performing X not conflict with an equal or greater obligation owed to another party — that the agent's overall moral situation, including competing duties, be taken into account.
passage: One might propose that an agent has a moral obligation to perform some action whenever the agent is capable of doing so, the action would prevent significant harm to another, and no one else is better placed to act. This characterisation captures the intuition that obligation follows capacity, urgency, and unique positioning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose a surgeon of considerable skill, walking through a town, comes upon the scene of a serious accident. A stranger lies gravely injured, and without immediate medical attention will almost certainly die. The surgeon is fully capable of providing the necessary first aid. No other medically trained person is in the vicinity. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet the surgeon is at that moment on her way to the hospital, where a child awaits an emergency operation that only she can perform, and where any delay will prove fatal. If the surgeon stops to aid the stranger, the child at the hospital will die. It appears that we should hesitate to affirm, without qualification, that the surgeon is obliged to assist the stranger at the roadside. The definition, by attending only to the agent's capacity, the harm at hand, and the absence of better-positioned alternatives, neglects the possibility that the very same agent may simultaneously bear a competing obligation of equal or greater weight. What is wanted, it seems, is a condition requiring that the performance of X not conflict with obligations of comparable or superior moral urgency.

definition: A person P deserves reward R if and only if (i) P has performed an action A that is meritorious, (ii) A was performed voluntarily, and (iii) A produced a beneficial outcome.
conditions: P has performed an action A that is meritorious
A was performed voluntarily
A produced a beneficial outcome
counterexample: Consider a scientist who devotes years of voluntary effort to developing a new medical treatment. The research is conducted with great skill and diligence — an action that is plainly meritorious. The work is entirely voluntary. And the resulting treatment saves thousands of lives — a manifestly beneficial outcome. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose it later emerges that the identical treatment had already been developed, independently and somewhat earlier, by another researcher, and that the first scientist's work therefore added nothing to the stock of available remedies. We should hesitate to say that the second scientist deserves a reward for the beneficial outcome, since that outcome would have obtained regardless of the second scientist's efforts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's meritorious action be causally responsible for the beneficial outcome — that the outcome not have been brought about independently of the agent's contribution.
passage: It is commonly supposed that a person deserves a reward when that person has voluntarily performed a meritorious action which produces a beneficial outcome. This characterisation appears to capture the essential elements of desert: the action must be praiseworthy, freely undertaken, and productive of genuine good. Yet one may construct a case which reveals a structural gap in this formulation. Suppose a scientist of considerable talent devotes several years of painstaking, voluntary effort to developing a new medical treatment for a widespread and debilitating disease. The research is conducted with exemplary rigour and creativity — a meritorious action by any reasonable standard. The treatment, once completed, proves remarkably effective and saves thousands of lives — a beneficial outcome of the highest order. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Yet suppose it comes to light that another researcher, working independently and without any communication with the first, had developed the very same treatment somewhat earlier, and that this earlier discovery had already been made available to the medical community before the first scientist completed the work. The beneficial outcome — the saving of lives — would have obtained in precisely the same measure had the first scientist never begun the research. It appears that we should hesitate to say that the first scientist deserves a reward for that outcome, however meritorious and voluntary the effort may have been. What the definition overlooks is the requirement that the agent's action be causally responsible for the beneficial result — that the good outcome would not have occurred, or would not have occurred in the same measure, without the agent's contribution.

definition: A person P has given valid consent to action X if and only if (i) P has expressed agreement to X, (ii) P was not subjected to physical coercion, and (iii) P was of sound mind at the time of agreement.
conditions: P has expressed agreement to X
P was not subjected to physical coercion
P was of sound mind at the time of agreement
counterexample: Suppose a tenant expressly agrees to a substantial increase in rent. No physical force or threat of force is employed. The tenant is of perfectly sound mind. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the tenant's agreement was secured by the landlord's implicit threat to withhold essential repairs to the property during winter — a form of economic pressure that, whilst not amounting to physical coercion, leaves the tenant with no reasonable alternative but to agree. We should hesitate to regard this as valid consent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the absence of undue economic or psychological pressure — that the agent possess a genuine and reasonable alternative to agreement, so that the consent is not merely formally expressed but substantively free.
passage: One might propose that valid consent is present whenever a person of sound mind has expressed agreement to some action without being subjected to physical coercion. This characterisation captures several important elements: the requirement of overt expression rules out merely imputed consent, the exclusion of physical coercion ensures that brute force does not masquerade as agreement, and the requirement of sound mind rules out cases of incapacity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the ordinary circumstances of economic life. Suppose a tenant, whose lease permits the landlord to adjust rent annually, is informed that the rent will be doubled. The tenant protests, but the landlord makes it subtly clear that essential repairs to the heating system — repairs for which the landlord is legally responsible — will not be carried out unless the tenant accepts the increase. No physical force is threatened or employed. The tenant, who is of entirely sound mind, considers the approaching winter and the impossibility of finding alternative accommodation at short notice, and expressly agrees to the new terms. Every condition of the proposed definition is met: agreement has been expressed, no physical coercion has occurred, and the tenant is mentally competent. Yet one might observe that this agreement was extracted under a form of pressure that, whilst not physical, left the tenant with no reasonable alternative. The consent is, in a morally significant sense, unfree. What the definition fails to require is that the agent's agreement be given in the absence of undue economic or psychological pressure — that there exist a genuine and reasonable alternative to agreement, without which the expression of consent is merely formal.

definition: A measure M constitutes just punishment of a person P if and only if (i) P has been found guilty of a specific offence by a legitimate authority, (ii) M is proportionate to the severity of the offence, and (iii) M is imposed with the intention of expressing societal condemnation of the offence.
conditions: P has been found guilty of a specific offence by a legitimate authority
M is proportionate to the severity of the offence
M is imposed with the intention of expressing societal condemnation of the offence
counterexample: Consider a person who has been duly found guilty of fraud by a legitimate court. The sentence imposed — a moderate term of imprisonment — is proportionate to the offence. And the court expressly states that the sentence is intended to express societal condemnation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose it later emerges, through new evidence unavailable at trial, that the person was entirely innocent and that the conviction resulted from fabricated testimony. We should hesitate to call the punishment just, notwithstanding the satisfaction of every stated condition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person actually committed the offence in question — that the finding of guilt by a legitimate authority correspond to genuine culpability, not merely to the procedural outcome of a flawed process.
passage: One might propose that just punishment consists in a proportionate measure imposed upon a person found guilty by a legitimate authority, with the express intention of conveying societal condemnation. This characterisation has considerable force: it ties punishment to due process, proportionality, and communicative purpose, thereby excluding arbitrary or excessive responses to wrongdoing. Yet a case which reveals a structural gap in this formulation may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose that a person is charged with a serious fraud and, after a full trial before a duly constituted court, is found guilty. The court imposes a moderate term of imprisonment — a sentence widely regarded as proportionate to the gravity of the offence. The sentencing remarks explicitly state that the punishment is intended to express the community's condemnation of fraudulent conduct. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Yet suppose that, some months later, new evidence comes to light which conclusively establishes the person's innocence. The conviction, it transpires, rested upon fabricated testimony, and the defendant had no involvement whatsoever in the fraud. It now appears exceedingly difficult to maintain that the punishment was just. The measure was proportionate, the authority legitimate, and the communicative intention sincere — yet an innocent person was made to suffer for an offence they did not commit. What the definition fails to require is that the procedural finding of guilt correspond to the actual facts of the matter — that the person punished did in truth commit the offence. Without this, the most scrupulously proportionate and expressively motivated punishment may yet be profoundly unjust.

definition: A person P is morally bound to keep a promise to Q if and only if (i) P has made an explicit verbal or written commitment to Q, (ii) Q has accepted the commitment, and (iii) P is capable of fulfilling the commitment at the time performance is due.
conditions: P has made an explicit verbal or written commitment to Q
Q has accepted the commitment
P is capable of fulfilling the commitment at the time performance is due
counterexample: Suppose that a person, under no duress, explicitly promises a friend to deliver a package to a certain address. The friend accepts the promise. The promisor is fully capable of performing the delivery at the appointed time. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the promisor subsequently discovers that the package contains materials intended to cause serious harm to an innocent third party. We should hesitate to say that the promisor remains morally bound to fulfil the promise.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the fulfilment of the promise not involve the agent in bringing about serious and unjustified harm to third parties — that the moral bindingness of a promise is conditional upon the nature of what is promised.
passage: It is widely held that a person is morally bound to keep a promise when an explicit commitment has been made and accepted, and the promisor remains capable of fulfilment. This characterisation captures the voluntary, interpersonal character of promissory obligation: the promisor has freely undertaken a commitment, the promisee has relied upon it, and the capacity for performance ensures that the obligation is not rendered void by impossibility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed as follows. Suppose that one friend promises another to deliver a sealed package to a specified address on a specified date. The promise is made explicitly and in writing. The friend accepts the commitment and arranges matters in reliance upon it. At the appointed time, the promisor is entirely capable of performing the delivery. Every condition of the proposed definition is satisfied. Yet suppose the promisor, on the morning of the delivery, discovers by accident that the package contains materials intended to cause grave injury to an innocent person at the destination. It appears that we should not merely hesitate but positively refuse to affirm that the promisor is morally bound to complete the delivery. The obligation to keep one's word, however important, cannot be absolute when fulfilment would involve the agent in the infliction of serious and unjustified harm upon a third party. What the definition fails to require is that the content of the promise be such that its fulfilment does not implicate the agent in grave wrongdoing. Without this qualification, the account would bind agents to the performance of acts that morality itself condemns.

definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) A performed the action that produced O, (ii) A intended to perform that action, and (iii) A was aware of the likely consequences of the action.
conditions: A performed the action that produced O
A intended to perform that action
A was aware of the likely consequences of the action
counterexample: Suppose a pharmacist intentionally and knowingly dispenses a standard medication to a patient, fully aware that the likely consequence is the alleviation of the patient's symptoms. The pharmacist performs the action, intends it, and is aware of its likely results. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the patient suffers a rare and entirely unforeseeable allergic reaction and dies. It appears that we should hesitate to hold the pharmacist morally responsible for the patient's death, even though the pharmacist performed the causal action intentionally and with awareness of the likely — though not the actual — consequences.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent could have reasonably foreseen the specific outcome in question — that awareness of likely consequences is insufficient when the actual outcome is of a radically different kind from anything the agent could have anticipated.
passage: One might propose that an agent is morally responsible for an outcome whenever the agent performed the action producing it, intended to perform it, and was aware of its likely consequences. This formulation captures important aspects of our ordinary thinking about responsibility: it excludes cases of purely accidental causation and ensures that the agent acted with both intention and foresight. Yet one may construct a case which reveals a structural deficiency in this account. Suppose that a pharmacist, exercising the ordinary care of the profession, intentionally dispenses a well-established medication to a patient. The pharmacist is fully aware that the likely consequence of this action is the relief of the patient's symptoms — an outcome supported by extensive clinical evidence and years of practice. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met: the pharmacist performed the action, intended it, and was aware of its probable effects. Yet the patient happens to possess a rare and hitherto unknown genetic condition that causes a fatal reaction to the medication. The patient dies. The pharmacist had no means of foreseeing this outcome, which is radically different in kind from anything that could reasonably have been anticipated. It appears that we should hesitate to hold the pharmacist morally responsible for the death. The definition, by requiring only awareness of likely consequences, fails to distinguish between outcomes that fall within the range of what the agent could reasonably have foreseen and outcomes that are entirely beyond the horizon of reasonable anticipation. What is needed, it seems, is a condition specifying that the agent could have reasonably foreseen the specific outcome for which responsibility is ascribed.

definition: An agent A is more blameworthy than agent B if and only if (i) A's action produced a worse outcome than B's action, (ii) both A and B acted with the same degree of negligence, and (iii) both A and B were in relevantly similar circumstances.
conditions: A's action produced a worse outcome than B's action
Both A and B acted with the same degree of negligence
Both A and B were in relevantly similar circumstances
counterexample: Suppose two drivers, equally negligent, run red lights at identical intersections under identical conditions. One happens to strike and kill a pedestrian who stepped into the road at the fatal moment; the other passes through without incident because no pedestrian happened to be present. The first driver's action produced a worse outcome. Both acted with the same degree of negligence. Both were in relevantly similar circumstances. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition implies that the first driver is more blameworthy. Yet one might observe that the moral difference between the two drivers is entirely a matter of luck — the presence or absence of a pedestrian — and that it is difficult to see how blameworthiness can be affected by factors wholly beyond the agent's control.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the difference in outcomes be attributable to differences in the agents' choices or character rather than to factors entirely beyond their control — that blameworthiness track what the agent can influence, not what fortune happens to produce.
passage: It is tempting to suppose that an agent who produces a worse outcome is, other things being equal, more blameworthy than one who produces a lesser harm — provided that both acted with the same degree of negligence in relevantly similar circumstances. This characterisation aligns with common legal and moral practice, in which the actual consequences of an action often determine the severity of blame and punishment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the most ordinary circumstances of daily life. Suppose two motorists, each equally careless, each driving at the same excessive speed, each running a red light at an identical intersection under identical conditions of weather and visibility. The first motorist happens to strike and kill a pedestrian who chanced to step into the road at precisely the wrong moment. The second passes through the intersection without incident, for the simple reason that no pedestrian happened to be crossing. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met: the first driver's action produced a manifestly worse outcome; both were equally negligent; both were in relevantly similar circumstances. The definition accordingly implies that the first driver is more blameworthy. Yet one might observe that the difference between the two cases is entirely a matter of fortune. The first driver did nothing that the second did not also do; the divergence in outcome is attributable solely to the presence or absence of a pedestrian — a factor wholly beyond either driver's control. It appears that we should hesitate to ground differences in blameworthiness upon differences in outcome that are produced by nothing more than luck. What the definition fails to require is that the disparity in outcomes reflect a corresponding disparity in the agents' choices, intentions, or character.

definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) P has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) others are under a duty not to interfere with P's enjoyment of X, and (iii) P's claim to X is recognised by the prevailing legal or social order.
conditions: P has a legitimate interest in X
Others are under a duty not to interfere with P's enjoyment of X
P's claim to X is recognised by the prevailing legal or social order
counterexample: Consider a person living under a regime that legally recognises the right to own other human beings as property. A slaveholder has a legitimate interest (as defined by the prevailing norms) in the labour of the enslaved persons, others are under a legal duty not to interfere with this arrangement, and the claim is fully recognised by the legal and social order. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to affirm that the slaveholder genuinely possesses a right to own another person, for the arrangement is morally abhorrent regardless of its legal or social recognition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the interest in question be morally legitimate in a sense independent of the prevailing legal or social order — that rights cannot be grounded solely in positive law or social convention when those conventions sanction grave injustice.
passage: One might propose that a person has a right to something whenever that person has a legitimate interest in it, others are under a duty of non-interference, and the prevailing legal or social order recognises the claim. This formulation captures much of what is ordinarily meant by the assertion of a right: there is an interest worth protecting, a correlative duty upon others, and institutional recognition that lends the claim practical force. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from history. Consider a society in which the institution of chattel slavery is firmly established in law and custom. A slaveholder, under the prevailing order, has what that order regards as a legitimate interest in the labour and obedience of the enslaved persons. Others — neighbours, officials, strangers — are under a legally enforced duty not to interfere with this arrangement. And the claim is fully and explicitly recognised by the legal and social institutions of the community. Every condition of the proposed definition is satisfied. Yet one might observe that it would be a grave moral error to conclude that the slaveholder possesses a genuine right to own another human being. The interest, the duty, and the recognition are all artefacts of a profoundly unjust order, and their satisfaction cannot confer moral legitimacy upon a practice that violates the most fundamental claims of human dignity. What the definition fails to require is that the interest in question be morally legitimate in a sense that does not reduce to the mere fact of legal or social endorsement — that genuine rights possess a normative foundation independent of the conventions that happen to prevail.

definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S habitually acts in the face of danger, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary.
conditions: S habitually acts in the face of danger
S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse
S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary
counterexample: Suppose a soldier has, over many years, developed a settled disposition to advance towards the enemy under fire. He does so voluntarily, without compulsion, and his behaviour is habitual rather than impulsive. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the soldier advances not because he recognises any good worth defending but because he is entirely indifferent to his own survival, regarding death and life as matters of no consequence whatever. His indifference robs his actions of the evaluative sensitivity that courage requires; he does not overcome fear but simply lacks it, and his conduct is therefore better described as recklessness than as courage.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's actions be guided by a correct apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking danger for, so that the agent's conduct reflects a proper weighing of the goods at stake rather than mere insensibility to peril.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses the virtue of courage if he habitually acts in the face of danger, does so from a settled disposition rather than momentary impulse, and acts voluntarily. This characterisation captures what we ordinarily associate with courageous conduct: consistency, depth of character, and freedom from external compulsion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from military life. Suppose a soldier who has served for many years has developed a thoroughly settled disposition to advance towards the enemy under fire. He does so voluntarily, and his behaviour is habitual rather than the product of fleeting emotion. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that this soldier advances not because he recognises any good worth defending or any duty worth honouring, but because he is entirely indifferent to his own survival. He regards death and life as matters of no consequence whatever. His indifference robs his actions of the evaluative sensitivity we associate with genuine courage; he does not overcome fear but simply does not experience it. His conduct is better described as recklessness or apathy than as courage. It appears that the definition, by requiring only habituality, settled disposition, and voluntariness, fails to demand that the agent's conduct be guided by a correct apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking danger for. Without this evaluative component, the definition cannot distinguish the truly courageous person from one who is merely insensible to peril.

definition: A distribution of goods D within a community is just if and only if (i) D assigns to each member a share proportional to that member's contribution, (ii) D is arrived at through procedures that treat all members equally, and (iii) no member of the community is left without the means of subsistence.
conditions: D assigns to each member a share proportional to that member's contribution
D is arrived at through procedures that treat all members equally
D leaves no member without the means of subsistence
counterexample: Consider a case in which a community of artisans distributes the proceeds of their cooperative workshop strictly in proportion to each member's measurable output, through a procedure that applies the same rules to everyone, and in which even the least productive member receives enough for subsistence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that one artisan's output is low solely because she was assigned, by the community itself, to care for the children and the infirm, a task whose value is not reflected in measurable workshop output. The distribution, though proportional to measured contribution, systematically undervalues an essential form of labour that the community itself requires, and it would be difficult to call such an arrangement just.
