missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the measure of contribution employed in condition (i) recognise all forms of socially necessary labour, including those whose value is not captured by the prevailing metric of productive output.
passage: One might define a just distribution of goods as one that assigns shares proportional to contribution, is arrived at through equal procedures, and leaves no member without the means of subsistence. This characterisation captures the intuitions both of those who emphasise desert and of those who insist upon procedural fairness and a minimum threshold of welfare. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the life of a cooperative workshop. Suppose a community of artisans distributes the proceeds of their shared enterprise strictly in proportion to each member's measurable output. The procedure treats all members identically: the same rules of accounting and allocation apply to each. And even the least productive member receives enough for subsistence. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that one artisan's output is low solely because the community itself assigned her to care for its children and its infirm, a task without which the workshop could not function but whose value is not reflected in measurable workshop production. The distribution, though proportional to measured contribution, systematically undervalues an essential form of labour that the community requires for its own continuation. We should hesitate to call such an arrangement just, for it penalises a member precisely for performing the service the community demanded of her. It appears that the definition, by tying shares to contribution without specifying how contribution is to be measured, permits arrangements in which socially necessary but unrecognised labour is systematically devalued. Without a requirement that the measure of contribution reflect all forms of essential service, the definition remains insufficient.

definition: A person S has a moral obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) S is capable of performing A, (ii) failing to perform A would result in foreseeable harm to another person, and (iii) no other person is better placed than S to perform A.
conditions: S is capable of performing A
Failing to perform A would result in foreseeable harm to another person
No other person is better placed than S to perform A
counterexample: Suppose a physician is the only person capable of performing a particular surgical procedure upon a patient who will otherwise die. No other surgeon is available; the harm of non-intervention is entirely foreseeable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the patient is a competent adult who has explicitly and repeatedly refused the procedure on grounds of personal conviction. In such circumstances, we should hesitate to say that the physician has a moral obligation to operate, for the patient's autonomous refusal appears to override the physician's duty to prevent harm, and performing the surgery against the patient's expressed will would itself constitute a moral wrong.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person who would be harmed has not autonomously declined the assistance in question, thereby neglecting the role of the beneficiary's consent in generating or extinguishing obligations of aid.
passage: One might propose that a person has a moral obligation to perform an action whenever he is capable of performing it, failure to act would result in foreseeable harm, and no other person is better placed to act. This characterisation captures the widely held intuition that moral duties arise from the conjunction of capacity, anticipated harm, and the absence of a more suitable agent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a physician possesses a rare surgical skill and is confronted with a patient who will die without the procedure. No other surgeon is available, and the harm of non-intervention is entirely foreseeable. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, suppose the patient is a competent adult who has explicitly and repeatedly refused the procedure on grounds of deeply held personal conviction. One might observe that in such circumstances we should not say the physician is morally obligated to operate. To perform surgery upon a patient who has refused it would itself be a moral transgression, for it would violate the patient's autonomous governance of his own body. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the agent's capacity, the foreseeability of harm, and the absence of a better-placed alternative, fails to account for the role of the beneficiary's own will. Without a condition requiring that the potential beneficiary has not autonomously declined the proffered aid, the definition generates obligations in precisely those cases where the intervention would itself be wrongful.

definition: A person S has validly consented to an arrangement X if and only if (i) S has expressed agreement to X through words or conduct, (ii) S was not subjected to physical force or threats at the time of agreeing, and (iii) S was aware of the general nature of X.
conditions: S has expressed agreement to X through words or conduct
S was not subjected to physical force or threats at the time of agreeing
S was aware of the general nature of X
counterexample: Suppose a tenant signs a lease agreement for a flat. She does so without any physical coercion or threat, she expresses her agreement by signing the document, and she understands that the arrangement is a tenancy. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the landlord has deliberately withheld the information that the building has been condemned by the municipal authorities as structurally unsafe and is scheduled for demolition within three months. The tenant was aware of the general nature of the arrangement but was deceived about a fact so material that, had she known it, she would never have agreed. We should hesitate to regard her consent as valid.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S was not deceived about facts material to the arrangement — that is, facts whose disclosure would have led a reasonable person to withhold agreement.
passage: One might define valid consent as the expression of agreement, given without physical coercion, by a person who is aware of the general nature of the arrangement in question. This analysis captures the voluntariness and minimal cognitive engagement we associate with genuine consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of letting residential property. Suppose a tenant signs a lease for a flat. She does so freely, without any physical force or threats; she expresses her agreement explicitly by affixing her signature to the document; and she is aware that the arrangement is a tenancy for a specified period at a specified rent. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the landlord has deliberately concealed the fact that the building has been condemned by the municipal authorities as structurally unsafe and is scheduled for demolition within three months. The tenant was aware of the general nature of the arrangement but was deceived about a fact so material that, had she known it, she would not have consented. We should hesitate to regard her consent as valid, for it was obtained through the suppression of information essential to a rational assessment of the arrangement. It appears that the definition, by requiring only expression of agreement, absence of force, and awareness of the arrangement's general nature, fails to demand that the consenting party not have been deceived about material facts. Without such a requirement, consent obtained through deliberate concealment of decisive information is indistinguishable from consent freely and fully given.

definition: An imposition of hardship H upon a person S constitutes just punishment if and only if (i) S has been found guilty of a specific offence through a fair procedure, (ii) H is proportionate to the gravity of the offence, and (iii) H is imposed by a legitimate authority.
conditions: S has been found guilty of a specific offence through a fair procedure
H is proportionate to the gravity of the offence
H is imposed by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Suppose a man is found guilty of petty theft through a scrupulously fair trial, a legitimate court imposes a proportionate fine, and all three conditions are met. Yet suppose that the law under which he was convicted criminalises the act of gleaning leftover grain from harvested fields — an ancient practice of the destitute — and that the man gleaned only because his children were starving and no other lawful means of obtaining food was available. The procedure was fair, the penalty proportionate to the offence as defined, and the authority legitimate. Yet we should hesitate to call this punishment just, for the law itself punishes conduct that morality does not condemn.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the offence for which S is punished is one that it is morally defensible to prohibit, thereby overlooking the possibility that a just procedure may enforce an unjust law.
passage: One might propose that just punishment consists in the imposition of proportionate hardship upon a person found guilty through a fair procedure by a legitimate authority. This characterisation captures our insistence upon procedural regularity, proportionality, and institutional legitimacy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the enforcement of agrarian law. Suppose a man is found guilty of petty theft through a scrupulously fair trial. The charge is that he gleaned leftover grain from fields after the harvest. A legitimate court imposes a fine proportionate to the gravity of the offence as defined by statute. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the man gleaned the grain because his children were starving and no other lawful means of obtaining food was available to him. The practice of gleaning has been recognised in many traditions as a customary right of the destitute, and it is by no means certain that morality condemns the act. The procedure was fair, the penalty proportionate, and the authority legitimate; yet the law itself punishes conduct that, under the circumstances, many would regard as morally permissible or even required. We should hesitate to call this punishment just. It appears that the definition, by attending only to procedural fairness, proportionality, and legitimate authority, fails to require that the offence for which the person is punished is one that it is morally defensible to prohibit. Without this requirement, just procedure may serve as the instrument of unjust law.

definition: A person S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S performed an action that was a cause of O, (ii) S acted voluntarily and was not compelled by external force, and (iii) S could foresee that O was a probable consequence of the action.
conditions: S performed an action that was a cause of O
S acted voluntarily and was not compelled by external force
S could foresee that O was a probable consequence of the action
counterexample: Suppose a chemist voluntarily mixes two common reagents in her laboratory, foreseeing that the result will be an ordinary and harmless precipitate, as it has been on hundreds of previous occasions. This time, however, an undetectable impurity in one of the reagents causes a violent explosion that injures a colleague. The chemist's action was a cause of the injury, she acted voluntarily, and she could foresee the probable consequence of her action — which, on all available evidence, was a harmless precipitate. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to the harmful outcome. Yet we should hesitate to hold her morally responsible for the injury, since the actual outcome was not merely unforeseen but unforeseeable given the information available to her.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the foreseeability of the intended or expected outcome with the foreseeability of the actual outcome, failing to require that the specific harmful outcome — rather than merely some consequence of the action — was one that S could reasonably have anticipated.
passage: One might define moral responsibility as arising whenever a person voluntarily performs an action that causes an outcome she could foresee as a probable consequence. This characterisation captures our intuition that responsibility requires agency, freedom from compulsion, and a connection between the agent's awareness and the result. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from laboratory practice. Suppose a chemist voluntarily mixes two common reagents, foreseeing that the result will be an ordinary and harmless precipitate, as it has been on hundreds of previous occasions. On this occasion, however, an undetectable impurity in one of the reagents triggers a violent explosion that injures a colleague. The chemist performed the action that caused the injury; she acted voluntarily; and she could foresee the probable consequence of her action, which, on all available evidence, was a harmless chemical reaction. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met with respect to the harmful outcome. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to hold her morally responsible for the injury. The actual outcome was not merely unforeseen but unforeseeable given the information available to any competent practitioner. The definition, by requiring only that S could foresee that O was a probable consequence, is ambiguous as to whether O refers to the intended outcome or to the outcome that actually occurred. Without a requirement that the specific harmful outcome be one that S could reasonably have anticipated, the definition assigns responsibility for consequences that lie entirely beyond the agent's capacity to predict.

definition: A person S deserves a reward R if and only if (i) S has performed an action that produced a benefit for others, (ii) S performed the action voluntarily and without external compulsion, and (iii) S intended to produce the benefit in question.
conditions: S has performed an action that produced a benefit for others
S performed the action voluntarily and without external compulsion
S intended to produce the benefit in question
counterexample: Suppose a sailor, driven by a love of adventure, voluntarily sets out on a solo circumnavigation of the globe with no thought of benefiting anyone. During the voyage, he discovers a new navigational technique that proves enormously useful to mariners everywhere. The benefit was genuinely produced by his action; he acted voluntarily; but he did not intend to produce the benefit. Condition (iii) fails. Yet many would say the sailor deserves recognition or reward for his contribution, since the benefit flowed from his voluntary exercise of skill and daring, and the absence of beneficent intention does not diminish the value of the contribution.
missing_condition: The definition requires that S intended to produce the benefit, but desert can arise from the voluntary exercise of skill, effort, or ingenuity that produces valuable results, irrespective of whether the agent specifically aimed at the benefit. The definition ought to recognise that unintended but foreseeable or naturally flowing benefits may also ground desert claims.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves a reward when he has voluntarily performed an action that benefits others and intended to produce that benefit. This characterisation ties desert to agency, voluntariness, and beneficent purpose. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of exploration. Suppose a sailor, motivated entirely by a love of adventure and with no thought of conferring benefits upon others, embarks on a solo circumnavigation of the globe. During the voyage, he devises a new navigational technique that proves enormously valuable to mariners everywhere. His action produced a genuine benefit; he acted voluntarily and without compulsion; but he did not intend to produce the benefit in question. Condition (iii) fails. Nevertheless, one might observe that the sailor deserves recognition for his contribution. The benefit flowed naturally from his voluntary exercise of seamanship, courage, and ingenuity, and the fact that he was not aiming at the benefit does not diminish the value of the result or the merit of the effort that produced it. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon beneficent intention, draws the class of deserving persons too narrowly. Desert may arise not only from purposeful beneficence but from the voluntary exercise of valuable capacities whose beneficial results, though unintended, are no less real. The definition fails to accommodate cases in which the connection between voluntary action and beneficial outcome, rather than the agent's purpose, grounds the claim of desert.

definition: A person S is morally bound to keep a promise to another person P if and only if (i) S has made a voluntary declaration of intention to perform a specified act for P, (ii) P has accepted and relied upon S's declaration, and (iii) keeping the promise does not require S to perform an unlawful act.
conditions: S has made a voluntary declaration of intention to perform a specified act for P
P has accepted and relied upon S's declaration
Keeping the promise does not require S to perform an unlawful act
counterexample: Suppose a man promises his dying friend that he will deliver a sealed letter to a third party. The friend accepts the promise and dies in reliance upon it. Delivering the letter is perfectly lawful. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the man later discovers that the letter contains false and deeply defamatory allegations against an innocent person, designed to ruin the innocent party's reputation. Delivering the letter would cause grave and unjust harm to a person who has done nothing wrong. We should hesitate to say that the promisor remains morally bound to keep his promise under these circumstances.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the performance of the promise not result in serious unjust harm to a third party. The lawfulness condition screens out illegal acts but does not address cases in which fulfilment of the promise, though legal, would inflict grave moral wrong upon an innocent person.
passage: One might define the moral obligation to keep a promise as arising when a person has voluntarily declared his intention to act, the promisee has accepted and relied upon the declaration, and performance does not require an unlawful act. This characterisation captures the voluntariness of the undertaking, the reliance of the promisee, and a minimal moral constraint. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from ordinary life. Suppose a man promises his dying friend that he will deliver a sealed letter to a third party. The friend accepts the promise and dies in reliance upon it. The delivery of the letter is a perfectly lawful act. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose the man later discovers that the letter contains false and deeply defamatory allegations against an innocent person, calculated to destroy that person's reputation and livelihood. Delivering the letter would cause grave and unjust harm to someone who has done nothing wrong. One might observe that in such circumstances, we should hesitate to say that the promisor remains morally bound. The lawfulness condition screens out promises whose performance is criminal, but it does not address cases in which performance, though legal, would inflict serious moral wrong upon an innocent third party. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that performance not be unlawful, sets the moral threshold too low. A promise whose fulfilment would cause grave unjust harm to an innocent person may lose its binding force even when every condition of the definition is satisfied.

definition: Two agents A and B are equally morally blameworthy for their respective actions if and only if (i) A and B performed the same type of action, (ii) A and B acted with the same intentions and degree of recklessness, and (iii) A and B had the same relevant information available to them at the time of acting.
conditions: A and B performed the same type of action
A and B acted with the same intentions and degree of recklessness
A and B had the same relevant information available to them at the time of acting
counterexample: Suppose two motorists each drive through a residential neighbourhood at the same excessive speed, with the same degree of recklessness, with the same intention of arriving at their destination quickly, and with the same information about the road conditions. One motorist arrives home without incident; the other strikes and kills a child who happens to dart into the road. They performed the same type of action, with the same intentions, the same recklessness, and the same information. All three conditions are met. Yet we commonly judge the motorist who killed the child to be more blameworthy than the one who arrived safely, and this difference in judgement is reflected in the different punishments the law assigns.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of actual outcomes in our moral assessments. It holds that identical actions with identical mental states yield identical blameworthiness, but our actual moral practice treats the occurrence of harm as an aggravating factor, attributing greater blame to those whose recklessness actually results in catastrophe.
passage: One might propose that two agents are equally morally blameworthy when they have performed the same type of action, with the same intentions and degree of recklessness, and with the same information available to them. This characterisation captures the intuition that blameworthiness ought to depend upon what is within the agent's control — his conduct, his mental state, and his awareness — rather than upon fortuitous circumstances. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the familiar situation of reckless driving. Suppose two motorists each drive through a residential neighbourhood at the same excessive speed, with the same reckless disregard for the safety of others, the same intention of arriving quickly, and the same information about road conditions. One arrives home without incident; the other strikes and kills a child who darts into the road at an unlucky moment. The three conditions of the proposed definition are met: the actions, intentions, recklessness, and available information are identical. Yet we commonly judge the motorist who killed the child to be more blameworthy, and the law reflects this judgement by imposing a far more severe penalty upon him. One might observe that our moral assessments are not, in practice, determined solely by what lies within the agent's control. The occurrence of harm — which may depend upon factors entirely beyond the agent's influence — enters into our judgement of blameworthiness. The definition, by insisting that identical conduct and mental states yield identical blame, legislates away a feature of moral life that, however philosophically troubling, is deeply embedded in our actual evaluative practice.

definition: A person S has a moral right to X if and only if (i) S has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) that interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others not to interfere with S's enjoyment of X, and (iii) S is currently exercising or making use of X.
conditions: S has a legitimate interest in X
That interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others not to interfere with S's enjoyment of X
S is currently exercising or making use of X
counterexample: Suppose a political dissident lives under a regime that has imprisoned her and rendered her unable to speak publicly. She has a legitimate interest in expressing her views freely; that interest is sufficiently important to impose a duty on others — and especially on the state — not to suppress her speech. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But she is not currently exercising or making use of her freedom of expression, for the regime has made it impossible for her to do so. Condition (iii) fails. Yet we should not say that the dissident lacks a moral right to free expression; indeed, it is precisely because she has this right that her imprisonment constitutes a wrong.
missing_condition: The definition requires that S be currently exercising or making use of X, but moral rights persist even when their exercise is prevented by the wrongful acts of others. The definition conflates the possession of a right with its exercise and thereby denies rights to precisely those persons whose rights are most urgently being violated.
passage: One might define a moral right as arising when a person has a legitimate interest of sufficient importance to impose a correlative duty on others, provided the person is currently exercising or making use of the thing in question. The first two conditions capture a widely accepted interest-based account of rights. The third might seem a reasonable additional requirement, ensuring that rights are tethered to actual use. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the circumstances of political oppression. Suppose a dissident lives under a regime that has imprisoned her and made it impossible for her to speak publicly or to publish her views. She has a legitimate interest in expressing herself freely, and that interest is of sufficient importance to impose a duty on others not to suppress it. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met. But she is not currently exercising her freedom of expression — the regime has rendered this impossible. Condition (iii) fails. Yet one might observe that it would be perverse to conclude that she therefore lacks a moral right to free expression. It is precisely because she has this right that her imprisonment constitutes a wrong. The definition, by requiring current exercise, confuses the possession of a right with its enjoyment, and in so doing denies rights to those whose rights are most urgently being violated. A person's moral rights do not evaporate when wrongful interference prevents their exercise; on the contrary, it is the persistence of the right that makes the interference wrongful.

definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts in the face of danger or hardship, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are directed towards a worthy end.
conditions: S regularly acts in the face of danger or hardship
S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse
S's actions in the face of danger are directed towards a worthy end
counterexample: Consider a case in which a soldier has for many years shown a settled disposition to charge into battle without hesitation, and does so always in defence of his country, which all parties would regard as a worthy end. He acts regularly, from a stable character trait, and towards an admirable goal. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the soldier is constitutionally incapable of experiencing fear; he charges into danger not because he overcomes fear through strength of character but because he is wholly insensible to the risk. His behaviour is indistinguishable, in external form, from that of the genuinely courageous person, yet it appears that something essential to courage is absent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's action involve the recognition of and appropriate response to fear or risk, so that a person who is simply insensible to danger satisfies all the stated conditions without possessing genuine courage.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses the virtue of courage provided he regularly acts in the face of danger, does so from a settled disposition rather than momentary impulse, and directs his actions towards a worthy end. This characterisation captures much of what we ordinarily mean when we praise a man as courageous: he is steady, habitual in his bravery, and motivated by something beyond mere recklessness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from military life. Suppose a soldier has for many years displayed a settled disposition to advance into battle without hesitation, and does so always in defence of his country, which all parties would acknowledge as a worthy end. He acts regularly, from a stable trait of character, and towards an admirable goal. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the soldier is constitutionally incapable of experiencing fear; his nervous constitution renders him wholly insensible to the dangers he faces. He charges into battle not because he overcomes a natural apprehension through strength of will but because he simply does not apprehend the risk at all. His behaviour is, in its external form, indistinguishable from that of the genuinely courageous man, yet we should hesitate to call it courage. It appears that the definition, by requiring only regular action, settled disposition, and worthy purpose, neglects the essential role that the recognition of danger and the mastery of fear play in genuine courage.

definition: A distribution of goods among members of a community is just if and only if (i) each member receives a share proportional to his contribution to the common enterprise, (ii) no member is arbitrarily excluded from the distribution, and (iii) the procedure by which shares are determined is transparent and known to all members.
conditions: Each member receives a share proportional to his contribution to the common enterprise
No member is arbitrarily excluded from the distribution
The procedure by which shares are determined is transparent and known to all members
counterexample: Suppose a cooperative of farmers distributes the year's harvest in strict proportion to the labour each farmer has contributed. No farmer is excluded, and the procedure by which shares are calculated is posted publicly and understood by all. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one of the farmers has been stricken by illness and could contribute almost nothing during the growing season. Under the proposed definition, his near-zero share is perfectly just. But the community recognises that the farmer's inability to labour was no fault of his own, and that denying him sustenance would leave him destitute through sheer misfortune.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the distribution take account of the needs of members who are unable to contribute through no fault of their own, so that a purely contribution-based scheme may produce outcomes that are just by the stated criteria yet manifestly inhumane.
passage: One might define a just distribution of goods as one in which each member of a community receives a share proportional to his contribution, no member is arbitrarily excluded, and the procedure of allocation is transparent. This formulation captures the meritocratic intuition that reward should follow effort, and it guards against both arbitrary exclusion and hidden dealings. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from agricultural life. Suppose a cooperative of farmers distributes the harvest in strict proportion to the labour each has contributed during the growing season. No farmer is excluded, and the method of calculation is publicly posted and understood by all. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Nevertheless, one might observe that one of the farmers has been stricken by a grave illness and was able to contribute almost nothing during the season. Under the stated conditions his share is negligible, yet his inability to labour was wholly involuntary. The community is confronted with a distribution that is proportional, inclusive, and transparent, yet which consigns a blameless member to destitution. It appears that the definition, by tying justice exclusively to contribution, neglects the claims that arise from need, particularly when a member's inability to contribute is a matter of misfortune rather than choice. We are led to conclude that a full account of distributive justice must reckon not only with what each person has given but with what each person requires.

definition: A person S has a moral obligation to perform action A if and only if (i) S has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform A, (ii) another person P is relying upon S's performance of A, and (iii) failure to perform A would cause P a significant harm.
conditions: S has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to perform A
Another person P is relying upon S's performance of A
Failure to perform A would cause P a significant harm
counterexample: Suppose a man walking along a riverbank comes upon a child drowning in shallow water. The man has made no commitment to anyone; the child is a stranger to him; and no one is relying upon him in particular, for there is no relationship of dependence between them. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet it seems overwhelmingly clear that the man has a moral obligation to wade in and rescue the child, for the cost to him is trivial and the harm averted is immense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for obligations that arise not from voluntary commitment or reliance but from the sheer capacity to prevent grave harm at minimal cost to oneself, so that duties of rescue and beneficence are excluded entirely.
passage: One might propose that moral obligation arises when a person has voluntarily undertaken a commitment, another person relies upon its fulfilment, and failure would cause significant harm. This characterisation captures a wide range of our ordinary moral judgements, particularly those involving promises and contractual relationships. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the simplest circumstances of everyday life. Suppose a man walking along a riverbank comes upon a child drowning in shallow water. He has made no promise, no commitment, and no undertaking of any kind; the child is a complete stranger, and no one is relying upon the man in particular for assistance. None of the three conditions of the proposed definition is satisfied. Nevertheless, it appears overwhelmingly clear that the man has a moral obligation to wade in and rescue the child. The water is shallow, the risk to the man is negligible, and the harm that will result from inaction is the child's death. We should hesitate to accept any account of moral obligation that excludes so plain a case. It appears that the definition, by restricting obligation to instances of voluntary commitment and mutual reliance, fails to recognise obligations that arise from the mere capacity to prevent grave harm at trivial cost. Such duties of rescue or beneficence are among the most compelling moral demands we face, yet the proposed analysis renders them invisible.

definition: A person S has given valid consent to action A if and only if (i) S has expressed agreement to A by word or gesture, (ii) S was not subject to physical coercion at the time of expressing agreement, and (iii) S was aware that the expression would be taken as consent.
conditions: S has expressed agreement to A by word or gesture
S was not subject to physical coercion at the time of expressing agreement
S was aware that the expression would be taken as consent
counterexample: Suppose a tenant is told by his landlord that unless the tenant signs a waiver of his right to contest an eviction, the landlord will make the tenant's life unbearable by legal but oppressive means: constant inspections, refusal to make repairs, and raising rent to the maximum permitted by law. The tenant signs the waiver freely, without any physical coercion, and is fully aware that his signature constitutes consent. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the tenant's agreement was extracted through a form of duress that, while not physical, left him with no reasonable alternative. The consent is formally valid under the proposed definition but substantively defective.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S have a reasonable alternative to consenting, so that consent extracted through non-physical forms of duress or through the exploitation of a grossly unequal bargaining position counts as valid under the stated conditions.
passage: One might define valid consent as the expression of agreement by word or gesture, in the absence of physical coercion, by a person who is aware that the expression will be taken as consent. This characterisation captures the voluntariness and awareness that we ordinarily demand of genuine consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of tenancy. Suppose a landlord informs his tenant that unless the tenant signs a waiver of his right to contest an eviction, the landlord will make the tenant's situation intolerable by every legal means at his disposal: constant inspections, refusal of repairs, and the raising of rent to the maximum the law permits. The tenant, facing these prospects, signs the waiver. He does so without physical coercion, by a voluntary gesture, and in full awareness that his signature constitutes consent. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as valid consent. The tenant had no reasonable alternative: to refuse was to invite a course of harassment that, while legally permissible, would render his life miserable. His agreement was extracted not by physical force but by the exploitation of a grossly unequal power relation. It appears that the definition, by excluding only physical coercion, fails to recognise that consent may be vitiated by non-physical forms of duress and by the absence of any reasonable alternative to agreement.

definition: The punishment of an offender O for an offence X is morally justified if and only if (i) O has been found guilty of X through a fair procedure, (ii) the punishment is proportional to the gravity of X, and (iii) the punishment is imposed by a legitimate authority.
conditions: O has been found guilty of X through a fair procedure
The punishment is proportional to the gravity of X
The punishment is imposed by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Suppose a man is found guilty through an impeccably fair trial of stealing a loaf of bread during a famine to feed his starving children. The sentence imposed — a short period of community service — is proportional to the offence. It is imposed by a duly constituted court. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might argue that punishing a man for an act he was driven to by desperate necessity, when no other means of feeding his children was available, is morally unjustifiable regardless of fair procedure, proportionality, and legitimate authority.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the offender had a reasonable opportunity to act otherwise, so that punishment may be imposed upon a person who committed the offence under conditions of extreme necessity that left no morally acceptable alternative.
passage: One might hold that the punishment of an offender is morally justified when the offender has been found guilty through a fair procedure, the punishment is proportional to the offence, and it is imposed by a legitimate authority. This characterisation captures the procedural, proportional, and institutional requirements that we customarily associate with legitimate punishment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from times of famine. Suppose a man steals a loaf of bread during a period of severe scarcity, his children being on the verge of starvation, and no other means of procuring food being available to him. He is brought before a duly constituted court, tried by fair procedure, and found guilty. The sentence imposed is a short period of community service, which is proportional to the gravity of the theft. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the man acted under extreme necessity: he had no morally acceptable alternative, and his offence was committed not from greed or malice but from the desperate need to preserve his children's lives. It is by no means certain that punishment under these circumstances is morally justified, however fair the trial, however proportional the sentence, and however legitimate the authority. The definition, by attending only to procedural fairness, proportionality, and institutional legitimacy, neglects the question of whether the offender had a genuine opportunity to act in accordance with the law.

definition: A person S deserves reward R for action A if and only if (i) S freely chose to perform A, (ii) A required significant effort or sacrifice on S's part, and (iii) A produced a beneficial outcome for others.
conditions: S freely chose to perform A
A required significant effort or sacrifice on S's part
A produced a beneficial outcome for others
counterexample: Suppose a man devotes years of arduous labour to developing a new strain of wheat that he intends to distribute freely to famine-stricken regions. He freely chose this path, the effort was immense, and the outcome — a more resilient crop — would benefit millions. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that, through no fault of his own, a blight destroys every specimen of the new strain before it can be distributed, and the beneficial outcome never materialises. Under the proposed definition, the man does not deserve reward, since condition (iii) requires that A produce a beneficial outcome, and no such outcome was in fact produced. Yet it seems that the man's freely chosen sacrifice merits recognition regardless of the accident that prevented its fruition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for desert arising from praiseworthy intention and effort independently of actual outcome, so that a person whose meritorious action is thwarted by circumstances beyond his control is denied the desert his effort and sacrifice would otherwise warrant.
passage: One might propose that a person deserves reward for an action when the action was freely chosen, required significant effort or sacrifice, and produced a beneficial outcome. This characterisation captures the common intuition that reward should follow meritorious effort that yields good results. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from agricultural research. Suppose a man devotes many years of arduous labour to developing a new strain of wheat, intended for distribution to regions afflicted by famine. He freely chose this course; the effort was immense, involving both intellectual toil and material sacrifice; and the anticipated outcome — a more resilient crop for the world's poorest — would be of profound benefit. All three conditions appear to be on the verge of satisfaction. Yet suppose a blight, wholly beyond the man's control, destroys every specimen of the new strain before it can be distributed, and the beneficial outcome never materialises. Under the proposed definition, the man does not deserve reward, for condition (iii) requires that the action produce a beneficial outcome, and no such outcome was produced. Nevertheless, it appears that the man's freely chosen sacrifice and his years of dedicated effort merit recognition quite apart from the accident that prevented their fruition. We should hesitate to deny desert to a person whose praiseworthy intention and genuine effort were thwarted solely by misfortune.

definition: A person S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S performed an action that causally contributed to O, (ii) S acted voluntarily and was not coerced, and (iii) S foresaw, or could reasonably have foreseen, that his action would lead to O.
conditions: S performed an action that causally contributed to O
S acted voluntarily and was not coerced
S foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen that his action would lead to O
counterexample: Suppose a chemist voluntarily and without coercion publishes a paper describing a novel chemical synthesis, foreseeing that it will be used by other chemists for legitimate research. Decades later, a malicious agent uses the published synthesis to manufacture a harmful substance. The chemist's action causally contributed to the outcome (without the paper, the agent could not have manufactured the substance); the chemist acted voluntarily; and one might argue that the misuse of published research is reasonably foreseeable in some general sense. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet it seems deeply unjust to hold the chemist morally responsible for an outcome separated from his action by decades and by the independent criminal decision of another person.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal chain between S's action and O not pass through the autonomous and culpable choice of an independent agent, so that moral responsibility may be attributed to persons whose actions are merely necessary background conditions for harms produced by others' voluntary wrongdoing.
passage: One might define moral responsibility as arising when a person performs a voluntary action that causally contributes to an outcome that was or could have been foreseen. This characterisation captures the voluntariness, causality, and foresight that we customarily regard as essential to responsibility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the ethics of scientific publication. Suppose a chemist voluntarily publishes a paper describing a novel synthesis, foreseeing — as any researcher might — that published work will be read and used by others. Decades later, a malicious agent uses the published synthesis to manufacture a harmful substance, causing great injury. The chemist's publication was a causal condition of the harm: without it, the agent could not have succeeded. The chemist acted voluntarily and without coercion. And one might argue that the misuse of published knowledge is, in some general sense, reasonably foreseeable. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, it seems profoundly unjust to hold the chemist responsible for an outcome separated from his action by decades and by the autonomous criminal decision of another person. The definition, by requiring only causal contribution, voluntariness, and foreseeability, fails to demand that the causal chain between the agent's action and the harmful outcome not pass through the independent, culpable choice of another autonomous agent.

definition: Two agents S1 and S2 are equally blameworthy for their respective actions A1 and A2 if and only if (i) S1 and S2 performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness, (ii) both acted from the same morally culpable motive, and (iii) both were equally aware of the risks their actions posed.
conditions: S1 and S2 performed actions of the same type with the same degree of recklessness
Both acted from the same morally culpable motive
Both were equally aware of the risks their actions posed
counterexample: Suppose two motorists, each equally reckless, each equally motivated by impatience, and each equally aware of the risk, run the same red light at the same speed in the same type of intersection. One motorist passes through without incident; the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who happened to be crossing at that moment. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the actions are of the same type and degree of recklessness, the motives are identical, and the awareness of risk is equal. Yet our moral judgements diverge sharply: we blame the motorist who killed the pedestrian far more severely, and the law punishes him more harshly, despite the fact that the only difference between the two cases is the presence or absence of a pedestrian — a matter of sheer luck.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the influence of moral luck, whereby the actual consequences of an action — determined by factors beyond the agent's control — affect the degree of blame assigned, so that two agents who are identical in recklessness, motive, and awareness may nonetheless be judged unequally on the basis of outcomes they did not control.
passage: One might propose that two agents are equally blameworthy when they perform the same type of action with the same recklessness, act from the same culpable motive, and are equally aware of the risks involved. This characterisation expresses the widely held intuition that blame should track the agent's mental state and the quality of his conduct rather than the vicissitudes of fortune. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from ordinary motoring. Suppose two drivers, each equally impatient and each equally aware of the danger, run the same red light at the same speed at identical intersections. One passes through without incident; the other strikes and kills a pedestrian who happened to be in the road. The actions are of the same type and the same degree of recklessness; the motives are indistinguishable; and both drivers were equally aware of the risks they posed. All three conditions are met, and the definition pronounces them equally blameworthy. Yet our moral responses diverge sharply: we blame the driver who caused the death far more severely, and the legal system treats him far more harshly. The only difference between the two cases is the presence of a pedestrian — a circumstance wholly beyond either driver's control. It appears that the definition, by attending solely to the agent's conduct, motive, and awareness, fails to reckon with the phenomenon of moral luck, whereby the actual outcome of an action, shaped by factors beyond the agent's control, profoundly influences the blame we assign.

definition: A person S has a right to X if and only if (i) S has a legitimate interest in X, (ii) the interest is of sufficient importance to impose duties on others not to interfere with S's enjoyment of X, and (iii) the right to X is recognised by the prevailing moral or legal norms of S's community.
conditions: S has a legitimate interest in X
The interest is of sufficient importance to impose duties on others
The right to X is recognised by the prevailing moral or legal norms of S's community
counterexample: Suppose a person living under an unjust regime has a legitimate interest in speaking freely and that this interest is of the highest importance — sufficient, one would think, to impose duties on others not to suppress his speech. Yet the prevailing legal norms of his community do not recognise any right to free expression; indeed, they expressly forbid it. All three conditions cannot be jointly satisfied, since condition (iii) fails. Under the proposed definition, the person has no right to free speech. Yet it seems that his right exists precisely in defiance of the prevailing norms, and that the norms are unjust because they fail to recognise it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the existence of rights that are not recognised by prevailing norms, so that a person living under an unjust regime is denied rights that, on any plausible moral view, he possesses independently of whether his community acknowledges them.
passage: One might define a person's right to something as consisting in a legitimate interest of sufficient importance to impose duties on others, where that right is recognised by the prevailing moral or legal norms of the community. This characterisation has the virtue of connecting rights to interests, duties, and social recognition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of persons living under unjust regimes. Suppose a man living under a despotic government has a legitimate interest in speaking freely on matters of public concern, and that this interest is of the most profound importance — sufficient, as all reasonable persons would agree, to impose duties on others not to suppress his expression. Yet the prevailing legal norms of his community do not recognise any right to free speech; they expressly criminalise it. Condition (iii) of the proposed definition fails, and the man, according to the analysis, has no right to free expression. Nevertheless, it appears that his right to speak freely exists independently of whether the laws of his community acknowledge it. Indeed, we commonly say that the laws are unjust precisely because they fail to recognise a right that the man possesses by virtue of his humanity alone. The definition, by making recognition by prevailing norms a necessary condition of right-holding, cannot accommodate the notion of rights that exist prior to and independently of social or legal recognition — rights that serve as standards against which prevailing norms themselves may be judged.

definition: A person S has a moral obligation to keep a promise made to P if and only if (i) S voluntarily made the promise to P, (ii) P relied upon the promise in making his plans, and (iii) keeping the promise would not require S to act immorally.
conditions: S voluntarily made the promise to P
P relied upon the promise in making his plans
Keeping the promise would not require S to act immorally
counterexample: Suppose a man promises his friend that he will attend his friend's wedding. The friend relies upon this promise in arranging the seating. Keeping the promise would require no immoral act. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet on the morning of the wedding, the man discovers that a stranger has collapsed in the road before his house and requires immediate assistance that only the man can provide at that moment. If the man stops to help the stranger, he will miss the wedding and break his promise. The obligation to rescue a person in immediate peril appears to override the obligation of promise-keeping, even though all three conditions for the latter are satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that no competing moral obligation of greater urgency override the duty to keep the promise, so that the obligation of promise-keeping is treated as absolute rather than prima facie, and the agent is bound even when a more pressing duty intervenes.
passage: One might define the obligation to keep a promise by requiring that the promise was voluntarily made, that the promisee relied upon it, and that fulfilment would not require the promisor to act immorally. This characterisation captures the voluntariness, reliance, and moral permissibility that we associate with binding promises. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the conflict of duties. Suppose a man promises his friend that he will attend the friend's wedding, and the friend relies upon this in making his arrangements. Keeping the promise involves no immoral act. All three conditions are satisfied, and the obligation to attend appears, on the proposed analysis, to be binding. Yet on the morning of the wedding, the man discovers a stranger collapsed before his door, in need of immediate assistance that only the man is in a position to provide. If he stops to render aid, he will miss the wedding and break his promise. It is by no means certain that the man ought to keep his promise under these circumstances; indeed, the duty to rescue a person in immediate peril appears to override the duty of fidelity. The definition, by treating the obligation of promise-keeping as decisive whenever its three conditions are satisfied, fails to recognise that promise-keeping is a prima facie rather than absolute obligation, one that may be overridden by competing moral demands of greater urgency.

definition: A person S has a moral obligation not to lie to another person P if and only if (i) S makes a statement to P that S believes to be false, (ii) S intends P to believe the statement, and (iii) S has no overriding moral reason to make the false statement.
conditions: S makes a statement to P that S believes to be false
S intends P to believe the statement
S has no overriding moral reason to make the false statement
counterexample: Suppose a doctor tells a terminally ill patient that his condition is 'manageable,' knowing this to be false, intending the patient to believe it, and having no strictly overriding moral reason — he simply wishes to spare the patient distress. All three conditions appear satisfied: the statement is believed false by the doctor, the doctor intends the patient to accept it, and the motive (compassion) is not typically classified as an overriding moral reason. Under the proposed definition, the doctor has a moral obligation not to make this statement. Yet many thoughtful persons would hold that the doctor's compassionate intent, while not an overriding justification in the strict sense, alters the moral character of the falsehood so fundamentally that the bare obligation not to lie does not straightforwardly apply.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the moral significance of the speaker's compassionate or protective motive and the vulnerability of the listener, so that all intentional falsehoods lacking a strictly overriding justification are treated alike, regardless of the moral context in which they are uttered.
passage: One might propose that a person has a moral obligation not to lie whenever he makes a statement he believes to be false, intends the hearer to believe it, and has no overriding moral reason for the falsehood. This characterisation captures the volitional, communicative, and justificatory elements of the prohibition against lying. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a doctor tells a terminally ill patient that his condition is manageable, knowing this to be false and intending the patient to believe it. The doctor's motive is compassion: he wishes to spare the patient the anguish of knowing his true prognosis. Compassion, however earnest, is not customarily regarded as an overriding moral reason in the strict sense that self-defence or the prevention of grave harm might be. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear satisfied, and the definition pronounces the doctor's falsehood morally impermissible. Nevertheless, one might observe that the moral character of this particular falsehood is fundamentally different from that of a lie told for self-interest or malice. The doctor's intent is protective, the patient is in a position of extreme vulnerability, and the truth might produce suffering without conferring any compensating benefit. It appears that the definition, by treating all intentional falsehoods alike unless an overriding justification exists, fails to recognise that the speaker's compassionate motive and the hearer's vulnerability may substantially alter the moral assessment of a given falsehood.

definition: A person possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) the person habitually acts in the face of danger, (ii) the person does so from a settled disposition rather than momentary impulse, and (iii) the person's action is directed toward a genuinely worthy end.
conditions: The person habitually acts in the face of danger
The person does so from a settled disposition rather than momentary impulse
The person's action is directed toward a genuinely worthy end
counterexample: Consider a case in which a seasoned firefighter habitually rushes into burning buildings from a settled disposition cultivated over decades of service, and whose actions are directed toward the genuinely worthy end of saving lives. Yet suppose that on every occasion the firefighter acts not from any concern for the imperilled persons but solely from an addiction to the physiological thrill of danger itself, an addiction so consuming that, were the thrill absent, the firefighter would refuse to act even to save a child. The firefighter satisfies all three conditions and yet one should hesitate to ascribe courage, for the motivational structure is not that of a virtuous agent but of one enslaved to appetite.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent's motivation must include appropriate concern for the values at stake, rather than being driven by appetite or self-regarding desire.
passage: It has long been supposed that an adequate analysis of courage must attend to the habitual character of the courageous person's responses to danger. One might observe that a merely impulsive act of bravery, however admirable in its consequences, does not bespeak the settled disposition we associate with genuine virtue. Furthermore, it appears essential that the courageous agent direct his efforts toward some end that merits the risk undertaken; reckless daring in the service of trivial or base purposes would scarcely deserve commendation. Yet we may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a firefighter of long experience who habitually confronts peril from a settled disposition and whose actions are invariably directed toward the worthy end of preserving human life. If, however, the true spring of his conduct is not any concern for the endangered but rather an overpowering craving for the physiological excitement of danger, we are led to conclude that something essential to courage is wanting. The agent acts as courage demands, yet the inner economy of his motivation is that of compulsion rather than virtue. We must therefore recognise that habitual dangerous action, settled disposition, and worthy aim do not jointly suffice; one must further require that the agent be moved, at least in part, by a fitting regard for the values his action serves.

definition: An allocation of goods is just if and only if (i) each recipient receives a share proportional to his contribution, (ii) no recipient is deprived of the minimum necessary for subsistence, and (iii) the allocation procedure is impartial and free from personal favouritism.
conditions: Each recipient receives a share proportional to his contribution
No recipient is deprived of the minimum necessary for subsistence
The allocation procedure is impartial and free from personal favouritism
counterexample: Suppose that in a small agricultural commune, the harvest is divided by a disinterested arbiter who allocates shares strictly proportional to each member's labour, ensures that every member retains enough grain for subsistence, and applies no personal favouritism whatsoever. Yet one member, through no fault of her own, suffers a debilitating illness that drastically reduces her capacity to labour. She receives a subsistence-level share, but it is far less than what her needs demand given her medical condition. The allocation satisfies all three conditions yet fails to be just, for it takes no account of differential need arising from circumstances beyond the individual's control.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the requirement that a just allocation must attend to differential needs arising from circumstances for which the recipient bears no responsibility.
passage: The question of distributive justice has exercised philosophers since antiquity, and one of the most persistent proposals is that shares ought to be proportioned to contributions. To this principle one may add the humane qualification that no person shall fall below the threshold of subsistence, and the procedural demand that the allocator act without favouritism. It might appear that these three conditions jointly capture what we mean when we call an allocation just. Yet let us imagine a case which presses against this analysis. In a small commune, a scrupulously impartial arbiter divides the harvest in exact proportion to each worker's labour, ensuring that none falls below subsistence. One member, however, has been struck by a grave illness that sharply curtails her capacity to work, and she accordingly receives only a bare minimum, quite insufficient for her medical needs. One might observe that each of the stated conditions is met, yet something essential to justice seems absent. The allocation takes no account of the fact that her diminished contribution arises from misfortune rather than indolence. We are thus led to conclude that proportionality, subsistence floors, and procedural impartiality do not exhaust the demands of justice; the analysis must be supplemented by a sensitivity to needs that arise from circumstances beyond individual control.

definition: A person has a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if (i) the action would prevent significant harm to another, (ii) the person is capable of performing the action without excessive personal sacrifice, and (iii) no other person is better positioned to perform the action.
conditions: The action would prevent significant harm to another
The person is capable of performing the action without excessive personal sacrifice
No other person is better positioned to perform the action
counterexample: Suppose that a physician walking past a collapsed stranger is uniquely positioned to administer life-saving aid, is fully capable of doing so without excessive sacrifice, and no other person nearby possesses the requisite skill. Yet the stranger is an enemy combatant who, if revived, will with near certainty resume hostilities that will cause the deaths of several innocent persons. The action would prevent significant harm to the combatant, the physician can perform it without excessive sacrifice, and no one else is better positioned, yet it appears far from obvious that a moral obligation obtains, for fulfilling it would foreseeably enable greater harm to others.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition requiring that performance of the action not foreseeably enable harm to third parties that outweighs the harm prevented.
passage: It is commonly held that we are morally obliged to prevent harm when we can do so at tolerable cost and when no one else is better placed to intervene. These three conditions, taken together, appear to delineate the boundaries of obligatory rescue. One might observe, however, that they do not suffice to establish obligation in every case that satisfies them. Let us imagine a physician who encounters a gravely wounded stranger by the roadside. The physician possesses the skill and the instruments to save the stranger's life; the personal cost is modest; and no other qualified person is at hand. Yet suppose the stranger is an enemy combatant who, upon recovery, will almost certainly resume attacks that will claim multiple innocent lives. Each of the three conditions is fulfilled, yet one should hesitate to say that the physician is morally obliged to render aid, for the foreseeable consequence of doing so is a net increase in harm. We are led to recognise that an adequate account of moral obligation must include not merely the capacity to prevent immediate harm but also a consideration of the downstream consequences of one's intervention. The analysis, as stated, is silent on the matter of harms that flow from the very act of rescue, and this silence constitutes a significant lacuna.

definition: A person deserves a reward if and only if (i) the person has performed a meritorious action, (ii) the action was performed voluntarily and without coercion, and (iii) the action goes beyond what is minimally required by duty.
conditions: The person has performed a meritorious action
The action was performed voluntarily and without coercion
The action goes beyond what is minimally required by duty
counterexample: Consider a case in which a factory worker voluntarily and without coercion devises an ingenious method of increasing production, an action plainly meritorious and well beyond the minimum duties of her post. Yet suppose that her innovation was the product of a fortunate accident: she misread an instruction manual and, through sheer luck, stumbled upon a procedure that happened to work brilliantly. She neither understood the principles behind her discovery nor could replicate it. The three conditions are satisfied, yet it appears doubtful that she deserves a reward in the fullest sense, for her achievement owes nothing to skill, effort, or deliberate ingenuity.
missing_condition: The definition lacks a condition requiring that the meritorious outcome be attributable to the agent's deliberate skill or effort rather than to mere luck.
passage: The concept of desert occupies a central place in our moral thinking, and the question of what makes a person deserving of reward has received considerable attention. A natural proposal is that desert requires a meritorious action, voluntarily performed, that exceeds the demands of duty. These conditions seem jointly to capture the core of desert claims. Yet we may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a factory worker who, entirely of her own volition and without any coercion, devises a novel production method that greatly benefits her employer, an accomplishment well beyond her contractual obligations. If, however, the discovery was the result of a fortunate misreading of technical instructions and the worker neither grasps the principles involved nor could reproduce the result, one might observe that something essential to desert is absent. The three conditions are met in full, yet we should hesitate to say the worker truly deserves commendation in the way that a person who achieves a similar result through sustained ingenuity and effort would. The difficulty is that our analysis makes no mention of the causal relationship between the agent's capacities and the meritorious outcome. Desert, it appears, requires not merely that a good result follow from voluntary supererogatory action but that it be in some meaningful sense the product of the agent's own rational agency.

definition: Valid consent to a transaction is given if and only if (i) the consenting party understands the essential nature of the transaction, (ii) the consent is given freely without duress, and (iii) the consenting party is of sound mind and legal capacity.
conditions: The consenting party understands the essential nature of the transaction
The consent is given freely without duress
The consenting party is of sound mind and legal capacity
counterexample: Suppose that a landowner of sound mind freely agrees to sell a parcel to a developer, fully understanding that the transaction is a sale of land. Yet the developer has deliberately concealed from the landowner the fact that geological surveys show the land contains valuable mineral deposits worth a hundred times the sale price. The landowner understands the nature of the transaction, acts freely, and is of sound mind, yet the consent seems vitiated by the asymmetry of material information, for the landowner would never have agreed had she known the land's true worth.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the consenting party must not be labouring under a material misapprehension induced or exploited by the other party.
passage: The notion of consent is foundational to the moral evaluation of transactions between persons. An analysis that requires understanding of the essential nature of the transaction, freedom from duress, and soundness of mind appears to capture the principal elements that render consent valid. Yet one might observe that these conditions, while necessary, do not jointly suffice. Let us imagine a landowner of unimpeachable mental capacity who, entirely free of coercion, agrees to sell a tract of land to a developer, understanding perfectly well that the agreement constitutes a sale. The developer, however, has in his possession geological surveys demonstrating that the land harbours mineral deposits of extraordinary value, and he has taken care to conceal this information from the seller. The landowner satisfies all three conditions yet would never have consented had the material facts been disclosed. It appears that the consent, though freely given by a competent party who understands the character of the act, is in some important sense defective. We are thus led to conclude that the analysis must be augmented by a further condition concerning the absence of material misapprehension, particularly where such misapprehension has been cultivated or exploited by the party seeking consent. Without this supplement, the analysis countenances as valid agreements that our moral sensibility firmly rejects.

definition: Punishment of an offender is morally justified if and only if (i) the offender has committed a wrongful act, (ii) the punishment is imposed by a legitimate authority, and (iii) the severity of the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence.
conditions: The offender has committed a wrongful act
The punishment is imposed by a legitimate authority
The severity of the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence
counterexample: Suppose that a person commits a minor act of vandalism, that a legitimate court imposes a proportionate fine, and that all three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose further that the offender committed the act while suffering from a severe psychotic episode that deprived her entirely of the capacity to understand the nature of her actions or to conform her behaviour to the requirements of the law. She committed a wrongful act, the punishment is imposed by authority and is proportionate, yet it appears morally unjust to punish a person who lacked the mental capacity to have acted otherwise.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the offender possessed the mental capacity to understand the wrongfulness of her act and to have refrained from it.
passage: The justification of punishment has long been debated, and a common proposal holds that punishment is warranted where a wrongful act has been committed, a legitimate authority imposes the sanction, and the severity is proportionate to the offence. These conditions capture much of what we ordinarily require before we are prepared to say that punishment is just. Yet they do not suffice in every case. Consider a person who, in the grip of a severe psychotic episode, defaces a public monument. A competent court imposes a fine proportionate to the minor character of the offence. Each of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet one should hesitate to say that the punishment is morally justified, for the offender was wholly incapable of appreciating the wrongfulness of her conduct or of governing her behaviour in accordance with the law. It appears that the moral justification of punishment presupposes that the offender possessed a certain minimum of rational agency at the time of the act — that she could have understood what she was doing and could have chosen otherwise. The proposed analysis, in omitting any reference to mental capacity, countenances the punishment of persons whom it would be manifestly unjust to hold responsible, and must therefore be supplemented.

definition: A person is morally bound to keep a promise if and only if (i) the person has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to another, (ii) the promisee has relied upon the promise, and (iii) the promised act is not itself immoral.
conditions: The person has voluntarily undertaken a commitment to another
The promisee has relied upon the promise
The promised act is not itself immoral
counterexample: Suppose that a man voluntarily promises his elderly neighbour that he will drive her to a medical appointment. The neighbour relies upon the promise and makes no alternative arrangements. The promised act is entirely moral. Yet on the morning of the appointment, the man discovers that his own child has fallen gravely ill and requires immediate hospital attention. All three conditions for the binding force of the promise are met, yet it appears that the man is not morally bound to keep his promise under these changed circumstances, for a more urgent moral demand has arisen that overrides the obligation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition that the obligation to keep the promise is not overridden by a more urgent moral duty arising from changed circumstances.
passage: The obligation to keep one's promises is widely regarded as a cornerstone of moral life, and a natural analysis might hold that it obtains whenever one has voluntarily committed oneself, the promisee has relied upon the undertaking, and the promised act is not immoral. These conditions seem jointly to capture the grounds of promissory obligation. Yet we may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us imagine a man who has promised his elderly neighbour to drive her to a medical appointment on a particular morning. The neighbour, relying on this undertaking, declines other offers of transport. The promised act is innocent of any moral taint. On the morning in question, however, the man discovers that his own child has been taken seriously ill and must be rushed to hospital without delay. All three conditions are satisfied in full, yet one might observe that the man is not morally bound to fulfil his promise, for a far more pressing obligation has arisen. We are led to recognise that promissory obligations are not absolute but defeasible: they yield when circumstances generate competing duties of sufficient gravity. The analysis, as stated, makes no provision for such defeasibility and must therefore be regarded as incomplete.

definition: A person is morally responsible for an outcome if and only if (i) the person performed an action that causally contributed to the outcome, (ii) the person acted voluntarily, and (iii) the person foresaw or ought to have foreseen the outcome.
conditions: The person performed an action that causally contributed to the outcome
The person acted voluntarily
The person foresaw or ought to have foreseen the outcome
counterexample: Suppose a motorist voluntarily and carefully drives along a country road at moderate speed, taking every reasonable precaution. A child, completely hidden behind a solid wall, darts suddenly into the road and is struck. The motorist's driving causally contributed to the injury, the act of driving was voluntary, and yet no reasonable person could have foreseen a child emerging from behind an opaque wall. Condition (iii) is not met, and indeed we should not hold the motorist morally responsible. Now alter the case: suppose a different motorist drives the same road negligently at excessive speed, but by sheer luck nothing happens and no one is harmed. This motorist acted voluntarily, ought to have foreseen potential harm, yet caused no harmful outcome. Condition (i) fails.
