In neither case does the analysis yield the intuitively correct result — the first motorist is not responsible despite meeting conditions (i) and (ii), while the second motorist seems blameworthy despite failing condition (i).
missing_condition: The definition does not account for the degree of control the agent had over the circumstances that led to the outcome, nor does it accommodate moral blameworthiness in cases of negligent action where harmful outcomes do not materialise.
passage: The question of moral responsibility is intimately bound up with the notions of causation, voluntariness, and foresight. A standard proposal holds that one is responsible for an outcome when one has voluntarily performed an act that causally contributed to it and when the outcome was foreseeable. One might observe, however, that this analysis encounters difficulties in both directions. Consider a motorist who drives with exemplary care along a rural road. A child concealed entirely behind a solid wall suddenly runs into the path of the vehicle and is struck. The motorist's act of driving causally contributed to the harm, and the act was voluntary, yet no reasonable person could have anticipated the child's appearance. Our strong intuition is that the motorist bears no moral responsibility. Now suppose a second motorist who traverses the same road at reckless speed, yet by fortune encounters no one and causes no harm. This driver acted voluntarily and ought to have foreseen the risk, but no harmful outcome occurred. The analysis assigns responsibility in the first case, where it seems unwarranted, and withholds it in the second, where blame appears apt. We are thus led to conclude that the proposed conditions do not adequately capture the relationship between agency and responsibility; something further, pertaining to the agent's degree of control over the morally relevant circumstances, must be incorporated.

definition: Two agents are equally morally culpable for the same type of reckless act if and only if (i) both perform the same reckless action, (ii) both act with the same degree of reckless intention, and (iii) the circumstances in which they act are relevantly similar.
conditions: Both perform the same reckless action
Both act with the same degree of reckless intention
The circumstances in which they act are relevantly similar
counterexample: Suppose two drivers, equally reckless, drive through the same intersection at the same excessive speed on the same evening, with relevantly similar circumstances — same visibility, same traffic conditions. Yet one happens to strike and kill a pedestrian who stepped into the road, while the other, by sheer fortune, encounters no one. Both satisfy all three conditions, yet common moral judgment and legal practice treat the driver who killed as far more culpable than the one who did not, even though the difference between them is wholly a matter of luck. The analysis implies equal culpability, but our actual moral assessments diverge sharply.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of moral luck — the fact that actual outcomes, though beyond the agent's control, systematically influence moral judgements of culpability.
passage: It is a common moral conviction that culpability should depend upon what is within the agent's control — upon the quality of the will, as it were, rather than upon fortune. A natural analysis of comparative culpability might therefore hold that two persons who perform the same reckless act with the same degree of reckless intention under relevantly similar circumstances are equally culpable. This principle has considerable appeal, for it purports to ground blame in the agent's choice rather than in the vagaries of circumstance. Yet let us consider two motorists who, on the same evening, drive through the same intersection at identical excessive speeds under indistinguishable conditions. One strikes and kills a pedestrian; the other arrives at the intersection when no one happens to be crossing. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied, and the analysis accordingly pronounces them equally culpable. Yet it appears that moral sentiment, and indeed legal practice, judges the first driver far more harshly than the second. One might protest that this disparity is irrational, but the fact remains that the analysis fails to capture the way in which actual moral judgement operates. We are thus led to recognise that a complete account of culpability must reckon with the phenomenon of moral luck — the unsettling fact that outcomes beyond the agent's control enter systematically into our assessments of blame.

definition: A person has a right to free expression if and only if (i) the person is a member of a political community governed by law, (ii) the person's speech does not constitute direct incitement to imminent violence, and (iii) the person's speech is on a matter of public concern.
conditions: The person is a member of a political community governed by law
The person's speech does not constitute direct incitement to imminent violence
The person's speech is on a matter of public concern
counterexample: Suppose a poet, who is a member of a lawful political community, composes verses of a purely aesthetic character — celebrations of nature, meditations on love and loss — that bear no relation whatsoever to matters of public concern. Her speech incites no violence and violates no law. According to the proposed analysis, she lacks a right to free expression because her speech fails condition (iii). Yet it seems plainly wrong to deny that a person has a right to express herself on private, aesthetic, or personal matters. The right to free expression is not, and should not be, confined to speech on public affairs.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly restricts the right to speech on matters of public concern, failing to recognise that the right to free expression extends to private, artistic, and personal speech as well.
passage: The right to free expression is among the most cherished liberties in any democratic society, and attempts to analyse its scope typically begin with the exclusion of speech that threatens imminent violence. To this one may add the requirement that the speaker be a member of a community under the rule of law, so that the right operates within a framework of reciprocal obligations. A further restriction sometimes proposed is that the speech must address a matter of public concern, on the ground that the political justification for free expression lies in its contribution to democratic self-governance. Yet this third condition proves too restrictive. Let us imagine a poet who composes verses of a purely lyrical and personal character, celebrating the beauties of the natural world and exploring the private emotions of love and grief. Her work incites no violence; it simply does not engage with questions of public policy or social organisation. According to the proposed analysis, she lacks a right to free expression in respect of these compositions. But this conclusion is difficult to accept, for the impulse to artistic and personal expression appears to lie at the very heart of what the right protects. We are led to conclude that the analysis, in confining the right to speech on public matters, draws the boundary too narrowly and excludes forms of expression that any adequate account must protect.

definition: An action is generous if and only if (i) the agent gives something of value to another, (ii) the giving is voluntary and not compelled, and (iii) the agent expects no reciprocal benefit.
conditions: The agent gives something of value to another
The giving is voluntary and not compelled
The agent expects no reciprocal benefit
counterexample: Suppose a wealthy industrialist voluntarily donates a large sum to a charitable institution, expects nothing in return, and satisfies all three conditions. Yet suppose that the donation is motivated entirely by a desire to humiliate a rival who has given a smaller sum, and the industrialist cares nothing whatsoever for the beneficiaries. The gift is voluntary, valuable, and disinterested in the sense of expecting no reciprocal benefit, yet it seems wrong to call it generous, for the underlying motivation is spite rather than benevolence.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent act from a benevolent motive — a genuine concern for the welfare of the recipient or some worthy cause — rather than from malice or vanity.
passage: Generosity is commonly reckoned among the virtues, and an intuitive analysis might identify it with the voluntary giving of something valuable without expectation of return. These three conditions appear to distinguish generous acts from exchanges, from gifts made under compulsion, and from calculated investments. Yet they do not suffice. Let us imagine a wealthy industrialist who donates a vast sum to a hospital, acts entirely of his own volition, and anticipates no benefit to himself. His sole motivation, however, is to publicly shame a business rival whose own charitable donation was more modest. The industrialist is wholly indifferent to the hospital and its patients; the gift is an instrument of competitive spite. One might observe that all three conditions are met, and yet the act strikes us as something other than generosity. It has the outward form of generosity but lacks the inner character — the concern for another's well-being — that we associate with the virtue. We are led to conclude that the analysis must include a condition pertaining to the agent's motive: genuine generosity requires not merely voluntariness and disinterestedness but also a benevolent regard for those whom the gift is intended to benefit.
